info: Michael Achner was the brother of Joseph and Philip. The model was elongated with very bulbous arching and small edges. It was generally considered an unattractive work.
Philip Achner created an oversized Stainer model. It featured neat work with a small edge.
Alexandre Alfred Acoulon was active in Paris in 1889. He was created Chevalier of the Legion of Honour in 1900.

William John Acton was active from 1868, initially working in Woolwich before moving to 472 Katherine Road, Forest Gate, London in 1898. By 1904, he had completed 210 violins, 19 violas, 29 cellos, and 21 double basses, utilizing Stainer, Stradivari, and personal models.

The most talented of the Adam bow makers, Jean "Grand" Adam was born in Mirecourt and learned the craft from his father, Jean Dominique. Adam worked with his father until 1842, at which point he moved to Paris to work for J.B.

Jean Adam was a bow maker and the founder of a notable dynasty of Mirecourt archetiers. He was the first of the Adam family of bow makers, and evidence of his work suggests he had some interaction with Charles Tourte, showing a Tourte influence in his designs. Adam later collaborated with his son, Jean Dominique. His sticks were generally octagonal and branded: ADAM.

Son of Jean Adam. Probably apprenticed to his father around 1807-1810, and became his father's assistant. He produced good quality work in the style of Etienne Pajeot, although his output was limited. His bows are stamped 'Adam'. Some bows before 1842 were made in collaboration with his son, Jean 'Grand' Adam.

One of Denmark’s most prolific makers. He trained in Jutland with Frederik Wilhelm Hansen, of whom his early instruments (c. 1876–1900) are very reminiscent. From 1904 his instruments become somewhat heavier. From 1922 onwards we see elaborate labels made from parchment with letters printed in gold.
Johann Georg Adler produced fine quality trade instruments. They were branded as 'J. G. Adler. '.

Hendrick Aerninck worked in Leiden, Netherlands until 1677, when he briefly moved to Amsterdam. He worked in Leiden from 1678 to 1703 before finally returning to Amsterdam. His fine workmanship was in the early Flemish style, featuring full arching and extended corners, along with painted or inked purfling. Two violins by him are in the Musical Instrument Museum, Brussels.
Marcel Aerts was the son of René Aerts. He studied under Emile Jeandat in Mirecourt and Emile Laurent in Brussels.
Rene Aerts apprenticed in Mirecourt. He worked in Germany and England before establishing his own shop at 7, avenue de la Régence in Brussels in 1921.

Luigi Agostinelli interned in Kenya during the Second World War, where he crafted instruments for the camp's orchestra. He returned to Italy in 1944 and established himself as a professional luthier in Torre Pellice, Turin, before moving to Milan in 1953. His work was respectable, following the style of various classical makers. He completed 14 violins, three violas, and two cellos.

Edmund Aireton was a pupil of Peter Wamsley working in Piccadilly and from Hog Lane after 1805. His instruments are made to a very high standard, consistent with the best workmanship from the Wamsley workshop, but typically finished with a thin yellow varnish.
Franco Albanelli was a pupil of Gaetano Pollastri and his work is closely related to the Pollastri model and style.
Giuseppe Albani was the son of Mathias Albani (II) and worked in Contrada de Milano in the year 17..

Johann Michael Albani was the son and pupil of Matthias Albani (I). He served as an assistant, son-in-law, and successor to W. Sagmayr. He was better known for cellos, some featuring carved heads, than for violins. His instruments had a slender form and a fine, thickly textured red-brown varnish.

Matthias Albani had an important influence on the work of Tyrolean violin makers in the 17th and 18th centuries. His work, which is often confused with that of his father, Matthias Albani I, is generally on a Brothers Amati model, with an attractive orange varnish inclined to craquel.
Johann Michael Albani was the son and pupil of Matthias Albani (I). He served as an assistant, son-in-law, and successor to W.
Charles Francis Albert was a violin and bow maker, the son of John Albert . He emigrated to the USA in 1854 and established his own business in 1865 after working for his father.
Eugene John Albert . Son and successor of John Albert .
John Albert practiced law in Germany. He emigrated to the USA in 1848, working in New York.

Ferdinando Alberti operated at the `segno di Corona', which had previously been the address of Giovanni Grancino. A later label indicated the address as `Contrada del Pesce', but it remained at the 'sign of the crown'.
Austin Alden created a Stradivari model that utilized American maple and light golden oil varnish. A manuscript label accompanied it.
R. E. Aldinger studied at the Bretch School of Violin Making in 1917.

Jean-François Aldric was third in a line of Mirecourt violin makers, but moved to Paris in 1785, where he worked for the next six decades in various locations. Historical evidence shows that he was the first French makers to acquire Italian instruments from famed dealer Luigi Tarisio in 1827.

A label was used in commercial Mirecourt productions that were imported to England by Beare and Son around 1925. The label read: modèle de / Rue de la Gare, No. 71 / Nicolas Aldric, Père / B. S. Luthier à Nantes, an.. L.

John Alexander was a pupil of J. Briefly, an otherwise unknown maker in Birmingham. He worked with Thomas Simpson of Handsworth, Birmingham. As a violinist and amateur maker, he had a limited production and produced good work on classical models.
American maker trained in Cremona. Went into partnership with Joseph Curtin in Ann Arbor, Michigan, in the 1980s; now runs own violin making workshop, Alf Studios.
Amateur maker. Guarneri model.
School of Mirecourt
Samuel Allen worked for the firm of W.E. Hill & Sons in the 1880s and is known as one of its finest craftsmen.
Norfolk

Armando Altavilla studied under F. Verzella, who was a student of Antonio Gagliano (II). His work exhibited fine craftsmanship in the Gagliano style and was very persuasive. He used good materials and applied a fine golden-orange varnish, often featuring a crackled texture.
Julius Altricher produced various grades of instruments in several models. Bows were made based on French and German patterns.
Brian Alvey worked for the firm of W.E. Hill & Sons from 1966 to 1978. His bows for the firm are marked with the number 15.

The Stainer model, occasionally featuring painted purfling, was rather ordinary work but possessed extraordinary varnish with a thick texture and a good red-orange color. An example dated 1724 was illustrated in Hamma (1986).
Japanese. School of Cremona

Known as the founding father of the violin, Andrea Amati appears to have been responsible for creating the definitive modern form of the instrument. His basic design was refined by later makers, particularly Stradivari, but in essence it remains unchanged today.

Antonio Amati, son of the man credited with inventing the modern violin, worked in his father Andrea's shop for the twenty years preceding Andrea's death in 1577. In fact, most of the instruments bearing Andrea Amati's label from the late 1560s-1570s are probably the work of Antonio.

Antonio and Girolamo (or Hieronymous) Amati, also known as Brothers Amati, inherited the workshop of their father Andrea in the late 1570s. The two brothers produced instruments together, developing and strengthening their father's models, which were then copied widely in Italy and in the Netherlands and England.

By the time Girolamo Amati entered the Amati workshop around 1575, his father Andrea Amati was about 70 years old, and his older brother Antonio was already an experienced maker at 35. At this point the instruments bearing an Andrea Amati label were in ... fact probably the work of Antonio, and it was Antonio who schooled his younger brother in the craft of violin making.

Nicolò Amati's great success in fostering the art of violin making in Cremona produced a generation of luminaries, including Francesco and Vincenzo Rugeri , Andrea ... Guarneri , and Antonio Stradivari , and contributed to the spread of violin making to other cities.

Nicolò Amati was the fourth and greatest in a line of influential Cremonese violin makers. His grandfather, Andrea Amati , is known as the inventor of the modern violin, and his father, Girolamo , refined the models Nicolò began to execute to great success ... around 1620, under his father's Brothers Amati label.
Graduate of the International School of Violin Making in Cremona and a teacher there since 1977.

Luiz Amorim was born in Curitiba, Brazil, and graduated in mechanics from the Federal Center of Technological Studies in 1979, and drawing, painting, and sculpture at the State School of Music and Fine Arts in 1985. After working as an artist for a decade, Luiz began his instrument making career in 1995 by making lyres and kanteles, before moving on to quartet instruments.
born in Naples
Bernard Anciaume established his workshop in rue Derriere le Palais. His work was characterized by painted purfling and yellow varnish. He branded his instruments with the name 'Bernard Anciaume. '.
Carl Andersen was chiefly a repairer. He produced a small number of his own instruments. The work was decent.
Instruments are rare. Mainly a restorer.

American bow maker, studied at the Violin Making School of America in Salt Lake City, before working with Frank Passa in San Francisco from 1978-1979. Is now established independently in Washington State.
A. Anderson created models based on Amati and Stradivari, which were typically oversized. They featured a fine amber varnish.

Arvil Olaf Anderson was a full-time violin maker since his retirement in 1971, having studied under Bela Latjay in San Diego. He created Guarneri model violins and later transitioned to Stradivari model violins and cellos, using Tertis and Clifford Hoing patterns for violas.

Francesco Andina learned violin-making in Milan. He established himself in Lugano in 1924. He won a medal at the Geneva Exhibition in 1927. The instrument followed the Stradivari model. It was signed and branded on the interior. A printed label read: Francesco Andina Luganese / fece a Lugano l'anno 19.. / No..
Bow maker / Violin maker (1886 – after 1951)
Maxime Angard was professionally active from 1890. He established himself at 23 Passage Delaruelle, Paris, and later at 82 rue Liebnitz.

Franz Angerer was a pupil of Ferdinand and Leopold Feilenreiten. He was a maker of violins and guitars. He established his business in Plaz Henrietta in 1885 and later relocated to Mariahilferstrasse. He received a gold medal at the Chicago Exhibition in 1893. His work was noted for fine craftsmanship with a gold-yellow varnish that had good texture and consistency.

Vincenzo Annarumma, a pupil of Postiglione in Naples, worked briefly in Rome after initially returning to Salerno. He was influenced by the Gaglianos and modeled his instruments in their style, using a clear spirit varnish that varied from yellow to dark red.
Contemporary Mantua maker. Instruments are branded "AG" to the back button and internally.
Gaetano Antoniazzi was presumed to be a pupil of Enrico Ceruti. He lived in Mantua from around 1855, likely assisting the elderly Giuseppe Ceruti.

Gregorio Antoniazzi was a rarely encountered but significant maker of the Bergamo school, not associated with the later Antoniazzi family of Cremona and Milan. His origins remained unestablished. He produced a very fine soft orange or darker red varnish along with a classically proportioned form, although his head was rather undistinguished.
Son of Ceruti pupil Gaetano Antoniazzi , Riccardo Antoniazzi was born in Cremona but moved to Milan around 1870. In 1880 he went to Nice, probably to work for Nicolò Bianchi and ... returned in 1886.

Romeo Antoniazzi may have been taught by his older brother Riccardo , whom he did not equal as a craftsman. He led a chaotic life, working alternately for his brother, on his own, for Leandro Bisiach , and Monzino ... and Sons .
based in Detmold
Georges Apparut was the son and pupil of Léon Apparut. He worked for Blanchard in Lyons from 1896 to 1899 and for Mougenot in Brussels from 1900 to 1903.
The partnership of Georges Apparut and Paul Hilaire in Mirecourt. Hilaire continued the workshop alone after Apparut's death in 1948.
The work was very crude, utilizing poor wood and unsuitable varnish.
Trieste and Milan
Ulderico Arcangeli received a diploma at the Cremona exhibition in 1937. His fine instruments were noted for their rich golden oil varnish.
Lorenzo Arcangioli worked in the Carcassi style, featuring an elegant slim model and Stainer soundholes. The varnish was gold to orange, though it was hard and glassy.

Galileo Arcellaschi was a pupil of C. Oddone. He initially established himself in Turin in 1935 before returning to his birthplace in Como. He concentrated on cellos, with one example presented at the Cremona competition in 1949. His work was characterized by a good, well-ordered approach based on a classically-derived personal model.
Job Ardern apprenticed as a carpenter. He began making violins in 1855 as a hobby and was self-taught.
Studied in Cremona and opened independent workshop there in 1989. Teacher at the Cremona International School of Violin Making.

Isaiah H. Arey produced neat work on classical models. His ideas about violin making developed through personal study and experimentation, leading to most of his patterns being of his own design. He crafted 86 violins and 37 guitars from old woods salvaged from demolished buildings. He also devised a new form of chinrest.
Studied with Roberto Ignesti Igino Sderci from 1979. Now established in Florence.
Rowan Armour-Brown studied under G. B. Morassi in Cremona  from 1972 to 1975 and later worked for G. Solomon in Somerset in 1976.
François Arnould collaborated with G. Contal in the production of 'Arnoldo Violaline', an original instrument that was short-lived.
Oberammergau
Leo Aschauer worked at Wilfer in Berlin and Gartner in Stuttgart before he returned to Mittenwald to become the director of the school there in 1923.
John N. Aschow apprenticed in Denmark and later became a pupil at the Mittenwald School.

John Askew was an amateur maker of fair competence active from 1874. He received a diploma at the International Exhibition in London in 1885. His work followed the Stradivari model and featured a transparent golden-orange varnish.
Samuel Askey worked for Morrison and Corsby. He owned a workshop at 9 Fleet Lane from around 1820. His work was slightly coarse and featured broad purfling.
Liverpool
Spanish priest and luthier who worked as a maker and restorer at the court of Madrid between 1773 and 1795. He taught his nephew, Silverio Ortega.

Earsel Vivian Atchley trained as an engineer under his grandfather Edward Atchley before starting a violin business in Kansas City in 1948. He created Stradivari and Guarneri models, along with copies of individual master instruments.
John Atkins was an engineer and an amateur violin maker and collector during his retirement. He opened a professional atelier in his final years but died unexpectedly.

William Atkinson worked as a seaman until around 1869, when he crafted his first instrument during an apprenticeship as a joiner. He became a professional violin maker in Tottenham, north London, operating from a general supply shop on Church Road.
Claude Aubert was a maker of plucked instruments based in Troyes, France. He employed A.

Joseph Aubry apprenticed as a cabinet maker. He began making violins in 1892. He received prizes in Paris in 1921 and in Metz in 1922. In 1926, he moved to Le Havre, first residing on rue A. Briand and later relocating to rue Jules Siegfried. His work was very distinguished, following the styles of Stradivari, Guarneri, and other Italian masters.

Luigi Auciello was active in Milan from 1935. The quartet exhibited in Cremona in 1937 and two violins in 1949. His work was unremarkable, featuring orange-brown clear spirit varnish. Each instrument was signed and branded, and given the name of a prominent musician. A printed label accompanied them.
Jacques Audinot was the son of Pierre Audinot . He apprenticed at R. & M.

Justin Audinot worked for George Withers in London before returning to Mirecourt in 1898, where he established a firm at 5 rue du Brueil that produced high-quality commercial instruments. He ran the family business until his death in 1903, after which he was succeeded by his brother, Emile Audinot. The firm continued to be operated by his family for many years.
Nestor Dominique Audinot was the son and pupil of Léopold Audinot in Mirecourt. He worked with Sébastian Vuillaume in Paris from 1863 to 1868 before establishing his own shop at 17 Faubourg St Denis.
Son of Jacques and succeeded him in 1984.
Pierre Audinot was the son and pupil of Victor Audinot and the father of Jacques. He received the prize for best young luthier in Mirecourt in 1907 and 1908.
Victor Audinot, son of Charles Audinot, studied under Chardin and T. Barbé.
See Victor Audinot.
Lyon

Aurelio Aureli was active from 1910. He created Cremonese and Neapolitan models featuring clear gold to orange spirit varnish. Medals were awarded to him in Rome in 1917 and 1919. His work was signed internally. A manuscript label was present.

Alfredo Averna was the son and pupil of Gesualdo Averna. He worked alongside his brother Alfonso and his son Enrico. Established in Palermo in 1930, he exhibited at Cremona in 1937. His work included Cremonese and Neapolitan models, and he also made bows.

Enrico Averna was a pupil of his uncle Gesualdo Averna. He established himself in Palermo in 1930. His work featured Cremonese and Neapolitan models with golden-brown varnish. He exhibited at Cremona in 1937.

Gesualdo Averna was the son and pupil of Giuseppe Averna. He worked in Rome for Giuseppe Rossi. For the initial years of the 20th century, he lived in America before returning to Sicily. He created various models with a golden-brown or orange varnish. His work was branded on the button with 'G. A. '.
Albert Aylor was a soldier in the American Civil War and a violin maker. He served during the conflict and created violins.

Luigi Azzola worked in the Turin style after Fagnola. He may have collaborated with Fagnola and his sons after around 1925. His output was small, with very few authenticated works known. The workmanship was good, featuring dark red varnish.

J. A. Baader & Co. manufactured various grades of instruments, ranging from machine-made to hand-crafted. All types of plucked and bowed instruments were produced. The factory was located at 155 Obere Markstrasse. Founded in 1790 in Mittenwald a/Isar, Bayern, it was owned by Max Baader and operated under the name Koniglich Bayerische Hof-Saiteninstrumenten Fabrik. No. 18.
Anton Bachmann was a repairer to the Prussian court. He was a fairly prolific maker of Cremonese models featuring pinched arches, decent workmanship, and varnish. He also made guitars.
Sweden

Born in Łodygowice near Żywiec in 1866, Władysław Piotr (Ladislaus) Baczyński was first employed as a clerk in the Billing Deparment of the Imperial Royal Railway in Kraków in 1887. He started learning violin-making in 1894 with Gustav Häussler in Kraków, before collaborating with Henry Richard Knopf in New York from 1904 to 1906.

Piero Badalassi was active in Pisa from 1936. He was a self-taught maker known for personal and classical models that were well finished. He gained high respect as a repairer and restorer. He received awards at Cremona in 1937, 1965, and 1973.
Carlo Badarello produced good professional work that was widely exported. He crafted all instruments of the violin family, as well as viola d'amores and viols.

Nephew of Antonio I, was also a maker, with his own shop in Venice from 1805, at the sign of S. Michele in the Merceria S. Salvador. His work is difficult to distinguish from that of Antonio (I), and most published sources conflate the two makers. trio.
England

Leslie Bailey worked for the firm of W.E. Hill & Sons from 1919 to 1939, generally working on lesser grade bows. After his departure he abandoned bow making altogether. His bows for the firm bear the number 4.

Charles Bailly, son of Joseph Bailly, worked in Mirecourt from the early 20th century and produced instruments for the Mougenot and Laberte-Humbert firms. He established his own shop in 1909 on rue General Mangin, where he remained active until 1926. His creations featured classical models with a transparent golden-brown varnish and were signed with his labels.
Jenny Bailly was the daughter and successor of Paul Bailly. She was one of the first recorded female makers.

French violin maker Paul Bailly was born in Mirecourt in 1844 and studied with Jules Gaillard before working in Brussels for Nicolas François Vuillaume , and in Paris for his brother J.B. ... Vuillaume .
René Bailly was the son and pupil of Paul Bailly. He emigrated to the USA and established himself in Dubuque, Iowa. His work featured a Stradivari model and red-brown oil varnish.

Luigi Bajoni created a large model, slightly over-square and somewhat Brescian in style, but it was quite ugly in truth. It featured a broad edge, large open scroll, and extended soundholes. The varnish was clear or lightly-tinted over a dark ground. Some cellos were useful. He made it in the year 1856 in Milano. 8.
Calvin Baker worked for A. W.

Timothy Baker was the last bowmaker employed by the firm of W.E. Hill & Sons assigned a numeral brand to identify his work. He began working for the firm in 1981 and left three years later to work for J. & A. Beare where he worked from 1984-2000.

William Baker was a significant early English maker, recognized for a collection of violins, violas, and at least one cello that still existed. A viola remained at the Oxford Music School until the early 20th century.
Istvan Balazs retrained as a maker following an accident that prevented him from playing. He was active from 1980.

Francesco Balbo was active from 1978. He was a pupil of G. Spataffi. In 1981, he established his own business in Gubbio, where he made all instruments of the violin family, including double basses and bows, using personal models. He moved to Perugia in 1983. Francesco Balbo liutaio / fatto in.. anno 19.. Francseco Balbo fecit / in Perugia Anno 19..

Simultaneously a musical instrument maker and repairer, bass player, and weapons manufacturer, Giuseppe Baldantoni probably learned his craft simply by imitating instruments available to him. The need to work quickly sometimes affected his work adversely, but on the whole it represents the finest of 19th century violin making in the Marche region.

Dante Baldoni, a self-taught Italian maker from Numana in the Marche region, emigrated to Buenos Aires around 1900. His reputation attracted commissions from notable musicians such as Ferenc von Vecsey and Jan Kubelik, for whom he created a copy of the Stradivari 'Emperor' violin.

Tommaso Balestrieri referred to himself on his labels as Cremonese, but no record of him has been found in the city to date. It is likely that he was born in one of the villages situated between Cremona and Mantua.
Frank Cannon Ball was a pupil of J. Bradford in Granby, Quebec.
Harvey Ball was a professional clock maker. He was an active luthier from around 1865 and made approximately 75 violins based on a Stradivari model. His workmanship was fine.
Robert Ballantyne worked in Edinburgh from 1827, sharing the same address as John Blair at 8 Potterrow. The two men evidently had some association, and there were obvious similarities in their work.

Leonard Percy Balmforth was a maker known for his new work on the Stradivari model and had a small output. He was a specialist restorer and a pupil of Paul Bailly, who worked for Dykes in Leeds during the 1890s. He founded L. P. Balmforth & Son, which became the leading business in the north of England after Dykes relocated to London.
Emil Baltensperger was a prolific maker, active in Chur from around 1901. He had a very original approach and produced well-varnished works.
Born in Tjotta, Norway and moved to Boston, USA, in the 1880s. Made first violin in around 1897 as a hobby. Worked for Ole Bryant and established his own shop in 1920. Made more than 200 instruments.

Pier Angelo Balzarini attended Cremona International School of Violin Making, where he studied under Pistoni, Zambelli, and Bulfari. He was a prize winner at Poznan in 1987 and in Cremona in 1985 and 1988.

Known as the ‘English Amati’, Banks emerged as a stringed instrument maker in the 1760s in Salisbury in the West of England with a fully developed style representative of the best London practices of the day. Despite living 75 miles from the capital city, he had considerable connection with the London trade.

Benjamin Banks moved away from the family shop in Salisbury before 1777, working in Golden Square, London, on what was presumably Sherrard Street (now Sherwood Street). He finally relocated to Liverpool with his brothers, where he died. A label in a cello of small Stradivari model also bore his signature on all parts.

James Banks was the son of Benjamin Banks (I). He continued the shop on Catharine Street in Salisbury with his brother James until 1811, when they relocated to Liverpool, opening a shop at 28 Church Street and later at 10 Great Nelson Street. Many unfinished instruments remained in the house, which the Palmer brothers bought and sold, finishing them up to sell within the London trade.

James and Henry Banks (1758-1831 and 1770-1830) assisted their father, Benjamin until his death in 1795, and continued his business in Salisbury until 1811 before moving to Liverpool where they worked until 1831. Although instruments are labelled to indicate a partnership between the two brothers, Henry’s skills involved the ... pianoforte, and signatures inside these instruments reveal that James was the only maker.
Dezsö Bárány, regarded as one of the most talented pupils of Pilat, worked in Vienna for T. Zach and J.
Worked in Dijon, Avallon and Paris, 1840. Son of Jacques.
François-Jacques Barbé worked in Dijon and Avallon from around 1810 to 1850. Henley regarded Jacques and François as distinct makers (father and son) in the first edition of Vannes.

Mathias Theophile Auguste Barbé was a bow maker. He possibly apprenticed to C. N. Bazin and later worked for Gand Frères. In 1886, he moved to Paris to work full-time for the business now known as Gand & Bernardel. He returned to Mirecourt around 1890 but continued to follow the Paris style of Voirin. His light brown round sticks were branded: A. BARBÉ.
Télesphore Amable Barbé apprenticed to Derazey. He was the son of Francois Jacques Barbé and was employed by Jacquot in Nancy, Miremont, Gand, and Bernardel in Paris, ultimately working for J.

Armando Barbieri was active in Forli from 1924. He was a sculptor, artist, and poet. He made all members of the violin family, including double basses, to a good standard. His work featured classical models and Antoniazzi-style designs. The varnish was dark red-brown, with a quite thick and brittle texture. The scroll was well-balanced and concentric.

Bruno Barbieri was a pupil of A. Zatta and was influenced by G. Ornati. He established himself in Mantua in 1947. He received a gold medal at the Cremona Biennale in 1971. Over 600 instruments were credited to him, modeled on Scarampella and Balestrieri. He was awarded medals at Cremona in 1967 and 1971, as well as at Bagnocavallo in 1971.
Brother of Bruno.
Anthony Barbuto was born in Italy and claimed descent from Giofredo Cappa. He completed about 60 instruments, utilizing Stradivari and Guarneri models and applying golden or red-brown varnish.
Giuseppe (di Saladino) Bargelli was a prolific maker in the Florentine tradition, assisted by his son Gastone (b. 1921). Initially self-taught, he was guided by Sderci after 1918.
Georges Emile Barjonnet was a bow maker. He was the son of Jules Henri Barjonnet.

Arthur John Barnes was a bow maker at W. E. Hill & Sons from 1919 to 1939. Originally trained as a cabinet maker, he produced bows stamped with the number 5. Although his deafness hindered his ability to communicate and limited him to making lesser grade bows, he was recognized as a skilled craftsman. He left the firm to serve in WWII.

Albert Mullins and S. Bowley Barnes established “The Jo” in 1894, initially as banjo specialists and manufacturers based in Bournemouth, but rapidly diversified to all kinds of musical instruments as importers and wholesalers.
Giuseppe Giovanni Baroni was active in Santa Margherita Ligure, Rapallo, Chiavari, and Milan. He was an amateur maker who utilized classical Cremonese models and applied a good red-brown varnish.

One of the first generation of violin makers to settle in Piccadilly, becoming the most prestigious group of makers in London in a period lasting from roughly the 1720s to the 1760s, both in terms of their refinement of style and their location in the heart of the wealthy and growing West-End. Barrett may have been active in the late 1600s, for 'A Double Bass with a Case for it made by Mr Barrett 16.' ... is recorded in the inventory of instruments belonging to Handel's patron, the Duke of Chandos in 1720.
Kershaw Barrett was an amateur maker who used a Stradivari model.
John Barrow was a pupil of Ernest Gamble in Leicester. He moved to Bridgeport, Connecticut around 1924.

Eduard Bartek was a pupil of Engleder. He also worked with Nemessanyi until 1872, after which he established himself independently. He employed A. Bergmann, M. Remenyi, and K. Langfurth. Bartek received a gold medal at the Paris Exhibition in 1878. His business was not successful, and he died young. Few violins by him are extant, showcasing fine workmanship based on Stradivari and Guarneri models.
Robert Barth was the son, pupil, and successor of Ludwig Barth. In 1934, he took over the direction of the works. He retired in 1944 after losing his left arm.

Christian Barthe apprenticed with Gilles Duhaut when he was 16. He perfected his skills with Stephane Thomachot and Jean-Jacques Millant, and has worked in Paris since 1997. He has won prizes in various competitions, including in 2016 in Paris for a viola bow.
Andreas Nicolaus Bartl was probably the son of Christoph Bartl. He created high-arched instruments featuring golden-amber or red-brown varnish. Several viola d'amores were extant.

One of two brothers (along with John Barton) making violins for the London trade. George Barton was established at Wych Street from 1776-1794 suggesting a close relationship with the violin maker John Carter from whom he probably learned his trade.

John Barton was the brother of George Barton and worked at 40 Shoe Lane, Fleet Street from around 1775 to 1791, and then at 26 Ship Yard, Temple Bar from 1791 to approximately 1810. Instruments were supplied to C. & S.

François Barzoni made trade instruments at Château-Thierry, France exclusively for Beare & Son of London from 1880 to 1920. They were very well-crafted Stradivari and Guarneri models, utilizing good materials, though the varnish was hard and ranged in color from golden-yellow to red-brown. They were branded: 'FB'. Bows were branded: BARZONI.
Richard Bassler was a pupil of Pfretzschner and produced high-quality modern bows. Bows were made for J. Stüber in The Hague.
Joseph Bassot established himself in Paris in 1774, at 1 rue Chabannais from 1788. His early instruments featured an undistinguished Mirecourtian style with yellow varnish.
Edmond Bastien worked for Deroux in Paris before settling in Nancy, where he was chiefly known as a repairer.
Alfio Batelli studied under V. de Zorzi. He worked for Paoletti Vezio before starting his own business in via Tito Speri, Florence.
W. J. Batho was cited by Vannes, Lütgendorff, and Henley specifically for cellos and double basses, but was not mentioned by Meredith Morris. No additional details were known.

Fritz Bauer studied under W. Bertholdt from 1921 to 1924. He established himself independently in Stuttgart in 1925. He moved to Bissingen from 1945 to 1955, but later returned to Stuttgart. He was credited with 127 violins, eight violas, and seven cellos, using Stradivari and Guarneri models.
Jean Bauer was the son of Gaston Bauer and a pupil of M. Didier in Mattaincourt.
Adolf Baur was the son and pupil of Martin Baur . He worked for J.

Martin Baur was stationed in Stuttgart during his military service. He acquired knowledge of lutherie from Thumhardt in Munich and established his shop in Stuttgart in 1824. His work was fine and done in the French manner. He also made good bows.

Josef Bausch trained at Markneukirchen. He worked in Schwerin in 1921, Bad Doberan in 1926, and Rostock in 1928. He was another claimant to the discovery of Stradivari's 'secret. ' Theories were published by him for graduating and shaping the plates, as well as describing the qualities of his varnish. He produced accurate copies of individual Stradivari and Guarneri instruments.

Karl Friedrich Ludwig Bausch worked in New York for several years before returning to Leipzig in 1860 to join his father, leading to the establishment of Bausch and Sons. He continued to make bows in the Bausch style. The violins produced were of no particular distinction.
Ludwig Christian August Bausch was a bow maker. He studied under Fritzsche in Dresden from 1818 to 1822.

Otto Julius Bausch was the brother of Ludwig Bausch jr. and a pupil of his father, Ludwig Christian Bausch. He also completed an apprenticeship with Vauchel in Offenbach. In 1871, he succeeded to the business in Leipzig and was later succeeded by A. Paulus. He produced fine bows and somewhat undistinguished violins featuring red-orange varnish.

Francois Xavier Bazin was one of the first of this great family of French bow makers. He trained in Mirecourt and is thought to have served an apprenticeship with Dominique Peccatte in Paris before returning home to establish his own business in the 1840s.

Son of Charles Louis Bazin , Charles Alfred Bazin first studied bow making with his father in Mirecourt beginning in 1922. He showed early promise as an interpreter of the family's signature style and as a businessman, and upon returning from WWII he set up his own shop in Mirecourt.

The youngest son of Charles Nicolas Bazin and the only one to pursue bow making exclusively, Charles Louis Bazin took over his father's shop in 1907. Charles Nicolas remained a presence in the shop until his death in 1915.

Son of Mirecourt bowmaker François Xavier Bazin , Charles Nicolas Bazin inherited the family workshop in 1865 following his father's death from cholera. Charles Nicolas was only 18 at the time, but already had ample experience from his father's training, and the shop flourished under his leadership.

One of the earliest Bazin bow makers. His father worked with Francois Didelot, a luthier and furniture maker, and the family had connections with Barthelemy Simon. It is possible that he served his apprenticeship with Dominique Peccatte, but there is no evidence to support this. He had six children, including Charles Nicolas II, who worked with and succeeded him.
English instrument dealer who founded Beare & Son, which later became J.& A. Beare.

Son of Arthur Beare, William trained as a makerwith Marc Laberte in Mirecourt in the late 1920s before joining the Beare firm in 1929. He took over the running of the business after his father's death in 1954. His career was as a connoisseur and expert and instruments made by him are scarce. The thirty-or-so that he made are reflective of fine French workmanship.
Carl F. Becker Jr. began working with his father Carl Sr. around 1934 and soon joined him at the Chicago firm of William Lewis & Son.
Carl G. Becker Sr's grandfather Herman Macklett was an early American violin maker, and Carl followed in his footsteps from a young age.

Mario Bedocchi first trained as a carpenter before taking up violin making. The influence of Venetian maker Ettore Siega is visible, as Siega was his primary teacher. Bedocchi's well-regarded work usually bears a spirit-based varnish, often pale in hue, but sometimes vivid red or brown.

An award winning bow maker, Emmanuel Bégin is based in Montréal and began his training at the age of 14 under the tutelage of his father, Louis Bégin. He has also spent time training with Yannick Le Canu in Lille in France in 2008 before returning to Montréal a few years later where he is stationed at Atelier de Lutherie and continues to make bows.
city: Montréal and began his training at the age of

info: Graduate of the Newark school. Has worked in Grenoble, Hamburg and Paris. Now has an independent workshop near Frankfurt.
Joseph Ulric Beliveau created a Stradivari model featuring golden-brown varnish. The workmanship was rustic. A manuscript label was included.

A student of Oreste Candi , Genoese violin maker Lorenzo Bellafontana established his own workshop around 1935. Work from his earliest period consists mostly of guitars, which seems to have been his preferred instrument, with some violins constructed using the external method favored by his teacher.

Seen as the last maker of the traditional Neapolitan school, Vittorio Bellarosa studied with his father Riccardo in Naples, with Rodolfo Fredi in Rome and also trained for a few months in Mittenwald. He returned to Naples around 1930 and a few years later established a close association with Giovanni Pistucci, and is believed ... to have done much of the varnishing work for Pistucci.
Giuseppe Bellinazzi exhibited at Cremona in 1937. He became a casualty of the Second World War.
Thomas J. Bellingham was a self-taught amateur maker active from 1903. He produced 90 violins of decent quality.

Luiz Bellini was born in Brazil and emigrated to the US, where he worked with Simone Sacconi at Rembert Wurlitzer’s shop in New York and later for Jacques Francais. He established his own business in 1975, becoming known for his stunning and accurate copies of Stradivari and Guarneri instruments. His clients included Ruggiero Ricci, Yehudi Menuhin, David Nadien, Gidon Kremer and Glenn Dicterow.

Pietro Antonio Bellone was known as `il Pescorino. ' Henley described him as a rare maker belonging to the Brescian school, primarily due to the large soundholes and full arching. An example from the Schambach Collection at Yale University featured inlaid mother of pearl discs around the edges.

One of the last important makers of the 18th century Venetian school, Anselmo Bellosio moved to Venice in the early 1760s and probably learned his craft in the workshop of Giorgio Serafin in the 1770s. Interestingly, some of his instruments bear labels dating from 1754, but are evidently back-dated as Bellosio was born in 1743. ...

Giuseppe Beltrami worked in the shop of Aristide Cavalli in Cremona. He was responsible for several craftsmen who created separate parts of instruments, which were later assembled and varnished by others. The work was generally of good quality, although it featured a rather dark spirit varnish.
László Benedek studied with K. Lengyel and L.

Aristide Benigni recorded 25 violins. The instruments featured spirit varnish and included early examples with manuscript labels; later works had printed labels: Lavorazione violini / Benigni / Aristide / Ascoli-Piceno.
Henry Bentin was a violin and bow maker. He was taught by his father and emigrated with him to Iowa.

Felice Beretta was a pupil of Giuseppe Guadagnini. His modelling was flattish, and the workmanship was rather indifferent. The style was very much in line with Guadagnini, although the head appeared slightly clumsy. He often used poor quality wood, which was probably not well-seasoned, as evident distortions were present in some cases. The golden-yellow varnish was a little thin.

Karl August Berger studied with Postiglioni in Naples. He later worked for Silvestre and Maucotel in Paris and in Mittenwald. He moved to New York and worked for Emil Herrmann before opening his own shop.

Theodor Berger studied under Geisser in St Petersburg and later with Otto in Markneukirchen. He established himself independently in 1918 there. His instruments featured classical models with a fine golden-brown oil varnish. Geigenhauer, Markneukirchen. 19..
Andreas (Andras) Bergmann worked as a pupil of Bartek. He was employed by Pilat, Remenyi, and Schunda.

Recent research suggests that Carlo Bergonzi, historically assumed to have been a student of Giuseppe Guarneri 'filius Andreae' , may have learned violin making primarily through the Rugeri family. While the influence of Guarneri is evident in Bergonzi's work, his physical proximity to Vincenzo ...

It has been suggested that Carlo Antonio Lorenzo Bergonzi, the last and least-known violin maker of his family, was a teacher of Giovanni Battista Ceruti in the 1790s, but direct evidence of this relationship is lacking. His earliest known instrument dates from 1780, and he officially inherited the family business in 1804, though ... he made guitars for much of his career.
20th-century maker, based in Mantua; not believed to be related to the Cremonese making family.

Son of Cremonese contemporary of Stradivari Carlo Bergonzi , Michele Angelo remained in the Casa Stradivari after his father's death in 1747. By that point he represented the whole of the Cremonese violin making tradition, the Stradivari and Guarneri ... families having faded from the scene in the 1740s.

Grandson of Carlo Bergonzi and elder brother of Carlo Antonio , Nicolò Bergonzi began making instruments around 1777 and was a close contemporary of Lorenzo Storioni , who preceded ... him into the trade of violin making by only a few years. The two men were neighbors on the Contrada Coltellai in Cremona from 1787 to 1794 and there are rare examples of collaborations between the two makers.

Bernardel père, as Auguste Sébastien Philippe is known, first apprenticed locally in Mirecourt before moving to Paris to work for Nicolas Lupot and, after Lupot's death in 1824, with Charles François Gand . In 1826 he opened his own shop across the ... street from the Gand firm, where he worked steadily for 40 years until his retirement in 1866, when he handed off the business to his sons Gustave and Ernest Auguste , who had worked with him from 1860.

Gustave Adolphe Bernardel, son and pupil of Auguste-Sébastien , joined his father, Bernardel Pére, around 1849 and became a partner in the family workshop in 1860. He took over the business with his brother and Charles Eugène Gand in 1866, forming Gand et Bernardel.
Léon Bernardel was the son and pupil of Ernest Auguste Bernardel. Before joining his family at Gand et Bernardel Freres, he worked with Derazey.

Enzo Bertelli moved to São Paolo, Brazil, in 1953 and remained there with his brother Gaetano. He was a violin and bow maker credited with 32 violins and six violas. He used a fine yellow-amber oil varnish.

Luigi Bertelli was a prize winner at the 'Santa Cecilia' competition in Rome in 1956. Initially an amateur maker, he completed 40 violins, seven violas, and two cellos by 1952. In that year, he moved to São Paolo, Brazil with his son Enzo, but he returned to Verona in 1953. His work featured good classical modeling with varnish ranging from golden to red-orange.

Nicolas Bertholini invented a name used in a Mirecourt factory that produced instruments of good quality in various grades. They were made in large numbers from around 1880. The instruments featured good materials with highly polished red or golden brown varnish.

Giacinto Bertolazzi, a pupil of Peluzzi in Milan, followed Euro Peluzzi's acoustical theories and utilized classical and personal models in his work. He applied fine varnish and was significantly involved in repair and restoration, branding his instruments as 'G. B.'.
Francesco Bertolotti, son and pupil of Gasparo da Salò, assisted his father for many years and inherited the family workshop after Gasparo's death in 1609. He was joined in 1598 by Gio.
Gasparo Bertolotti 'da Salò' was one of the greatest Brescian makers. He was born into a family of musicians in da Salò and played the violin himself.

Fausto Mario Bertucci developed a guitar design featuring violin-like 'f' holes and was a nephew and pupil of Constantino. He collaborated with G. Aurelis and made a few violins himself, but he sold many commercially-produced instruments through his business in Rome.

Arthur Betts was presumed to be a workman of Richard Duke, creating instruments in his style based on a coarsened Stainer model with dull brown varnish. The manuscript label indicated: Arthur Betts Maker / at ye top of West Lane / Rotherhithe London 1765.

Younger brother of John Betts , Arthur Betts was a good violinist and had lessons from Viotti himself. He famously bought the 'Betts' Stradivari violin for the sum of one guinea from a naive customer around 1820.

Arthur II and John Betts II inherited the family business at London's Royal Exchange from their father, Arthur I in 1847 and instruments with the label 'Arthur and John Betts' were made from this point until the shop closed in 1867. By this time, the family were well established as the leading London dealers of antique instruments ... of the day.
Edward Betts was the nephew of John Betts (I), and was known as 'Ned'. He worked for Richard Duke and later for his uncle John at the Royal Exchange.

Little is known about the early life of John Betts, son of Edward Betts, a farmer from Stamford. In 1765 he moved to London to apprentice with Richard Duke and after seven years he became shop foreman, eventually buying the business from Duke's daughter.

Emil Beyer operated a business at 37 Neuwerkstrasse. He produced quality work using excellent materials and was also a bow maker. From 1908, he received assistance from his wife, Zigarren Bertha Beyer. A cello was cited by Vannes as a Stradivari model, featuring golden yellow varnish and labelled accordingly.

George W. Beyer was a pupil of Albert Karr, V. C. Squier, and William Lewis. He was active from 1912 and established himself in Bloomington, Indiana, in 1926. He used a Stradivari model and commercial oil varnish. His output was small, as he was greatly occupied with repair work. He branded his instruments on the back.

Robert Beyer employed several luthiers to create instruments for him, which were generally good copies of specific Cremonese instruments. He collected numerous gold medals and testimonials. A personally devised impregnation in the wood was used by him to enhance tone.

Though he was a crucial force behind the renewal of the Genoa school, Nicolò Bianchi made relatively few instruments during his career in Italy and France. Little is known of his life before his move to France in the early 1840s, but evidence suggests that he may have studied with Ludovico Rastelli .

Harry Biddulph was an amateur maker active in Wandsworth from 1925. He studied under Frank Howard and created Guarneri models. His varnish ranged from deep orange-brown to golden-yellow with a fine texture. An inked monogram appeared beneath the varnish on the back button. The front and back were inscribed: `No. 6 / H. Biddulph / Aberystwyth 1866 / London 1922. '.

Otello Bignami was initially self-taught before becoming a student of Gaetano Pollastri around 1952. In addition to his independent work as a violin maker, he founded a violin making school in Bologna in 1979, where he taught until 1983.
Ferenc Bikszegi was a pupil of Andreas Bergmann. He taught L. Kovacs. His work was very fine, often utilizing a personal model, with a scroll that featured a distinctively large eye.
Louis Fernand Billottet apprenticed to C. Enel. He worked in the Boulevard Bessieres, Paris, demonstrating very fine workmanship on classical models and utilizing excellent materials.
An instrument dealer in Sienna from c. 1750, he was probably a pupil of Gabrielli in Florence c. 1760. Remained in Florence as luthier to the Grand Duchy of Tuscany.
Johann Nepomuk Bina studied under F. Lehner.

Paul Bisch was a pupil of Mougenot and also worked for Caressa and Français in Paris. In 1919, he returned to Mirecourt. His work was fine and accurate, reflecting the style of Gand, and featured a red-brown oil varnish on a yellow ground. He received a silver medal at Nancy in 1928. Luthier No. / Rue Chantaire, Mirecourt. 19..

Oskar Bischofberger worked for Möckel in Berlin and was a pupil of Zust. He returned to Zurich in 1941. His instruments featured Stradivari and Guarneri models and were oil varnished. He completed over 50 violins and 14 cellos.
Edgar Bishop apprenticed to William Retford at the firm of W.E. Hill & Sons alongside Albert Leeson after WWI ended in 1918, and the two became the cantankerous Retford's favorite ... pupils.

The Bisiach family of makers and dealers ran one of Milan's most important violin shops during much of the 20th century. Founded by Leandro Bisiach in the late 1880s, the firm produced new instruments made by talented employees such as the Antoniazzi brothers, Giuseppe Ornati and Gaetano Sgarabotto, as well as dealing in old ones.

Andrea Bisiach, son of Leandro Bisiach, worked extensively with his father and brothers, Giacomo and Leandro Jr. He initially apprenticed in Mirecourt with Mougenot and became one of the finest makers and experts of his generation.

Carlo Bisiach, son and assistant of Leandro Bisiach, established his workshop in Florence in 1926 on via Piccinotti. Known for his fine and conscientious workmanship, he utilized high-quality materials and an orange-red varnish. Bisiach received gold medals at several competitions and exhibited in Cremona in 1937 and 1949.
Youngest sons of Leandro Bisiach who worked together as dealers and makers. Most of their instruments were made in the white by employees of the firm. Both retired in 1973.

Leandro Bisiach was a talented craftsman and an astute businessman, and his shop was foremost in his country during his lifetime. After studying with Riccardo Antoniazzi in Milan in 1886, he established his business around 1890, where he employed numerous makers, including his teacher and Antoniazzi's father Gaetano ... and brother Romeo , and traded in antique Italian instruments.

Leandro Bisiach was the son of Leandro Bisiach (I) and worked alongside his brother Giacomo, both instructed by I. Sderci in Siena during and after their father's tenure at the Accademia Chigiana from 1921 to 1924.

Born in Soresina, Bissolotti was a pupil of Pietro Sgarabotto at the Cremona violin making school, where he later taught himself until 1982. He established his own workshop in Cremona, and also made instruments for the Wurlitzer firm. Four of his sons became violin makers: Tiziano (d. 1995), Marco Vinicio, Maurizio and Vincenzo.
Son of Francesco Bissolotti and a teacher at the Cremona violin making school since 1980.

Josef Bitterer was the son and pupil of Andreas Bitteren. He received his diploma from the violin making school in Luby in 1926. He established his workshop in Mittenwald in 1946, later assisted by his son Georg. He produced ordinary good-class trade violins.

Karl Bitterer was the son and pupil of Josef Bitterer. He achieved his Master's certificate in 1958. His work included Stradivari, Guarneri, and Maggini models, as well as historical instruments after J. Tielke. He received awards in New York in 1980, Salt Lake City in 1982, and Kassel in 1983.
Alois Bittner created classical models and several copies of the 1733 Guarneri del Gesù violin. The oil varnish featured various hues.
James Black was influenced by Matthew Hardie. A thin golden-yellow varnish was present.

John Blair established his work at 8, Potterow and was said to have been a pupil of Matthew Hardie, although the differences in their respective techniques and styles were far greater than any similarities. In terms of workmanship, he had more in common with William Ferguson , another contemporary from Edinburgh, who may have ... had some association with him.

One of the most prolific French makers of the 19th century, Paul Blanchard made roughly 1,200 instruments in his lifetime. He was born in Mirecourt, grandson of François Blanchard, and apprenticed with Auguste Darte from 1865.

Alberto Luigi Blanchi was the son and pupil of Augustin Bianchi. He won gold medals at Milan in 1906 and London in 1908. His work included 500 violins, 60 cellos, double basses, guitars, and mandolins. The personal models displayed a very high degree of craftsmanship and finish, featuring a good golden-orange varnish.

Augustin Blanchi moved to Nice in 1840. He was apprenticed to P. Pacharelle and later worked with other makers in Piedmont. In 1860, he returned to Nice. His son Alberto succeeded him. He produced fine work in the classical style and used good varnish.

Herbert E. Blasier was self-taught and active from 1900. His personal model was derived from Guarneri. He used oil varnish in various colors. In 1915, he won first place in the American Guild of Violinists competition held in Chicago.
Louis Blitz was the son of violinist Carel Blitz, who established a violin shop in Rotterdam in 1905. He studied under J. van de Geest.

Hugues Emile Blondelet joined Thibouville-Lamy in 1890. He became co-director with Acoulon in 1908. In 1923, he was made Chevalier of the Legion of Honour. His instruments featured Amati and Stradivari models with his name on the label, showcasing fine workmanship, including Stradivaris with scroll chamfers highlighted in black and rich red oil varnish.

Williamson Blyth was a colourful character, referred to by Henley as 'a pretender and charlatan. ' Although credited with over 2, 000 instruments by Honeyman, he also worked as a tinsmith and plumber and played traditional instruments. His address in Edinburgh was 7 Middleton's Entry, Potterow, near the premises of Blair, Ballantyne, and James Hardie.
Giovanni Battista Bodio was the son of one of the leading Venetian violin players of the time, also named Giobatta Bodio. He possibly studied under his uncle Giuseppe.
Salvatore Bofill was an important maker of the Spanish school. His work featured a very interesting and characterful personal model.
Roman Boianciuc was a pupil of W. Pohoryles.

Claude Boivin established his workshop at the sign 'a la guitare royale' on rue de la Poterie, later moving to rue de Grenelle St Honore and rue Tiquetonne. He became Master of the Violin-Makers' Guild in 1752.
Trained with Johan August Friderich Hoffmann in Copenhagen. He opened his own shop in 1901 and worked until around 1940, both repairing and making instruments.
John Alfred Bolander was the son and successor of John Bolander (I). He became active in San Francisco in 1915 and spent many years repairing band instruments before moving to San Jose in 1931.
Joseph Böllinger was associated with Meinrad Frank. He produced notable work on a model inspired by Amati, featuring prominent edges.
Chiarissimo Bondanelli was a chemist by profession and an active maker of violins and bows starting in 1946. He exhibited in Ancona in 1957 and received a gold medal in 1971.
Philip J. Bone founded Bone & Co. in Luton from 1892 to 1918. They primarily made and sold mandolins, although some commercial violins of medium quality featured the label.
Raffaele Bongiovanni worked as a repairer and restorer for Emil Herrmann.
Son and pupil of Joseph Bonnel `Le Vieux', below. Took over the family business in Rennes 1879.
London firm, created in 1930 by the merging of Boosey & Company and Hawkes & Son (later Riviere & Hawkes). Manufactured string, brass and wind instruments alongside publishing music.

William Booth was the son and pupil of William Booth (I) and was generally regarded as a superior craftsman compared to his father. His work featured fine details and was finished with a rich golden-brown varnish

Jacques Boquay was an important member of the Vieux Paris school, alongside his contemporary Claude Pierray . The inventory of his workshop at his death revealed a large stock of instruments and materials suitable for a major commercial enterprise.
Andreas Borelli rented accommodation in Parma from the Borelli family from 1758 to 1763. He created a large model featuring a rich varnish in various shades of gold. Fine cellos were also recorded.

Pietro Borghi trained as a wood-carver at the Industrial School in Forli and was a self-taught violin maker active from 1915, influenced by Giuseppe Strocchi's instrument collection. He received first prize at the Forli Exposition in 1921 and established his workshop in Modena in 1925, later moving to Lugo in 1942.
Francesco Borrerio created coarse amateur work. The varnish was a dull orange-brown, and it featured a flat arch.

Carlo Bosi worked for Cavalli at the Monteverdi workshop in Cremona starting in 1925. He created a typical Stradivari model in the modern Cremonese style. A violin he entered was displayed at the 1937 Cremona Exhibition. His work was signed internally.
Giuseppe Bossi created commercial instruments of various grades and patterns. He also produced orange spirit varnish.
Bottali-Roth-Pelitti was an amalgamation of three businesses that provided good quality student instruments and bows.

Benvenuto Botturi studied with Diguini at Cremona and returned to Brescia in 1929, but he retired from violin making in 1941. He was prodigiously prolific during that time, producing 100 violins, several violas, and cellos, one quartet, and zoo bows, which were successfully exported throughout Europe and America.
Maurice Bouette studied under William Luff in Ealing, west London. He succeeded Luff as the tutor at the Ealing and Northwood violin making classes in 1962.
Emile Boulangeot apprenticed to E. Laurent in 1890.

Jules Camille (Gabriel) Boulangeot worked for Darche in Brussels, established his own shop on rue du Boulet, and was appointed luthier to Queen Elisabeth of Belgium. He won awards at Brussels in 1897 and 1907, Liège in 1905, and Ghent in 1913.

In the middle of the nineteenth-century, London and Paris enjoyed close links with experienced makers, restorers and dealers frequently crossing the channel. Amongst the most significant French violin makers working in London were Charles Maucotel who left the Gand workshop in 1844 to work with the violin dealers Richard & ...
Nicolas Bourbon was the godfather of Nicolas Vuillaume. His work was considered better than that of François Bourbon; it featured a decent flat-arched model with red- or golden-brown varnish.

Jean Bourgard worked in Prague, Czech Republic. The Médard school, characterized by slightly primitive execution, featured yellow-brown or superior red varnish. He later collaborated with J. F. Charotte. Vannes referenced a viola d'amore from 1786.

Maurice Bourguignon apprenticed to Thibouville in 1898. He subsequently worked in Paris and with G. Mougenot in Brussels, acquiring the business in 1910. He received several medals and diplomas at various Exhibitions. His work was very refined, based on classical models, and featured good red varnish.
Bourlier created commercial work characterized by a flat arch, broad outline, and yellow-brown varnish.
Nicolas Bourlier produced decent work, though it was marred by poor quality thin yellow-brown varnish. The cellos were of compact size and useful. They were branded internally as 'Bourlier B. N. '.
André Boury is associated with the same name that features the double 'r' spelling in Paris. He is also a repairer from a slightly earlier date, and he is presumably the same individual.

Joseph Benoit Boussu created finely worked, albeit slightly mannered, interpretations of the Amati model in a characteristic Flemish style, made without a separate upper block. He featured a deeply carved scroll with an extended final turn and a prominent eye, along with a generally rather full arch.
Charles Bovis was the son of Francois Bovis , who passed away when Charles was five years old. His apprenticeship took place in Mirecourt at the shop of Rene Jacquemin .
Francois Bovis worked for Bianchi from 1874 and eventually took over the business in 1880. After his death, the business was continued by his widow and later by his son Charles.

Arthur Bowler was the nephew of George Chanot . He trained as a joiner but later he apprenticed with Joseph Anthony Chanot in Wardour Street from 1895 to 1899, before establishing his own business as a restorer in Islington in 1899.
Fl. 1890s. Worked in Norwich, NY, but moved to San Bernadino, CA, and later to Clintonville, CT, where he died.
Frédéric Boyer trained initially as a luthier by R. & M. Millant in Paris, then from 1943 in Mirecourt under R.

Raffaello Bozzi was a self-taught violin and bow maker, active from 1936. He created a good Stradivari model and used a golden-yellow spirit varnish. Around 30 instruments were recorded, and he made bows in the style of Lamy and Sartory.
Graduated the Cremona School of Violin Making in 1982.

Morsino (Marsino) Bran initially taught himself and was later assisted by Ettore Siega in Venice. He made instruments very much in Siega's style, producing 40 violins and some violas up to the year 1950. He exhibited in Cremona in 1949.

Filippo Brandilioni was a rarely encountered maker of obscure origin. His style was compared by Vannes to that of Leopold Widhalm, while Henley made a connection with Mittenwald, leading to the supposition that he had German origins with an Italianised name.

Jacopo Brandini was a rarely encountered maker. The varnish was a very thin pale grey-yellow. The model was an Amatise style, somewhat resembling the Genoese manner. An awkwardly placed small volute was on the pegbox, and there was a broad chest with small, widely spaced soundholes. Henley cited examples that featured painted purfling on the table and carved decoration on the back.
Carl (Károly) Brandl studied under Tischenant and Hoffmann starting in 1835. He later worked for Gutermann and A.

August Hermann Braun was a pupil of K. H. Kessler until 1882. He joined the firm of Karl Gottlieb Schuster in 1896 and established himself in the Klingenthalerstrasse in 1921, where he served as chief workman. He produced good quality copies of Tyrolean models that featured rather crudely-coloured spirit varnish. He was also a maker of plucked instruments.

Ewald Braun apprenticed to E. Liebich in Breslau and later worked for Heckel in Dresden, Piegendorfer in Augsburg, Geijse in St Petersburg, and Neuner in Berlin. He returned to Markneukirchen in 1903 to establish his own workshop. He was a prolific maker of student instruments.
Michael (Mihály) Braun was the son of Johann Braun. He was active in Szeged until 1895, after which he moved to Kolozsvar until 1918, when he returned to Szeged.

Frederick T. Braund completed approximately 70 instruments between 1927 and 1980. He was a pupil of F. W. Channon. Each instrument featured a name on the label, and Channon was acknowledged through the inclusion of his initials. The instruments were classical models with a good clear red-gold varnish.

John Breckinbridge was an amateur maker of some merit, favoring an Amati model. The varnish was a pale golden-brown or yellow spirit, rather thin but attractive. He crafted neat, narrow edges, with soundholes that were upright and close to the edge in Dutch fashion. The head was eccentric in the manner of Ford.

J. B. Bredall was a pupil of Laurent in Mirecourt and worked for Bisiach in Milan as well as with Francois in Paris. For 12 years, he served as the principal repairman at Lyon and Healy in Chicago before establishing his own business in Chicago in 1920. He gained a fine reputation as a copyist of classical instruments, utilizing a rich varnish of his own preparation.

Olav Breivik emigrated to America around 1914. He trained as a violin maker in Chicago starting in 1927. Before settling in Milwaukee in 1934, he worked in Neenah, Wisconsin. His instruments included Stradivari and Guarneri models. He was credited with 140 violins, 40 violas, and eight cellos.
François Breton was a luthier to the Duchesse d'Angourême. He was a very prolific and much-imitated maker.

Joseph François Breton created a large Stradivari model that was somewhat crude in execution and utilized generally second-class materials. The varnish was of the 'Vieux Paris' type, thick and brown in color, and was prone to craquelure. It was branded on the back with 'Breton a Paris. ' A label of the violin was located in the Berlin Museum.
Cipriano Briani studied under Leandro Bisiach from approximately 1906 to 1910. His work exhibited fine craftsmanship in that style, adhering to classical principles. He died in poverty.

James William Briggs was a pupil of Tarr in Manchester starting in 1869 and established himself in Wakefield in 1878. He won a gold medal at the Leeds exhibition in 1890, followed by diplomas in Paris and Vienna.

Stephen Bristow served his apprenticeship with the firm of W.E. Hill & Sons until 1972. His Hill bows are marked with the number 16. He later worked for John Clutterbuck (bows stamped 'J.S. Rameau') before establishing his own workshop.
Carlo Broschi created fine work on the Amati model. It featured a full arch, with neatly worked scroll and soundholes. Vannes, along with Henley & Hart, assigned his dates a century later.
Eugène (Victor) Brouillier. Son of Eugène Brouillier.

The instrument cited in the Hill Archive featured an unpurfled Stainer model with small neat edges, upright soundholes, and a pale yellow spirit varnish. It was in the style of Cross or Barrett and was possibly only a merchant retailing London work.

Frank F. Brown was a pupil of H. Rockwell. He produced over 100 violins, violas, and cellos using models from Stradivari, Amati, and Guarneri. He utilized fine local maple and commercial oil varnish. A manuscript label was included.
An Amati model featured yellow spirit varnish.

James Brown was originally a silk-weaver in Shoreditch and became a pupil and assistant of Thomas Kennedy around 1800. He later established his own shop in Wheeler Street, Spitalfields. His work was of good quality, though it was unremarkable for the time and place. Some pieces featured good red varnish, while others tended towards a dull brown.

James Brown was the son and pupil of James Brown (I), a maker of violins and bows known for trade quality and workmanlike finish. Active since 1834, he established his business at 18 White Lion Street, Norton Folgate in 1854. His cello bows were well-regarded. The brand was J. BROWN Jr.

William Brown is a little-known maker, whose instruments demonstrate a strong connection to Peter Wamsley , and probably much of his work exists either attributed to Wamsley School or originally sold under his label as lower grade work. One violin labeled ‘Wm Brown London 1749’ shows that he was a ‘small-master’, a craftsman ... who lacked a retail premises of his own, providing further support for his identity as an outworker.

John Browne apprenticed to George and Rebecca Miller and became free in 1684. His workmanship featured the Parker style and utilized a thin golden brown varnish. One example, which had its top and scroll replaced, was cited in the Hill Archive and labelled.

Antoine Brubach was employed by N. Bonnel before he became the chief workman for Klein in Rouen in 1884. He was a prolific maker of variable quality, primarily at a commercial level. His work was branded and labelled as 'A. Klein & Cie Rouen'. Some of his superior efforts were reportedly given labels from more prestigious Paris makers.

Charles Brubach was the brother of Antoine Brubach. He worked for Albert Caressa in Paris starting in 1877. He received a gold medal at the Brussels Exhibition in 1897. Although very few instruments bore his own name, he had a reputation as a highly skilled and gifted maker.
August Wilhelm Brückner established himself in Erfurt around 1900. He traded in quality violins and bows.

Erich Bruckner made Stradivari and Guarneri models using fine materials. He followed the acoustic theories published by M. Grossman of Berlin. Some instruments were endorsed and signed by Grossman himself. He also produced violins and bows of cheaper quality. His brother Ernst assisted him starting in 1925.
Ernst Richard Brückner worked for Ellersick in Rostock and was a pupil of Petzold. He established himself in Markneukirchen in 1895. Various models and colors of varnish were produced.
Fritz Oswald Bruckner was the son and pupil of August Brückner. He established himself in Strass, Westphalia in 1923.

Nandor (Ferdinand) Brückner was a pupil of Mönnig and an assistant of J. W. Schunda. He worked at 4 Magyarut, Budapest from 1874 to 1926. He specialized in making double basses and also produced violins and cellos, which were greatly appreciated locally.
Otto Friedrich Brückner was a pupil of H. Jager. He worked for Roth & Lederer in Markneukirchen.
Richard Bruckner worked in Berlin with his brother Franz before relocating to London in 1890. He gained a good reputation there as a repairer.
Charles Georges Brugère apprenticed to Etienne Drouin at Mirecourt from 1878 to 1882. He worked for Paul Blanchard in Lyons and Gand, as well as Bernardel in Paris from 1885 to 1892.
François 'Malakoff' Brugère was the brother of Charles Joseph Brugère. He worked for Pierre Silvestre in Lyons, Daniel in Marseilles, and Bonnel in Rouen.
Mateo Bruni studied under Gaetano Sgarabotto from 1900 to 1904. He worked for G.

Carlo Colombo Bruno was active in Turin from 1890 at 21 Via Roma, from 1897 at 3 via Accademia Albertina / 23 Principe Amedeo, and at 10, Piazza Carlo Felice. He received medals at the Paris, Turin, and Marseilles Exhibitions.
James Brunskill created fine work on a Stradivari model featuring good red-gold varnish in A. D. 1889. He was a maker and dealer located at 175 Westgate Road in Newcastle-on-Tyne in 1898.

Edmund Franklin Bryant was a violinist and maker who studied under Ira White in Boston. He worked with his nephew Ole from 1905 to 1908 and then independently until 1938. During his career, he utilized classical models and completed four violins, three violas, and two cellos.
George E. Bryant was the son of Charles Henry Bryant. He produced about 15 instruments. The personal model featured an orange-brown varnish and included a manuscript label.
Worked from c. 1900. Strad model.
Ole H. Bryant was the son of Charles Henry Bryant and also a pupil of Charles Gage.

Percival Wilfred Bryant worked for George Withers in Leicester Square, London from 1920 to 1932. He established himself at Ovingdean in 1939. He had a fine reputation as a bow maker and was one of the few distinguished English makers of the 20th century who was not trained at Hills.

Kurt Brychta was a violinist and violin maker from 1930. He established himself in Buffalo from 1947 to 1974. He made 162 instruments, which were oil varnished using his own recipe, with some constructed from imported prefabricated parts. In 1976, he received a certificate for workmanship from the Violin Society of America.

Ignaz Johann Bucher was the son, pupil, and successor of Johann Bucher. He worked in Vienna and received medals at the Vienna Exhibition in 1873 for his quartet. His work was typical of the Viennese style, exhibiting a strong influence from Stradivari. He was known for his fine and accurate workmanship and was well regarded locally for his guitars and zithers.
Gabriel David Buchstetter was the son of Christoff Buchstetter. He was a highly regarded maker known for his great individuality.
Frederick James Buckley moved to Boston in 1892. He was a pupil and assistant of Clark Powers .
George Hatton Buckman was a self-taught maker and violinist from 1880. He learned his technique from Heron-Allen and received encouragement and assistance from Chanot.
Arthur Bultitude began his apprenticeship to the firm of W.E. Hill & Sons in 1922 at the tender age of 14.

Josef Bunnagel studied under Aschauer in Mittenwald from 1920 to 1923. He worked for Hempel in Hamburg and F. C. Louis in Sarrebruck. He established a shop in Cologne in 1929, which continued in the family throughout the 20th century. His work was very fine.
American maker, trained with Hans Weisshaar and based in Ann Arbor since 1976, winner of 12 gold medals in various making competitions.

Noel Burke apprenticed with Charles Espey and Stéphane Thomachot. His work has since received numerous awards at international competitions including eight gold medals at competitions of the VSA in 1994 and 1996, the RNCM Strad Cello Festival in 1998 and the Concours Internationaux de la Ville de Paris in 1999.
Domenico Busan was born outside Treviso and probably arrived in Venice around 1743-44. If this date is correct, the location of his apprenticeship is unknown, as Matteo Goffriller had died in 1742.
E. Busch was a viol and lute maker. Examples are held in museums in Copenhagen and Nuremberg. His label also appeared in a mute violin at the Brussels Musical Instruments Museum.

Charles Buthod worked for Vuillaume in Paris before joining Husson in Mirecourt, where he established an extensive workshop. Initially, the firm was known as Husson, Buthod et Thibouville, producing approximately 1250 instruments each year with a large workforce.

Maurice Whittaker can be traced back to the 1760s, when he had a shop at the sign of the Violin and German Flute near the North Gate of the Royal Exchange in London. In practice he had established himself as the successor of Joseph Hare as a general music and instrument seller whose family had been active at the ‘Viol & Flute’ near the Royal Exchange (and several iterations thereof) since 1694. ...
George Byrom mainly occupied himself with repair and restoration. His own instruments were fine, though rare.
John Byrom was the brother and assistant of George Byrom. A few instruments bore his label.
Prosper Cabasse worked for Georges Chanot in Paris and spent some time in Baltimore, USA, around 1854 before returning to France. He produced a golden-brown or yellow shellac varnish of good quality.

Two generations of the Cahusac family worked in London from 1755 to 1816. Thomas Cahusac (I) was a prolific music publisher working from the sign of 'Two Flutes & Violin' from 1755 near St Clement's Church, and after 1784 they moved west to 196 Strand, close to the present day Savoy hotel, and Haymarket from 1801.

Peter Cairns created a Guarneri model featuring broad and deeply channelled edges. It had a strong, well-carved scroll and broad, upright soundholes. The varnish was a soft light brown. Some experimental instruments were made with hardwood fronts, and the back and front were curiously differentiated, with the ribs tapering between them.
Giuseppe Calace was the son and pupil of Raffaele Calace (I). He was a virtuoso mandolinist with the Calace sextet.

Raffaele Calace, son of Antonio Calace, worked in Naples alongside his brother Nicolas-Marie starting in 1900. A mandolin virtuoso and composer, he is best known for perfecting the design of the large Neapolitan mandolin and also made guitars, mandolins, lutes, and various bowed instruments, including a violin for Mussolini in 1927.

Bernardo Calcagno, or Bernardus Calcanius, as his Latinized labels read, is perhaps the finest Genoese maker of the 18th century. Though his work is unmistakably Italian, he may have trained with the German maker Andreas Statler , given that they shared a house and probably a workshop around 1730.
Active in Modena at the turn of the 20th century. Few instruments survive. These show skilled workmanship but suggest he was not formally trained.
Frank J. Callier was a violin and bow maker of French descent.

William Calow was a collector and maker of double basses. He established a shop in Sussex Street. The instruments featured a viol-shaped form with extended Guarneri- or Maggini-like soundholes and an orange-brown oil varnish. Approximately 20 were produced, and some violins and cellos were also known. An eccentric bass bar, cut in the lower bouts, had flared ends.

Bartolomeo Calvarola was born in Torre Baldone and was active in Bergamo. He created a narrow model featuring a pinched arch and extravagantly cut Staineresque soundholes. The workmanship was good, though the style was eccentric, particularly in the extremely slender head. The varnish was of good quality, ranging from golden-yellow to gold-brown. He made his instruments in Bergamo in 1762.

Along with his contemporary Tomaso Balestrieri , Camillo Camilli is considered one of the finest 18th-century makers of the Mantua school. His work is strongly inspired by Pietro Guarneri , who made an enormous impact on Mantuan violin making upon his ... arrival from Cremona in the 1680s, but by the time Camilli was old enough to begin apprenticeship Guarneri had died, and in all likelihood his primary teacher was Antonio Zanotti .
Jacques Camurat trained with Dieudonne in Paris. He established his own workshop at 49 rue de Rome in 1957 and retired in 1990, with his sons Pascal and Bernard succeeding him.

One of the best violin makers of his generation, Cesare Candi was born in Bologna and studied with Raffaele Fiorini alongside his brother Oreste before moving to Genoa around 1888. He started his own business in 1892 but began to make violins several ... years later, probably after additional guidance from Eugenio Praga and Enrico Rocca .

The Candi brothers Cesare and Oreste worked alongside one another in the Bolognese workshop of Raffaele Fiorini in the 1880s before moving to Genoa in 1888. There Oreste and his brother started their own workshop, where they presumably collaborated, though ... they labeled their instruments separately.
Pupil of Grandilli in Naples. Worked at Rome 1886, and Spezia where he won a gold medal 1906.

Antonio Capela was born in Espinho, Portugal in 1932. He started learning to make violins from his father, Domingos Capela. In 1961 he received a grant from the Gulbenkian Foundation to study with Etienne Vatelot in Paris and the Morizot brothers in Mirecourt. In 1964 he enrolled at the International School of Violin Making in Cremona, where he studied with Pietro Sgarabotto and Fernando Sacconi.Capela won awards at many expositions, including Liège in 1963, Cremona in 1965 and 1969, and Poznań in 1967 and 1972. In 1991, he recieved the Medal of Cultural Merit from the Presidência do Concelho de Ministros in Portugal. Capela helped to found the European Violin and Bow Builders Association, where he currently serves as vice president. He is also a member of the International Violin and Bow Builders Association.
Domingos Ferreira Capela apprenticed as a cooper (barrel-maker) and cabinet maker. He began repairing violins and commenced making them in 1924.

Virgilio Capellini studied at the Cremona International School, graduating in 1947. He worked at Crotta d'Adda (near Cremona) and opened a shop in Cremona with Alfredo Primavera in 1975. His work featured Stradivari and Guarneri modeling with precise craftsmanship and utilized a red-brown spirit varnish.

Another self-taught maker of the Emilian region, Marino Capicchioni took up violin making after working in a French guitar factory for a year and settled in Rimini in 1929. His style evolved somewhat over the course of his 30-year career: early instruments display a studied but impersonal precision, while more mature works, which include violas and cellos as well as violins, are personal interpretations ... of classical models, especially 'del Gesù' .
