Collaborations by father and his assistant son based in Rimini.

Mario Capicchioni was the son and assistant of Marino Capicchioni. He worked in a similar style starting in 1943. He obtained a diploma in Rome in 1952 and received a gold medal in Ascoli Piceno in 1959.
Sicilian maker.

Both the training and legacy of Gioffredo Cappa have been the focus of significant speculation since at least the 19th century, when he was assumed to have been a pupil of Nicolò Amati . While Cappa's best works do bear Amati labels, his reliance on Amati models probably stems simply from the authority that family wielded on ... violin making in the late-17th century.

The older of the two Carcassi brothers , Lorenzo produced most of his works in collaboration with his brother Tomaso . His independently produced instruments are, like the brothers' joint works, modeled on Stainer ... and bear a yellow-brown varnish.

Lorenzo & Tomaso Carcassi worked both together and individually as pupils of Giovanni Baptista Gabrielli, achieving varying standards of finish and accuracy, generally following a high-arched Stainer model that was prevalent in Florence at that time. Their scrolls were often crudely cut but featured a distinctive large eye and slender chamfers.

Most of Tomaso Carcassi’s instruments were produced in collaboration with his older brother and business partner Lorenzo using a joint Carcassi Brothers label, but he did occasionally work independently. Autonomous works by both brothers share the ...

Vincenzo Carcassi was the son and pupil of Tomaso Carcassi. He worked at some point in Naples with his brother Salvatore. He employed Amatisé modelling and exhibited fair workmanship. A transparent brown varnish was used.

Albert Caressa apprenticed at Gand and Bernardel until 1901, when he established his own business at 12, rue de Madrid, Paris, alongside Henri Français . He was decorated as Chevalier de la Légion d'Honneur in 1910 and became an Officer of the Academy in 1922.

Albert Caressa and Henri Français were the junior partners in the Gand & Bernardel firm, and on Gustave Bernardel's retirement in 1901 they continued the firm as Caressa & Français. When Henri Français retired in 1920, it was renamed Albert Caressa and after Caressa's retirement it was continued under Henri Français's son Emile as Emile Francais.
Pupil of Fiorini, Pollastri, and Sofritti. Also worked for Leandro Bisiach, making instruments 'in the white'.

Gabriele Carletti is the son and pupil of Natale Natale Carletti and the nephew of Orfeo and Carlo. He is an active maker, writer, and teacher at violin making schools in Milan, Bisignano, and Pieve di Cento.
Genuzio Carletti won first prize for viola at Cremona in 1949. He was associated with Pieve di Cento.

Son and student of Carlo Carletti , Natale Carletti took over his father's workshop in Pieve di Cento with his younger brother Nullo in 1941. His output was impressive, consisting of virtually every kind of stringed instrument except guitars.

Nullo Carletti was the youngest son of Carlo Carletti and was active as a maker from 1948 to 1960. He exclusively used the 'Carletti' Stradivari model, and most of his instruments were exported to the USA.

Orfeo Carletti was a violinist and maker, the son and pupil of Carlo Carletti and the younger brother of Natale. He established a workshop in Bologna at Via de Pepoli in 1928/9, where he crafted well-made instruments that were strongly influenced by Pollastri and bore similarities to those of Natale.

James Reynold Carlisle worked in Charleston from 1900 to 1914, focusing on varnish development and creating a dark red formula known as 'Italian sunshine varnish.' He subsequently joined Wurlitzer in Cincinnati, where he collaborated with A. W.

"The Carrodus Violin" was a trade name for violins imported from Mirecourt by Hawkes & Co in London from the late 1880s to the early 1900s. They were named in honour of the violin virtuoso John Tiplady Carrodus (1836-1895), a prolific concert master, soloist, and professor of the violin who enjoyed nationwide celebrity status.

John Carter's precise dates are not known, but he worked from the 1770s up until the end of the eighteenth century from addresses close to the Theatre Royal in Drury Lane, first at a shop in Little Drury Lane off the Strand from at least 1773, and from 1781 in Wysh Street. Despite having a shop of his own, Carter largely worked for the trade.

Luigi B. Carzoglio emigrated to Buenos Aires in 1898. He made violins, violas, and cellos, as well as plucked instruments. He was a constructor of guitars and violins located at Rivadavia No. 3585, Buenos Aires.
Antonio Casini was a fine and interesting maker, although rare. His style was reminiscent of Giuseppe Guarneri 'filius Andreae', featuring a rich varnish but a slightly coarse finish.
Lapo Casini was the son and pupil of Serafino Casini . He also trained with Del Perugia.

Serafino Casini established his business first at Campi Bisenzio, near Florence, in 1899 as a maker of plucked instruments. He later moved to 8 Via San Elisabetta, Florence, where he expanded into making violins.
Antonio Cassarelli used good materials and deep brown varnish, as noted by Henley. The work remains unknown and unverifiable.
Italo-French violin maker Andrea Castagneri was born in Turin in 1696 and moved to Paris around 1720. He worked as a dealer of antique Cremonese instruments as well as a maker and restorer.
Giuseppe Castagnino was active from 1906 and trained briefly with G. Fiorini in Munich in 1913.
Ageo Castaldini was active from 1912. He had a workshop in Via S. Lorenzo and was noted for cellos; he also made guitars. He worked in Bologna in the year 19..
Pietro Castellani was primarily a guitar maker, although violins also existed. He used a golden-colored varnish.

Cesare Castelli trained in violin making by C. Celani from 1940. He received medals at Ancona and Cremona. His work included fine Stradivari copies. Caesar Castelli / Fecit anno 1949 N. 16 / Asculi in Piceno.

Paolo Castello was a follower of Calcanius. His workmanship was unpredictable, featuring mean scrolls and rather low-set soundholes. The varnish had a dull pinkish-red hue. He created this in Genuae in the year 1775.

Aldo di Luigi Castellotti was self-taught before he joined the International Violin Making School in Cremona in 1947. He exhibited in Cremona in 1949. In 1955, he moved to Pavia and retired from violin making. His work followed the Stradivari model.

Pietro Cateni created a flat model violin in 1810, which was listed in the Lyon & Healey catalogue of 1929. It featured unremarkable workmanship, a small scroll, and an ordinary brown varnish that was not especially attractive.
George Catlin created American 'church basses'. They were unpurfled and made with local materials and dark red-brown varnish.

Enrico Cattenar was active in Turin from around 1650 and succeeded Hans Angerer (Johann Angerer, Giovanni Angerero), whom he married the widow of. He is commonly regarded as the founder of the Turin school and influenced G.
François Caussin was a pupil of Gaillard in Mirecourt. In 1815, he established an atelier in his hometown, later moving to Neufchâteau after 1830.

François Hippolyte Caussin was the son and pupil of Francois Caussin. He also trained in Mirecourt with Gaillard before returning to Neufchâteau to work for and eventually succeed his father. He continued to produce high-quality reproductions of classical Italian work.

Nicolas Caussin was the elder son of François Caussin and worked initially in Neufchâteau with his father before spending a period in Paris, where he operated a business at Place de la Croix-Rouge until 1857. He later returned to Neufchâteau, specializing in varnish.

Antonino Cavalazzi was a pupil of Domenico Veggi. He established his work in 1923, using Stradivari-derived models. The varnish, a product of his own research, was of good golden-yellow. He was active in Ravenna in 1947.

Giuseppe Cavaleri was a follower of Calcanius with a reasonably refined technique, working on a loosely Amatisé model. He used modest materials, often plain wood and sometimes unusual species, like many Genoese makers.
Aristide Cavalli was the son and pupil of Savino Cavalli and also trained with G. Beltrani.
Lelio Cavalli was the son of Aristide Cavalli. He obtained a doctorate in physics and mathematics. From 1930, he served as the director of the 'Officina C. Monteverdi' in Cremona.

Oreste Cavallini was the son and pupil of Luigi Cavallini and specialized in double basses. He was also known for violins, violas, and cellos. His work featured a thin orange-yellow varnish. Oreste Cavallini di / Luigi fece in Arezzo / Anno 1810.

Giovanni Cavani was a pupil of Leandro Bisiach and an excellent craftsman who worked for the conservatories in Parma and Bologna. He made instruments primarily based on the Guarneri del Gesu model, with the last two violins crafted at the age of 84.

Vincenzo Cavani, active from around 1900, was the son and pupil of Giovanni Cavani and also worked in the Leandro Bisiach shop. He utilized classical models, particularly those of Pietro Guarneri, and is noted for his fine golden-red varnish. His labels include "Cavani Vincenzo a. del. p. Giovanni / Fece in Spilamberto, A. 19.." and "Cavani Vincenzo / da Spilamberto Modena / Fece Anno 19.."
Constantino Celani was the brother and pupil of Emilio Celani . He was a prolific maker and received numerous awards.

Emilio Celani was the brother of Constantino Celani. He was known as 'The Turk' and was a prolific maker of violins modeled after Andrea Guarneri, using a transparent yellow varnish similar to that of his brother. He also produced over 200 guitars and mandolins. He worked in Ascoli Piceno in 1881 and again in 1896.

One of the most distinguished makers of the Turin school, Giovanni Francesco Celoniato -- or Celoniatus in Latin -- is thought to have been a pupil of Giofreddo Cappa . Although chronologically this is plausible since Celoniatus was born after Cappa, geographically it is less convincing since Cappa worked in Saluzzo, some 30km ... outside of Turin.

Marco Antonio Cerin was a pupil of Anselmo Bellosio from around 1785 and attained guild membership in 1799, opening his shop beneath the Torre dei Mori (Torre dell'Orologio) in Piazza San Marco. His early works exhibit the influence of Bellosio, characterized by graceful designs, rich red-brown varnish, high arches, and elongated corners.

Jan Cerny was a self-taught violin maker, active from around 1922 to 1950. He invented a new type of violin featuring soundholes located in the middle ribs, and he replaced the bass bar and sound post with his own patented devices. He won gold medals at Prague in 1924, Brünn in 1928, and The Hague in 1949. Additionally, he crafted conventional violins based on the Stradivari form.

The last of the Ceruti family and also considered to be the last of the traditional Cremonese makers, Giuseppe Antonio Ceruti's son was born Riccardo Fabio but known as Enrico, which explains the alternating existence of the initials "R.F.C". and "E.F.C". on his labels. He was trained by his father, and both were also professional ... double bass players.

Giovanni Battista Ceruti, traditionally believed to have been a student of Storioni , was born just outside Cremona in the town of Sesto. He moved to Cremona in 1786 and entered the violin making trade about ten years later, probably after becoming acquainted with the Bergonzi brothers Carlo ...
Giovanni Maria Ceruti creates trade instruments in the workshops of Aristide Cavalli at Cremona.

Son of Giovanni Battista Ceruti and father of Enrico , Giuseppe Antonio began working in his father's shop around 1805. His productive years of violin making are intermittent, and even after inheriting his father's business in 1817 he worked as a woodcarver ... and double bass player in addition to making instruments.
J. Challard established in Montpellier and around 1935-1937 at 27 Rue Richard Coeur de Lion in Agen.
René Champion was a follower and possibly a pupil of Boquay. Some pardessus-de-violes and violins also survived. He worked in Paris at rue des Bourdonnois in 1735.

The late nineteenth-century saw books on violin making by Davidson, Heron-Allen and Walter Mason, producing a generation of self-taught craftsmen who dominated the English landscape for violins, and few craftsmen typify this period of English making more than Frederick William Channon. Born in Totnes in 1862, he apprenticed as a cabinet maker in Davenport (Plymouth) becoming foreman of one of one of ... the largest cabinet shops in the city at the age of twenty-one, and established his own business four years later.

Alfred Ernest Chanot established himself at 29 Rathbone Place, London, in 1930, as the son and pupil of Frederick William Chanot. He was a fully realized maker and copyist in the family tradition, although his work was rarer than that of many others in his family due to his role as a repairer.

François Chanot was a gifted mathematician and scientist whose primary career was as a naval engineer. He trained in Mirecourt with his father, Joseph Chanot, and during a break from the navy due to an injury from 1816 to 1818, he designed a simplified and acoustically enhanced version of the cornerless violin.

Frederick William Chanot, son of Georges Chanot (II), trained in Paris with his grandfather Georges (I) from 1864 to 1872. He returned to London to work for his father until 1881, after which he established himself independently at 179 Wardour Street and later Berners Street.
Georges Chanot I, brother of François Chanot ranks alongside J.B. Vuillaume as one of the most prominent makers and businessmen in the world of 19th-century French violin making.

Georges Chanot was the son and pupil of Georges Chanot (I) and worked in his father's workshop. He moved to London in 1851 as an assistant to Charles Maucotel and established his own shop at 86 Wardour Street in 1858, later relocating to no. 137 in 1862.

Georges Adolphe Chanot was the eldest son of Georges (II) , born in London in 1855. Over the five generations of the Chanot dynasty, the practice appears to have been to set the elder children up in independent business.

Joseph Anthony Chanot, the youngest son, pupil, and successor of Georges Chanot (II), collaborated with the Dolmetsch family in the pioneering revival of the viol. He produced over 150 violins and an equally large number of bows between 1881 and 1912, although his production slowed significantly in later years due to restoration, repair, and business dealings.
William Arthur Chanot was the son and pupil of Joseph Anthony Chanot and succeeded to the family business in partnership with his brother John Alfred.
The Chanot business was inherited by Georges I's illegitimate son, Marie-Joseph Chardon and was renamed Chanot & Chardon in 1872. It produced both instruments and bows.
Nicolas Augustin Chappuy trained in Mirecourt and was active in Paris from around 1750. He was prolific, exhibiting a wide range of qualities typical of both the Mirecourt and 'vieux Paris' schools.
André Chardon was the son and pupil of Marie Joseph Chardon. He became active in 1911 but was called for military service in 1915.
Son of the violin maker Joseph Marie Chardon, he took over the family firm after his father's death in 1930 and worked in collaboration with his son, André.
Joseph Marie Chardon was the son, pupil, and successor of Georges Chanot. After his father's death, the business became known as Chanot & Chardon.
Joseph Chardon and his son Georges. Active c. 1900-1925.
Joseph Charotte established his practice at 36 rue Beauvoisin, Rouen, around 1830. He was a teacher of Jeandel.

Victor Joseph Charotte produced superior work compared to other makers in the family and created imitations of Stradivari and Guarneri. He won medals and diplomas at the Paris exhibitions in 1900 and 1925.
Joseph Charotte-Millot was a pupil of Aldric. His work was very characterful, resembling a vaguely Guarnerian model with open 'c' bouts and small outwardly pointing corners.
Eugene Chartreux made good quality violins in an antiqued finish for Beare & Son, London.

Carlo Chericoni was a pupil of G. Ornati. He worked briefly in Lodi and eventually established himself in Massa Apuania, Pisa. He was a prizewinner in Rome in 1954. His work reflected the Ornati style and the modern Cremona School manner, characterized by pale golden-brown varnish.
Georges Cherpitel was the son of Nicolas Émile Cherpitel. He worked for Cuesnon in Paris starting in 1896. He was a refined maker, though not prolific.
Louis Cherpitel was the son of Nicolas Emile Cherpitel . He trained in Mirecourt and apprenticed in Paris with Moinel from 1900 to 1903, after which he returned to Mirecourt.
Nicolas Emile Cherpitel worked as a pupil of Grandjon. He was employed by Gand Brothers in Paris starting in 1859.
Andre Chevrier worked for Caressa & Français in Paris before spending three years in Lyons with Boulangeot starting in 1920. He returned to Mirecourt to succeed his father at Thibouville-Lamy.
Antoine Chevrier was a French instrument maker active in Turin around 1823. He was rarely identified, and his instruments were usually neither signed nor branded

The work was very typical of Mirecourt and reflected the style of Nicolas. It featured a somewhat oversized design and a dull yellow varnish. The brand was 'C. Chevrier' and it was sometimes inscribed internally. A printed or manuscript label was rarely seen.

Joseph Chevrier was the son of Claude Chevrier and worked briefly for Berthod in Paris. From 1866, he served as the manager of the Thibouville-Lamy workshops in Mirecourt. He was considered one of the best makers in Mirecourt at that time.
Luigi Chiericato was a player and maker of double basses, as well as violins and violas. He is noted for using deep brown varnish in his instruments. Chiericato was a pupil of Eugenio Degani.
Gaetano Chiocchi was a violinist and musical director at the Teatro Grande in Padua. He learned violin making from Giuseppe Ceruti around 1858.

Paul Jean Baptiste Chipot was the son of Chipot-Vuillaume and apprenticed in Mirecourt. He worked for Deblaye among others before settling in Vendôme in 1923. In 1928, he was awarded the Grand Prix as the best artisan in France. His instruments featured very well-crafted copies of Italian and Tyrolean makers with appropriate labels.

Jean-Baptiste Chipot-Vuillaume was a pupil of Derazey and later worked in the Colin-Mezin shop, where he was recognized for his fine workmanship. In 1870, he married Stella Vuillaume, the daughter of a shoemaker, who was not related to the dealer J.B.
James Christie loosely modeled his work on Stradivari and Guarneri. The materials used were of poor quality, and the varnish was a dark orange-brown.
Nazzareno Ciarma was the son and pupil of Domenico Ciarma. He abandoned violin making to focus on accordion production. A printed label indicated: Ciarma Nazzareno / fece in Ascoli nel 1906.
Jeromos Cigl was a pupil of Pilat. He used a Stradivari model and applied red varnish. He created the work in Budapest in the year 1913.
Julian H. Clark retired from the car industry and established a violin shop in Seattle from 1962 to 1983.
Oscar L. Clark was a pupil and successor of Andrew Hyde. He demonstrated good professional quality workmanship. Made in Northampton, Mass, in the year a. d. 19..
Samuel Clark used local materials and applied a dark red-brown varnish. The instrument featured long slender soundholes and included a manuscript label.
Trained in Mirecourt, then employed by the Thibouville Lamy firm. After WWI moved to Paris where he worked independently. His bows tend to follow the style of Emile Ouchard and are of good quality.

Albert Claudot was the son of Felix Claudot and worked with his uncle Francois in Dijon in 1920. In 1936, he established his own shop on rue Amiral Roussin in Dijon. His work featured Stradivari and Guarneri models, showcasing fine workmanship with oil varnishes. The brand endpin and interior bore the inscription: 'Albert Claudot'. A printed label read: Albert Claudot, Luthier / à Dion 19.. no..

Augustin Claudot was the elder son of Charles Claudot (I). He was a maker of violins and bows, known for good workmanship and light orange varnish. A good Stradivari model violin and a lyre-guitar signed 'Augustin Claudot' were held in the Berlin Museum. Bows were branded with 'AUGUSTIN CLAUDOT'.
Charles Claudot was the son of Charles Claudot (I) and the brother of Augustin. His work was typical of Mirecourt, featuring a flat model with an unflattering yellow varnish.

Charles Claudot worked for three years in Edinburgh and another three years in Glasgow before returning to Grandjon's shop in Mirecourt, then moved to Rennes to work for Bonnel. Violins bear the manuscript signature: Charles Claudot Edinburgh, 1852.
François Claudot was the brother of Felix Claudot. He apprenticed to A.
Nicolas Claudot was the second son of Charles Claudot (I). His work featured a strong arch and was typical of Mirecourt, resembling that of Augustin. It was branded internally.
Paul Claudot was the son and pupil of Augustin Claudot. He worked for Husson in Paris. While his violins were unremarkable, his double basses were much sought-after.

Pierre Claudot was the son of Felix Claudot. He apprenticed with Thibouville and later with Dieudonne. From 1928, he worked for Granier in Marseilles, taking over the business in 1937. His work was decent, following a Bergonzi model with red oil varnish.
Jean Laurent Clement established a workshop at 16 rue des Petits Champs, Paris, from 1810 to 1848. Some labels indicated the address as rue St-Orge or rue des Bons-Enfants.
A fictitious name was used in trade work of the third order, produced by the Laberte-Humbert workshops in Mirecourt around 1890.
George Clough made copies of various makers, including Montagnana. The instrument was also inscribed with the name of the person for whom it was made.
John Clutterbuck was employed by the firm of W.E. Hill & Sons as a bow maker until 1971. His bows for the firm bear the number 14.

Albert Coad was active in Redruth from 1916 and in Penzance from 1930. Influenced by W. Mayson, he created copies of the 1716 Stradivari 'Messie' violin. His work featured good craftsmanship and high-quality materials. The interior of the back was signed, and some instruments were branded on the back button with 'A. Coad'. A printed label was also present.

Lawrence Cocker was a self-taught, ingenious, and innovative maker of violins and bows, highly respected and admired by colleagues in the English violin trade. In 1949, he received a Diploma of Honour at The Hague.
James Radford Coggin was an artist, sculptor, and musician originally from Salisbury, NC. He began making violins in 1968 in New York and worked full-time as a violin maker from the 1980s.
André Coinus was a pupil of L. Cherpitel. His professional work featured a decent red-brown varnish. He was located at 12, rue Fernuni, Mirecourt, No. 19.

Firm information about Prosper Colas' early life and training is not forthcoming, but it seems that he was born in Coincourt and apprenticed locally before arriving in Paris in 1871, probably to work for J.B. Vuillaume .

James Cole was a pupil of Tarr and was associated with Craske. His work varied greatly in quality, although his cellos were generally more successful. After 1858, his work was internally branded as 'J. Cole. ' He was based at Raby Street in Moss Side, Manchester, and produced work in Manchester in 1857.
Jean Colin was known for a quinton. He also created a violin housed in a Berlin museum, featuring very open 'c' bouts and a resinous yellow-brown varnish. It was branded: 'J. Cohn / J. C'.

Jean Baptiste Colin worked in the Mirecourt style. The model was based on Stradivari. It was labelled or inscribed from Paris. His work was similar to that of Collin-Mezin, though of inferior quality. Some pieces featured shaded varnish, typically a hard spirit recipe. The color ranged from clear and glassy yellow to red.
Louis Collenot was the grandson of F. Caussin and a pupil of H.

Raymond Collenot was the elder son and pupil of Louis Collenot. He had a workshop in Paris on rue Labat starting in 1931. Known for copies of Stradivari and Cappa, he used an 'antique' varnish. His manuscript label read: Collenot fils aine / Luthier / Paris 19..

Claude Nicolas Collin studied under N. F. Vuillaume in Brussels. He returned to Mirecourt in 1823. His workmanship was efficient, producing instruments of good trade quality with decent, well-colored varnish.

Charles Collin-Mézin was the son and assistant of Charles Jean-Baptiste Collin. He traveled extensively and spent time in America before returning to Mirecourt in 1900. Instruments made by him were labelled `Le Victorieux'. The business continued after his death under Jean Larcher and his son Charles Jean-Baptiste Collin-Mezin (III) (b. 1910).
Son of Charles Jean-Baptiste Collin-Mézin fils.
Charles Jean-Baptiste Collin-Mézin, originally from Mirecourt, was the son of Claude Nicolas Collin and studied under N. F.
French maker. Present shop in Munich.
F. W. Collingwood was an amateur maker. He created a Guarneri model and included a manuscript label that read: F. W. Collingwood / Londiniensis / Fecit 9 Sep. 1919.

Joseph Collingwood established himself in Ottumwa from 1882. He was a prolific self-taught maker who worked on Stradivari and Guarneri models. He also created a smaller pattern called the 'Collingwood de Luxe. ' His own varnish was used, and he was assisted by his son Drew Collingwood. Over 700 instruments were completed by him. Joseph Collingwood Fecit Ottumwa, Iowa.

Camillo Colombo studied under L. Bisiach around 1913 and later established himself independently at 13 viale col di Lana. He exhibited two violins in Cremona in 1937. His work featured classical models and undistinguished craftsmanship, with yellow to red spirit varnish.

Matthew Coltman worked for the bow department of the firm of W.E. Hill & Sons from 1977 until 1982, when he left to work for Edward Withers Ltd. Coltman started his own business in 1984. His bows for the Hill firm bear the number 19.
Walter Ewing Colton was a violinist and a self-taught amateur maker in New York around 1880. He was influenced by George Gemunder.

Antonio Comuni was a maker based in Piacenza, primarily known for his guitars, though he also produced violins noted for their characteristically long soundholes. His violin output dates from around 1816 onwards.

Louis M. Condax was a research chemist for Eastman-Kodak. He became an amateur violin maker around 1920 and later collaborated with Simone Sacconi on the analysis of classical Italian varnish. He completed 40 instruments, including a viola that was illustrated in the catalogue of the Sacconi Loan Exhibition in New York in 1966.

Georges Cone succeeded Boulangeot in 1928 and served as a repairer for the conservatoire and philharmonic orchestras in Lyons. A prolific maker, he was credited with over 200 violins and 25 cellos. His personal model was based on Stradivari. Georges Coné et Fils / à Lyon l'An 19.. No.. fait dans l'atelier / de Georges Cone a Lyon en 19..

Contemporary Hungarian maker. Son of Istvan Konya Senior and named after him. Later Italianised his name. Studied at the Cremona School with Sgarabotto, Morassi and Bissolotti and later became a teacher there himself. Remains based in Cremona and has won various violin making awards.
C. G.

The work exhibited decent quality in the typical Mirecourt style. It featured a flat model and an unremarkable yellow varnish. Cellos from this maker had a good reputation. They were branded as 'F. Contal / A Mirecourt' or 'F. Contal à Mirecourt. '.

Georges Contal was a pupil of Poiron and settled in Paris in 1899 at 113 Avenue Daumesnil after extensive travels across Europe. He was associated with Arnould in the development of a new mandolin form.

Luigi Contavalli was a proficient maker of his own models and Stradivari patterns. He was a prolific maker of cellos, using good golden-yellow varnish and locally sourced timber. He received gold medals in Marseilles in 1903, London in 1905, and Bologna in 1907.
Primo Contavalli was the son and pupil of Luigi Contavalli. He was a fairly prolific maker of instruments, using personalized Stradivari and Guarneri models, and applied a golden-yellow varnish.
Alfredo Contino was a pupil of Vincenzo Postiglione from 1902 and succeeded to the business in 1916. He was best known for his very precise copies of work from the Gagliano family.

José Contreras was the greatest Spanish violin maker, known as 'Il Grenadino. ' He worked in Madrid from around 1740 and was strongly influenced by Stradivari, whose instruments were becoming recognized in the Spanish court at that time. However, his style differed significantly from Stradivari's own, exhibiting an impromptu feeling in both finish and technique.

Hugh Wyatt Cooper was a watchmaker and violin maker. He was active in Glasgow from 1893. His work included Stradivari and 1744 'Sainton' Guarneri violin models. The workmanship was very fine and disciplined. He used commercial 'Whitelaw' oil varnish, which over time became rather dark, hard, and chippy. His address was 75 Dundas Street, Glasgow No. 19.

After starting work in the case department in 1917, Arthur Copley transferred W.E. Hill & Sons' bow department, where he remained for 30 years, though he had little affinity for the work. Copley's bows are marked with the numeral 1, the first in a new identification system used by the firm.

Work shows similarities with that of Andreas Statler as well as Bernardo Calcagno, with whom he lived and probably shared a workshop around 1730. Not the same maker as the Jacopo Filippo Cordano who was active in Genoa around 1770.
George Corsby was originally a shoemaker from Northampton. He operated at 8 Princes Street, London from 1835 to 1841, and then moved to 9 Princes Street from 1841 to 1869.
Giorgio Corsini was a violinist and a self-taught violin maker, guided by R. Fredi.
Andrea Cortese was a self-taught violin maker from a wealthy Genoese family, influenced by C. Candi.

Eugène Corvisier worked for Deroux and Audinot after being a pupil of Cunault, taking over Audinot's shop located at 17 Boulevard Bonne-Nouvelle in 1908, which was originally the atelier of S. Vuillaume. His workmanship was very effective and professional, featuring a red-orange varnish.
Antonio Costa was active from 1919. He created copies of Guadagnini.

Giovanni Battista Costa created fine work in a distinctively Seraphin style, possibly as a pupil. The soundholes were elegant and Amati-like with a high arch. The scroll resembled Seraphin's, though it slightly lacked finesse in comparison. A rich red-brown varnish was thinly applied. He made this in Venice in 1774.
Louis Charles Courtier studied under Aubry. He utilized Stradivari and Guarneri models. The varnish was a red-brown oil type. It bore an internal signature.

Camille Couture worked in the rue Jeanne-Marie, Montreal. He created original models with good workmanship. He made 'alto-or-octave' violins and violas that were supposedly characterized by a cello-like depth of tone.
Joseph Couturieux was a pupil of Vuillaume. He was rarely encountered and was both signed and branded.
The item featured double purfling and was finished with yellow or red varnish. It bore the brand: 'Couturier Michel A Paris. '.
Nicolas Couturieux created some well-made examples in the style of Bernardel. Others were of the more typical flat, yellow Mirecourt type, inspired by Didier Nicolas.
William Cox created models based on Stradivari and Guarneri. He prepared the amber varnish himself.

Thomas Craig was a music publisher, instrument maker, and repairer. He varnished and sold imported German trade instruments. He established his business at 408 George Street, Aberdeen from 1896 to 1900 and at 365-369 George Street from 1901 to 1923. The shop continued to operate at 220 Union Street after his death.
Charles Cramond trained under Joseph Ruddiman and took over his business in 1810 at 27 Netherkirkgate, Aberdeen. One violin was jointly labelled by Ruddiman and Cramond.
George W. Crank exhibited at the Music Trades Convention in St. Louis in 1914.

George Craske first studied violin making with William Forster in London and began making instruments for Thomas Dodd and the firm of Muzio Clementi in the early 19th century. After relocating several times in search of a satisfactory place to open ... a shop, he settled in Birmingham, where he began an extremely prolific career as a talented copyist of Amati , Guarneri , and Stradivari .
James Crawford was employed by V. C. Squier and eventually became the head of the repair department.
Bartolomeo Cristofori was a significant figure in the history of musical instruments. He was most renowned as a maker of keyboards and as the inventor of the piano.
Herbert Charles Cross worked for T. Toomey from 1950. He was made in Barnet, Hertfordshire, in the year 19.

Nathaniel Cross, son of an engraver, began his violin-making career in the early 1700s and partnered with Barak Norman around 1713. A Norman viol from that year is inscribed 'Nathaniel Cross wrought my back and belly', and Cross' name appeared alongside Norman's on a violin label in 1724, which included the address 'St Paul's Churchyard'.
John Crowther began work for John Kennedy in Clare Market. He subsequently operated from his own shops at 2 Ely Court around 1778 and at 22 Fetter Lane around 1792.
Armand Cuisset studied the violin at the Brussels Conservatoire. He subsequently trained as a maker in various Belgian and German workshops.

Georges Cunault trained with Sebastien Vuillaume and Miremont from 1874 to 1882 and established his own workshop in 1880. He occupied several addresses in Paris, including 53 Faubourg Poissoniere in 1882, 29 rue des Martyrs in 1884, 6 rue Clauzet in 1889, and 31 rue Navarin in 1893, before moving to 19 rue Navarin.

Mirecourt bow maker Eugène Cuniot, known as "Cuniot-Hury", trained and worked with his father until his father's death in 1884, when Cuniot-Hury took over the firm. Mirecourt was in the midst of tremendous growth as a center of the violin trade, and in particular the influence of Charles Nicolas Bazin held sway in bow making ... circles.

Student of Otto Erdesz and subsequently worked in Toronto, Paris and Cremona. In 1984 Curtin set up a joint making studio with Gregg Alf in Ann Arbor. They dissolved the partnership after 12 years and Curtin now works independently in Ann Arbor.
Joseph Curtin and Gregg Alf set up a joint violin making studio, Curtin & Alf, in Ann Arbor in 1984. They dissolved the partnership after 12 years.

Johannes Bernardus Cuypers was the son of Johannes Theodorus Cuypers and the younger brother of Johannes Franciscus. He was a lesser maker compared to his father and brother, serving as an assistant in the family workshop.

Johannes Francis Cuypers, son and assistant of Johannes Theodorus, worked for his father until his death in 1808, including a period from 1783 to 1823 spent in Liedestraat, Amsterdam. Recognized as the most refined maker of the family, he produced more Stradivarian flat arches with a polished finish, sometimes imitating the blackened chamfers of a Stradivarius head.

One of the most prominent makers from The Netherlands and among the first to turn away from Amati in favor of a Stradivari model, Johannes Theodorius Cuijpers worked in the Hague from 1752 to till the early 1800s. It has been suggested that he received ... some training in France, but historical evidence on the subject is inconclusive.

Mario D'Alessandro trained at R. Wurlitzer & Co. under Roman Klier starting in 1948 and later studied with Simone Sacconi. He established his own business in Nutley, New Jersey, in 1974. His work included classical models, some featuring an antique shaded finish. A violin was illustrated in the catalogue of the Sacconi Loan Exhibition in New York in 1966. He recorded nine violins and two violas.

Dario D'Attili emigrated to the USA in 1935. He worked for Jargo Petrenela in New York from 1937 to 1938, for Emil Herrmann from 1938 to 1951, and for Rembert Wurlitzer from 1951 to 1974, alongside Simone Sacconi.

Alessandro D'Espine is one of the more enigmatic makers of the 19th Century Turin school. A nobleman by birth, D'Espine worked as a surgeon and dentist to the nobility, and probably engaged in violin making as a serious hobby rather than as a profession.
Jean Frédéric Schmitt's workshop in Lyon. Output three levels of quality: "Vincent", "Delaune" and "Daber".
Franck Daguin was born in Lyon, where he studied cabinet-making before apprenticing in bowmaking in the J.F Schmitt workshop. He opened his own workshop in 1991.

Frans Waldemar Dahlén was active as an amateur from 1884 and turned professional around 1890. He established himself in Göteborg in 1920. His instruments featured Stradivari and Guarneri models, utilizing orange-red and red-brown oil varnish. Some were close copies of individual Stradivari instruments. He received a gold medal at the Stockholm Exhibition in 1925.

The instruments of Giuseppe Dall’Aglio present a fascinating blend of refinement and eccentricity. At first glance they can seem irregular—sometimes even awkward—with middle bouts that draw too sharply inward and an overall geometry that refuses to conform to the cleaner, more measured outlines of his contemporaries.

Pietro Antonio Dalla Costa worked in nearby Treviso and was classified as a maker of the Venetian school. His work was neat and charming in the Amati manner, although it was somewhat weaker than that of the finest Venetian makers of the period.
Sebastian Dallinger created very good work on the Stainer model, featuring a high arch and deep edge flute. Instruments produced later were less extreme.
Edmond Daniel was the son of Charles Daniel and worked for Gand in Paris. Later, he took over his father's business in Marseilles at a new address, 5 rue Paradis, until after 1852.
Silvio Danieli owned one violin featuring ebony inlaid edges. A cello was also reported by Vannes. The label indicated a gold medal awarded in Rome in 1926.

Samuel Wesley Daniels was the husband of Constance Daniels. He was a self-taught amateur maker starting in 1936. A farmer, he retired to Jerome, Idaho, where he established a violin business. He created Stradivari and Guarneri models using commercial varnish. Most instruments were made with good imported wood, while some utilized local materials. He produced over 100 violins and three violas.

George Darby was a violin and bow maker. He created models based on Stradivari, Guarneri, Amati, and Gagliano. His work featured orange-red varnish and generally utilized fine materials and good craftsmanship; contemporary commentators noted it was comparable to French work. He operated in Cremona House, Bristol, in the year 1902.
Charles Claude François Darche was a pupil of N. F.
Hilaire Darche was the son of Joseph Darche and trained at Gaillard and Nicolas Ainé in Mirecourt. He later worked for N.
Nicolas Darche was the brother of Joseph and Charles Francois Darche. He trained in Mirecourt and was employed by N.
Paul Darche was the son and pupil of Charles Claude Francois Darche. He trained in Mirecourt before rejoining his father in Brussels.
Jean-Auguste Darte worked in Mirecourt from 1865. He was a pupil, son-in-law, and successor of N.

William Davidson worked in Edinburgh, creating instruments based on a roughly Stradivarian model. He used commercial amber varnish, which was either yellow or dark red. A manuscript label was present.
The viola featured very deep ribs, with purfling positioned well in from the edge. It had a very flat arch and was low set over long soundholes.
John Day was a violinist and an amateur maker. He created copies of his own Guarneri del Gesii violin.
William Samuel Day took up violin making in 1924 after retiring from the Customs service, working in his shop in Plymouth with his son, who was also named William Samuel.

Evidently an adventuresome young man, Paolo de Barbieri ran away from home to become a sailor at the age of 13 and returned to Genoa six years later, in 1908. He then apprenticed to Cesare Candi before serving in the navy and returning to work for his teacher.
Thomas De Bomé was a dilletante, yet he achieved good results. Instruments were labeled in the Musèe de la Musique, Paris.
Filippo De Filippi produced good work and varnish. It remained currently unknown and unverified.
Remo De Giorgio created Guarneri, Stradivari, and personal models featuring dark golden or golden-red varnish. He gained awards in Padua in 1932 and in Cremona in 1936.
Augusto De Gregori sent several instruments of his own make to Russia through a musician he knew. He created Gagliano models and was also recognized for a quartet made in the manner of Dalla Corte.
Giovanni Battista De Lorenzi was not a violin maker; he was an organ builder and a general music dealer. Some instruments bore his label.

Gennaro De Luccia worked in Casigliano and Mercato Cilento, Salerno. He was primarily a maker of guitars and mandolins but also produced a significant number of violins with clear orange-yellow varnish, which were mostly exported to the USA.
Michele De Luccia was a pupil of his cousin Gennaro de Luccia and his uncle Michele (I). He worked early in Italy, primarily focusing on plucked instruments.

Vincenzo De Luccia was the brother of Michele de Luccia (II). He was a pupil of Matteo and his father Michele (I). He worked in Philadelphia from 1901 to 1950, assisted by his brother Giovanni. His creations included violins, mandolins, guitars, and bows.

Carlo De March was a Venetian violin and bow maker who studied under Vestoli in Lugo before relocating to Venice in 1940. His fine workmanship was influenced by Degani, and he became an active bow maker in 1955.

Umberto De Peccati specialized in double basses and also made plucked instruments with yellow-red spirit varnish in classical models. He exhibited in Cremona in 1937. He worked in Soncino (Cremona) in the year 19..
Augustino De Planis created rather loose work, typical of the Genovese school. The soundholes were long and angled, and the arch was flat with pale yellow-gold varnish over a grey ground.
Augusto (Angelo) de Rub. The model resembled those of Stainer, Amati, or Guidantus. It featured fine materials and a quality golden-yellow varnish.
Nicola de Rubeis was known as Italiano detta il Romagnolo. He created his work in Nizza in the year 192..
Ralph de Saint-Croix created an amateur work on a clumsy model. It featured red varnish and was made in London in 1905.

Cosmo De Souza worked with the Chanot family in Manchester, London, and Paris around 1890. He operated independently in Leeds and Bradford starting in 1899, in Birmingham in 1903, and at Lacey Green (Cheshire) in 1906. His work included strongly Chanot-influenced copies of classical instruments.

Pietro Paolo De Vitor was of Venetian origin according to his label. His work was rarely encountered, but it featured striking form and workmanship, representing an interesting blend of early Brescian and later Rogeri styles.

Valentino De Zorzi opened a shop in Pistoia in 1880, having previously fought with Garibaldi's forces before working as a blacksmith and armourer in Bologna. He was commissioned by Count Vieri Ganucci Cancellieri in Pistoia to build his first violin.
Mark Dearlove worked in Leeds from 1808 to 1849. He employed Thomas Absam, John Gough, and John Fryer at different times.

Mark William Dearlove, son and pupil of Mark Dearlove, took over his father's workshop in Boar Lane in 1828. He entered a brief partnership with John Charles Fryer, with their business listed as 'Dearlove and Fryer' at Benson's Yard, 23 Boar Lane.

Michele Deconet was born near Strasbourg and traveled widely as a violinist. Traditionally it has been assumed that he took up violin making in Venice in the mid-18th century, but in fact there is no conclusive historical evidence that he ever held a formal apprenticeship or registered as a violin-maker in that city or elsewhere.
Georges Defat was the son and pupil of Émile Laurent. After apprenticing with P.
Pierre Defresne trained in Paris. He was appointed luthier to the Duke of Luxembourg. His output was modest, and he created a small model. The varnish was golden-yellow.

Domenico Degani created Amati and Maggini models, frequently featuring carved female heads instead of the scroll. He also made guitars, viols, and lutes. The finish was coarse, but the varnish was a fine light red-brown.
Eugenio Degani was the son and pupil of Domenico Degani . In 1868, he moved to Montagnana, where he began making violins at least by 1872, winning a gold medal at the Treviso Esposizione.
Giulio Degani was the son of Eugenio , with whom he worked from an early age. After his father’s death in 1901 he took over the family’s Venice workshop until emigrating to the USA in 1915.

A very rare and interesting maker whose well-defined work is of high quality but somewhat eccentric in form. A little reminiscent of Rogeri, the outline has short `c' bouts and the scroll is distinguished by the very deeply cut throat beneath the volute.

Alfredo Del Lungo was the son and pupil of Giuseppe del Lungo. He worked at the Conservatorio Cherubini in Florence and the Accademia in Sienna. He later emigrated to Argentina, where he served as the director of the School of Lutherie in Tucuman. His craftsmanship featured fine workmanship on classical models and utilized orange spirit varnish. Fece in Firenze nel 1949.
Gérard J. Deleplanque made many and various bowed and plucked stringed instruments.
Louis Delignon was the younger son of Charles Delignon. He worked for Collin-Mezin, G.
Auguste Delivet served an apprenticeship in Mirecourt. He was the nephew and pupil of Victor Rambaux.
Alfonso Della Corte created copies that were very competent and, in some instances, quite convincing. He used a suitable clear golden-yellow varnish.

Marcel Deloger apprenticed to C. Quenoil. He established himself independently in Versailles in 1936. He created large and small pattern violins that were fully varnished and not 'antiqued', as well as double basses and various types of historical instruments. He received prizes in Paris in 1937 and 1938.

François Joseph Delprato worked in Paris with Marchand, then in Chicago with Lyon & Healy, and in New York in 1918, before returning to Toulouse, France, in 1926, and finally to Houecourt, Vosges. He created copies of classical instruments that displayed very finely controlled workmanship. His work was branded: 'F. Delprato'.

Marcel Demey was a pupil of Pierre Jean Henri Hel from 1909 and later served as his assistant until Hel's death in 1937. He continued to work at Hel's shop until 1943 for Hel's widow before establishing his own business in Lille.

H. Denis made commercial instruments at the Laberte-Humbert factory. His work was labeled from '3 Faubourg à Mattaincourt'. Vannes described the instruments as poor quality, while Henley indicated that they featured good workmanship and materials, along with clear orange-red spirit varnish.
Honoré (Jean-Joseph) Derazey apprenticed in Mirecourt and was later employed in various ateliers in Paris. He established his own business in Mirecourt, which quickly became very successful.

Justin Amedée Derazey was the son and pupil of Honoré Derazey . He established his own workshop in Mirecourt around 1850 and succeeded Nicolas fils in 1884, after which his instruments featured the brand of J.

Georges Deroux was a pupil of Honore Derazey and was known as 'Deroux père. ' In 1846, he established his own business in Mirecourt, which became a training ground for many luthiers, including the Hill family members who later founded the leading European firm of expertise and restoration in London in 1882. He left Mirecourt in 1880 to work with Mennesson in Rheims.

Sébastien Auguste Deroux worked as the son and pupil of Georges Deroux . He was employed by Silvestre in Lyons from 1866 to 1869, and then by Miremont in Paris from 1873 to 1884, where he became the head of the workshop.
Giuseppe Desiato worked in the Via Speransella in Naples, where he made several very respectable instruments known in the style of Stradivari and Gagliano, using good materials.

Pietro Paolo Desideri created models of Guadagnini or Guarneri derivation, characterized by coarsely finished and rather undistinguished workmanship that utilized mediocre material, enhanced by a good quality golden-brown varnish with good texture and consistency. The printed labels included: P. Paulas Desideri / fecit Ripae 1794 and Pietro Paolo Desideri / fecit Ripae 1837.
Joseph D. Deulin moved from Madison to Washington D.
John G. Deveau used varnish that was prepared by Calvin L. Harrison.
John Devereux worked for Bernard Simon Fendt. He emigrated to Fitzroy, Australia in 1864, drawn by the gold rush of the time (according to the Hill Archive).

Frank Devoney received guidance from several local makers but developed a strong interest in Savart's work on acoustics and the principles of Ghladni-pattern plate tuning. In 1893, he published a book of his findings titled The Acoustics of Violin-making in Edinburgh.
Armando Di Lelio studied under Rudolfo Paralupi. He created a personal model that was well finished. The same varnish as his master was used. He worked in Rome in the year 19.
Alban Dick apprenticed to A. Wilfer and was later employed by A. Vogt in Frankfurt, succeeding to his business in 1907. He produced fine professional work.
Edward Dickenson worked on a coarsely exaggerated Stainer pattern.

William Dickie worked on Cremonese models with red-yellow varnish, according to Henley. His work is presently unknown. He was also a bow maker, known for an original design that featured a rather straight stick and a modified head.
A fine small viola was signed on the interior, with the address listed as Drury Lane. One example resembled the early Hill workshops, featuring a flat arch and pale golden yellow varnish.
Dmitri Didchenko was a refugee of the Russian Revolution. He worked in Poznan and Warsaw, Poland, starting in 1917.
Antoine Didelin was the son of Henry Didelin . He registered as a luthier in 1779.
J. Didelot used a fictitious name on factory-produced work. The name was associated with Lutherie Lorraine.

Marius Didier apprenticed at Thibouville-Lamy in Mirecourt. He was one of the finest makers from Mirecourt and received several awards for his work that imitated classical Cremonese instruments, particularly the 1716 Stradivari 'Nessie' violin. He branded his instruments and included a printed label.

Paul Didier was the son of Marius Didier and worked for his father until 1936, after which he was located in Metz. He received medals at Paris and Toulouse. His work featured oil varnish, occasionally shaded to imitate age.

August Diehl was the son and pupil of Friedrich Diehl. He worked in Hamburg starting in 1875. He received a gold medal at the St. Louis Exhibition in the USA. His creations included copies of classical instruments and personal models, featuring good quality varnish, some with carved heads and other decorations.
Johann Diehl was the eldest son of Martin Diehl (I). His work was mostly inspired by Stradivari, although it exhibited some crude details.

Born in 1890, Amédée Dieudonné learned violin making with Gustave Bazin , son of bow maker Charles Nicolas . He worked for the Darché firm in Brussels before settling in his ... hometown of Mirecourt around 1920.

Luigi Digiuni was active from 1920. He created an individual outline and model, with many made based on classical proportions. In 1922, he devised the 'Tioletto, ' an instrument that lay between the cello and viola.

Gaetano Dionelli worked in the style of Scarampella. He was a pupil of Dall'Aglio and was particularly known for cellos and basses. His varnish was a good orange or red spirit. He made his instruments in Mantova in the year 1866.
Justin Victor Diter apprenticed to Paul Bailly. He worked for Blanchard in Lyons from 1890 to 1896.
Harry Dobbs was a self-taught maker starting in 1970. He completed over 100 instruments, primarily violas.
Marco Dobretsovich moved to Cairo in 1914. He was a pupil of Pollastri in Bologna around 1920.
L. Dobrianski was chiefly a repairer and restorer.

Makers of the Dodd family were among the most important in the 19th century London bow trade. While John I stands out for his craftsmanship and immense productivity, his father Edward, brother James I, and nephews James II and Edward ...

Edward Dodd was a bow and string maker. He worked in partnership with his brother James (II) at various addresses, as listed for James (II), until around 1836, when he remained alone in Vauxhall after James departed for Finsbury. His niece, Emma Rosenberg, learned string making from him.

James Dodd was a bow maker of considerable importance. He was the son of Edward Dodd and established his business at 9 Granby Buildings, Lambeth, with the assistance of his sons James (II) and Edward (II).

Known as the 'English Tourte ' for his leading role in the development of the modern bow in Britain, John 'Kew' Dodd was initially employed as a fine metalworker. Proximity to this trade afforded him the tools and expertise to develop the screw mechanisms being pioneered in Paris around the same time, and Dodd probably took up ... bow making in the 1770s–80s.

Younger brother of John Dodd , Thomas Dodd began working as a bowmaker around 1784 but by 1794 had switched to making and dealing in violins. In the early years he employed Bernhard Simon Fendt I and John Frederick Lott I, both of whom were responsible for the great number of excellent cellos and double basses the shop produced. ...
Edward Newman (Edward II) Withers. Son and pupil of Edward (I), active from 1856.

Giovanni Dollenz was initially thought to have learned his craft from Cremonese violin maker Lorenzo Storioni during Storioni's itinerant travels to Trieste in the early 1800s. However, proof of Storioni's presence there is limited to several instruments bearing Trieste labels.

Giuseppe Dollenz was the son and pupil of Giovanni Dollenz, a Trieste violin maker. More prolific than his father, he followed a similar style in the 19th century, although his varnish was somewhat inferior, being less transparent and light in color.
Heinz Dölling was a first-class bow maker. He was the son and pupil of Otto Gustav Dölling. He established his work in Wernitzgrün, Saxony, in 1938. His brand was HEINZ DÖLLING.

Hermann Dölling was the son and pupil of Hermann Moritz Dölling. He worked for Ludwig Glasel, followed by Ernst Liebich in Breslau, and finally August Riechers in Berlin before returning to the family business in Markneukirchen, which became the 'Vogtländische Musikinstrumenten-Fabrik' in 1886.

Louis Dolling studied under Karl Gustav Otto in Leipzig. He later worked for Ernst Liebich in Breslau from 1877 to 1883. In 1884, he returned to Markneukirchen to assist his father. He produced commercial copies of Cremonese master works using spirit varnish.
Max Dolling was the son and pupil of Hermann Dölling (I). He made commercial instruments on a factory basis.

Arnold Dolmetsch was a violinist and a pupil of Vieuxtemps in Brussels from approximately 1871 to 1873. He came to London to finish his studies at the Royal College of Music, where he developed an interest in the historical performance of music.

Lucien Dolphyn studied the violin at Ghent Conservatoire. He had a limited output as a violin maker and used orange-red oil varnish. Instruments de Solistes / Lucien Dolphyn / Luthier-violiniste A Bruxelles / année.. No..
James W. Dooley was a self-taught violin maker. He used fine materials and rich gold oil varnish, along with Stradivari and personal models.

Nicolaus Döpfer was the father-in-law of Martin Diehl and Jacob Steininger , and he effectively founded the Mainz school of violin makers. He was appointed as the instrument maker to the Court in Mainz.

Johann (Hans) Andreas Dörffel was the best maker of this family. The model was large, with an arching that was not too exaggerated. His work was well-finished, featuring neat scrolls and a good golden brown varnish. Violas were of very useful size and were worthy instruments. He was also known for richly ornamented viola d'amores.
Michael Dötsch apprenticed to Michael Kohlbacher in Luby (Schönbach). He worked in various ateliers across Europe from 1894 to 1908.

William Henry Dow emigrated to Australia in 1854. He was a prolific maker who used Stradivari and, later, Guarneri models. His work was well made and finished. He established a very good reputation in Australia.
Charles Drouin was the son and pupil of Etienne Drouin and succeeded Baur-Bourdot. He took over the name and business of 'Chipot-Vuillaume' in 1897.
Etienne Drouin was a well-regarded workman in the upper class of commercial work. The varnish was a typical yellow Mirecourt. A manuscript label was present.

Louis Drouin was born and trained in Mirecourt. He emigrated to the USA in 1915 and worked with Delunet in New York. Some fine copies of classical work were created, but he was chiefly employed as a repairer and restorer.
Frantisek Xavier Drozen studied under J. B.
Victor Paul Dubois worked for F. P. Brown, E. F. Bryant, and W. S. Goss, and he finally joined O. Ditson in 1917, where he became the head of the violin department.
Nicolas Duchaine apprenticed at Mirecourt as a violin and bow maker. He supplied bows to F.
Nicolas Duchaine was a bow maker. He was the son, pupil, and assistant of Nicolas Duchaine (I).
Colin Duchene created a Stradivari model featuring orange varnish, as reported by Henley; however, this claim was not verified by other sources and may be a misreading of Jean-Baptiste Colin.

Nicolas Duchesne branded and signed instruments 'à Paris', although this was a common fiction among Mirecourt makers. His shop was known as 'A la Ville de Padoue'. The model was slender with a flat arch and an oversized scroll. It featured extended `f' holes with a distinctive central nick and had a dull yellow varnish. It was branded: Nicolaus Duchesne A Paris.
André Paul Dugad was a violin and bow maker. He worked for Caressa & Français in Paris from 1920 to 1932.
Aubrey Dugarde reported the information, but it remained unverified by other sources.

One of the most successful and prolific English violin makers of the 18th century, Richard Duke established his workshop in central London around 1743. Over the course of its existence the enterprise grew to encompass multiple locations within the city and employed numerous makers of instruments and bows, including William Napier ... and John Betts , who bought the shop from Duke's daughter Anne after his death in 1783.
George Duncan was a pupil and successor to Walter Plain . He worked in Glasgow from 1875 at 21 Brunswick Street, which was previously the premises of Walter Plain , and later at 33 Queen Street.

James Duncan was active from around 1890. He crafted violins and cellos based on the Stradivari pattern, featuring large scrolls. His varnish, which was thick and orange, was of his own recipe. Cluny, Aberdeenshire was his location, and he produced No. 19.
Robert Duncan was the most significant maker of the Aberdeen school. His work, very refined and based on the Stainer model, was comparable to contemporary Florentine craftsmanship.
John Dunthorne made a decently constructed cello based on a loosely Amati form; the front featured very thick wood with a light golden colored varnish and painted purfling, bearing this label.
Adolphe Dupary apprenticed to Darte and was employed by P. Bailly. He established himself in Paris in 1906. His work featured Stradivari and Guarneri models and was well finished.
Georges Louis Dupuy was a pupil of Vatelot and Brugere. In 1939, he established a workshop with his father at 4 rue Demours.

Philippe Dupuy was the son and pupil of Georges Dupuy and the grandson of E. Sartory. He apprenticed with L. Mougenot in Mirecourt before joining his father in Paris. He succeeded to the business in 1968 and was joined by bow maker C. Schaeffer in 1977.
Herman Gerard Duwaer was the son of Pieter Duwaer, a piano maker. He began his career as a cabinet maker and registered as a violin maker in 1883.

Carolus Joseph Dvorak was the son and pupil of Jaroslav Dvorák. His interpretations of Cremonese work featured shaded varnish and were quite similar to those of Lupot. The workmanship was very fine. A printed label indicated: Carolus Joseph / Dvorák / fecit Pragae 19..
Jan Baptista Dvorak apprenticed to Jan Kulik from 1840 to 1844. He worked in Budapest for J.
Jaroslav Antonin Dvorák was the son and pupil of Jan Baptista Dvorak . He independently established himself in Prague starting in 1885.

George Dyker produced well over 40 violins and some cellos based on the Stradivari model. His work resembled that of George Haxton from Glasgow; it was very cleanly executed and featured a thick oil varnish of orange color.
Arthur W. Dykes was born in Leeds, Yorkshire.

George Langton Dykes was the son and pupil of Harry Dykes. He became an active violin maker at the age of 12 and also received training from Paul Bailly, who had worked for his father. He created very fine French-style copies of classical instruments, utilizing a varnish recipe that supposedly originated from Vuillaume's workshop, which Bailly taught him. He was greatly admired during his time.
David Earl was one of the last bow makers employed by the firm of W.E. Hill & Sons , where he apprenticed under Brian Alvey .
Eugène Eberle was the son of Oscar Eberle, a professor of cello at Rotterdam Conservatoire. He apprenticed with C.

Johann Ulrich Eberle was a pupil of Thomas Edlinger (III) in Prague and established his own workshop in 1727. Recognized as one of the finest makers of the Prague school, he followed the Stainer model while occasionally incorporating elements from Cremona.

Only recently has credible biographical information concerning the violin makers of the Neapolitan school come to light. Until the experts who have researched this material publish their archival findings, we leave the birth and death dates of these makers as previously accepted.
Gustav Edel was a musician and an amateur maker. The instrument was a Stradivari model, well executed with his own varnish.
Ernest Edler was the son and pupil of J. Henry Edler. He primarily worked as a repairer, although several new instruments were recorded.
Son of Friedrich Edler (III), brother of Friedrich (IV), and Richard. Worked for J.
Contemporary bowmaker in LA

Born in Salt Lake City, Rock Eggen is now based in Denver, Colorado where he makes, sells, and repairs instruments. Eggen’s recognitions include first place in Violin Workmanship at the 1988 VSA International Competition.
20th-century Neapolitan maker, mainly of plucked instruments (mandolins, guitars) and some bowed strings.
George Nicolas Einsele worked for William Lewis & Son in Chicago, where he made approximately 70 instruments from 1912 to 1914. He returned to Brunswick around 1915.
Sandor Elek was a pupil of Bohak in Budapest. He created good copies of classical Italian work.

Albert Ellersieck was a self-taught German maker who worked in Rostock, Mecklenburg from 1878 to 1892, in Greiz, Thuringia in 1895, and finally in Berlin starting in 1900. He received several exhibition medals for his craftsmanship, which was noted for being good, although often slightly oversized.

Helmuth Ellersieck, son of Albert Ellersieck, apprenticed with Ernest Kreul in Markneukirchen from 1900 to 1903. He later worked for Otto Möckel and August Friedel in Berlin, and subsequently for Hjorth in Copenhagen.
William Elliot was a fairly prolific maker known for his refined French style based on Stradivari and Guarneri models. He produced works that reflected this distinctive approach.

J. F. Christian Emde was active from 1836. He chiefly occupied himself as a repairer, but his original work was supposedly rather odd, employing various unconventional techniques with generally inferior results. He was a Bogen-Instrumentenmacher in Leipzig and manufactured in 1841.
Contemporary maker and repairer based in Wales, UK.

Francesco Emiliani worked on via del Governo Vecchio, Rome. He persisted with a hybrid Amati/Stainer model of slightly diminished scale, which was beautifully made and very Italianate, featuring fine soft golden oil varnish.

John K. Empsall was a violinist of the Bradford Symphony Orchestra and an amateur maker. He experimented with varnish and imported mass-produced instruments in the white, finishing them with various varnishes that yielded widely varying results. He created a few instruments entirely of his own make.
The production was commercial factory output, branded as 'F. & R. E' or 'F. R. Enders, Markneukirchen. '.
Charles Enel apprenticed to Grillon and Gustave Bazin. He worked for Mougenot and Deroux in Mirecourt, then in Switzerland and Germany, and finally with Silvestre and Maucotel in Paris.
Apprenticed to Leon Mougenot 1916. Worked with his uncle Charles in Paris 1919-1934. Established in Lyons 1935. Pierre Enel Neveu /Lyon en L'an 1938 Pierre Enel Neveu / a Lyon. Anne' e 19..

Gulbrand Enger was a pupil of Jacobson and Vuillaume. He served as a maker to the Danish court. His instruments followed the Stradivari model and featured a strongly worked scroll and sound holes. They had a brown varnish and exhibited fine craftsmanship and materials.
Hagbarth Enger was the son and pupil of Gulbrand Enger. He worked in several cities across Europe before returning to his father's shop in Copenhagen.
Andreas Engleder was a pupil of his uncle Joseph Fischer in Regensburg and of J. C.
Moved to Budapest in 1927 and studied as an artist at the Academia of Fine Arts. Active violin maker from 1941.
Franz Anton Ernst was a celebrated violinist and concert master. He was a violin maker from 1785, using the Stradivari model. His work was finely crafted and featured very good quality varnish.

Johann Anton Ertl established his workshop at 863 Schulerstrasse, Vienna. He was probably the brother of Karl Ertl. In 1811, he served as the vice-president of the Corporation of Luthiers in the city. His work was known for its fine quality in the Viennese style. He also made plucked instruments.
Bow maker / Violin maker (c. 1893 – 1925)
The personal model sometimes featured doubled purfling. The workmanship was undistinguished. It was branded on the interior and under the button: `Espana en Barcelona'.
Raffaele Esposito was the son of Giovanni Esposito. He created a Gagliano model that featured yellow-brown varnish.
Clément Eulry was a bow maker and a pupil of Nicolas Maire. He also occasionally made violins and at least one mandolin.
Jean Eulry apprenticed at Laberte & Magnié from 192é to 1923. He worked for A.
Bow maker / Violin maker (c. 1725 – 1786)

Jacob Eury, for some time mistakenly identified as Nicolas Eury, initially studied violin making with his father François but soon took up bow making. After eight years of military service Eury returned to Mirecourt and may have apprenticed in the workshop of Jean ... 'Grand' Adam .

Karl Euschen worked in Saarbruck. He was employed at Hug & Co in Zurich from 1919 to 1922 under Tenucci. J. E. Zust awarded him a master's certificate. He later worked for M. Macke in Berlin and for Huni and Amsler in Winterthur. In 1939, he established his own business there. His work featured clean execution on classical forms, though details were unrefined, and he used commercial brown varnish.
Jacques Charles Eve established himself at the sign 'A La Fortune' at various addresses: rue Dauphine, S. Germain around 1757, rue Culture S.

David Ewan was a violinist and composer in the traditional Scottish style. He was also a skilled and professional violin maker. He produced 100 violins and 20 cellos. His instruments featured a heavy Stradivarian form, with early work characterized by high arches. They had a thin yellow or brown varnish and were branded beneath the button.

Charles Eyles was an artist and a fairly prolific amateur violin maker from around 1890. He was active in Bedford, Wangford, Harpenden, and Bournemouth, using models derived from Stradivari and Guarneri.
Antonio Fabiani was a violinist and professor of music. He won diplomas in Rome and studied under Andrea and Leandro Bisiach as well as Simone Sacconi.

Gennaro Fabricatore was the son and pupil of Gennaro Fabricatore (I). He primarily made guitars, and a few violins and violas in the Gagliano style are known. Gennaro Fabricatore / anno 1826 / Strada Toledo, Napoli.
Giovanni Battista Fabricatore made plucked instruments, with only a few violins known. He created them in Naples in 1811, in S. M. dell' Ajuto.

Venetian violin maker Luigi Fabris probably learned his craft from Antonio Indri or Pietro Valentino Novello , who were the only active makers in Venice in the early 1800s. Evidence of Fabris' notoriety dates from the 1830s, so it is reasonable to assume ... he started to work independently around 1830.

Fra Agostino Facini was a monk of the Order of Saint Jean de Dieu. He created Cremonese models featuring nicely carved scrolls and a fine yellow-brown varnish. It was suggested by Henley that he was a pupil of Tononi. An inscription cited by Vannes read `Facini di`.

Before moving to Turin in 1894, Annibale Fagnola was employed as a baker and seems to have had no direct relationships with any violin makers. The workshop of Marengo-Rinaldi was the only active violin making operation in Turin in the years immediately following Fagnola's arrival, and it is likely that he cultivated his first contacts there before opening his own business in 1899.

Annibalotto Fagnola was the nephew of Annibale Fagnola and an unwilling pupil of his uncle. After 1939, he made only a few instruments in his uncle's workshop, having inherited the business. He later became preoccupied with military service, followed by a career in medicine.

Harvey Fairbanks trained as a violinist in New York. He moved to Binghamton and began making violins with Arthur Howe around 1940. His violins were based on Stradivari and Guarneri models. He specialized in violas, primarily of the Tertis model. He used a light-colored oil varnish of his own formulation. Fairbanks completed over 40 violins, 20 violas, and one cello.
Joseph Falaise created models based on Amati and Stradivari; they were somewhat clumsy and featured high-set soundholes. The varnish was a thin golden-brown. Inscribed inside was: 'J. Falaise'.
Auguste Falisse apprenticed to Delinet in Mirecourt. He worked briefly for Silvestre and Maucotel in Paris, and then with Mougenot in Brussels.

Initially employed by the firm of Monzino and Sons , Erminio Farina became acquainted with colleague Riccardo Antoniazzi in the first years of the twentieth century and was quickly taken under his wing. The two collaborated closely from about 1910 until ...
Musical ambitions dashed by World War I. Apprenticed to József Metzker.
Celeste Farotti, born Celeste Farotto, became interested in violin making around 1895 at the age of 31. After some amateur experimentation, he was encouraged to move to Milan.

Trained in his uncle Celeste's workshop, many of Celestino Farotti's early instruments bear his uncle's label and are almost indistinguishable in style. After Celeste's death in 1928 Celestino began to cultivate a more individual approach, with wider purfling and softer edges.
William James Farrell was a self-taught maker. He was active from 1916 in Caledonian Road, London, and emigrated to Australia in 1920, living in Brisbane and then in Concord, near Sydney from 1923.
Maurice Fauconier was a violin and bow maker from a family of wind instrument makers. He studied under Georges Apparut in Mirecourt.
Thomas L. Fawick was an automotive engineer who became involved in violin making around 1948 in Cleveland.

Walter Richard Feiler was the son and pupil of Richard Feiler. He used models derived from Stradivari and Guarneri. His varnish was based on propolis and was of good quality. He branded his work on the lower rib with 'Walter Feiler Bad-Brambach. '.

Bernard Simon Fendt worked as a nephew and pupil of François Fent at his workshop in Paris from about 1783 until François' death in 1796. In 1798, he moved to London, possibly due to events stemming from the French Revolution, and immediately joined Thomas Dodd's shop alongside J.

Bernard Simon Fendt II undoubtedly learned the craft of violin making from his father Bernhard Simon I in the workshop of John Betts , where the two men worked until the mid-1820s. Following his departure from the Betts firm, Fendt entered into several interesting partnerships, one with a teacher of dancing and fencing.
Jacob Fendt was the third son of Bernhard S. Fendt (I).

Martin Fendt was the second son of Bernhard S. Fendt (I). He lived in Bell Alley, Coleman Street, and worked for John Betts throughout his life. Instruments bearing his name are extremely rare, but his skills were consistent with those of the rest of his family.
William Fendt was the son and assistant of Bernard S. Fendt (II). His own label was very rare.

Luigi Fenga studied and worked in Paris, London, Rome, and Naples. He established himself in Catania from 1890. His personal model was much admired and well finished with fine varnish and materials. He also made plucked instruments.
François Fent was a pupil of A. Gedler and moved to Paris around 1760, working in Montmartre near the rue du Mail or cul-de-sac St Pierre.

Alvina de Ferenczi came from a family of important Hungarian violin makers. She immigrated to America in 1904 and lived in Colorado. In 1910 she married and stopped violin making shortly thereafter to begin a pastry business with her husband in New Jersey. She moved to New Smyrna Beach in 1944.

Karl (Károly) (Charles) Ferenczy-Tomasowsky, nephew of Sandor Ferenczy, apprenticed with Zach in Vienna before moving to Berlin in 1888 and later to Rotterdam in 1895. In 1902, he emigrated to New York, where he died the same year.

William Ferguson established TRTF Mary's Wynd and was likely a pupil of Matthew Hardie, employing a similar 'long Strad' pattern with a back length of 36 cm (141/8"). His work showed a strong connection to that of Blair and his cousin George Ferguson, though it was not as effective.
Eric Tore Ferm won a gold medal at Stockholm in 1948 and received a diploma at The Hague in 1949 for a Stradivari model.
Giuseppe Ferrari was a trained musician inspired by E. Sofritti, a pupil of A.
Pietro Alfonso Ferraroni was active as a luthier and sculptor from 1929. He was an associate of Digiuni, Schiavi, and Tatar.
William Ferrier was active as a photographer in Dundee from 1880. He made 30 violins of modest quality, which featured yellow varnish.
Fernandino Ferroni was a pupil of De Zorzi. He was an influential maker of the Florentine school in the 20th century, established in Borgo San Frediano.
Charles Claude Fétique studied under Paul Bailly. He worked for the firms Thibouville-Lamy and Laberte in Mirecourt. His signature appears on many well-crafted instruments.
Emile Fétique worked for Derazey and later for Gautrot before establishing himself in rue de Turenne, Paris. He made well-crafted instruments.

Jules Fétique was born in Mirecourt three years after his brother of Victor , and followed in his footsteps by apprenticing with Émile Miquel and Charles Nicolas Bazin . In 1902 ... he moved to Paris to join the workshop of Eugène Sartory , also collaborating with his brother on occasion.
Marcel Gaston Fétique was a bow maker and the son, pupil, and assistant of Victor Francois. Active in Paris since 1913, he established his workshop at 93 rue de Maubeuge in 1930.

Born in Mirecourt, Victor François Fétique was apprenticed to Charles Claude Husson and trained further with Sigisbert Maline, Émile Miquel , and Nicolas Bazin . His early bows ... bear the hallmarks of this schooling, with narrow heads and heels mounted with miter joints.
Johann Ulrich Fichtl created a Tyrolean Amati-Stainer model. The workmanship was neat, well-proportioned, and utilized good materials. It featured a golden-orange or darker red-brown varnish.

Martin Mathias Fichtl was the son and pupil of Martin Mathias Fichtl (I) and ranked among the finest early Viennese makers. He utilized a disciplined Stainer model, characterized by a beautifully finished appearance and devoid of the exaggerated traits often found in Stainer imitations.

Carl Friedrich Ficker was the son, pupil, and successor of Johann Christian Ficker III. He was admitted to the violin maker's guild in 1806 and took over his father's business in 1822. Ficker produced inexpensive trade quality instruments based on the Stainer model, which featured a dull red varnish of little substance. His instruments were branded internally with 'C. F. F.'

Christian Samuel Ficker was admitted to the violin maker's guild in 1791. He produced professional work based on a Stainer-inspired model, characterized by a fairly clean and professional finish, though the detail work was done poorly. His instruments featured a thin clear yellow-brown varnish and were branded internally with 'C. S. F.'. A printed label inside read: *C*S*Ficker* / *18*.. *.

Active in the 20th century. In 1902 set up business in Markneukirchen with his cousin, Ernst Heinrich Roth. Produced a large volume of instruments of different models, many for William Lewis & Son in Chicago. Instruments branded internally G.A.F.

Johann Christian Ficker was the son and pupil of Johann Caspar Ficker in Markneukirchen and was admitted to the violin makers guild in 1755. Among the better makers of this family, he used the common Stainer model, which he executed fairly accurately.

Johann Christian Ficker was the son of Johann Christian Ficker II and a notable maker within the Ficker school. He crafted high-arched Stainer models characterized by narrow edges and well-defined purfling, with Staineresque scrolls that featured an exaggerated last turn of the eye due to an extended cut into the boss.

Johann Gottlob Ficker was the son and pupil of Johann Caspar Ficker, admitted to the violin makers guild in 1764. His work exhibited a superior quality of the typical Ficker style based on the Stainer model, characterized by a well-cut scroll featuring a concentric volute with an extended last turn behind the eye and a swan-neck pegbox.
Johann Otto Ficker was the son and pupil of Wilhelm Ficker. His career, which included fine pieces based on classical and Tyrolean models, was cut short by his death in World War I.

Georges Charles Fillion apprenticed at Mirecourt. He worked in Paris, Berne, and spent two years with Hill & Sons in London. In 1896, he established himself in Strasbourg. His instruments followed the Stradivari model and exhibited workmanship of the highest order, featuring fine orange-yellow varnish.
James Findlay worked as a weaver and was also known as a fiddle player. He was an active maker from around 1829 and was credited with 500 instruments.

Siegfried Finkel was a bow maker and a pupil of his father-in-law Paul Weidhass. He moved to Brienz, Switzerland, in 1952. He crafted fine bows in both French and German styles. His son Johannes (b. 1947) continued the business. The brand was SIEGFRIED FINKEL.
Brazilian maker based in San Paolo.
