Giuseppe Antonio Finolli created the Amatisé model. The work featured fine materials and a well-made scroll. It had a substantial orange-brown varnish. Vannes compared it to Camilli and suggested that some pieces might have been relabelled as such.

Son of Bolognese violin maker Raffaele Fiorini , Giuseppe Fiorini first trained with his father and opened his own independent business in 1885, when he was just 24 years old. In the late 1880s Fiorini traveled to Munich to set up a partnership with Andreas Rieger, which lasted until Rieger retired in 1896, leaving Fiorini to ... run the firm.
Fictional name for a line of good quality Mirecourt violins produced by Beare and Son in London during the early 20th century. 'Modele Paolo Fiorini, Taurini / B&S faciebat anno 1923'

Generally considered the father of the modern Emilia-Romagna school, Raffaele Fiorini developed an interest in violin making around 1847 and moved to Bologna to open his own shop in 1867. Fiorini's workshop became an important starting place for a number of distinguished Bolognese makers, including Augusto Pollastri , Oreste ... and Cesare Candi , Armando Monterumici , and Raffaele's own son Giuseppe Fiorini .

A. E. Fischer was the director of a music publishing firm that expanded into a significant violin making establishment, employing several craftsmen who produced commercial instruments and bows in various grades. The brand was labelled: A. E. Fischer / Bremen 19..
Albert William Fischer was an automotive engineer and amateur maker. He created 17 violins, one viola, and two cellos using personal models with amber-coloured varnish.
Herman Fischer was originally a bow maker active from 1895. He trained with R.
Johann Adam Fischer created decent work in the Tyrolean tradition. He applied a red-brown varnish. He was a Geigenmacher in Neukirchen in 1773.

Joseph Fischer was a pupil of Gedler in Füssen and worked briefly in Vienna before settling in Regensburg. His work included a large Stainer model that was well finished and accurate. He also created fine imitations of Stradivari with well-cut soundholes. Various varnishes were used, featuring glossy yellow-brown or red-brown. Printed and manuscript labels were present.

Lorenz J. Fischer trained by his grandfather and at the Austrian Violin-making School; he was also a pupil of Gagl and Reinhold Schmidt. He was active from 1906 and emigrated to America. In 1909, he established himself in Milwaukee, assisted by his son Lorenz. He created interesting work using the Stradivari model.

Philipp Jacob Fischer created a Stainer model of average achievement. The scroll was more Italianate, featuring a short last turn, but it appeared somewhat clumsy overall. Various varnishes were used, with the best being clear and of good texture, while others tended to be opaque and darkly colored.

Zacharias Fischer was the son and pupil of P. J. Fischer. He worked with a Tyrolean Klotz or Amati model and demonstrated fine workmanship. Some instruments featured an embellished angular purfling pattern. He used a red-brown crackled varnish and also created plucked instruments. Labels were in Gothic type.

Carl Fischer Inc. was directed by Louis Havermann as violin importers and dealers. They retailed the work of H. T. Heberlein and Mathias Heinike, among other European companies, as well as the work of their own employee, Curt Eschenbach.

Léon Fischesser worked in Geneva in 1885, Staufen in Baden in 1900, Mulhausen in 1904, and Paris in the Faubourg Poissonière in 1905, later moving to Avenue de Villiers in 1914. He collaborated with Lucien Greilsamer on varnish research around 1906.
Charles F. Fivaz established his work in Essex Road. He produced good professional work with a golden-red varnish and included a fine scroll featuring blackened chamfers.
Charles Flambeau created unremarkable workmanship. The spirit varnish appeared dull brown. It was branded under the back button with 'C Flambeau' and on the interior with 'Flambeau Faille a Paris. '.

Pierre Flambeau trained in Mirecourt and with Koliker in Paris. His work featured a recognizable imitation of the Stradivarian model and was sturdy, finished with yellow-brown varnish. It was branded below the back button. A manuscript label indicated: Flambeau / élève de M. Koliker / à Paris.
William Fleck was an amateur violinist and maker. He produced good work based on Stradivari and Guarneri models.
Harry Fleisher was a part-time bow maker. A silver diamond or violin Thole was inlaid in the frog.

Ignacio Fleta joined his elder brother Manuel in Barcelona. He made fine violins and cellos that featured rich golden-yellow varnish. He specialized in viols and ancient plucked instruments. Barcelona / Construido el año 19.. 115.
Albert Fleury established his workshop on rue Linn and worked in the style of Fetique. His late bows were rather exaggerated, featuring a deep throat to the frog.

Benoit Fleury created a flat-arched model using plain materials. The small scroll was located on the extended pegbox, and the soundholes were crudely cut and widely set. The varnish appeared dull yellow to orange. He made violins, violas, cellos, and double-basses.
H. Fleury made bows that featured strong sticks, modeled after Tourte, and typically included whalebone lapping. He branded his work with the name H. FLEURY.

Nicolas Florentin created a large pattern featuring flat arching and widely-set 'f' holes. The workmanship was ordinary. A thin yellow varnish covered the instrument. Some violins of the guitar-shaped Chanot model were also known.

Hans Flück studied with König at the Brienz School of Violin making in 1944. He was employed by A. Sprenger in St Gallen and served as workshop foreman at Hug & Co in Lucerne since 1952. His work featured clean, well-finished craftsmanship on Cremonese models.
Edouard Foetisch studied at Markneukirchen. He worked for M8ckel in Berlin, Hammig in Markneukirchen, and in Leipzig, Dresden, Frankfurt, Stuttgart, Vienna, and Mirecourt.
Claude Joseph Fonclause was a bow maker who was presumed to be a pupil of É. Pajeot and was active in Paris from around 1832.
Alfredo Fontana was a pupil of Carletti in Pieve di Cento. He made violins and viols.

Aristide Fontanini was the son and pupil of Andrea Fontanini. He served as the Director of the Violin-making School at Salò from 1946 to 1953. Preoccupied with teaching and research, he made few instruments himself. Instruments that bore his label were credited to Bianchini and Castellotti.

Jacob Ford worked from various addresses, including 'corner of Davies Street and Berkeley Square' and 'South-Street, Grosvenor Square', between 1770 and 1795. He was a 'Gentlemen's Servant' by profession before becoming a lodging-house keeper.

Youngest son of William Forster III , Simon Andrew Forster followed in the family tradition of violin making and studied with his father and brother William IV. His instruments fall into two categories: lesser examples bearing a spirit varnish signed "Forster," and higher quality ones signed, "S.A.

Though William Forster II was known as "Old Forster" to distinguish him from his son William III , he himself was the son of William Forster I , who is credited with the earliest surviving instruments by a member of the family. According to the thorough ... family biography composed by his grandson Simon Andrew Forster , William II made violins, along with spinning wheels and gun stocks, in addition to being a successful music publisher.

Third in a line of important London violin makers, William Forster III made his first instrument in 1779 and worked alongside his father from that time onward. Under his tenure the family business continued to engage in a motley assortment of activities, including an ill-fated foray into the greengrocer trade that precipitated Forster's death in 1824.
Giovanni Foschini studied under Primo Contavalli. He received a silver medal for quartet at Bagnacavallo in 1982. His work was based on the Stradivari model.
Fourier-Magnie made commercial instruments at the Laberte Factory in Mirecourt, France. The year was 19..
French bow maker based in Quimper. Studied with Gilles Duhaut. Winner of various awards including five VSA gold medals.

Arturo Fracassi was a violin and bow maker. He was a pupil of C. Biondi and was active in Cesena until 1959, when he relocated to Rimini. In his honor, his tools and bench remained in Cesena. He exhibited in Cremona in 1937. His personal model featured yellow or brown varnish.
Emile Marcel Français was a violin and bow maker, the son and pupil of Henri Français . He worked for Fenzel in Markneukirchen and for Lyon & Healy in Chicago from 1912 to 1914.

Lucien Français worked in Paris and London before joining Jacquot in Nancy, where he established his own shop in 1922. His instruments featured a Stradivari model with dark red oil varnish. He is noted for the inscription: L. Français, Luthier / ex-Premier ouvrier des Premières / maisons de Paris et de Londres / No.. Nancy. Anno 19..
Celso Franchi was active from 1922. He created classical models with orange or brown varnish. His workshop was located at via Corti alle Mura, 54 in Ravenna, Italy.

Meinradus Frank created fine work on a large personal model. The middle bouts were broad and straight. It featured a full arch, with soundholes that were set upright and very widely apart. A small, rather tightly coiled scroll was present. The varnish was golden- or orange-brown. Violas were quite commonly encountered.
Alfred Franke was the son, pupil, and assistant of Paul Franke. He established himself independently in Dusseldorf in 1948.

Max Franke studied under Otto Jaeger in Frankfurt from 1894 to 1898. He then worked for H. Kessler in Mannheim and for R. Heberlein in Nuremberg. From 1900, he served as a senior assistant to A. Wilfer in Leipzig before founding his own business there in 1921. His son Joachim succeeded him in 1956. He utilized Stradivari and Guarneri models and branded his work as `Franke Leipzig'.

Paul Franke was the elder brother of Max Franke. He died as a result of the workshop being destroyed by Allied bombing in 1945. It was a large Stradivari model made with fine golden-red varnish and materials.
Rodolph Franke was the son and assistant of Max Franke. His workshop in Weisbaden was destroyed while he was a prisoner-of-war. He re-established it in 1947. He was succeeded by his son Michael.

Patrix Franot created some instruments in the large, flat Mirecourt model, as well as other examples in the Maggini style featuring double purfling. They had yellow or brown varnish. Instead of a label, they were branded on the back interior with `Px Franot' or `Patrix Franot à Paris'.

Hans Franze trained with M. Schmidt in Marneukirchen. He worked for Ackermann & Lesser and H. Schlesinger in Dresden, Kessler in Würzburg, E. Helt in Cologne, E. Gartner in Stuttgart, and P. Meinel in Basel. From 1920, he served as the chief repairer and maker for Hug & Co. in Basel. His fine work was modeled on a 1714 Stradivari violin.

Jean-Baptiste Frebrunet was recognized by Henley, following Lütgendorff, as a fine craftsman of the `vieux Paris' school, creating instruments based on an Amatisé model featuring coarsely-cut scrolls and red-brown varnish. Some instruments bore a fleur de lys brand on the back.
Count Fabio Fredi was a titled aristocrat and an enthusiastic luthier. He produced high-quality work on a personal model.

Rodolfo Fredi was the son and pupil of Count Fabio Fredi and moved to Rome in 1889 to study at the Conservatoire, ultimately establishing a business at 24 Via Vicenza. He was an accomplished maker, credited with 450 violins, 70 violas, and 50 cellos, as well as double basses and viols, using models primarily derived from Stradivari. ...
Paul Richard Friedel studied under Nürnberger in Markneukirchen and later worked for Koch in Tressingen. His instruments were marked with a triangular monogram: A. T. F.
Bow maker / Violin maker (c. 1909 – 1926)

John Friedrich was a pupil of Schonger in Kassel and worked for Möckel in Berlin, Hammig in Leipzig, and other locations in Germany. In 1883, he emigrated to New York and established the company John Friedrich & Bro. with his brother William.
Maximillian (Miksa) Frirsz was a leading maker of his time and period. He studied under Paul Pilat from 1899 to 1902.
Maximillian (Miksa) Frirsz. Son and pupil of Miksa Frirsz (I).

Jean Fritsch apprenticed to Dieudonne in Mirecourt. He worked for Serdet in Paris and established his own premises there in 1934. His models included classical Cremonese and Gagliano styles, featuring red-orange to red-brown varnish.

Robert Fritsch was the brother of Jean Fritsch. He studied under Dieudonné and Enel. He worked in Paris at the sign of Charlemagne. In 1927, he received a Diplôme d'honneur. Coronae / Sub Signum Caroli Magni / Robert Fritsch. Luthier à Paris / No.. Année 19..
Johann Benjamin Fritzsche was the brother of Carl August Fritzsche. He was appointed Maker to the Dresden court.
Mario Antonio Frosali trained as a violinist at the Florence Conservatoire. He was active as a violin maker there starting in 1932, before emigrating to the USA.
Studied violin making with Anders Rasmussen in Aarhus, Jutland, until 1910, when he opened his first workshop. In 1917 he moved to Aalborg and worked until 1927, when he went bankrupt.
John Charles Fryer was an associate of M. W. Dearlove in Leeds from 1830 to 1845. He later became active in London. His independent work remained little known.

Born in Lausanne, Switzerland, Pierre-Yves Fuchs initially learnt cabinetry, then violin making and finally apprenticed in bow making with Gilles Duhaut, Pierre Guillaume and Benoit Rolland. He has won several international awards: Gold medal contrabass, 3 awards of merit violin, viola, violoncello USA (VSA) 2000 Gold medal viola, 3 awards of merit violin, violoncello, contrabass ...
Henry Fuller created a Stradivari and Guarneri model with good workmanship. Some were made on a small model, featuring golden-red to red-brown shades of varnish. A manuscript label was present.
Annibal Fulquet was the son, pupil, and successor of Sébastian Fulquet. He produced fine work based on classical models, utilizing excellent materials and a fine transparent varnish.
David Furber apprenticed traditionally in London to John Johnson. His signature appeared inside work that bore the brand of Longman & Co.

Henry John Furber was the son and pupil of John Furber and worked for John Betts, specializing in copies of the 1704 'Betts' Stradivari violin. Established in Grafton Street from 1825, he was located at 50 Britannia Row, Islington from 1846 to 1864, and at 79 Dean Street in 1885.

John Furber worked in Turnmill Street in 1810 and at 10 St John's Row from 1813. He moved to 26 Wellington Street in 1840. He was credited with most of the instruments sold and labeled by Betts during this period. He was a considerable and versatile craftsman, as well as a copyist of Stradivari and Amati. Some examples were branded with 'Furber' below the back button.
Matthew Furber was the son or younger brother of David Furber. He was possibly a pupil of Thomas Smith. One violin was signed and dated internally: 'Matthew and James Furber 1770'.
Georg Fürst worked with brown varnish that was undistinguished. He was located in Mittenwald an der Iser in 1790.

Giovanni Battista Gabrielli was a pre-eminent maker of the Florentine school. He may have received some instruction from Bartolomeo Cristofori , who had worked for Nicolò Amati before arriving in Florence in 1709, where he obtained a royal appointment.

Sole pupil and true follower of Stefano Scarampella , Gaetano Gadda began to learn the trade of violin making in 1919. The two worked side by side during Scarampella's later years, with Gadda playing a crucial role in the production of his instruments, and in 1924 he agreed to supply Scarampella with one violin per month in exchange ... for his teacher's tools and models.
Joint instruments by Gaetano and Mario Gadda tend to appear after 1950, until Gaetano's death in 1956. Mario continued to copy his father's work after this point.

Mario Gadda was the son, pupil, and successor of Gaetano Gadda, establishing himself in Sant'Antonio di Porto Mantovano. A prolific maker, he worked with many patterns and specialized in copies of Balestrieri and Scarampella. Anno.. / fece in Mantova.
Maker and cellist based in Bienne, Switzerland.
Joseph Gaffino apprenticed to Castagneri in Paris early in the 18th century. He established his own workshop in 1742 at the sign of 'la Musette de Colin' on rue des Prouvaires.
Pierre Gaggini was the nephew and pupil of Albert Louis Blanchi, with whom he served his apprenticeship. In 1924 he came to Paris for three years to fulfill his military service.

Alessandro Gagliano was not only the first in this great and prolific family of violin makers, but also the first known Neapolitan maker. Little is known about his training, and his highly individual work is quite unlike that of the rest of his family.
Antonio Gagliano was the third son of Nicola Gagliano (I) and worked alongside his brother Giuseppe. His instruments often featured decorative inlay.
Antonio Gagliano was the son of Giovanni Gagliano and worked in partnership with his brother Raffaele. Their instruments were noted for their good sound but lacked distinction in workmanship.

Only recently has credible biographical information concerning the violin makers of the Neapolitan school come to light. Until the experts who have researched this material publish their archival findings, we leave the birth and death dates of these makers as previously accepted.

Ferdinando Gagliano, son of Nicola Gagliano, likely trained primarily under his uncle Gennaro. His work exhibited elements from both, characterized by a more restrained craftsmanship, with varnish typically thinner and harder.

Only recently has credible biographical information concerning the violin makers of the Neapolitan school come to light. Until the experts who have researched this material publish their archival findings, we leave the birth and death dates of these makers as previously accepted.

Alessandro Gagliano's two sons Nicolò and Gennaro are said to be the finest makers of the family, but of these two Gennaro's work may be the more highly prized. His instruments are of a personal character, but unlike his father he often employed ... href="/cozio-archive/browse-the-archive/makers/maker/?Maker_ID=722">Stradivari and Amati models.

Giovanni Gagliano, son of Nicolò Gagliano , initially worked with his brothers Giuseppe and Antonio before developing a distinctive style of his own. He faced challenges due to the relatively inferior quality of varnish used during his time, which often had a slightly greenish tint from the dark stained ground beneath.

Giuseppe Gagliano, son and pupil of Nicola Gagliano, was an effective and consistent maker during his career, which spanned from 1770 to 1800. While he initially attained a level of craftsmanship that rivaled his father's, his later partnership with his brother Antonio led to a general decline in quality.

A somewhat lesser maker from the Gagliano family, Antonio sometimes collaborated with his brother Giuseppe . Their combined efforts, however, rarely produced instruments as visually appealing and skillfully crafted than those of Giuseppe alone.

Sons of Nicolo and worked in partnership. Only recently has credible biographical information concerning the violin makers of the Neapolitan school come to light. Until the experts who have researched this material publish their archival findings, we leave the birth and death dates of these makers as previously accepted.

Among the great makers of the Neapolitan tradition, Nicolò Gagliano, son of Alessandro , stands out for his consistently high standards of craftsmanship and sound quality. His instruments are among the most highly sought-after of the Gagliano family.
Sons of Giovanni and business partners. Their violins are mainly of commercial quality, some with broad and ugly f-holes, indifferently varnished, some with double purfling, others with none.

Vincenzo Gagliano, the son and pupil of Raffaele Gagliano, worked alongside his father in Strada Monte Oliveto. He joined the workshop of Raffaele and Antonio Gagliano before their deaths in 1857 and 1860, contributing to the final instruments produced by the brothers, particularly cellos.
Giovanni Battista Gaibisso was a pupil of Pasquale Baraldi in Alassio. He established himself in Alassio, Genoa, after Baraldi's death in 1896.
Cesare Gaida was the son and assistant of Giovanni Gaida. He produced fine work based on classical models, but his production under his own label was small. He made it in the year 1921. No.
Began work in France. In London from 1890, but returned to Ivrea 1895-1904 where his sons Silvio and Cesare were born.
Charles Gaillard was a violin and bow maker, the son of Louis Gaillard. He was the first apprentice of C.A.
Jules Gaillard-Lajoue was the younger brother of Charles Gaillard . He received a silver medal at the 1855 Paris Exhibition for his good work.
Alfredo G. Galea studied under Coschiera in Alexandria around 1920.
Adelino Galeazzi was a musician and sculptor who collaborated with his brother Eugenio Galeazzi. The varnish used was a clear golden light or pinkish-red brown.
Eugenio Galeazzi was the brother and assistant of Adelino Galeazzi. The work was not sophisticated and featured a dark brown varnish.
Luigi Galimberti worked as a pupil of Rottola and Antoniazzi. He was employed by the Monzino company from 1928 to 1930.
Anton Galla was active in Prerov in 1926 and in Kosice, Slovakia until 1938. He subsequently established himself in Brno.

Luc Gallicane was a proponent of a varnish recipe that claimed to date from 1716, known as the 'Secret Processes of Van Eyck. ' He applied the varnish to instruments produced in factories in Mirecourt. He received medals at Geneva in 1927.

Pietro Gallinotti won medals at Geneva, Rome in 1931, and Bologna in 1936. He worked with Stradivari and Guarneri (1743 'Cannone' pattern) models, demonstrating very precise craftsmanship. After 1950, he concentrated on making guitars. His work featured an internal brand: 'P. Gallinotti'. There was also a manuscript label on the upper block.
Tetto Gallo worked for George Einsele in Brunswick, Indiana, from 1925 to 1955 before establishing himself in Chicago Heights. He specialized in varnishing and antique finishes.
Claudio Gamberini was a pupil of Mozzani. He produced good quality commercial work.

Charles Adolphe Gand, son and pupil of Charles François Gand, became active around 1830 and took over the family business in 1845, running it alone until 1855 when he collaborated with his brother Eugéne under the name 'Gand Frères' until 1866. He held the title of Luthier de la Musique du Roi et du Conservatoire de Musique and was later recognized as Luthier du Conservatoire Imperial de Musique. ...

Charles Eugène Gand was the son of Charles François Gand and a pupil of both his father and his brother, Charles Adolphe. He trained as a violinist under Baillot at the Paris Conservatoire from 1840 to 1842.

Known as Gand père, Charles François Gand was born in Versailles and apprenticed with Nicolas Lupot in Paris from 1802 to 1806. During this time he worked alongside and taught August Sébastien Philippe Bernardel , whose sons would later join Gand's ... son Charles Nicolas Eugène to form the firm of Gand & Bernardel .

Francisco Gand was likely from the Gand family of Mirecourt. Paramon noted that his work was "well made, with poor wood and undistinguished varnish. " Jacquot referenced a viola featuring a transparent yellow varnish, labelled: Echo por Franco Gand en Madrid, año 1788. Vannes provided a manuscript label.

Guillaume Charles Louis Gand was the second son of Charles Michel Gand and learned the craft from his father before taking over his father's shop in Versailles. His work was rare and reflected the style of his father. He was also the brother of Charles François dit "Gand Père" and had further training at Nicolas Lupot's.

Two important houses of violin making and three makers formed the basis of the prolific and successful Parisian firm of Gand and Bernardel: Charles Nicolas Eugène Gand and the Bernardel brothers Gustave and Ernest . ... The firm was established as Gand & Bernardel Frères in 1866, after the death of Charles Eugène's brother Charles Adolphe Gand .
Brothers Charles Adolphe and Charles Nicolas Eugène Gand, who partnered as Gand Frères from 1855-1866.
Giuseppe Gandolfi worked for Mozzani and Gamberini. He produced good work with gold-red varnish.

William Ganshirt trained with Walter Goss, J. A. Young, and F. A. Mayer. He was employed by Oliver Ditson at the Musician's Supply Company. From 1926, he served as an assistant and pupil of O. H. Bryant in Boston. He made about 30 instruments based on Stradivari and Guarneri models, using a light red-brown varnish.

Pearson Gardiner worked in the Duke style, which was seldom seen and rarely labelled. His craftsmanship was quite precise. The form followed Amati, although the soundholes were more in the Stainer style and set close to the edge. He used broad purfling with good materials and applied a thin but decent golden-brown varnish. Internally, he branded and signed his work: 'P Gardiner. '.

A student of Romeo and Riccardo Antoniazzi , Ferdinando Garimberti worked for Leandro Bisiach and Giuseppe Pedrazzini ... before starting his own shop in Milan around 1920, where he performed repairs of antique instruments in addition to making new violins. His work, which shows great consistency over time, displays exceptional craftsmanship and elegance and fine attention to detail.

Eugen Gärtner established his own workshop in Stuttgart in 1891 after studying under Sprenger in Stuttgart and Simoutre in Paris. He was appointed as the maker and repairer for the King of Württemberg.

George Gaskin was an amateur maker. One instrument received endorsement from Czech violinist and composer Jan Kubelik. He used reclaimed timber and a commercial varnish in a golden-yellow shade. He won a gold medal at the Crystal Palace Exhibition and a prize at the People's Palace Exhibition in East London.

Not much is known about Giorgio Gatti's background apart from his arrival in Turin in 1890. Among the active Turinese makers of the period, including Francesco Guadagnini and Romano Marengo 'Rinaldi,' Enrico ...

Joseph Gaudé trained in the Adam style but may have worked for Pajeot around 1835. He established himself independently in 1839, though he appeared to have declined and vanished from the Mirecourt records after 1881. His bows featured round sticks and Pajeot-style faced underslides on frogs. The brand was GAUDÉ-J.
François Jude Gaulard was the son of Louis Gaulard. Early works indicated the influence of Pajeot.
Willis M. Gault founded the Gault School for the Professional Making and Appreciation of Bowed Instruments in 1950.

Pierre Gauthie studied under P. Lorange. He worked for Mangenot in Mirecourt and G. Cunault in Paris, eventually settling in Toulouse, where he took over from Simonin in 1912 at 9 rue Romiguières. His son assisted him and later succeeded him, specializing in pianos and wind instruments.

Georges Gavalda studied violin at Montpellier Conservatoire. He was taught by Huetter and later established himself in Villefranche. His violins featured classical models and were well-finished. They utilized good materials with a shaded varnish of fine texture.
Alcide Gavatelli studied under G. Antoniazzi. He emigrated to Argentina in 1918. His instruments were Stradivari models, crafted in the style of Antoniazzi and Degani.
François Gavinies established a workshop in Bordeaux on the rue des Minimes and later in Paris on rue St-Thomas-du-Louvre. He was appointed head of the Corporation of Luthiers in 1762.

William George Geary received guidance in violin making from W. B. Prince of Tooting and in bow making from L. T. Chappell. He utilized Stradivari and Guarneri models, employing good materials and workmanship. Commercial oil varnish supplied by Millington was used.
Johann Anton Gedler was the son of Norbert Gedler, who died young. He was brought up and trained by Sympertus Niggel.
Key founder of the 19th-century Viennese school. Geissenhof was born in Füssen (Allgäu) and moved to Vienna in 1772.

August Martin Gemünder, son of August Martin Ludwig, assisted his father from 1875 and took over the August Gemünder firm with his brother Rudolph upon their father's retirement in 1890. He was the creator of the "Gemünder Art Violins" and published 'The Violin World' journal starting in 1892.
August Martin Ludwig Gemunder was the son and pupil of Johann Georg Heinrich Gemünder. He worked in Regensburg and emigrated to America in 1846.
German–American violin maker George (originally Georg) Gemünder worked for J.B. Vuillaume in Paris before joining his brothers in Massachussets in 1847.
George Gemünder was the son and pupil of George Gemünder I, known as George Jr. He began working with his father in the 1870s and became a partner in the firm in 1882.
Hermann Ludwig Gemunder was the son and pupil of George Gemünder (I). He had a small output.

Oscar Henry Gemünder was the youngest son of August Gemünder and took over the family firm, August Gemünder & Sons, in 1928 after the death of his brother, August Martin. His work focused on the 'Gemünder Art Model' as well as Cremonese and Maggini models, which were mostly labeled with his father's name and countersigned by him.
Michel Genin apprenticed as a bow maker in Mirecourt, possibly under a member of the Adam family. He was the brother-in-law of P.

Giovanni Battista Genova rarely encountered as a maker; no evidence of his origins emerged from archives in Turin or Genoa. Speculative connections with Guadagnini and Guidantus lacked historical support.

Riccardo Genovese was a musician and pupil of Fagnola, active in Montiglio starting in 1922, before working in Lecco from 1927 and returning to Asti in the last year of his life. He began to pursue violin making seriously around 1922, and his early work appears less experienced compared to his output after 1930.
Carl H. George migrated to Toronto, Canada in 1886 with his family and moved to Chicago, USA in 1896.
Pierre Gerber was a pupil of Richard Meinel. He later worked for Millant-Deroux in Paris.
Émile Germain was the son of Joseph Louis Germain . He apprenticed in Mirecourt starting in 1865.

Joseph Louis Germain trained in Mirecourt before relocating to Paris in 1840. He worked for C. F. Gand and then for Vuillaume from 1845 to 1850. In 1851, he was appointed principal workman for Gand fils. He established himself independently in rue Saint-Denis in 1862. His work featured fine French craftsmanship based on classical models with shaded varnish.
Bows stamped Roger Gerome or Gerome-Devoivre. Employed Georges Barjonnet.
Giovanni Gerosa created models based on Stradivari and Guarneri. He used fine wood and spirit varnish. His work was exhibited in Cremona in 1937 and 1949.
Treffle R. Gervais worked for Elias Howe from 1885 to 1886, and subsequently for A.

Giuseppe Giacchetti studied the violin at the Rome Conservatoire. He was a pupil of Rodolfo Fredi in lutherie. He worked in Paris starting in 1920 and later in Montreuil. His instruments featured classical models with oil varnish.
Simeone Giamberini created a typical Florentine work featuring a golden-yellow varnish. The model was derived from Stainer. It had widely-spaced soundholes and a broad yet well-cut scroll.
Fabrizio Giannini created a personal model, some featuring extravagant carved ornamentation. The varnish was golden-yellow, utilizing both oil and spirit recipes.

Alfredo Gianotti learned the basic craft while a prisoner of war in the USA from 1943, with guidance from R. Bechini. He received awards in Pegli and Rome in 1956, Cremona in 1967, and Bagnacavallo in 1976. His work featured good quality with red or darker red varnish.

Arthur William Gibbons was a pupil of W. J. Piercy of W. E. Hill & Sons. He crafted violins based on various classical patterns and violas modeled after the 1696 Stradivari 'Archinto'. Both oil and spirit varnish were used in his work. He had a small output.
Antonio Gibertini was active in Parma from 1820 to 1833 and in Genoa from 1834 to 1845, returning to Parma in 1845. He was known as a skilled repairer and worked for Paganini on occasion.

Giulio Cesare Gigli, born around 1724, apprenticed to Michael Platner in 1738 and became his successor in 1753 after Platner's death in 1752. He inherited the workshop, materials, and tools, continuing to work until his death in 1794.
Jeffrey James Gilbert was the son of Jeffrey Gilbert and largely self-taught. He established himself in New Priestgate, Peterborough in 1875.

Simon Gilbert was presumed to be the son of Nicolas Gilbert. He served as a violinist in the Cathedral Orchestra at Metz from 1745 to 1768. Two quintons, dated 1744 and 1749, along with a pardessus de viole from around 1755, were cited by Vannes in the Kinsky catalogue. His violins followed an Amati model.

James Gilchrist trained as a scientific instrument maker and engineer. He lived in Glasgow from 1850 and became active as an amateur maker starting in 1880. He established his professional practice at 109 Hope Street, later moving to 29 Stanley Street. No more than 100 instruments are known. His finely made model, derived from Stradivari, featured a very flat arch, but several...

One of England's great cello makers, Samuel Gilkes first worked for William Forster III starting around 1810. He may have had a small role in training Forster's son Simon Andrew , but Simon minimizes this relationship in his account.
William Gilkes was the son and pupil of Samuel Gilkes. He established his business at 14 Dartmouth Street in 1835.
C. Lovett Gill was an amateur maker credited with approximately 30 instruments of good workmanship.

Louis Henri Gillet was born in Nancy but probably did his early training in Mirecourt. He worked for Jérôme Thibouville-Lamy from 1906 to 1911 and served in WWI before ending up at the Laberte firm in 1921.

Benedetto Gioffredo was initially employed as a shoe maker, and took up the violin trade seriously only after relocating to Turin in 1852 with his wife Paola. In 1855 his father-in-law Teobaldo Rinaldi joined them, presumably to help run the shop thereafter known as 'Gioffredo-Rinaldi,' or simply 'Rinaldi,' which Gioffredo adopted as a sort of surname over the course of his career.
Giovanni Antonio Giorgetti created coarse unfinished work based on a Stradivari-derived outline. He used a golden-orange opaque varnish. His son later assisted in a similar style.
Mario Girardi worked in Milan from 1920 and in Trieste from 1930. He exhibited in Cremona in 1937.
Andrea Gisalberti created very coarse work. The 'c' bouts were uneven and deeply rounded, with projecting corners. The edges were very broad, and the soundholes were extended with a paddle shape.

Armando Giulietti made guitars and also produced violins, violas, and cellos of good quality. He used red spirit varnish. Instruments made after 1950 were branded as 'Giulietti'. He worked in Milano in the year 1948.
Robert Gladstone applied his label to factory instruments imported from Mirecourt or Saxony.
August Hermann Glaesel was born in Markneukirchen, Germany. The model was large and flat; it was well-made but featured unsophisticated red varnish.

Carl Wilhelm Glaesel was admitted to the Markneukirchen guild in 1792. He was said to have worked for Thir in Vienna and was also active in Berlin. His work featured fine craftsmanship and a choice of materials, exhibiting a typical Tyrolean style with medium height Stainer type arching, although the varnish was rather ordinary brown.

Conrad Glaesel worked in Leipzig, Berlin, Brussels, and Chemnitz before returning to Brussels in 1937. He received gold medals at Brussels and Turin. His work featured Cremonese and Gagliano models with a fine red-brown varnish over a golden ground. He branded his instruments: 'Conrad-Glaesel Bruxelles anno 19.. '. A printed label read: Conrad Glaesel / (Brussler) / Chemnitz Anno 19..

Edmond Glaesel was the son and pupil of Ludwig Glaesel (I). He was later assisted by his son Theo. He utilized Cremonese and Neapolitan models and exhibited good workmanship. His work was branded: 'Edm Gläsel & Sohn'.
Friedrich August Glaesel was a violin and guitar maker. He was associated with the Hopf school and produced decent but limited work. His instruments were branded as 'F. A. G. '.

Ludwig Glaesel was a member of the firm `Glaesel and Herwig' starting in 1867 and established his own workshop around 1889. He received several medals at international exhibitions and was a commercial manufacturer of instruments in graded qualities.

Glaesel & Mössner operated a commercial manufactuary in Markneuldrchen from 1873, managed by Ernst Glaesel, the son of Johann Ludwig Glaesel. Various grades and styles were identifiable by inscribed shields carved into the back of the scroll. Some featured carved heads and were produced in both 'antiqued' and new varnish finishes. Trade bows bore the stamps of various illustrious French makers.

Christian Friedrich Glass was a violin and bow maker associated with the Hopf school. He created instruments with a rather square outline featuring outward pointing corners and purfling set very close to the edge.

Franz Johann Glass was the son and pupil of Johann Traugott, established in Strenwartensrasse, Leipzig from 1879. He was succeeded by his son, Johann, below. He produced good work on the Stradivari model using oil varnish.

Friedrich A. Glass was the son of Johann Traugott Glass. He was active in Altona, Hamburg from 1892. His fine work featured a personal model, with the best pieces receiving good oil varnish in various shades. Others were finished with a lower quality spirit.
Friedrich August Glass acknowledged good work on the Stainer model on his label. The varnish was a red-brown color of good consistency. It was made in Absam, near Oenipontum, in the year 18..

Friedrich August Glass produced fine professional work and was awarded a gold medal in London in 1851. His models were inspired by Stradivari and Stainer, and he used a yellow brown varnish. His work was branded with `F. A. G. '.
William Glenister established himself at Beak Street as a businessman and self-taught amateur maker. He favored classical models, particularly the 1690 Stradivari 'Tuscan' violin.
Hermann Glier established Bellevue according to Vannes. His place of work was Cincinnati, Ohio, as stated by Wenberg.
Johann Gottleib Glier was the most successful maker of the family. He created a Tyrolean model featuring a high arch and a golden-brown varnish.

Robert C. Glier trained in Markneukirchen. He emigrated to the USA in 1885, where he found employment with Wurlitzer. Later, he established himself independently in Cincinnati. His workmanship was undistinguished; some instruments were imported part-finished from Mirecourt, while others were made from local materials and spirit varnished.
Son of Robert I and successor to his Cincinnati shop.
John Gloag was an amateur maker known for well-made Stradivari copies. A manuscript label indicated: John Gloag / Galston / Jan.. 19..
Adolf Gloor created a well-finished work based on the Stradivari model, although the detail was uncertain. He was located in Zurich at Riedackerstr. 6 in 19..

Research suggests that Francesco Gobetti may have learned the craft of violin making at home. After arriving in Venice in 1699, Gobetti got work as a shoemaker and continued to work in that capacity, and there is no evidence that he was employed at any of the important violin shops in Venice.

Francesco Goffriller, son of Matteo Goffriller, left his father's workshop in Venice in 1714 to work in Udine. He seems to have predeceased Matteo and produced instruments characterized by a coarser finish and slightly less discipline than those of his father, yet they possessed great character, enhanced by the beautiful dark red varnish and golden ground typical of Venice.

Considered to be the father of the Venetian school, Matteo Goffriller was born in the Alpine town of Bressanone close to Bolzano, where he may have been a student of Matthias Albani . After moving to Venice in 1685 to apprentice with Mathias Kaiser , ... he married Kaiser's daughter and by 1690 had inherited his teacher and father-in-law's business.
Robert Gofton was an amateur maker credited with 30 violins of decent quality.
Friedrich Goldnagl graduated from the Violin Making School in 1965 after studying under Sgarabotto in Cremona. He worked in Vienna and was later appointed as a senior restorer in Florence.

Jacob Heinrich Goldt was the son of Samuel Goldt. He primarily made lutes and viols, with a few violins produced that were considered unremarkable, although they featured fine varnish. Jacobus Heinr. Goldt / fecit Fecit Hamburg.. 1767. Jacob. H. Goldt fecit /17..
Karel Goll created classical models. He utilized fine materials and spirit varnish. His workshop was located at Schwertgasse 6 in Brünn, and it operated in the year 19..
Aurelio Gonzalez was the nephew and pupil of Aurelio Aureli . He apprenticed to Bertucci and won a silver medal in Rome in 1930.

Solar Fernando Gonzalez was a maker of guitars, violins, and bows. He established his workshop at 3 Calle del Rollo and was appointed luthier to the Royal Palace, where he was responsible for its Stradivari quartet of decorated instruments and guitars. His family became one of the most celebrated guitar-making families in Spain.
Amateur violin maker who worked 1920-1938. Instruments are numbered on label.

Bert Goodwin was born in Canterbury, UK. He studied violin repairing under his grandfather, John Lucas Goodwin. His work featured Stradivari and Guarneri models, showcasing good workmanship and red-brown varnish. He was credited with 200 instruments.

Hugh Gordon was born in Stoneyford, County Antrim. He was active in Belfast from 1825. His work was undistinguished. The date was inscribed on the interior. He branded the back below the button with 'Gordon'.

James Gorrie created an amateur maker's large Guarneri-derived model, occasionally featuring factory-made heads. He used commercial varnish produced by Whitelaw in yellow or red shades. This work was completed in Glasgow in 1892.
Walter Solon Goss moved to Lynn to work for the Musicians' Supply Co. and Brown Music. He established himself independently there in 1896.
Jean Gosselin was born in Bordeaux. He studied under Koliker in Paris and worked in the Stradivari model.

Anselmo Gotti was the nephew of Orsolo Gotti and a violinist who studied under Ettore Soffritti in Ferrara starting in 1922. After establishing himself independently in Ferrara, he completed several instruments left unfinished by Soffritti after his death in 1928.

Orsolo Gotti studied under Carletti, Soffritti, and Pollastri in Bologna. He won first prize in Rome in 1918. His fine work featured personal models with a good dark red or golden-brown varnish. He also created close copies of various old masters, which were accurate enough to have been fraudulently re-labelled later.
Commercial production.

Johann Michael Gotz trained as a woodcarver. He was a prolific violin maker known for his medium high, flat-topped arches. His instruments featured a small volute with a large, Germanic tail to the eye. Generally, he used one-piece fronts and backs and applied a thin brown varnish. He operated as a Lauten- und Violinmacher in Neukirchen im Voigtlande.
John Alfred Gould trained in Liverpool with W. F.
Goulding imported French and Saxon instruments as a commercial business. He also sold work made for him by Thomas Kennedy, James Brown , and other London craftsmen of the period.
Guglielmo Govoni trained as a sculptor. He had a small output and created models based on Stradivari and Gagliano. Govoni was committed to researching varnish and developed his own recipe.

Michael Gozzo was a pupil of his grandfather, who shared the same name. He emigrated to the USA and associated with George Lindsey in Los Angeles starting in 1926. He established himself in Hartford, Connecticut in 1930 and ultimately moved to Glendale in 1942. He made over 100 instruments, using Stradivari, Guarneri, and personal models, and applied a red-brown varnish.

Antonio Gragnani probably studied in Florence, though his style differs considerably from that of late-18th century Florentine makers. He worked in Livorno, whose seaside location may have afforded him different materials from his contemporaries some 50 miles inland.
Younger son of Antonio Gragnani.

Onorato Gragnani was the son and pupil of Antonio Gragnani. His work was very similar to that of his father, though generally heavier and comparatively less refined, with the scroll being crudely finished. Some confusion arose previously as Onorato was the name of both the father and son of Antonio, but surviving instruments confirm that the son was the violinmaker

Giovanni and Francesco were important figures in the 17th-century Milanese school. Many of the early Grancino work has been assumed to be by Giovanni and Francesco, but there is little definitive evidence of Francesco's role. It is possible that Francesco was a pre-deceased brother of the famous Giovanni, but the connection between Giovanni and Francesco and Giovanni himself remains unclear
Born in 1637, Giovanni Grancino was one of the finest makers of the Milanese school. It is likely that his father Andrea, and perhaps even his grandfather Francesco, were also violin makers.

Son of leading 17th-century Milanese violin maker Giovanni Grancino, Giovanni Battista assisted his father in his successful workshop on the Contrada Larga, and was probably responsible for much of its output by the end of the century. Sadly a quarrel with rival violin maker Antonio Maria Lavazza resulted in the fatal stabbing of Lavazza, after which Giovanna Battista was sentenced to death in 1708. ...

According to the Hills in their book on the Guarneri family, he was a pupil of Nicola Amati; however, no evidence has been discovered to confirm this or even the existence of him. Instruments exist with his label, but determining authenticity is difficult.

Jean-Baptiste Grand-Gérard branded his work as from Paris but utilized the Mirecourt style. He created a large flat model finished with yellow varnish. His branding included `Grandgérard' or 'Grand Gerard a Paris'.

This Italianised name appeared on instruments crafted by J. Thibouville-Lamy in Mirecourt during the 20th century, although the labels indicated "Paris. " Instruments were produced in various grades and featured a red-brown varnish. Geronimo Grandini, Senr. / Paris. J. T. L., and Le Petit Fils de / Geronimo Grandini / Paris. J. T. L. were also associated with this name.
Jules Joseph Grandjon was the son, pupil, and successor of Prosper Gérard Grandjon. He initially worked in Paris and Amsterdam, where he took over the business of his brother L.

Prosper Gérard Grandjon was a prolific maker of various classical models, known for their good proportions and fine craftsmanship. He utilized quality materials and applied various shaded varnish colors. Although his work is rarely labeled, it is typically attributed to 'Paris.' He was also the father of L. Grandjon and Jules Joseph Grandjon. The work itself remains undated
Andre Granier was the son and successor of Edmond Granier. He trained with Brugere and Louis Bazin in Mirecourt.
Thomas Grater established a workshop in Birmingham on Ryland Street in 1874. He produced decent, unpretentious work based on various classical models.

William Green was associated with William Bent in Boston from 1798 to 1800 and with Deacon Allen in Medway from 1815 to 1818. He created American 'church basses' that featured a 'through-neck' design and did not have a separate top-block.

George William Greenwood was a pupil of Hesketh in Manchester. He established his own business in Rochdale in 1912. His work featured Stradivari and Guarneri models, showcasing good craftsmanship with golden- and red-brown varnish.

Manfred Gregoric studied in Mittenwald. He worked for Wurlitzer in New York, then later in Philadelphia and Los Angeles. He ultimately returned to Germany due to ill health. Primarily engaged in repair work, he was credited with a few violins.
Joseph Grienberger created instruments that were rarely seen. The model was a coarse Stradivari, featuring a well-carved scroll, fine soundholes, and dark golden-brown varnish.
Samuel Grierson created a model of Alexander Smillie. The head was commercially made and featured a soft amber varnish.
Guy Aubrey Griffin emigrated to Sydney, Australia, in 1924. He served as an assistant to A. E. Smith.
Karl Grimm was a musician and violin maker. His work was coarse, but the varnish was decent. He operated in Saatz in 1842.
Otto Groh was active in Göttingen from 1903. He produced fine copies of classical instruments.
Leonard O. Grover worked as a music teacher and violin repairer. He also made several violins and was employed by Thompson & Odell.

Husband and wife team, Joseph Grubaugh and Sigrun Seifert began making instruments together in 1982. They have received multiple awards and prizes for tone and workmanship, including five Gold Medals and four Silver Medals from the VSA. In 1998 they were awarded the VSA HorConcors status.
C. P. Gruhier produced good trade violins that were well finished, made with quality materials, and featured nicely coloured oil varnish.

Pietro Grulli, one of the last traditional late Cremonese violin makers, initially made pianos with Francesco Ghisi before turning to violin making during the wars of Italian independence in the 1860s when demand for pianos declined. His violins, derived from Amati, often feature poorly finished work, with awkward outlines and imprecise purfling.
Contemporary German bow maker taught by his father, Richard. Joint owner of the Grunke family workshop in Bubenreuth.

Bow maker born in Poland. Trained by Pfretzschner and later worked with Roderich Paesold. Set up independently in 1975 and then founded a family business with his sons Klaus and Thomas in 1996; based near Bubenreuth in Germany.
Contemporary bow maker.

Antonio Guadganini, son of Gaetano II , was one of the last makers of this Turin family and was responsible for expanding the commercial activity of the family workshop. The connection to workshops in Mirecourt and Paris, especially J.B.

Carlo Guadagnini was the son, pupil, and successor of Giovanni Battista Guadagnini. After his father's death, he worked alongside his brother Gaetano as Tratelli Guadagnini, making guitars and performing violin repairs and adjustments.
Little is known about the brief life of Felice Guadagnini, grandson of G.B. Guadagnini .
Second only to his great-grandfather G.B. Guadagnini in productivity, Francesco Guadagnini inherited the family workshop in 1881 at the age of 18.
G.B. Guadagnini's grandson Gaetano II inherited the family workshop in 1817, when he was 21 years old.

Gaetano Francesco Lorenzo Guadagnini was the son, pupil, and successor of Giovanni Battista Guadagnini. In 1771, he preceded his father from Parma to Turin, but the family joined him shortly thereafter.

G.B. Guadagnini is widely regarded as the greatest violin maker of the second half of the 18th century, a title he earned through 44 years of labor and experimentation, perseverance in a multitude of regional markets, and an original style that produced some of the best instruments in history.

Giuseppe Guadagnini was the second son of Giovanni Battista Guadagnini and was known as '11 Soldate'. He was a pupil and assistant to his father, working alongside his brothers Gaetano, Carlo, and Fillippo.

Although tales dating from at least the early 19th century allege that Lorenzo Guadagnini, father of the renowned Giovanni Battista , studied with Antonio Stradivari , recent research does not support this theory of supposed lineage between G.B. Guadagnini ... and the great Cremonese tradition.
Paolo Guadagnini was the son and pupil of Francesco Guadagnini and the last in a 250-year lineage of violin makers. He first studied with his father and later collaborated with Annibale Fagnola .

Several great violin makers apprenticed directly with Nicolò Amati , and among those Andrea Guarneri holds the highest reputation as the proponent of the Amati tradition. He trained with Amati in two distinct periods from 1641 to 1654, with a four-year gap starting in 1646, and adopted his model so masterfully that some of Guarneri's ... works are indistinguishable from his teacher's.

Giuseppe Guarneri's two sons Pietro of Venice and Giuseppe 'del Gesù' both trained with and assisted him in the 1710s, but in keeping with the individuality of the Guarneri tradition their conceptions of violin making developed along strikingly dissimilar ... lines. While Pietro earns respect for his deft combination of Cremonese and Venetian styles, the rich and complex tonal quality of 'del Gesù''s instruments distinguish him as one of the greatest violin makers in history.

Giuseppe Guarneri I, known as 'filius Andreae' to distinguish him from his son Giuseppe "del Gesù" , inherited his father Andrea's shop in 1698. He had already been working for his father for quite some time, and in fact many of Andrea's later instruments ... were primarily made by Giuseppe.

Older brother of Giuseppe Guarneri filius Andrea , Pietro Guarneri began to work in his father Andrea's shop from an early age, around 1670. Equally talented as a violinist and violin maker, Pietro relocated to Mantua in the 1680s to further his playing ... opportunities at the court.

Pietro Guarneri, the elder son of Giuseppe 'filius Andreae', should rightly have been first in line to inherit his father's shop in Cremona, but after 10 years of apprenticeship in the family business he left for Venice in 1717. Violin making in Cremona was at that time dominated almost exclusively by the Stradivari family, which made doing business more difficult for the Guarneris, and Pietro found ... job security in Venice, where he was employed in the Sellas workshop alongside Carlo Tononi .

Alfredo Guastalla was a pupil of Scarampella in Mantua and worked alongside his brother Dante Guastalla . The brothers were prolific makers, credited with over 250 violins, violas, cellos, and double basses.
Dante Guastalla was the elder brother of Alfredo Guastalla. He studied under Scarampella in Mantua and worked independently in Reggiolo for a short time before Alfredo joined him.

Alexandre Sauveur Guérin was a pupil and successor of E. Daniel on rue Saint-Suffren. He transferred the business to 18 rue du Paradis in 1866. His output was small and he mainly focused on repair and restoration.
Alberto Guerra was a pupil of Pollastri. He created several models that were well-made and featured a golden-red oil varnish.

Initially a violinist, Evasio Emilio Guerra showed an early talent for violin making and is thought to have received his training from Carlo Oddone and Romano Marengo Rinaldi , by extension. His modest financial circumstances required him to work quickly, ... and his lack of business acumen restricted him to building instruments almost entirely for other makers, including Fagnola , Oddone, Marengo Rinaldi and Francesco Guadagnini , supplying violins in the white.

Louis Guersan was a pupil and successor of Claude Pierray, becoming a member of the corporation of luthiers in 1748 and making head in 1769. His workshop was located in the rue Fossé-St-Germain des Prés, where he was appointed as maker and repairer to the Dauphin and to the Opéra.
Contemporary maker born in Spilamberto and currently based in San Damaso, near Modena. Initially self-taught, then a pupil of Ansaldo Poggi in Bologna from 1969 to 1980.

Though the familial relationship between them is unclear, historical documentation shows that Giovanni Guidanti inherited Floriano Guidanti's position as repairer of the Bologna Accademia Filarmonica's instruments shortly after his death in 1715. There is some evidence that Giovanni was well regarded as a maker, yet there is ... little extant information about his working life.

One of the best Spanish violin makers, especially in workmanship and style, was Joan Guillamí of Barcelona. He worked from 1721 to 1767 in his own shop, building instruments whose construction is heavily inspired by Stradivari .

Juan Guillami 'filius' was the son of Juan Guillami I and took over the workshop after his father's death. His work was influenced by his father, though it was somewhat less disciplined and utilized inferior materials. The arching of his instruments was low and slightly boxy, finished with dark spirit varnish

Known as 'Le Messin', he was said to have trained with Pressenda in Turin (Vannes), although there appears to be no actual connection. He worked for several Italian workshops and established himself in Bordeaux in 1832. His work featured fine craftsmanship on classical models and was signed on the interior.

Charles Joseph Théodore Guinot established himself independently around 1836. His early work reflected the style of Fonclause, while he later incorporated influences from Pajeot, featuring a metal facing at the tip of the frog. He primarily used round sticks that were dark red-brown. His brand was C. GUINOT.
Richard Guiton specialized in Stradivari and Guarneri models. His workmanship was effective.

August Guth studied under Bausch in Leipzig and Grimm in Berlin. He worked briefly in Breslau before establishing himself in Antwerp in 1888. His instruments featured models by Stradivari, Maggini, and others, using spirit varnish. He branded his work with 'A. G. ' and included a printed label stating: August Guth me fecit / Antverpiae anno 1908.
Carl Friedrich Gütter was the son of Georg Adam. He produced decent work based on the typical German models.
Fritz Gütter was a bow maker active from 1919. He produced fine quality work and was a pupil of Pfretzschner, having worked for Nürnberger. His bows were branded: 'F. GÜTTER'.

Johann Georg Gütter was the son of Georg Adam Gütter II. He worked briefly in Erfurt around 1799 and was recognized as one of the better makers in his family, known for professional work that utilized good materials and featured yellow or dark golden-brown varnish. His craftsmanship also extended to Neukirchen and Markneukirchen.
Johann Gottlob Gütter produced a Stainer model. The work was decent, featuring slightly dull brown varnish.

Julius Gütter worked in Leipzig for Franz Glass and in Aachen for Holm Viertel before he emigrated to Philadelphia in 1893. There, he collaborated with Arnold Voigt and later established himself independently from 1894 to 1950.

Kurt Arno Gütter studied under O. B. Heinel. He worked for Glaesel in Berlin and H. T. Heberlein in Markneukirchen. He established himself there in 1921, later receiving assistance from his son Albert Conrad. His professional work included usual models as well as some personal forms.

Oskar Richard Gütter apprenticed to A. Zeitler and worked for Heckel in Dresden, Gärtner in Stuttgart, R. Nürnberger, and R. Penzel. He established himself independently in Markneukirchen. His work included good craftsmanship on both Italian and Tyrolean models.
George H. Hadley was active in Fairfield from 1887. He made approximately 35 violins, which featured a soft orange-red varnish.
Frederick Ewald Haenel trained in Saxony, Germany, and began working in 1918. He emigrated to Winnipeg, Canada, around 1938, later moving to Toronto, then Buffalo, New York.

Joseph Anton Haff moved from Augsburg to Nuremberg around 1840. He produced good work on a flat-arched model using fine materials and a brown varnish made from a combination of spirit and oil. A guitar dated 1841 is cited by Vannes.

Joseph Anton Haff established himself in Munich in 1894, later moving to Senftenau, Bodensee, and finally Stuttgart, where he worked for Fridolin Hamma. He focused on classical models, particularly those of Amati, producing accurate and finely-finished work.
Jacques Wolfgang Hakkert was the son of Philipp Hakkert and trained in Mirecourt with Didier. He worked in Düsseldorf for L Otto and in Cologne for J.
Roscoe G. Hall utilized imported pre-carved necks and local materials. His models were derived from Stradivari and Guarneri. He was a prolific maker and applied fine varnish.
R. M. Halliday used local materials to create a Stainer-derived model that featured a golden brown varnish.
Anders Enfried Halvarson was a violin and bow maker. He emigrated to the USA in 1924 to work for William Lewis & Son in Chicago.
Alban Hamm created good commercial instruments based on Cremonese and Neapolitan models.
Christian Gottfried Hamm was the son and pupil of Johann Gottfried Hamm. He crafted violins in Stadr Neukirchen, near Adorf in Voigtland. He completed his work in the year 1..
Johann Gottfried Hamm became a Master of the Violin Makers' Guild in 1764. He was regarded as the best maker of the family.
Walter Hamma was the son and successor of Fridolin Hamma (I). He trained at Mittenwald under Aschauer from 1933 to 1935 and worked for Ferdinand Jaura in Munich and Caressa & Français in Paris.
Thomas Hammett used fine materials and varnish in his Stradivari model. The initials were engraved on the interior of the back.

Johann Christian Hammig was the son and pupil of Johann Georg Hammig and was admitted to the Violin Makers' Guild in 1751. His work featured well-executed Tyrolean patterns and an extravagant soundhole design derived from Stainer, with the upper and lower wings extending across the table, bringing the lower finial very close to the corner.

Wilhelm Hermann Hammig was a violin and bow maker, the son and pupil of Wilhelm August Hammig. From 1850, he worked for Carl Grimm in Berlin, later moving to Amsterdam before returning to Markneukirchen in 1864.
John Hammond was a self-taught amateur maker and a farmer in West Burton, Wensleydale. He was credited with creating 20 violins, two violas, and one cello.
Henry Handley was an amateur violinist and an acquaintance of Sir Edward Elgar. He was a self-taught maker, credited with crafting 106 violins, six violas, and two cellos.
J. Rummelhof Hansen worked for Möckel in Berlin. He established himself in Christiania (Oslo), Norway, in 1909. A medal was awarded to him at Copenhagen in 1914.
Grandson and pupil of Peter Hardie. Continued working as a schoolteacher until 1863, when he established his shop at 14 Marshall Street, Edinburgh.

John Hardie was not known to be related to any other members of the Hardies. His work was unrefined, sometimes featuring painted purfling, hollowed cheeks on the pegbox, and a thin orange-brown varnish.

The circumstances of Matthew Hardie's training are unknown. While some experts have suggested parallels between his work and that of Vincenzo Panormo and John Betts , it is entirely possible that Hardie learned his craft locally in Edinburgh.

Thomas Hardie worked independently from 1826 at 15 Shakespeare Square, Edinburgh, and from 1837 at 79 High Street, with various other addresses following. He faced challenges in maintaining a steady existence, much like his father Matthew , and ultimately died after falling down some stairs near his last residence at 5 Advocate's ...
Ronald Harding began making bows for the firm of W.E. Hill & Sons in 1949. He left the workshop and gave up bow making in 1959. Harding's bows are marked with the numeral 9.
John Edward Hardwick was a pupil of George Wulme-Hudson. He created a Stradivari model using oil varnish. His work was associated with G. Wulme Hudson in Ashtead, Surrey, in the year 19.. No..

John Hare apprenticed to Rebecca Miller, the widow of George Miller, in the Draper's Company. He established himself independently in 1694 at the Fleece Tavern, located near the Royal Exchange, Cornhill, and moved to Freeman's Yard the following year.

Born in Leeds, England in 1948, Roger Hargrave initially studied fine arts and taught painting, he then transitioned to instrument making in the 1970s, training at the Newark school before joining the prestigious workshop of W. E.
Harmand was part of a family of luthiers in Mirecourt, France. Their work generally involved trade, and they were individually indistinguishable.
Louis Harmand created decent work with golden orange varnish. It was branded internally as 'L. Harmand à Paris'.

Nicolas Harmand was a master in the Violin Makers' Guild in 1772 and is recognized as the finest maker of the Harmand family, active in the second half of the 18th century. He was known for his small model violins, the use of fine materials, and a distinctive good red-yellow varnish. His work was branded 'Au Roi David'.
Nicolas Joseph Harmand apprenticed in Mirecourt as an organ builder and bow maker, being the son of Louis. His work was fine but rare, and he seemed to have ceased production altogether around 1845.
Albert Harper created a Stradivari model using local materials and his own hard golden-brown varnish.

Arthur Harrass was the son and pupil of Adolf Harras. He worked for Held in Cologne and Meiter in Brunswick. From 1925, he established himself independently in Chemnitz. He retired to Hermesdorf around 1945.

Charles Harris was born the son of a violin maker, with whom he trained. Following a fortuitous inheritance, he ascended to the aristocracy at the age of 37 and left violin making behind for more glamorous pursuits until he squandered his fortune in the mid 1830s.
Gordon Harris completed 31 violins, 33 violas, four cellos, and numerous historical instruments. He was self-taught. The location was London, and the year was 19..

John Edward Harris established himself independently in Shildon, Bishop Auckland from 1894, after being a pupil of George Chanot in London, and moved to Gateshead in 1908. He was assisted by his two sons, Ernest and John Edward Jr. His violins were finely crafted using Stradivari and Guarneri models and good materials. He retailed his own amber varnish.
Active since 1937, he was a pupil of Edward Ikenburger. He established himself in Berkley in 1966, with assistance from his wife, Marion Fox Harrison .

Marion Fox Harrison worked with her husband Benjamin Harrison from 1966. The first violin was made in 1972, and about 20 were completed. She used a Stradivari model with pre-cut necks and terpene resin varnish. A manuscript label was included.
Homer A. Harvey was an amateur maker active in Batavia from around 1916. He created approximately 60 violins based on a Stradivari-derived model, which featured orange-red oil varnish.
Stephan (István) Havas was a fine maker of violins and bows. He studied under Brückner.
The earliest work was dated 1755. Meinrad Franck succeeded him in business and continued to use his label, with manuscript amendments, for some time.
Simon Jan Havelka was presumed to be the brother of Johann Baptist Havelka. His work featured a similar finely-executed style with a thin textured dark red-brown varnish. Such work was rarely seen.

Hawkes and Son were a highly successful British company active from 1865. The firm published sheet music and sold instruments, some of which were made in their workshop in Edgware, North London. Like most big retailers, they imported instruments from a wide range of European workshops and sold them at different grades: these included extremely fine instruments and bows. Hawkes and Sons merged with Boosey & Company to become Boosey & Hawkes in 1930.
George Haxton was a self-taught maker active from 1907. He established himself in Glasgow in 1910.
Jacob Haynes created typical London trade work on a Stainer model, featuring an exaggeratedly full arch and a greenish-brown varnish.
Second son and pupil of Heinrich Th. Heberlein Jr.
Son of Johann Gottlob and his pupil from 1820–23. He taught his own son, the famous Heinrich Thomas Heberlein Jr.
He is in fact one and the same person as the one called Jr.

Heinrich Theodore Jr is the most famous member of the Heberlein family and one of the best Markneukirchen makers of his era. He studied with his father, Carl August Heberlein, then moved to Leipzig to work for Ludwig Bausch and finally became the pupil of August Riechers in Hanover.
First known maker of the Heberlein family, Johann Gottlob was a musician as well as violin maker. His second son Carl August studied with him.

August Heck created personal and Stradivari models of violins, characterized by a low hollow arch and fine workmanship. He worked in Chicago around 1887 and applied a good orange-red oil varnish to his instruments. Notably, he severely canted his violins at both the top and lower blocks.
Karl Willy Heckel apprenticed to Heinrich Todt from 1912 to 1915. He worked as Todt's assistant until 1923, after which he moved to Kraslice (Graslitz) to work for A.

Rudolf Heckel studied under Ernst Gläsel. He worked for Reichers in Berlin before establishing himself independently in Dresden in 1884. He created fine and accurate copies of Stradivari and Guarneri models, using his own rich oil varnish that had a convincing appearance.
Edward Heesom worked on a typical London trade piece based on an exaggerated Stainer model. It was branded below the button.

Luther D. Heiges completed 200 instruments using his own orange-colored oil varnish. He made models based on Stradivari and Guarneri, as well as some copies of Amati and Maggini. The artist-grade violins were produced in Baltimore, Md, with the designation No 91 in 1927.
B. A. Helmer studied under Axel Sington. He utilized Stradivari and Gagliano models and applied yellow- and red-brown varnish. The work was completed in Stockholm in the year 19..

Marton Hein studied under Schunda in Budapest. He worked in Vienna alongside Haudek and Gutermann. Several medals were received by him at various exhibitions. His professional workmanship was noted. Manuscript label: Javitotta Hein Márton / Mühegedü Készitö / Székesfehérvár. Printed label: Javitotta Hein Márton / hangszer Készitö Székesfehérvárott 19..

Karl Paul Heinel was a pupil of A. L. Paulus and was employed by J. Bruckner and A. Sandner. He moved to Copenhagen in 1927 to work for P. Merling. From 1951, he established himself independently in Copenhagen. He was a highly regarded restorer.
Oskar Bernhard Heinel worked as a pupil of Hans Jaeger . He was employed by C.

Oskar Erich Heinel was the son and pupil of Oskar Bernhard Heinel. He created fine copies of classical Italian works and also made historical instruments, viols, viola d'amores, and more. Erich O. Heinel / Markneukirchen / Faciebat. Anno 1929 Oskar Erich Heinel / Markneukirchen / Expertise No..
Mathias Heinicke studied in Markneukirchen and Italy. He was active in Budapest and Berlin.

Nicolas Heinz studied under Frederick Konig from 1906 to 1911. He worked in Philadelphia for Julius Guetter and in various workshops in Chicago, including Lyon & Healy. He established himself independently in New York starting in 1926. He created 200 instruments using a personal model with brown oil varnish. He was also a repairer, dealer, and collector.

Pierre Jean Henri Hel was a violin and bow maker, the son, pupil, and successor of the French maker Joseph Hel of Lille. His work gained international recognition when Georges Enesco played one of his violins during a US tour in 1924. Additionally, his violins were performed by other celebrated violinists, including Eugène Ysaÿe and Jacques Thibaud.
Pierre Joseph Hel apprenticed to Salzard at Mirecourt for seven years before working for Sebastien Vuillaume and N. Darche at Aix-la-Chapelle in 1864.
Johann Joseph Held was a flautist and violinist in Euskirchen in 1861. He was a friend of Ole Bull and an amateur violin maker.
Carl Joseph Hellmer was the son of Johann Georg Hellmer . He was a pupil and assistant of Ulrich Eberle.
Johann Georg Hellmer was a pupil of Thomas Edlinger. He exhibited fine workmanship on a model derived from Amati and Klotz, featuring a diminutive scroll.
Petter Hellstedt was likely a pupil of Olof Arling and succeeded to his business. The earliest label was dated 1736.
Julius Hempel worked for Möckel in Berlin in 1897. He established himself in Hamburg from 1902, producing a small output.

Arno Erwin Hendel was active in Budapest, Breslau, and Vienna. He established himself in Markneukirchen in 1921. He created fine copies of Cremonese models and specialized in imitations of Lupot and Vuillaume.
John C. Hendershot established his work in Cleveland around 1893. He created a broad Guarneri model with a well-carved scroll.
Frank Vernon Henderson was a violin and bow maker since his professional retirement in 1978. He studied under George Orth and authored How to Make a Violin Bow (Seattle, 1977).

Gustav Henning established his work initially in Miami, Florida, then in Denver, Colorado, from 1920, and finally in Seattle from 1928. He returned to Sweden upon retirement. Over 600 instruments were made based on Stradivari and Guarneri models, featuring fine amber or orange-brown varnish. G. Henning 1926 / 358 2424 Gaylord St., Denver, Colo.

T. Henri created bows characterized by typical French style and craftsmanship. The sticks were made from Pernambuco, available in both octagonal and round shapes, and were well balanced. His work was branded as T. HENRI.
Charles Henry was the son, pupil, and successor of Jean Baptiste Henry. He was a prolific maker of violins, violas, and cellos in various patterns.
Dominique (Henry JV) Henry was the brother of bow makers Jean Antoine and Etienne. He apprenticed in Mirecourt and established himself independently there around 1810, ceasing production around 1851.

Eugène Henry was the son, pupil, and successor of Charles Henry. He produced fine copies of Lupot featuring rich orange-red varnish. His work included good commercial Stradivari models. He received a bronze medal at the World Fair in 1889.

Like many of his contemporaries who passed through the major workshops of Paris, Joseph Henry was born and received his early training in Mirecourt. He moved to Paris in 1837, at the age of 14, to work for Georges Chanot , where he produced workshop bows with the generic Chanot stamp.
Johann Baptist Herbig made an Amatise model that featured fine workmanship and was finished with golden-brown varnish.
Charles Edward Herbrig was a violin and bow maker. He was active in Montgomery from 1908 and established his work in St.

Frantisek Herclik was a wheelwright and violinist. He later trained as a violin maker, using Cremonese models in the Bohemian style. His early work featured inferior spirit varnish, while he later employed a golden-brown oil varnish.
Josef Bohumil Herclik was the son and pupil of Frantisek Herclík. He trained in Luby (Schönbach) and used Stradivari and Guarneri models.

Ladislas Herclik was the son and pupil of Frantisek Herclík. He also worked for Züst in Zurich and established himself in Kolin, Bohemia. His works included copies of classical models in the Bohemian style, featuring brown varnish on a yellow ground.
Edwin Lothar Hermann was a bow maker known for fine bows based on personal models. His brand was L HERMANN (with three stars).

John G. Herrick was active in New York, moved to Boston in 1900, and finally established himself in Terre Haute, Indiana, in 1914. He used models from Stradivari and Guarneri, as well as his personal design. His fine work featured a good orange-red varnish of his own formulation. Paul M. Herrin / Terre Haute, Ind. U. S. A. / 1892-1928. No.
August Friedrich Herrmann was a bow maker in the Knopf style, active from 1900.
Emil Herrmann was a world-renowned authority on fine instruments, dealer, and connoisseur. He employed many distinguished craftsmen in his New York shop, including Simone Sacconi and Dario D'Attili.

Karl Herrmann was a pupil of Max Dölling. He also worked in Erlbach and Wernitzgriin. He used the pseudonym `Andreas Morelli' for branding. A printed label indicated: Angefertigt im Jahre 19.. / von / Karl Herrmann / Erlbach, Germany. / Reproduction of.. / anno.
Lothar Edwin Herrmann was a bow maker. He was the brother of August Herrmann. Bows were branded: A. HERRMANN.
Walter Herrmann was a bow maker and the son of Karl Hermann. Bows were branded with the name W. HERRMANN.
Erwin Hertel was a student at Mittenwald starting in 1928 and moved to the USA in 1933 to work in Philadelphia. Later, he was employed by Emil Herrmann in New York alongside Simone Sacconi.
Franz Herzlieb created fine accurate copies of Stradivari and Guarneri using appropriate materials and shaded varnish. He received a gold medal at the London Exhibition in 1851.
Thomas Earle Hesketh studied under G. A.

Harrison Harvey Heskett was born in Londonderry, OH. He was active in Columbus, Ohio, and began working in Minneapolis in 1887. His workmanship was very decent, and he used a Guarneri-derived model with golden-orange varnish.
Dutch maker, based in Cremona since 1975. Studied with Scrollavezza and Sgarabotto.

Georges Heynberg was the son and assistant of Émile Heynberg. He used models from Stradivari, Guarneri, and Gagliano. His work featured fine oil varnish and well-selected wood. Dolcitudo/Fortitudo/Claritas / Luthier à Liège / Vernis Crémonais / 1926.

George Herbert Hicks worked for G. A. Chanot in Manchester in 1901. He established himself independently in Market Street, Oxford, from 1910. Instruments made before that were labelled by Chanot. He created 60 violins based on Stradivari models, showcasing fine and accurate work with varnish in several shades.
László Hidy graduated from the Hungarian State violin making school under G. Varady. He was an amateur maker.
Successor of Apparut. Whose workshop he took over
Albert Edgar Phillips Hill was the stepson of Arthur Frederick Hill. He apprenticed in Mirecourt and worked for Fiorini in Munich for two or three years around 1904.

One of the finest craftsmen of the Hill family, Henry Lockey (also known as Lockey) was the son of the first Lockey Hill . It is possible that he trained under his uncle Joseph Hill II, and it is certain that he was working for John Betts by 1806.

Born in 1715, Joseph Hill was the first of the great Hill family of makers, restorers and experts.His earliest known label dates from 1753 and states his address as "High Holborn" in London, but it is possible that he arrived in the city from Alvechurch as early as 1746. By 1761 his workshop had relocated to Pall Mall, and in 1765 he made one final move to Haymarket, where he remained until 1780, when ... his oldest son Joseph II took over the shop.

Joseph Hill was the eldest son and successor of Joseph Hill (I). He took over the Haymarket shop in 1780, which was destroyed by fire in 1789. As a result, he worked for John Betts around 1790-1791, before managing to establish a new business at Lock's Fields, Newington. His work remained comparatively rare.
Joseph Hill's fifth and most distinguished violin-making son Lockey Hill was born in 1756. He produced a great many trade instruments, often for the firms of Longman & Co. or Longman & Broderip.

William Ebsworth Hill began working in his father Henry Lockey's>/link_maker> (or, Lockey) shop around 1831 and was later apprenticed to Charles Harris after his father's death in 1835. The earliest origins of W.E.
