The London-based firm W.E. Hill & Sons dominated the fine instrument and bow market in the late 19th – early 20th centuries.

Maximilian Vitalis Himmer studied the violin, but an accident hindered a career as a concert player. He began making instruments in Berlin around 1900 and established himself in Wilmersdorf. He developed a personal model around 1905, showcasing fine workmanship.
John Wesley Hinchcliffe was a pupil of J. B.
Son of Johannes Hjorth and trained in the family firm. From 1863–65 he studied with Gabriel Lemböck in Vienna and Bernardel Père in Paris and his instruments show stylistic elements from both cities.
Knud Hjorth was the second son of Emil Theodor Hjorth and the brother of Otto Hjorth. He joined the family workshop with Otto in 1905 and worked alongside him from 1906.
Son of Emil Theodor and brother of Knud. The brothers joined the family workshop in 1905 and ran it together after Emil's retirement in 1906.

Emil Hjorth & Sons was founded by Emil Theodor Hjorth, who was the son, pupil, and successor of Johannes Hjorth. He worked for Lembock in Vienna in 1863, for Bernadel in Paris in 1864, and spent two years in London.
Ludwig Höfer studied under Baader in Mittenwald. He utilized Cremonese and Tyrolean models.
After graduating from the violin making school in Mittenwald, Andrea came to New York City where she was employed in the workshop of Jacques Francais Rare Violins, under the tutelage of Rene Morel.
Ignaz Hoffmann was a maker of violins and harps in Wölffelsdorff in the year 1776.
Anton Hofmann apprenticed to J. Martin Stoss in Vienna and succeeded to his business in 1838.
G. William Hofmann created Cremonese models. He signed his work as 'G. W. Hofmann' in Dublin in the year 1909.
Johann Martin Hofmann created a flat personal model that featured red-brown varnish and fine materials. He was a Hof-Laut- und Geigenmacher in Hochfürstl, located in Schillingsfürst, in 1805.
The earliest of the Hofmans family makers whose works survive today. He was probably assisted by his son, Matthias Hofmans IV. Original labels are usually undated.

Labels have been reported as early as 1619 and as late as 1740, leading to speculation that there might be two makers with the same name, possibly a father and son. Several instruments, including violins, pochettes, violas, and a theorbo dated 1619, are found in the Musical Instruments Museum in Brussels.
Karl Hofner was active from 1888 and removed to Bubenreuth after the Second World War around 1945. He established the business with his brothers Joseph and Walter.
Clifford A. Hoing trained as a wood carver and worked with Eric Gill before he began making violins in 1935.

Ernest Leopold Holder was the son and assistant of Thomas Jacques Holder and the brother of Thomas James. He was active in his father's workshop from approximately 1892 to 1914. Later, he established himself independently on Lee Road in Blackheath, producing good copies of various Italian models.

Thomas Jacques Holder established himself in Wardour Street in 1892, moved to Blackheath in 1898, and then to Leyland Road, Lee in 1912. He created clever and deceptive copies of various classical and minor Italian makers. He rarely used his own label and was a teacher to George Wulme-Hudson.

Thomas James Holder, son and pupil of Thomas Jacques Holder , was born in Cardiff and established his workshop at 16 place du Havre, Paris in 1913. He gained respect as a copyist and restorer, particularly known for his ingenious replicas of Italian instruments, especially those by Gagliano and Guadagnini.

Joseph Hollmayr was the son and pupil of Balthasar Hollmayr. He was active in Ingolstadt and began working in Neuberg in 1773. He created typical Tyrolean models with a golden-brown or deep brown varnish of poor opaque quality.

Heinrich Holste was a violinist and wood carver. He was a self-taught violin maker who created over 600 violins, cellos, and basses. His workmanship was fine. He branded his instruments with 'H. Holste'. The printed labels included: Angefertigt von / Heinr: Holste / Steinkirchen / in Alten Lande 1919 and Heinrich Holste / Geigenbauer / Steinkirchen 18.. No..

Carl C. Holzapfel emigrated to the USA in 1892. He established himself in Baltimore in 1898. He used Stradivari, Amati, and Guarneri models with suitable shades of varnish. Local materials were utilized, occasionally featuring five-stranded purfling. He was a very prolific maker and also crafted bows and plucked instruments. Some of his instruments were branded on the pegbox with 'C. Holzapfel. '.
Emanuel Adam Homolka was a pupil of Strnad. He was active from 1821 and created a personal model featuring golden-brown varnish. There were printed and manuscript labels.

Ferdinand August Vincenz Homolka was the son and pupil of Emmanuel Adam Homolka. He became known as "the Prague Stradivarius" due to a particularly successful reproduction of a 1719 Stradivari violin.

Ferdinand Joseph Homolka was a pupil of his brother Emmanuel Adam Homolka. He worked in Stary Bydzov (Neu Bydschow), Czech Republic, starting in 1837, and from 1843 at Kutná Hora. His work included various personal and classical models with some eccentricities, and he used dark red or yellow varnish.
P. A. Hone created a personal model featuring his own varnish in various shades.
British maker.

Carl Friedrich Hopf was active from 1862 in Brunndobra. He was the first maker of the 'Hopfgeigen' or Hopf model for the violin, which was subsequently utilized by several generations of his family and many commercial manufacturers. His work featured fine craftsmanship with high-quality golden-brown varnish. The instruments were branded internally: 'C. F. Hopf. '.

Christian August Hopf produced various grades of work, including some unpurfled for trade sale and better instruments featuring purfling and superior wood. He utilized a very low flat arch with a broad edge channel. The varnish was light brown or orange. His branding appeared on the inside of the back as 'C. A. Hopf or 'Hopf, Manuscript label.

Jacob Horil's work was inconsistent, though it sometimes exhibited a very accurate and precise finish. He employed a Tyrolese style characterized by a high squared-off arch and used a pale yellow or orange varnish. He branded his pieces with 'J. Horil'.
Karl Adam Hörlein studied under Joseph Vauchel. He spent three years in Vienna with Anton Hoffmann and Gabriel Lembock.
Franz Hornsteiner created a Klotz model. The varnish was yellow-brown. They were small violas featuring flatter arching.
Franz Georg Hornsteiner was from Mittenwald an der Iser and was active in 1795.
Son and assistant of Johann Hornsteiner II and trained at the Mittenwald violin making school. Initially made zithers but made some violins later in his career.
Joseph Hornsteiner produced coarse and unequal work finished with meagre yellow-brown varnish. They were useful for cellos and double basses.
Joseph Hornsteiner was a pupil of Joseph Hornsteiner (I). He improved his workmanship, although the model remained similar. The varnish was a decent brown.
Martin Hornsteiner used good materials and applied an orange-brown varnish of fine texture. Several small violas were created.
Mathias Hornsteiner was known as Tax'. He was the most distinguished maker of the family, influenced by Cremonese modeling and featuring Tyrolean archings of medium height.

Mathias Hornsteiner added the title `Gratzs' to his labels, likely to differentiate himself from Mathias Tax'. His work featured high arches and was characteristic of typical Mittenwald craftsmanship of high quality. The varnish was extremely variable, generally appearing dark red-brown.
Joseph Horvath was a prolific amateur maker who specialized in violas based on Stradivari and Guarneri models. He used his own amber varnish.

Rudolph Höss was a lute and viol maker for the Bavarian Court. He was also credited with violins and violas based on the Stainer model. His varnish was a pale golden-brown, and he often utilized one-piece backs made of birds-eye maple.

Gabriel Houfflack was a pupil of Joseph Chardon. He established himself as an expert and dealer in Paris from 1910. Active as a maker of violins, violas, and cellos from 1934, he worked in the rue Leningrad.

Keith W. Hovis was a bow maker. He was associated with Walter Hagen until Hagen's death in 1983 and subsequently worked alone. Certificates were awarded to him at the Violin Society of America competition in 1978. His bows were branded as HOVIS-HAGEN and HOVIS.

Arthur W. Howe was a self-taught maker, receiving some assistance from Sewell Boyce starting in 1897. He was active in Union, New York around 1939 before relocating to Endicott. His work included Stradivari and other classical models, utilizing oil varnish and demonstrating fine craftsmanship. Henley credited him with 50 violins by 1940, while Wenberg noted 'about 20 violins' in total.

T. Howell made a cornerless patent violin. He was the inventor of the Improved 18 (Patent) 36 Violin, Tenor & Violoncello, Double-bass & Spanish Guitar at his Manufactory and Music Warehouse in Bristol.
Andreas Hoyer produced poor Stainer copies, with most features exaggerated.
Friedrich Hoyer created undistinguished Tyrolean-style work with golden-brown varnish in Nürnberg in 1787. He also worked in Klingenthal in 1817.

Johann Christoph Hoyer was a violin maker known for his decent craftsmanship in the Tyrolese style. He worked in Klingenthal, where he became Principal of the Violin Makers' Guild in 1792. His instruments were finished with red-brown varnish, and he labeled them 'Johann Christoph Hoyer / Statuari Cremonalis Bavibat. 1766 Johann Christoph Hoyer / in Klingenthal 1798.'
Johann Friedrich Hoyer. Label printed in red:.
Otto A. Hoyer trained with E. Sartory in Paris. He established himself in Markneukirchen in 1925. His fine bows featured both round and octagonal sticks, branded: O. A. HOYER PARISER 1922.
Edward Hoyle created personal models that featured some eccentric ideas. They generally utilized dark red oil varnish.

Johann Georg Huber was from a Füssen family of instrument makers. He sometimes utilized wood from old viols and employed neat workmanship. His instruments featured dark brown varnish. Joannes Giorgius Huber / fecit Viennae 1761.

Julius A. Hubicka was a pupil of F. Spidlen in Moscow. He created fine and well-observed copies of Cremonese models, using appropriate varnish colors. Julius A. Hubicka / No. 107 / Pragae Anno 1926 Julius A. Hubicka / fecit Pragae Anno 1919 Julius A. Hubicka / Praha roku 19..

George Hudson worked from 'Cremona House' in Skegness as a self-taught maker. He completed about 100 instruments, using amber oil varnish in various shades of yellow and red. Each instrument was labelled, branded, and numbered.

William John Huggler established his work in 1959 and was advised by Simone Sacconi. He created six violins and 14 violas based on Stradivari and Guarneri models, using his own orange-brown oil varnish. In 1976, he received prizes at the Violin Society of America competition. His instruments were branded internally.

Thomas Andreas Hulinzky. He was a pupil of Eberle and created a high-arched personal Amatise model featuring small soundholes. The instruments were well finished with a good golden or red-brown varnish, although sometimes an inferior brown spirit recipe was used. He made violins, violas, cellos, and plucked instruments. Hulinzky worked in Prague and dated his work to the year 1770.

John Justus Hull was a barber and a self-taught maker. He created models inspired by Stradivari, Guarneri, and his own designs. His fine workmanship utilized local materials and his own varnish. Some of the instruments consisted of hand-finished imported factory work. He also made several bows.

Andrew (Alexander) Hume was a self-taught musician and violin maker who was active in Dumfries, then in Peterborough, around 1915-1916, and finally at 34 Great Portland Street, London. He almost certainly relied on factory-made instruments that he imported, which he then adjusted and labelled.
Albin Hums was a pupil of E. Knorr and H. Prell. His fine bows included some stamped with the name Henri Marteau as well as his own.
Christoph Friedrich Hunger was the son of Samuel Hunger and a pupil of A. B.

Charles Hunnicut was a farmer and self-taught violin maker, receiving some guidance from David Struble in Washington. He made approximately 60 violins using the Stradivari model, utilizing local materials and imported commercial oil varnish that was golden- or orange-red in color. His workmanship was fine. A copy of the Alard Strad was made by him in Wilmington, Ohio, in 1928, and is numbered 43.

Richard Husband created untutored work on a Stainer model. The instruments included violins and a small viola, all featuring thin yellow varnish. They were inscribed: 'Richard Husband 1748 ad exemplar Stainer. '.
Charles Claude Husson worked for Vigneron, Lamy, and others as a bow maker. His brand was CH. HUSSON.

Charles Claude Husson was the son of Charles Claude Husson (I). He worked for J. B. Vuillaume starting in 1873, then for Voirin in 1875, and for Gand & Bernardel from 1878. He established himself independently around 1880 at rue du Faubourg St. Denis. His work was in the style of Vigneron. He branded his bows as HUSSON PARIS or CH HUSSON A PARIS.

Claude Charles Nicolas Husson was a bow maker of the Peccatte school who later developed a lighter Vuillaume style. He served as an important teacher to many Parisian archetiers, including Vigneron and Lamy. Few bows are known, and none were branded.
Mars Auguste Husson was the son of Jean Baptiste Husson. He was a bow maker who worked for Vigneron.

Pierre Husson was a bow maker and the brother of Claude Charles Husson. Known as 'Husson-Dufour', he incorporated his wife Francoise's maiden name into his brand. Bows were rarely encountered, branded: HUSSON DUFOURS À PARIS.

Théodore Husson was a pupil of Mougenot who collaborated with Bonnel in Rennes in 1887, taking over the Emile Bonnel workshop. He produced 100 violins and 10 cellos, primarily for Bonnel, who labelled them. His work was noted for its fine craftsmanship and featured a distinctive yellow-red varnish.

Auguste Husson Mariet, son and pupil of Pierre Husson, was a bow maker known for his work with J. B. Vuillaume, C. Bazin, C. Thomassin, and Vigneron. He is also referred to as 'Husson Mariet,' a name derived from his wife's surname. Although his brand 'HUSSON-MARIET' is rarely seen, he later established himself independently at 3 rue Renauld, Paris.
Carleen Maley Hutchins was an academic scientist and violin maker. Initially self-taught, she later received guidance from Karl Berger and Simone Sacconi starting in 1947.
Andrew Hyde was a musician and mechanic. He was an active violin and bow maker in Northampton from 1886.
Contemporary maker.
The violin model was a Guarneri del Gesù from 1742, featuring dark brown oil varnish.

Vincenzo Iorio was a prolific maker. His work represented the typical style of the modern Neapolitan school. It was unpretentious yet well modeled, featuring fine orange and golden-red varnish along with quality materials. He taught Postiglione.
Contemporary maker.
Edward Poppleweil J'Anson was a pupil of William Booth. He was a skilled copyist of classical Italian work, and much of his output appeared under false labels.
Cyril William Jacklin was a pupil of Frank Howard in Nottingham before joining Frances Ciesla in business in 1921. He established himself independently in Robert Street, London, from 1927.

One of the best and most important 17th century Dutch makers, Hendrik Jacobs was rumored to have spent time in Italy, but it is more likely that his knowledge of the Italian tradition came from instruments circulating in the Netherlands during this period. His early work, which dates from roughly 1650 to 1670, follows Amati very ... closely, with slightly wide f-holes.
Rene Jacquemin studied under Audinot. He worked for Mougenot-Jacquet-Gand and Caressa in Paris. He established himself independently in Mirecourt in 1919. He received a gold medal in 1926.
Gabriel Xavier Jacquet specialized in double basses and was assisted by his son Moise-Gabriel. He was the son of Joseph Xavier Jacquet.
Charles Jacquot apprenticed to Nicholas ainé in 1819 and later worked with Breton. He established himself independently in Nancy from 1823.

Fernand Jacquot was the son and successor of Etienne. He apprenticed with Mougenot-Gauche in Mirecourt before working with his father in Nancy. He took over the business in 1925 and received a gold medal at Liège in 1905 for his fine work.
Pierre Charles Jacquot, son of Charles Jacquot and nephew of J.B. Vuillaume, was a leading French maker of the 19th century.
Hans Jaeger was a pupil of Glaesel. He worked in Regensburg and Amsterdam. From 1885, he established himself independently in Markneukirchen. His work featured fine oil varnish.

Andreas Jais was a pupil of Matthias Klotz, active in Tölz from 1711. He crafted instruments featuring a highly arched Stainer model, demonstrating fine workmanship with details such as ornately carved heads and, occasionally, painted purfling.

Anton Jais, son of Franz Jais, was active from 1769 and is noted for his use of a high-arched Stainer model. He demonstrated fine workmanship and materials, with varnish characterized by a good yellow-brown and dark red-brown.
Johann Jais, son of Franz Jais, worked in Bozen (Bolzano) and Tolz. He was a pupil of Matthias Kloz and created an Albani-like model using fine materials and a deep red-brown varnish.
Contemporary maker.

William James was a self-taught maker who created Stradivari and Guarneri violin models, as well as a Tertis viola model. He utilized terpenes resin varnish and local materials. A total of 22 instruments were made, along with some guitars and 12 bows crafted on the Henderson pattern.

Gabriel Jamis was a pupil of G. Apparut and later became associated with Gaston Meyer. He created copies of Widhalm, finished with orange-red varnish. His work was branded: 'G. Jamis et G. Meyer / Mirecourt année 1919. No.. ' It was labelled: Modèle exposition.

Ferdinand Wilhelm Jaura apprenticed to his uncle Wilhelm Jaura in 1905. He established himself independently in Vienna from 1917 and in Munich from 1940. He was credited with 1, 500 stringed instruments of all kinds, all showcasing refined craftsmanship.
Wilhelm Thomas Theodor Jaura was the eldest son of Wilhelm Josef Jaura, a guitar maker. He worked for W.
Henry Jay (Jaye) had no known connection with the earlier Henry Jaye. He was probably a pupil of Peter Wamsley.
F. S. Jean created a Stradivari-derived model. It featured fine materials and a dark yellow-brown varnish. The work was not highly refined.

Pierre Napoléon Jeandel was a pupil of Charotte in Mirecourt starting in 1830 and moved to Rouen in 1835 to work with Joseph Charotte . He succeeded to Charotte's business in 1848 in partnership with Lucien Delau.
Contemporary maker. Won first prize 2001 at the Triennale competition in Cremona.

Shan Jiang started making violins in 1990, and received his master degree in violin making from the Central Conservatory of Music. He currently works in NYC and has won several medals and awards in VSA Competitions.
Anton Jirowsky worked for Thomas Zach from around 1887 and collaborated with Ignaz Lutz and Haudek from approximately 1896. He established himself independently in 1902.
Contemporary maker.
William Grieve Johnston apprenticed at the London piano firm of Broadwood before beginning work at the firm of W.E. Hill & Sons building the company's inlaid and veneered instrument cases.

Franz Jühling was associated with Richler until 1884. He manufactured very good commercial work in large numbers. Gold medals were awarded to him in Paris and Brussels. He also made lutes and strings.

Curt Jung was a pupil of Adolf Hammig. He worked as an employee and successor of Otto Möckel in Berlin from 1925 to 1937. He created fine copies of the Cremonese models, which were branded on the back and sides.
Jan (John) Juzek. Guarneri model, fine workmanship.

Albin Kämpffe was a pupil of Kessler. He created copies of Italian, Tyrolean, and French works. A. K., Geigenbaumeister, was located in Markneukirchen in 1890. He marked his work with "Fecit anno 19.. Copie.. ".
Contemporary prize-winning bow maker based in Port Townsend, USA.

Born in 1923 in the small Slovenian town of Okraj Ljutomer, Joseph Kantuscher enrolled at the Violin Making School in Mittenwald in December 1941. He worked for the Reiter Violin Shop for a decade, before opening his own shop in 1955.
Ladislav Kaplan emigrated to New York in 1893 and made his first violin in 1895. He was in business as Muller & Kaplan in 1922.
Bartholomaus Karner produced Klotz and Stradivari models. The workmanship was good, featuring thin opaque varnish.
Georg Karner was a pupil of Georg Klotz. He was in Mittenwald / an der Iser in 1795.
Antoni Karolkowski was a pupil of Rymwid-Mickiewicz and F. K. Pruszak. He exhibited in Poznan in 1952 and was appointed luthier to the conservatoire.
Born in Dluzec, Poland. Studied violin performance under Kochanski and Jarzebski in Poland. Studied violin making in 1958 under Etienne Vatelot in Paris.
Albert H. Karr taught violin making by Silas Benjamin and John Hendershot starting in 1910.
Henryk Kaston was a bow maker from 1943. He was also a professional violinist and jeweller.
A. W. Kaufman created personal models featuring extended Guarneri-like soundholes. The varnish used was red spirit varnish.
Apprenticed at Thibouville-Lamy. Worked for Silvestre and Maucotel in Paris 1900-1905.
Joseph Kaye made over 150 violins using Guarneri and personal models, featuring yellow to red-brown oil varnish.

Edward Keenan made instruments based on Stradivari and Guarneri models. The scrolls were outlined in black, and they featured a yellow-brown varnish. Some instruments utilized local materials. He won first prize in Dublin in 1913. The instruments were crafted in Dublin in the year 1918.

Helmuth Keller was the son-in-law of Martin Nebel, a violin maker in Philadelphia. Keller came to Philadelphia from Germany after WW2 to work for William Moennig & Son, and opened his own shop in the 1960s.

Philippe Keller created Stradivari models and personal models designed based on the principles of Max Grossman. The varnishes were golden-yellow or golden-red. He was a Königl. Bayer. Hoflieferant in Würzburg, established in 1832. He manufactured in the year 19.. with the number...
Andreas Kempter's name appeared in the records of Füssen, where he was likely apprenticed. He was active in Dillingen starting in 1725.
Alexander Kennedy was a pupil of Nathaniel Cross and taught his nephew John Kennedy . He worked in Market Street, off Oxford Street (now Great Titchfield Street), London.
Alexander Kennedy created a personal model with yellow varnish. He was based in Wallsend, 19..
Nephew of Alexander Kennedy . Active in Cooper's Gardens, Houghton Street, Clare Market, St Clement's Lane, Long Alley, and from 1778 in Johnson's Court.

Thomas Kennedy was the oldest son of lesser British violin maker John Kennedy , from whom he probably learned the basics of his craft before being apprenticed to Thomas Powell in 1795. He worked briefly for William Forster III before setting up his own ... workshop in London's West End, making instruments and bows.

Franz Xaver Kerschensteiner was a pupil of Peter Schulz. He exhibited very fine workmanship and finish. He was a copyist of various classical models. The varnish was fine and transparent, though slightly hard and glassy.

Anton Kessel worked commercially, primarily on the Guarneri model, which was retailed in England by Foucher and later by Haynes and Co. His workmanship was good, featuring a decent golden-brown varnish. Bows of comparable quality were branded: ANTON KESSEL. Kessel was a Geigenmacher in Breitenfeld.

Trained at the Swiss Violin Making School at Brienz under Adolf Konig. Worked for Arnold Dolmetsch Ltd. in Haslemere, UK, later moving to Albert Arnold Ltd. in Charing Cross Road with Cyril Jacklin until that business closed in 1955.
Ernst Kessler was a violin and bow maker. He was a pupil of August Voigt and Ludwig Otto .

Heinrich Kessler studied under Hammig in Dresden and later with Johann Padewet in Karlsruhe. He established himself independently in Mannheim from 1880. He served as the repairer to the Grand Duke of Baden from 1888 to 1919. His work was inconsistent; some pieces were of fine execution, while others were of trade class.
Johann Georg Kessler was a Master in the Violin Makers' Guild from 1756. He used a Stainer model characterized by a high arch and often included carved heads. The varnish was golden-brown.
Paul Kessler worked with his brother Ernst. He established himself independently in Markneukirchen in 1908. He was a fairly prolific maker.

Wilhelm Albin Kessler worked for Wenzel Lenk and Louis Noebe in Frankfurt-am-Main. He established himself independently there in 1897. The model was derived from Stradivari, executed with fine workmanship and a good oil varnish of orange-brown tint.
Wilhelm August Kessler was the son and pupil of Heinrich Kessler. He established himself independently in Mannheim in 1882. His model was derived from Stradivari.
Dominik Kief created a small model with a medium arching, diminished scroll, and dark varnish. The work was generally meagre and inartistic. He was located in Büsan / in Tyroli in 1743.
Hermann Kilian was the principal cellist of the Munich orchestra. He studied under Fiorini in violin making around 1906. He worked as a repairer for A. Schmid around 1912. His production was small.

Contemporary violin maker and violinist. He studied violin making under Harry Reeve in Batesville, AR, from 1973. His violins are numbered on the label and are branded "R. D. Kimble" on the top and back plates.
Johann Christian Kinpolth demonstrated fine workmanship on the Amati model. He used well-chosen materials and applied a good orange-brown varnish.
Violin Bow St. Petersburg the 'Auer, Heifetz'

Son and student of Nikolai Kittel Sr.; also studied briefly with Honore Derazey in Mirecourt around 1859–60. Continued the family business in St Petersburg after his father's death in 1868, finally selling it in 1873.
Aloys Klein founded A. Klein et Cie in 1884, producing high-quality instruments on a commercial scale, with the workshop managed by Antoine Brubach .

Cornelius Kleynman was the step-son and probable pupil of cittern maker Franciscus Lupo. He was a contemporary of Hendrick Jacobs and an equally sophisticated early follower of Amati, although his surviving work was rare.
Franz Josef Klier was the son of Joseph. He apprenticed with R.
Otto Klier established in Bratislava (Pressburg) Slovakia in 1935. He moved to Neunkirchen Germany in 1966.
Joseph Klimits was a Geigen und Guitarmacher in Wien No. 50.

Albert W. Klinth, who worked as a publisher, studied violin making on the west coast of the USA, possibly in Seattle. He made more than 50 violins based on the Guarneri model, utilizing shaded oil varnish.
Aegidius Klotz was the son of Sebastian Klotz. He was regarded as one of the finest among this large family of Mittenwald makers.

Aegidius Sebastian Klotz is one of the best makers in the family tradition that founded the Mittenwald school of violin making. As with many other members of the family, Sebastian hewed closely to an Amati model, but in keeping with his father Sebastian I's ... work, Aegidius Sebastian's instruments have flatter arches than the usual Stainer model.
Ferdinand Klotz was the son of Johann Karl Klotz and produced typical yet undistinguished work in the family style. His location was Mittenwald an der Iser 17.

Mittenwald violin maker George Karl Klotz is sometimes designated a descendant of Matthias I , but expert opinion is divided on the question, and he may not have been part of that branch of the famous family. Like other makers of the Mittenwald school, his work shows a preference for Amati ... models.

Johann Carl Klotz was the son of Mathias Klotz (I) and is believed to have been a pupil of his half-brother Sebastian. He trained one apprentice, Phillip Sailer, from c. 1737 to 1742, as well as his sons, Wolfgang and Michael.
Joseph Klotz was the son of Joseph Thomas Klotz (I). His workmanship was typical of the Klotz family, though it lacked great distinction.

Joseph Thomas Klotz was the son of Sebastian Klotz. His work was very inconsistent, presumably created for sale in various grades, and featured the typical Klotz style characterized by low and medium arching and yellow varnish.
Son of Urban Klotz, a tailor. First maker of this extended and highly important family of violin makers.

Michael Klotz was the son of Johann Karl Klotz and is known for his rare instruments, which often bore the labels of Joseph Klotz. He created decorated work featuring ebony and ivory inlay on the outer edges and around the scroll, with some instruments having backs made of bird's eye maple.

Sebastian Klotz, son of Matthias Klotz, was a distinguished craftsman from the Mittenwald family of violin makers. He produced a violin labeled as "Haynes" and sold by Haynes & Co., London, around 1907, identified with the number 152.
Sebastian Klotz was the son of Aegidius Klotz. It was typical of Klotz's work, though undistinguished. He was located in Mittenwald in 1803.
Matthias Kloz I and his descendants represent a continuous lineage from the 17th-century origins of the Mittenwald school to the 20th century. Georg I , Sebastian I , Aegidius ...

The lack of historical documentation surrounding the origins of Aegidius Kloz makes it unlikely that he is a descendant of Matthias I , founder of a major branch of the Mittenwald violin making family. His extant instruments date from the late 17th and early 18th centuries, and are markedly superior to those of Matthias I's ... grandson Aegidius Sebastian Kloz , who was born in 1733.

Son of Matthias I and older brother of Sebastian I , Georg Kloz I seems to have enjoyed some sort of honorable status during his career. His violins, which are well-made and on a somewhat wider model than those of his brother, possess an attractive yellowish-brown ... or reddish-brown varnish.
Frank R. Knight was an amateur maker and a pupil of W. Glenister. He used a Stradivari model and applied his own golden-brown oil and spirit varnishes.
Knilling was a family of luthiers in Mittenwald, Germany, known for their mostly indistinguishable trade work.
Josef Knitl was one of the better makers of the Mittenwald school. The Klotz model was well made and featured fine transparent golden-brown varnish. It was signed internally on the back.
Family of luthiers in Markneukirchen Germany.
August Moritz Knopf studied under O. B.

Carl Heinrich Knopf was a violin and bow maker trained by his uncle Christian Friedrich . He established himself independently in Berlin in 1868 and was employed at the Lowendall factory from 1873 to 1885.

Christian Friedrich Wilhelm Knopf was a bow maker active in Dresden, known for his fine craftsmanship. He was the youngest son of Christian Wilhelm senior and trained his nephews, Johann Wilhelm and Carl ...
Christian Wilhelm Knopf was a bow maker known for his fine work. He was a senior member of the family.

Heinrich (Henry) Richard Knopf, son of Carl Heinrich , studied under his father and learned from his uncle Johann. He worked for Bausch in Dresden and Christian Adam in Berlin before emigrating to New York in 1880.
Son of Karl Wilhelm. Trained with his uncle, Christian Friedrich. Worked in Markneukirchen before establishing himself in Dresden in 1884. Stamp: 'W. KNOPF DRESDEN' (earlier bows unstamped).
Karl Wilhelm Knopf was a bow maker, the son and pupil of Christian Knopf. He was the second son of Christian Wilhelm senior and the father of Johann Wilhelm and Carl Heinrich . ...
Albert Albrecht Knorr was the son and pupil of Albert Knorr. His work was similar to that of his father.

Paul Knorr worked in Breslau and Berlin as a pupil of M. Dolling. He was employed by F. & R. Enders in Markneukirchen starting in 1918 and established himself independently there in 1921. He was a highly regarded craftsman known for his exceptional varnish, which had a clear orange-brown color and fine texture. He used very good materials, and his violas were particularly appreciated.

William B. Knox established a business in Utica, where he made strings and instruments. He was a pupil of Walter Colton and created Stradivari and Guarneri models featuring hard orange varnish, with some assembled from prefabricated parts. His brand was 'W. B. Knox. '.

Albert Knupfer worked for A. Wunderlich in Markneukirchen and later with E. Schmidt and H. Glass. He established himself independently in Wemitzgrün in 1904. His son Otto later assisted him. He produced fine work based on classical Italian models and signed his pieces internally.

Franz Joseph Koch devised a method of wood impregnation that he believed recreated the Cremonese method. He treated instruments known as 'Homogeneous Stringed Instruments, ' which included classical models, some featuring aged and shaded varnish. Prof. F. J. Koch Dresden. / Anno 1922. No. 534.
Friedrich Kochendörfer was the son and pupil of A. F.
Ludwig Köhler studied under Pilat in Budapest. He worked in Berlin and Chicago, Mirmic, before returning to Budapest. His output was small.
Samuel Kolstein was a double bass player. He was largely self-taught as a violin and bow maker.

Sándor Komáromy apprenticed to Andras Bergmann from 1903 to 1906. He subsequently worked for Otto Karl Zimmer and Stowasser in Budapest, for Bela Babas in Hermannstadt, and for Nandor Hock in Rakospalota.

First of the Konya makers of Hungary. Initially a photographer, then taught himself to make violins in the 1960s. Later studied in Cremona and was asked to make violins for the Menuhin School in the UK. Won various medals. His two sons, Istvan II (who later Italianised his name to Stefano Conia) and Lajos both became makers.
Contemporary Hungarian maker. One of two sons of Istvan Konya.
Born in Chicago. Worked for John Hornsteiner and the William Lewis firm in Chicago and later established own shop there. Used a handmade brand 'F. kovanda'. Some bows unstamped.

Born in Washington, Cody Kowalski is a contemporary bow maker who studied under Charles Espey and is currently based in Germany. Kowalski won three gold medals in the 2016 Violin Society of America International Competition and two Grand Prix Awards in the 1st International Darling Bow Making Competition in 2016.

info: Contemporary maker.
Adolph J. Krug created a Stradivari model featuring slim soundholes and shaded oil varnish. The workmanship was fine.
Lorenz Krumbholz trained in Markneukirchen. He worked for Otto Möckel in Berlin, Karel Van Der Meer in Amsterdam from 1916, and Joseph Vedral in The Hague from 1917.
John Thomas Kuczer completed 60 violins and six violas. He also made viola bows.

Johann Kulik was a highly regarded maker of refined Cremonese models. He used fine spirit varnish in appropriate shades and crafted particularly well-carved scrolls. Some instruments featured eccentric small patterns with deep ribs, made according to the suggestions of Leopold Savoi. Genaunach Antonius Stradivarius gemacht / von Johann Kulik, 1852. Joh. Kulik. Geigenmacher in Prag. A. 18..

Born in Czechoslovakia in 1930, Joseph Kun settled in Ottawa, Canada in 1968. Best known for his design of a shoulder rest patented in 1972, he was also an accomplihsed bow-maker, winning three gold medals and one silver medal at the biennial International Violin and Bow Makers Competition in 1983. He also co-authored the book The Art of Bow-Making.

Martin Kuntze-Fechner studied chemistry and earned a Doctor of Philosophy at Boston University (USA). He studied violin in Munich before taking up violin making. He worked in Brussels around 1905-1912 before returning to Munich and later moving to Wall. His fine workmanship featured good materials and brown oil varnish. Kuntze-Fechner created No. 30 in Brussels in 1910.

Laurent (Lorenz) Küntzel was active in Breslau from 1815 to 1857. He subsequently established himself in Berlin, where he was appointed luthier to the court in 1858. He was a prolific maker, though his work was of unremarkable quality, featuring Cremonese and personal models. His varnish came in various shades and was made with excellent materials. In 1862, he exhibited a quartet in London.

Wilhelm Paul Kunze worked for Kok in Amsterdam from 1892. He established himself independently in The Hague from 1902. His careful and accurate copies included Stradivari and other Cremonese models. He developed his own mineral wood treatment. A prize was awarded to him at the Geneva Exhibition in 1927. Wilhelm Paul Kunze / fecit 's-Gravenhage No. 122. Anno 1924.
Frederick Kuster made 43 violins based on the Stradivari model and 39 violas modeled after the Tertis design.

admin2_name: City and County of San Francisco

info: Emile L'Humbert was a pupil of Derazey and Mougenot in Mirecourt. He worked for Blanchard in Lyons and for Jombart, Bernadel, and Silvestre in Paris.
Lucien L'Humbert was the son, pupil, and successor of Emile L'Humbert. He created this in 1949 at 34 Rue de Passy, Paris.

Marc André Joseph Laberte was the son of Pierre Alexis and served as the Director of Laberte-Humbert Frères, which merged with Fournier Magnié in 1920. He inherited the family firm and employed hundreds of makers, producing a wide range of instruments and bows from student to soloist level.

Founded in 1780, it became one of the largest and most significant businesses in Mirecourt. The company was known as Laberte Frères until 1925, when it merged with Fournier Magnié, which had been established in 1775 by Joseph Thiriot .
Mirecourt firm founded in 1876 by the violin making brothers Maurice-Emile and Pierre Alexis Auguste Laberte. At first it produced only factory instruments, some with fictitious labels such as 'J.
Fils Lacombe produced work in the style of Vuillaume, but it was of inferior quality.
Jacques Lafleur was a violin and bow maker. He apprenticed in Mirecourt and associated with the Adam family there.

Joseph René Lafleur was the son and successor of Jacques Lafleur . He became an active bowmaker around 1830 and was employed by Lupot from approximately 1833, working in his master's style with low plain frogs.

Antoine Lagarde was the son and pupil of Gabriel Lagarde from around 1815. He was a violin and bow maker who established himself independently from approximately 1825. Rare but fine bows in the Pajeot style were produced from that time, featuring ivory frogs with star inlay. His work was branded: A. LAGARDE.

Louis Auguste Lagarenne apprenticed to Derazey. He became the son-in-law and partner of Hilaire Darche in Brussels in 1908. He received a gold medal at the Ghent Exhibition in 1913. He was branded: 'A. Largarenne / Bruxelles'.

Studied making in Indianapolis and Chicago. Worked in San Antonio, TX, then established own shop in Madison, WI, c. 1959. Made about 200 instruments of various grades of quality, including double basses. Stamped bows, 'L. Lamay', just past the winding.
Jean Nicolas Lambert was Master of the Luthiers' Guild from 1745 to 1746. His premises on rue Michel-le-Comte continued to operate after his death, managed by successors until 1789.

Albin Charles Lambotte was a medical doctor and a self-taught luthier. He was credited with 100 violins, violas, and cellos based on classical models. He retired to Antwerp. Inlaid Maggini copies were found in the Conservatoires of Liège and Antwerp. He also made several viola d'amores. His brand appeared below the back button: `C. L. '.

Alfred Lamy was a bow maker. He was the son of Jean Joseph Baptiste Lamy. He apprenticed with Bazin and later worked for Jacquot in Nancy and Cuniot-Hury from 1906 to 1911. He briefly established himself independently from 1919 to 1920 but joined Laberte in 1921. He branded his work as A. LAMY or ALFRED LAMY.
Alfred Lamy was a bow maker and the son, pupil, and successor of Joseph Alfred Lamy. He branded his work as A. LAMY PARIS.

Georges-Léon Lamy was the son, pupil, and assistant of Joseph Alfred ('Lamy père'). He became a casualty of the First World War. Since he never worked independently, his individual style and brand are not known.

Hippolyte Camille Lamy was a bow maker. He was the son, pupil, and successor of Joseph Alfred ('Lamy père'). Called up for National Service during the First World War, he returned to assist his father and ultimately succeeded him in 1919. His work, which was generally very similar to that of his father, deteriorated from around 1930 due to failing eyesight.

Joseph Alfred Lamy did his early apprenticeship in Mirecourt with Claude Charles Nicolas Husson , where the Vuillaume - Voirin school was probably the dominant influence. After an ... eight-year period in Château-Thierry, where Lamy formed a friendship with Joseph Voirin , he moved to Paris in 1876 to work for Voirin's older brother François Nicolas and became his assistant.

Léon Jules Lamy worked for Thibouville-Lamy. He established himself in Paris in 1890 and primarily worked as a repairer. His classical models featured orange-brown varnish and fine materials. They were branded as 'Lamy'.
Louis Lamy was a bow maker and a pupil of Cuniot. He produced several grades and qualities of work.

Born in Vicenza, Lanaro studied with Giuseppe Pedrazzini and Gaetano Sgarabotto and won a prize at the 1949 Cremona International Violin Making Competition. Lanaro emigrated to Argentina in 1951, then in 1955 he founded the National School of Violin Making in Mexico City at the invitation of the Mexican National Institute of Fine Arts, remaining as the school's director until 1972.

Umberto Lanaro was the brother and pupil of Luigi Lanaro and worked with him from 1945 to 1953. After spending five years in Argentina, he returned to Albignasego. He created classical models and was awarded a gold medal at Bagnacavallo in 1976.

Although Carlo Ferdinando Landolfi was born in 1710, he does not seem to have taken up violin making before the middle of the 18th century. One possible impetus for this belated start in the trade is the arrival of G.B.

Pietro Antonio Landolfi studied under his father, Carlo Ferdinando , whose prolific output dominated making in Milan from the 1750s onwards. Following the deaths of the older generation makers such as Carlo Giuseppe and Carlo Antonio Testore, as well as the Grancinos, it was Carlo Ferdinando Landolfi who became the preeminent ... flag bearer of the Milanese school.
Contemporary New York-based French maker of violins and bows.
Benedikt Lang was the son of Ferdinand. He made cellos and double basses.
Franz Lange repaired it in November 1920 in Eulenberg.
Alfred Charles Langonet was the son and pupil of Charles Frank Langonet . He worked in the workshop of W.
Eugene Langonet was the youngest brother of Charles François Langonet . He apprenticed at Chipot-Vuillaume and was employed by Paul Mangenot before joining his brother in London at Hills.

Alfred Lanini was born in California and studied violin making locally before traveling to Milan in 1911 to study with Riccardo Antoniazzi . Upon his teacher's death six months later, he entered the workshop of Celeste Farrotti , where he remained until ... 1914.
Loris Lanini created 130 violins, eight violas, eight cellos, and two double basses using personal models with golden-yellow oil varnish. He worked in Pontedera in the year 19..
Ernest Francis Lant was a pupil of Harry Bidduiph. He specialized in violins, violas, cellos, double basses, and bows.

Bohuslav Lantner was the son and pupil of Ferdinand Lantner. He worked in Germany before settling in Prague in 1891. His models included Stradivari, Guarneri, and personal designs. He used various varnishes: his early work featured a brittle spirit recipe, while later examples utilized a soft oil varnish of red-brown color.
Ferdinand Martin Lantner was a pupil of Lehner. He established himself independently in Prague in 1862. His work featured personal models and fine workmanship.

J. A. Lapaix created personal models that were quite eccentric and excessively experimental. He received medals and diplomas from the Society for the Encouragement of National Industries in 1848, the Imperial Society of Lille in 1852, and at the Paris Exhibition in 1855.

Bowmaker Marcel Lapierre cast his net widely among the many Mirecourt workshops active in the twentieth century. He trained with Jérôme Thibouville-Lamy from 1921 to 1923 and began working for François Lotte in 1931.

Etienne Laprevotte was appointed luthier to the Duke of Bordeaux. The quality of his work was inconsistent; some instruments were rather meagre in wood and varnish, while others featured better materials and workmanship. He also made guitars.

Jean Larcher was a pupil of Collin Mezin. He created classical models featuring personal arching. His work was associated with Lutherie dArt, and he held the title of Maître-Luthier and Ingénieur des Arts & Métiers. His order number was 93, and the year was 1926.

Christian Frederik Larsen, born in Odense on March 16th, 1846, was the son of Lars Christian Henric Larsen. He took over the family business with his brother Wilhelm (1848 - 1919) after their father's death in 1866.

Joseph Anton Laske was a pupil of J. Kolditz. He subsequently worked in Dresden, Berlin, Vienna, and Brno. From 1764, he established himself in Prague at the sign 'Au Petit Serpent d'Or'. His models were derived from Stainer and used plain materials. He applied a golden-brown varnish until around 1790, after which he switched to a deep red-brown. He crafted bowed instruments of all forms.

Enzo Lassi was the son and pupil of Francesco Lassi. He also studied briefly with Gaetano Pollastri. His models were derived from Stradivari and Guarneri and featured dark orange-red varnish. Some violins were crafted from wood salvaged from the wartime bombing of the Masini theatre in Faenza.

Francesco Lassi was a self-taught maker, active from 1906. He completed over 400 instruments based on classical and personal models. His varnish featured various golden-red tints. He received prizes at Florence in 1921 and Cremona in 1937.
William Laugher was an amateur maker credited with 50 violins and 10 violas, often utilizing the Reichers system.
Robert Laumann studied under Nandor. He worked for J.

Albert Laurent was the son, pupil, and successor of Emile Laurent. He created fine copies of classical Cremonese models with golden- and red-brown varnish. They were signed and dated internally as 'Laurent Fils'.

Émile Laurent was the son and pupil of Emile Laurent (I). He worked for E. Germain and established himself independently in Bordeaux before joining his brother Albert in Brussels in 1912. He finally moved to Paris in 1925. Medals were awarded at Liège and Brussels.
Émile Laurent apprenticed to Auguste Dane. He subsequently worked for Joseph Hel in Lille.

Jean Paul Lauxerrois was a bow maker who studied under Lotte in Mirecourt. He established himself independently in Paris in 1959 and later retired to Bordeaux. His work included copies of Tourte and Peccatte. He branded his bows as: LAUXERROIS A PARIS.

Son of Jean-Paul Lauxerrois, he also studied with Bernard Ouchard in Mirecourt. In 1984 he established himself in Grenoble, then moved to Lyon in 1988. His last move was to Paris, where he died aged 35. Considered to be one of the most talented makers of his generation.
Antonio Maria Lavazza died prematurely in a duel with G. B. Grancino in Milan, Italy. There were no known authenticated works. He lived in contrada Largha in 1703.
Carlo Antonio Santino Lavazza was the son of Pietro Antonio Lavazza. His work, a fine example of the Grancino school, exhibited some influence from Landolphi.

Jean Lavello established himself independently in Paris. He was a pupil of Pierre Audinot and served as the luthier for the Conservatoire and Colonne Orchestra. In 1930, he received a gold medal. His workmanship was noted for its quality.
Francesco Lazzaretti reported fine work in the Veneto style with orange-red varnish. It was little known. Manuscript labels were present.
Giovanni Lazzaro was known as `Rosario. ' He studied under Pietro Pettinato, a sculptor and amateur violin maker. His work included Stradivari and personal models featuring dark golden varnish.
Guillaume Le Blond worked in London after Dunkirk, according to an inscription on a piano in the Berlin Music Museum. He plucked instruments in the Brussels and Berlin Museums.

J. C. Pierre Le Lièvre was born in Mirecourt. He was a pupil of Grosset and had a Germanic appearance characterized by a high arch, deep fluting, and clear yellow varnish. He made various types of instruments.

Charles Le Lyonnais apprenticed with E. Audinot in Mirecourt. He established himself in Nantes in 1913. After completing military service from 1914 to 1918, he renewed his activities in Nantes and collaborated with D. Chenantais. He received second prize at an exhibition in Paris in 1921. His models were derived from Stradivari, showcasing fine workmanship and materials with red-brown varnish.
Rome-based contemporary maker. Originally from Switzerland; studied in Cremona.

Antonio Lecchi probably worked at Cavalli's `Monteverdi' workshop. He established himself independently around 1920. Instruments were described by Blot in the style of R. Muncher; they featured a large flat model with red-brown varnish.

Giuseppe Lecchi was one of many Italian violin makers descended from a family of woodworkers. After serving in WWI and returning to cabinet-making, Lecchi became an apprentice of Cesare Candi in 1923.
Antonio Lechi created a large personal model featuring flat arching. The workmanship was decent, and the varnish displayed various tints.
Jacques Leclerc was the name used in violins made by Laberte-Magnié. Instruments d'Artistes were associated with him in the year..

Joseph Nicolas LeClerc worked in the `Quinze-Vingt' Hospital. One violin, dated 1770 and characterized as mediocre with yellow varnish, was part of the Snoeck collection and is now housed in the Berlin Museum of Music. Another violin, cited by Vannes, was of better quality and dated 1765.
Born near San Francisco. Worked for Bein & Fushi.
Percy Lee was a viola player and a maker of violins, violas, cellos, and viols, credited with approximately 50 instruments.

Korean-born violinist who studied violin making in Mittenwald and moved to the USA in 1970, working for the Kenneth Warren firm. Co-founder of the Chicago School of Violin Making (originally the Kenneth Warren and Son School) and since 2003 its Director-Emeritus. Output of over 300 instruments.

Andreas Carl Leeb was the first maker in Vienna to adopt the Stradivari model, preceding Geissenhof. Initially, he used dark varnish that later turned black; during his Viennese period, this varnish gradually became lighter, achieving a fine clear golden-brown color.
Together with Edgar Bishop , Albert Leeson produced some of the finest gold-mounted bows in the history of the firm of W.E. Hill & Sons .

Jean Baptiste Lefèbvre was active in Amsterdam from around 1726. He worked in two distinct styles: one followed the traditional Dutch manner, featuring the Arnatisé model with long corners and whalebone purfling; the other was more aligned with the Mirecourt tradition, characterized by low arches and short corners, using yellow and golden-brown varnish.
Toussaint Nicolas Germain Lefèvre was a violin and bow maker. He established his workshop in the Rue de Cimetière Saint-Jean and worked at 20 rue Saint-Antoine starting in 1813.
Charles Leggatt began working at the firm of W.E. Hill & Sons shortly after Sydney Yeoman , around 1885.

Alfred Leicht was a pupil of Emil Stark. He worked in Brunswick and for Mockel in Berlin. He established himself independently there in 1934, creating fine copies of classical models, including violins and bows. His work featured a manuscript label and was branded.

Rudolf Herbert Leicht was the brother and pupil of Max Leicht. He was active in Berlin and Hamburg. He established himself independently in Hohendorf. His violins were made in the Möckel style. He produced good bows, which were branded: R. H. LEICHT HAMBURG, R. HERBERT LEICHT.
Johann Christoph Leidolff was the son of Nicolaus Leidolff . He was appointed as a luthier to the Viennese court.

Work was rarely encountered. He made a large Amatisé or Stainer model that was neatly constructed. The materials were good, but the varnish was inconsistent: some had dark opaque coatings, while the best featured transparent red- or orange-brown tints. He also created small high-arched violas. Leidolff worked in Vienna in 1769. He was a Lauten und Geigenmacher in Wien 17..

Emile Leipp studied violin making with Carchereaux in Paris starting in 1932. He authored Cremona Varnish (Paris, 1946) and produced a small output, utilizing his own orange-brown varnish. His instruments were branded: 'E. Leipp'.

Alois Leja was a pupil of A. Ferenczy. He established himself independently in Vienna in 1928. He became a casualty of the Second World War. His instruments were based on the Stradivari model and featured a large scroll with red-orange spirit varnish.
Dino Lelli created personal designs. He demonstrated good workmanship with various shades of oil varnish.
Gabriel (Gábor) Lemböck. Pupil of Peter Teufelsdorfer and J.
E. Lemoine worked in Belgium, France, and Germany before establishing himself in Liège. He was a repairer and restorer, with few instruments known. Réparé le.. 19.. / Lemoine E.

Joseph Rudolf Lenhart emigrated to the USA in 1902. He created classical Italian and Stainer models in various grades, utilizing both spirit and oil varnishes. One eccentric design from 1869 featured soundholes cut in the center ribs instead of in the belly, with the treble foot of the bridge extending through the belly onto the back. He also varnished the interior.
According to Grillet, a pupil of Dominique Peccatte, although Millant sees no working connection. On military service 1848-1862 and stationed at Rennes, where he may have encountered Bonnel.

Johann Nicolaus Lentz was born in Tyrol, Austria. He was employed by Bernhard Fendt and worked in Duke Street, St James's, and Chelsea. Various styles of violins and cellos were made for dealers. The varnish had darkened considerably with age.
John Frederick Lentz was the younger brother of Johann Nicolaus Lentz. He created similar work. His address was No. 31 Lower Sloane Street, Chelsea, No. 3 ISL.
Eleuterio Leonardi was a pupil of Francesco Fabbri. He was credited with 100 violins and cellos. His work was fair.
Giovanni Leoni created a Stradivari-derived model. The work was unremarkable and featured red-brown varnish.

Guido Leoni trained as a cabinet maker in San Benedetto from 1927 and became active as a violin maker there in 1961. He was credited with 70 violins, 15 violas, and 2 cellos. His work featured decent craftsmanship with golden-orange or light brown varnish. A viola from his collection is housed at the Museum of Art in Ascoli Piceno.
Paolo Leonori was a cellist and luthier. He was credited with 40 violins, nine violas, eight cellos, three viols, and various other instruments.

Giovanni Francesco Leonporri was possibly of Tyrolean origin and worked in Milan, Rome, and L'Aquila during the mid-18th century. He created a small model featuring a slightly pinched arch, with inconsistent workmanship reminiscent of Testore and Landolphi.

Giuseppe Lepri was a pupil of Augusto Pollastri and was active in Sant'Arcangelo di Romagna from 1914. His instruments featured Stradivari, Pollastri, and personal models with fine golden varnish. He exhibited at Cremona in 1937 but later ceased making instruments to focus on research into wood.

Joseph Dominique Lété apprenticed to Moitessier. He worked for Gand in Paris and with J. B. Vuillaume from 1825 to 1828. He later established himself independently in Nantes. His work featured a slightly heavy style reminiscent of Gand and Vuillaume. He used classical models with fine materials and applied hard red or golden brown varnish, which was sometimes shaded and aged.
J. C. Letellier designed an octave violin of unusual shape, featuring a back length of 39 cm (153/8") and thick strings that offered a range an octave lower than that of the conventional instrument.

Edward Lewis was the presumed son and successor of Edward Lewis (I). Various addresses in London were provided, with Drury Lane being the only one that was easily decipherable on manuscript labels. He made Stainer-modelled violins. Sometimes, they were branded on the back button and on the table on the bass side of the tailpiece with `E L'.

Chicago-based firm, formerly one of the largest violin houses in the USA. Founded in 1874 by William Lewis (1835-1902) with partners Newell and Gibbs, and originally named Lewis, Newell & Gibbs, before being renamed the Chicago Music Company. The name was changed to William Lewis & Son when Frederick Lewis joined the firm. Bows were branded "Wm. Lewis & Son".
John Leyden credited with approximately 50 violins. The violins were based on Cremonese models and featured well varnished finishes. Manuscript labels were present.
Eric Johan Lidholm studied under E. A.

Johann Gottfried Liebich was a pupil of Sebastian Rauch in Wroclaw. He established himself independently there in 1789 and also made harps. His models were derived from Stradivari and Amati. He used fine materials and workmanship, with varnishes ranging from golden-brown to orange-red.
Gottfried Liebig created a slender model featuring extended middle bouts, a high arching, and a narrow edge. The materials used were of good quality, though the varnish was an opaque brown.
Johann Liebl created a Mittenwald style instrument using quality materials and a hard yellow varnish in 1823. He was a violinemaker in Griesbach.
Remerus Liessem was a maker of citterns and guitars, as well as some violins and cellos of trade quality. His work featured a Germanic style and utilized dark golden-yellow varnish.

Albert Lind established himself in Boston after gaining experience in Europe. He created 112 violins, along with some violas and cellos, using Stradivari, 'Grand Amati', and personal models. They were finely made.
David Lindsay was credited with around 40 violins of some distinction, including Stradivari and Guarneri models.
Michael H. Lindsay was a musician and self-taught violin maker, active from around 1860 in Stockton-on-Tees.

Lloyd Liu was a bow maker and jeweller in New York. He established a bow making factory in Old Bennington, Vermont. In 1975, he moved to Arlington, Vermont, where he produced 30 bows per week in various grades. The shop closed in 1977, and he retired from bow-making in 1980 following an accident. He then moved to Hong Kong.
George Herbert Lock was a professor of mathematics at Shrewsbury College and an amateur luthier. He was credited with 60 violins and three cellos.
John Logan credited with 50 violins based on Cremonese models. He used well-cut scrolls and fine yellow oil varnish.
Jacob Lomax is a pawnbroker and amateur luthier. His work is decent. He is from Bolton and was active in 1900, with the designation No. 31.
Giovanni Longiaru trained as a wood carver with Gottardo in Venice and studied violin making with G. V.
Important firm of London musical instrument agents active from around 1767–1790. It was founded by James Longman, then became Longman Lukey & Co.

Company of music dealers founded by James Longman. Their brand (used on the back beneath the top button) was 'Longman & Co.' between 1767–1769, then became 'Longman Lukey & Co.', then 'Longman, Lukey & Broderip' and finally 'Longman & Broderip'.
Firm of London musical agents established by James Longman, which used the brand 'Longman Lukey & Co' between 1769 and 1775. Later became Longman and Broderip.

Paul Lorange worked for Blanchard in Lyons, Gautie in Toulouse, and Mougenot in Brussels. He established himself independently in Lyons in 1899. His work included Stradivari, Guarneri, and particularly fine Amati models. He used very fine materials and various shades of red oil varnish with good consistency, sometimes shaded to imitate age.

Son of Paul Lorange (I). Worked in Marseilles with Diter. Established independently there from 1933. Fine workmanship on classical models with various shades of red varnish. Diter & Lorange, Luthiers / à Marseille, l'an 19.. Paul Lorange / Marseille 19.. Paul Lorange, luthier / à Marseille 19.. No..
Gaspar Lorenzini was probably a pupil of J. B.
Associated with B. S.

One of the more colorful characters in 19th-century British violin making, John Frederick Lott II followed in the footsteps of his father John Frederick I, who worked for Thomas Dodd , mostly building well-regarded double basses. John Frederick II began making violins and guitars for the London trade early in life before abandoning ... violin making in 1823.

Son of violin maker Georges Lotte , François Lotte probably apprenticed with Charles Nicolas Bazin in Mirecourt, where he is listed as an employee until 1921. From 1922 to 1925 he worked for Eugène ...

Georges Lotte was a violin maker who worked for J. B. Vuillaume and Derazey, contributing to the commercial work associated with the Vuillaume school. Many of his instruments were exported to England.

Roger François Lotte was a bow maker, the son, pupil, and assistant of Francois Lotte. He began making bows alongside his father around 1936 and initially adopted his father's elegant and traditional style, later developing a more modern and heavier approach.
Alfred J. Loughton copied instruments of Gasparo da Salò, Stradivari, and Guarneri, using red and yellow varnishes.

Jean Louvet was known as Louvet le jeune. The flat arch featured coarse workmanship and a dull yellow-brown varnish. It was branded: `J. Louvet / A Paris'. Printed labels included: Louvet à la Vielle / Royale, rue de la Croix des Petits / Champs, à côté de la porte / Saint-Honoré à Paris 1750 and Fait par Jean Louvet / à Paris. 1756.

Giuseppe Lucci was a pupil of Pietro Borghi and Leandro Bisiach. He created Cremonese models with fine workmanship and a good golden-red varnish. Credited with over 700 instruments, he was one of the premier Italian makers of the 20th century. He achieved 15 gold medals at competitions in Rome, Liege, Cremona, and other locations. His instruments were branded internally and beneath the endpin.
Berlin-based bow maker. Previously worked for Hieronymus Köstler in Stuttgart.

Otto Luderer was a pupil of E. Reinhold Schmidt. In 1910, he emigrated to the USA to work for Oscar and Ernst Schmidt in Cleveland, where he stayed until his retirement in 1975. His personal model featured red-orange spirit varnish. He primarily focused on repair work.
William H. Luff was an important and influential post-war violin maker in England.

Jacob O. Lundh emigrated to the USA in 1882. He settled in Aberdeen, South Dakota, before relocating to Minneapolis in 1910. He worked for Blakkestad Music Co. from 1912 to 1920. In 1927, he established Lundh & Rowe. His personal model featured some double-purfled designs and used hard oil and spirit varnish, with a color range from yellow to red. Over 400 instruments were completed.

François Lupot was the son and pupil of Laurent Lupot and was active in Luneville around 1746-1754. In 1754, he was appointed violin maker to the Duke of Württemberg in Stuttgart, a position he held until 1768.

Born in Orleans, François Lupot II was trained as a violin maker by his father, Francois I, and was the younger brother of the renowned violin maker Nicolas Lupot. It is unclear how he learnt bow making, but his early work shows a pronounced Tourte influence and he seems to have worked for Leonard Tourte in Paris.

Among the great French violin makers, Nicolas Lupot deserves special recognition for his talents. Known as the French Stradivari , Lupot trained with his father François in Orléans before moving to Paris in around 1796 and set up his business near the ... shop of François Pique on the rue de Gramont.

Gustav Lutschg was a violinist and violin maker. He was a pupil of Methfessel and worked with Mougenot in Brussels. He established himself in Zurich and later in Berne from 1892 to 1925. He was credited with 300 violins, known for their fine workmanship and red oil varnish. Gustav Lütschg / No. 310 fecit Bern& 1926. Gustav Lütschg / fecit Turici, 18..
Ignaz Anton Lutz was the son and pupil of Ignaz Lutz (I). Later, he worked for various workshops in Vienna, Berlin, Paris, and London before emigrating to the USA.
admin2_name: City and County of San Francisco

info: Louis Lyeki worked actively from 1906 for Johann Stowasser in Budapest before emigrating to the USA. He established himself in St.

Mario Maccaferri was a guitar virtuoso who worked in Sienna. He was primarily a guitar maker but received 2nd prize for a cello in Rome in 1927. He resided in New York from 1945 and possibly made some violins there.

Carl Mächler trained at Mittenwald under Kochendörfer. He was employed by Winterling in Hamburg, Kreiling in Munich, Niedt in Würzburg, Held in Cologne, Otto in Dusseldorf, Kessler in Mannheim, and later with Wahl in Karlsruhe and Zust in Zurich.

Cesare Maggiali was an amateur maker active in Fossola, Carrara from 1951, later establishing himself at 52 Corso Cavour, La Spezia. He received guidance from I. Sderci and worked with Stradivari and Guarneri models featuring golden-brown varnish. He exhibited at Cremona in 1937 and won a gold medal at Rome in 1954. He was credited with 150 violins, as well as violas and three cellos.

The best known maker of the Brescian school, Giovanni Paolo Maggini was born in Botticino and moved to Brescia around 1586 to train with Gasparo da Salò . After twenty years of work for da Salò, Maggini struck out on his own and began to develop his own models.

Stelio Maglia studied at the Cremona school, graduating in 1946. He exhibited in Cremona in 1949. His work included Stradivari, Guarneri, Maggini, and personal models. He established himself in Buenos Aires in 1952.

Gabriel Magniere cited one instrument dated 1890 and numbered 233. Another instrument, dated 1819, was cited by Vannes. It was possibly a name adopted by a mass manufacturer. He produced commercial work based on the Stradivari model using fine materials and red or red-brown varnish. It was branded: `Magniere'.
Frederick Daniel Mahoney worked for F. W. Chanot and Joseph Chanot. He made a few violas based on a design by General Pelly at Windsor Castle.
Etienne Maire was the son and pupil of E. Maire-Breton.

One of the great bow makers of the mid 19th century, Nicolas Rémy Maire was probably apprenticed to Étienne Pajeot . He set up his own establishment in Mirecourt in 1826 but went bankrupt during the French economic crisis of the 1830s.

Erminio Malagutti was a pupil of S. Scarampella. He established himself in Milan in 1938 and continued to work in his master's style. Many of his instruments were successfully exported to the USA. He received first prize at Bagnacavallo in 1976, as well as a gold medal for cello at Cremona in the same year.

Johann Stephan Maldoner created coarse and unattractive work, although it featured delicate scrolls. He used plain materials and ordinary brown varnish. Generally, cellos, double basses, and viols were more successful than violins. He worked in Füssen in the year 17..
François Alexandre Maline was a bow maker. He was the son and pupil of Jean Joseph Maline .

Guillaume Maline was the father of Nicolas Maline and a maker of violins and bows. His work featured a large Stradivari model, characterized by clean professional craftsmanship, quality materials, and a dull yellow-brown varnish. The instruments were branded internally with `Maline'.

Born in Mirecourt to family of violin- and bow makers, Nicolas Maline probably apprenticed with his father, Guillaume, and may have worked for the Pajeot workshop. In 1840 he started to make bows for J.B.
Nicolas Auguste Eugène Maline was a bow maker, the son, pupil, and assistant of Nicolas Maline. He served his apprenticeship with his father and assisted him from 1870.

Pierre Maline was a bow maker and the son of Francois Alexandre Maline. He worked for Cuniot-Hury in 1906 and for Feret-Marcotte starting in 1911. His work was of decent quality and belonged to the Bazin school. He branded his bows with the name: PIERRE MALINE.
Eugene Malteau recorded one cello by Vannes, featuring long corners and a dry yellow varnish that recalled the work of Mast.

Pietro Antonio Malvolti was possibly a pupil of Christofori. The model was derived from Stainer and exhibited the Florentine style. It featured fine delicate workmanship with good materials and a soft golden-yellow or brown varnish.
Camillo Mandelli was known as 'Camillo da Calco' and was a pupil of Bisiach and Antoniazzi. He established himself in Buenos Aires from 1889 to 1920 before returning to Calco.

Francesco Manfredi was a violinist and composer. He completed 90 instruments and introduced a personal model around 1926. A gold medal was awarded to him at Vignola in 1929. His work was signed internally.
Albert Michel Mangenot was the son and assistant of Paul Alexandre Mangenot. He was killed in World War I and was known for creating fine viola d'amores. His work was signed internally.

Amati Mangenot was the son and pupil of Paul Alexandre Mangenot and also trained with Laurent in Brussels. He established himself independently in Clermont-Ferrand in 1922 and moved to Bordeaux in 1925. His work featured classical Cremonese models and fine workmanship, using oil or amber varnish of a reddish shade on an old gold yellow background.
Apprenticed to Justin Derazey. Subsequently made cellos for Collin¬Mezin.
Pietro Mangiani described a violin featuring flat arching and golden-brown varnish. There was no other support for this maker; a fabricated label was possibly involved.
Gerard Mangin created cellos based on the Stradivari pattern and used red-brown varnish. They were branded in a triangular form: 'Gerard Mangin Mirecourt'.

Louis Mangin produced poor commercial work characterized by dull yellow-brown varnish and low-quality materials. His products were branded as `Mangin a Mirecourt', `Mangin a Paris', or 'Louis Mangin'.
Thomas Howells Mann created a violin noted by Woodcock, featuring a Stradivari model, yellow-brown varnish, and a particularly well-cut scroll. It was made in Bedford in 1909.

Pierre Mansuy established himself in Mirecourt, though he sometimes gave Paris as his place of work. He produced superior Stradivari copies in the Mirecourt style, featuring the characteristic dull yellow varnish typical of that region, but occasionally utilized an improved orange or red-brown recipe.

A student of Carlo Ferdinando Landolfi , Pietro Giovanni Mantegazza is considered the greatest maker of the Mantegazza family, which became linked to the great connoisseur and collector Count Cozio di Salabue in 1776. The relationship was a fortunate one for the family, as Cozio's patronage afforded them access to his extensive ... collection of fine instruments as well as steady repair work and commissions.
Guido Maraviglia created Classical Cremonese, Florentine, and personal models. He was a teacher of G. Natali. He worked in Pistoia and made his creations in the year 19..

Many violins, cellos, and guitars of ordinary workmanship were known, according to Henley. Vannes mentioned that the work was unknown, which remains true. The brand appeared below the button or inside: `Marchal' `Marchal a Paris'.
Eugène Marchand worked as a pupil of Durand and Laurent. He was employed by W.
Abbondio Marchetti produced a personal model that featured good professional workmanship and a fine red-brown varnish.
Edoardo Marchetti, son of Enrico Marchetti, briefly partnered with his brother Vittorio before establishing himself independently around 1902. He worked in association with R.

One of the finest Turinese makers of the 20th century, Enrico Marchetti moved to Turin in 1874 and apprenticed early on in the workshop of Benedetto Gioffredo ‘Rinaldi’ and later in the shop of Antonio Guadagnini before opening his own business in ... 1881. His early work showed great promise and was awarded numerous medals.
Giovanni Antonio Marchi moved to the city of Bologna around 1745 after the death of his father, a carpenter. He began making violins in 1755, likely as a pupil of Alessandro Fiorini.
Pupil and successor of Giuseppe Lucci in Rome.

The identity of violin maker Don Nicolo Amati has been the subject of considerable speculation for several hundred years. Thought to have been a nom d'etiquette of a violin making Bolognese priest with the surname Melchioni or Marchioni, the name 'D.

Giuseppe Marconcini, son of Luigi Aloisio Marconcini, produced fine work on the Amatisé model, utilizing hard clear orange or yellow-brown varnish. His purfling was made of ebony, and he generally used plain materials. He is reputed to have studied with Storioni.

Luigi Marconcini moved from Bologna to Ferrara around 1766. He created a large model derived from Cremonese designs. The arch was medium to low, featuring fine workmanship and materials. He also produced good cellos, which were made on a large model.

Custode Marcucci began his career as a farmer and cooper before training at the Institute of Art in Bottajo. He established his workshop in San Agata, Ravenna, where he worked alone and became a prolific maker, credited with over 300 violins, 600 cellos, and 49 double basses.

Little is known about the early life of Romano Marengo, but at some point in his youth he moved to Turin, where he was employed by his relative Enrico Marchetti . After Benedetto Gioffredo "Rinaldi's" death in 1888, Marengo took over his thriving shop ... and worked for Rinaldi's widow.

Antonio Mariani often received comparisons to Brescian work, which frequently featured double purfling and decorative purfling designs, although no direct connection could be established. The decorative inlays appeared rather crude and unimaginative.
Ludovico Mariani was presumed to be the son of Antonio Mariani. He created rustic work in Pesaro in 1694.

Albert Marissal was the son and pupil of Olivier Marissal. He was a violinist and luthier who received a diploma in The Hague in 1949. His work featured Stradivari and Guarneri models with red-brown varnish.
Olivier Marissal was the son and pupil of Auguste Marissal. He worked with Stradivari and Guarneri models and produced fine work using red-brown oil varnish. A manuscript label was present.
John Marnie was a weaver and gardener. He later became a pupil of J. Findlay in violin making. He created 40 violins based on the Guarneri model. His work was considered undistinguished.

John Marshall established his business in New Street, Covent Garden, possibly succeeding Benjamin Cooke, whose 'Golden Harp' enterprise was located there until shortly after 1745. Active in the 1750s-60s, Marshall is recognized as one of the best English interpreters of Stainer , producing instruments characterized by solid ... craftsmanship and a balanced approach compared to other Stainer copyists.
Biagio Caruana Marsigliese was a double bassist and violin maker who was active from 1905. He received gold medals at Padua, Rome, Florence, and Cremona.
E. Martin was a commercial bow maker. He used dark sticks and German nickel-silver mounts. His work was branded: E. MARTIN SACHSEN.
Bow maker / Violin maker (1883 – c. 1955)

Born in Mirecourt in 1837, Jean-Joseph Martin began his apprenticeship early to provide financial support to his family after his father's premature death. At 21, he moved to Paris to work for Jean Baptiste Vuillaume , lacking even the funds to travel there by coach.
Max Alfred Martin was the son, pupil, and assistant of Wilhelm Ernst Martin.

Otto Oswald Martin worked in various locations in Germany from 1884 to 1893. He emigrated to the USA in 1893 and established himself in Milwaukee, Wisconsin, the same year. He was primarily a repairer, using Stradivari and Guarneri models with gold-red varnish.
Paul Martin worked as the chief assistant for Jean Bauer in Angers starting in 1946. He was subsequently awarded medals at Cremona in 1982 and Kassel in 1983.

Marcello Martinenghi was the son and pupil of Stelio Martinenghi. He was active in Milan starting in 1933 and in Bassano del Grappa from 1950. He received awards in Florence, Padua, and Rome. In 1937, he exhibited in Cremona. His work was of fine professional quality, featuring a good transparent varnish with golden-yellow or brown coloration.
Oreste Martini was a pupil of Scarampella. He received medals at Cremona, Padua, and Paris.
Giuseppe Martino, originally from Messina, Sicily, emigrated to the USA in 1915. He was employed by O.

The model featured a large flat arch and exhibited undistinguished workmanship. It had a thick, hard spirit varnish that ranged from dark red to very dark brown, primarily due to oxidation. Although all of his work was produced in Mirecourt, the output was stamped with `Paris'. It was branded beneath the back button and internally as `V. L. Mast A Paris' or `L. Mast à Paris'.
Joseph Laurent Mast was the son and pupil of Jean Laurent Mast . He apprenticed to Didier Nicolas and established himself independently in Toulouse around 1800, on the rue des Balances.
Instruments were labelled from Paris and featured a large flat model with red-brown varnish.
Nicolas Mauchant produced commercial work in various grades and models. He created Maggini copies of notable distinction.

Charles Maucotel was a pupil of Blaise Mast. He was employed by Gand in Paris starting in 1834. From 1844 to 1860, he worked in London for Davis, then he retired to France. His work was careful on Cremonese models, and he used fine varnish in appropriate colours.

Charles Adolphe Maucotel worked for Vuillaume and was the brother of Charles Maucotel . He established his business in the Galerie Vivienne, Paris, in 1844, moved to Croix des Petits Champs in 1852, and finally settled in rue Princess.
Justin Maucotel created fine work based on classical Italian models, which included double-purfled Brescian types. He branded his work as 'Justin Maucotel Luthier. '.
Carolus Maurizi is the name used in commercial instruments imported and retailed by Beare and Son in London. He is based in Bologna, 19.
Francesco Maurizi created an Amatise model characterized by a poorly controlled outline, narrow edges, and short corners. The soundholes were rather constricted, particularly in the upper half.

Leonhard Maussiell's earliest work dated from around 1707 featured very fine craftsmanship on a model reminiscent of both Stainer and Tecchler. It had a deep edge flute with a medium high arch and a tightly wound scroll, which was sometimes substituted with carved lion's heads.

Andreas Ferdinand Mayr served as a luthier to the Archbishop's court. He created Stainer models that featured high arches and a scroll that was quite oval-shaped. The workmanship was fairly neat, and the soundholes were widely spaced and upright. He made Mozart's first violin.
Leo Mayr trained at Mittenwald under M. Klotz, H.

Born near Manchester, Walter Mayson was an and prolific eccentric maker who seems to have been self-taught.
