Despite the relative mediocrity of his work, he appears to have done reasonably well in the city of Manchester until the arrival of George Adolph Chanot , whose training far outclassed that of Mayson.

Contemporary maker based in Piacenza, Italy. Graduate of the Istituto Liutario A. Stradivari in Cremona; also studied with Bruce Carlson in Cremona. Worked for Massimo Negroni before opening his own workshop in Piacenza.
James McIntosh worked early on a high-arched model featuring painted purfling. After 1869, he adopted a more classical Cremonese model. He was credited with 200 violins, 40 cellos, and a few violas.
John McIntosh specialized in copies of J. B. Guadagnini. He used commercial varnish produced by F. S. Angell of Bristol.
William McNeill created a Guarneri model featuring a high arch and oil varnish. It was made in Edinburgh in 1891.
Jean-Jacques Meauchand was a bow maker. He was the son and pupil of Joseph Meuchand. From 1790, he was strongly influenced by Tourte.

Born in Naples in 1964, Mecatti studied at the International School of Violin Makers in Cremona under Vincenzo Bissolotti, graduating in 1986. He moved to Paris and worked with Pierre Barthel and Bernard Sabatier until 1990 before studying bow restoration with Jean and Pierre Grunberger.

Nicolas Médard was the son of Henri Médard (I). Instruments were labelled from both Paris and Nancy, although it remains uncertain if he ever worked in Paris; this became a common practice among later Mirecourt luthiers.

James Meek completed 80 violins and violas, utilizing classical Italian models and his own design. He employed oil varnish in various shades with quality materials. He was well regarded as a repairer. Carlisle, 1914.

Karl Meier trained in Luby (Schönbach). He worked at various workshops in Markneukirchen. He established himself independently in Luby from 1889 to 1933. A diploma was awarded to him in Eger in 1892. He created fine copies of classical Italian and Tyrolean models using good materials and decent spirit varnish. Karl Meier, jun. / Schönbach bei Eger / 1893.
Wilhelm Meier-Pauselius studied under August Diehl and Georg Winterling from 1910 to 1913. He worked for various makers in Germany, Italy, France, and Scandinavia.
Hans Meierl was a maker of violins and guitars. He was assisted by his son, Hans Meierl (II).

Friedrich Wilhelm Meinel was the son and pupil of Christian Friedrich Meinel (I). His work was similar to that of his father. He used a yellow-brown varnish and was branded: `Meinel'. A printed label was also present.

Paul Meinel was the brother of August Meinel (II). He apprenticed in Markneukirchen and was employed by N. E. Simoutre in Basle. He worked in various places in Germany but returned to Basle in 1889 to succeed to the Simoutre business.
Jakob Meinertzen was a maker to the court and a viol maker. He was credited with a few violins.
Walter Heinrich Meinhold was a pupil of E. Tauscher and established himself independently in 1948 in Erlbach. He created bowed and plucked instruments.
Friedrich August Meisel was a prolific maker known for a wide range of grades, from decent hand-finished work to rather crude commercial productions.
Friedrich Wilhelm Meisel engaged in coarse trade work. The soundholes were long and featured a poor opaque varnish.

Johann Georg Meisel created Stradivari-derived models. His rustic workmanship utilized fine materials. The varnish exhibited inconsistent quality, ranging from thin yellow-brown to an improved but somewhat opaque red-brown.
Henry Meissner was brought up in Germany but returned to the USA in 1925. He traveled with an evangelical choir.

The Melegari brothers Enrico Clodoveo and Pietro are unique figures in the history of violin making in Turin. Presumed to be self-taught, they worked independently from their contemporaries and their work has little in common with the Turin school.

The Melegari brothers Enrico Clodoveo and Pietro are unique figures in the history of violin making in Turin. Presumed to be self-taught, they worked independently from their contemporaries and their work has little in common with the Turin school.
Adolfo Melli was a pupil of Dante Guastalla. He demonstrated good workmanship with lighter shades of golden-orange varnish.

Johann Georg Melzl worked in Munich before he took over the business of G. A. Thumhardt in Straubing in 1830. He used a Stradivari-derived model and produced mediocre work with red-brown varnish. He also made guitars. Georg Melzl was a Saiten-Instrumentenmacher in Straubing in 1842. Joh. Georg Melzl was a Guitarren- und Geigenmacher in Straubing in 1831.
Charles Mennégand apprenticed in Mirecourt. He was a pupil of Rambeaux in Paris from 1840 to 1845.

Emile Mennesson worked in Paris before joining Martin and Joseph Guarini in Mirecourt, where he later established his own extensive workshop from 1876 to 1881. He took over the Guarini business and used the name as a professional trademark for high-quality commercial instruments.
Andrew Mentiply was active in Ladybank and Gateside, Fife, before moving to Glasgow in 1915. He apprenticed as a joiner and later worked on the railway.
Jules Merciolle was a pupil of Grillon and worked for Mangenot and Audinot in Mirecourt, later moving to Paris to work with G. Bernardel in 1899, and subsequently with Caressa and Francais.
Pietro Mercolini worked in various locations in southern Italy. He was a pupil and associate of Maurizi, using classical Cremonese models.

Antonio Merighi moved to Milan around 1803. He completed a significant amount of work for Count Cozio di Salabue until 1810 and is recorded by the Count as having possessed the notable 1707 Stradivari cello known as the `Countess of Stanlein'. His own creations were in the style of Rocca, though they were somewhat coarsely finished with a hard red varnish.

Luigi Merighi established himself professionally in 1976 as a self-taught maker. He demonstrated good craftsmanship on Cremonese models, using varnish supplied by Pellacani in golden-brown shades. A manuscript label was present.
Pietro Merighi made decent instruments with dark red varnish in Parma in the year 1794, known as leoni'.

Charles Meriotte trained in Mirecourt and was active in Lyon from approximately 1750 to 1763, after which he established himself in Paris. His work featured the old Mirecourt style with dull yellow-brown or red-yellow varnish. The workmanship was decent, with neat purfling and flat arching.

Joseph Merlin worked in Paris around 1750 before moving to London in 1760. There, he received a commission to construct a barrel-organ for the Princess of Wales and collaborated with a goldsmith in New Street, Covent Garden, near John Marshall 's workshop.
Pauli Merling apprenticed with Bojesen Petersen before relocating to Dresden to work for Rudolphe Heckel. He was later employed by Rummelhoff Hansen in Oslo and Emil Hjorth in Copenhagen.
Maurice Mermillot apprenticed to Gaillard in Mirecourt. He was employed by Buthod in Paris in 1852 and moved to Rennes in 1853.

Alfio Messina was active in Catania from around 1920. He was well-regarded as a teacher. His models were derived from Stradivari and featured dark golden-yellow varnish. They were branded, typically within a black circle: `A. M. H. ' The prefix 'J. M. J. ' on his labels represented 'Jesus, Mary and Joseph'.

Pietro Messori studied under Giuseppe Sgarbi starting in 1893. He worked for Fiorini in Munich before returning to Modena. He received numerous medals and honors in Milan, Paris, Turin, St Louis (USA), and Geneva. His work featured Stradivari, Guarneri, and Amati models, showcasing good workmanship and a red to golden-yellow amber varnish.
Josef Metelka was the son and pupil of Josef Metelka (I). His professional workmanship was very fine, utilizing excellent materials and varnish. He made violins, violas, cellos, and double basses.
Gustave Methfessel apprenticed to P. Schulz in Regensburg and worked for Lembock in Vienna before establishing himself independently in Berne from 1864, later relocating to Hilterfingen.

Enzo Meucci was a pupil of G. Arcellaschi. He was active in Como until 1977 and then worked in Fino Mornasco. He was both a maker and restorer, known for his fine transparent yellow-orange varnish. Fece in Como - Anno 19..
Joseph Meyer was the maker of the Alemannische school. He was the son of Theus Meyer and a pupil of his step-father Adam Kirner.

Magnus Andreas Meyer was possibly a pupil of Joachim Tielke. He created highly decorated viols and also produced violins on a large broad model featuring yellow or yellow-brown varnish. His cellos were high-arched, with some including carved female heads.

Reinhardt Meyer began making violins in 1888. He established himself in Worcester, Massachusetts as a violinist and maker from 1918 to 1926. He took over the business of Samuel Parsons. About 300 violins were modeled on the 1714 Stradivari 'Dolphin' violin, and occasionally on Guarneri patterns. Wurlitzer varnish was used. A few violas were also recorded.

Alessandro Mezzadri was a musician and violin maker, recognized as the founder of the Ferrara school of violin making. His style was individual and loosely modeled on the Amati, though it varied, with some instruments also reflecting Maggini influences.
Commercial brand for late 20th century Mirecourt violins.
Johann (Janos) Michelberger worked as a pupil of Andreas Bergmann. He collaborated with Mihaly Reményi, Karl Otto Zimmer , and Robert Laumann .

Jacques-Pierre Michelot established his business at the sign 'A la Mélodie' on rue St-Honoré. He was primarily a maker of guitars and lutes. A printed advertisement from 1791 also mentioned quintons and violins.
Plinio Michetti was a pupil of E. Peluzzi in Genoa and possibly acquainted with Candi and De Barbieri.
Otto Migge established a violin making school in Koblenz in 1880, known as Atelier für Cremoneser Geigenbaukunst. He worked in London starting in 1896 and moved to Eastbourne, Sussex in 1908.

George Miles was a self-taught British maker based in London, known for producing around 90 instruments during his lifetime. His early work on the Stainer model was eccentric and poorly accomplished, but he later developed a more satisfactory Stradivari pattern, utilizing his own red-brown varnish.
Jean-Jacques Millant was a bow maker and the son of Roger Millant . He apprenticed with Morizot Freres in Mirecourt from 1946 to 1948 before returning to his father's workshop in Paris.
Max Millant was the brother of Roger Millant . He was the grandson and pupil of S.
Roger Millant was the brother of Max Millant . He was a grandson and pupil of S.
Alexander Millar was a hairdresser and violin maker. He was a pupil of Thomas Hardie. His violins and cellos were crafted in the Vuillaume style based on the Stradivari model.

Norman Miller was a self-taught violin maker starting in 1956. He completed nine violins and three violas, with several based on a 1742 Guarneri violin model and later on a personal pattern. They were well made using imported tonewood and commercial varnish provided by Harris. The interior work utilized native Australian 'quondong' wood.
Samuel Jansen Miller established himself professionally in Dryden from 1884. He was self-taught by reading Heron Allen's Violin Making as it was and is.

George E. Millis was a carpenter and a self-taught violin maker. He moved to Cleveland in 1889 and was employed by the Eastman Violin Shop. He created about 200 instruments based on Stradivari and Guarneri models. His hard varnish was of his own formulation, and he also made several bows.

Patrick Gordon Milne was the son of Peter Milne and established himself in Aberdeen in 1896, initially at 11 Windmill Brae, then moving to Back Street after 1898, and later to Guild Street after 1812. In 1920, he relocated to Glasgow.
Frank Milner was a violinist and amateur maker known for good workmanship. One example he created, named 'The Flame, ' was made from root wood sycamore featuring complex grain and figure.

Louis Frank Milton established himself in Bedford in 1923. He was a pupil of A. L. Scholes and was credited with creating over 50 violins based on a Guarneri-derived model. His work featured good professional craftsmanship and materials, along with a red brown varnish.

Active from around 1897, he was a musician and a self-taught violin maker. He completed 130 violins, eight violas, four cellos, and three double basses, all based on Cremonese models. His work was characterized by good craftsmanship and a pale golden-brown varnish.
Contemporary maker in Ravenna, Italy. Trained with Renato Scrollavezza in Parma and has worked with other makers including Horacio Pineiro and Rene Morel.
Son of Paul Emile Miquel.

Paul Émile Miquel was a bow maker. He was the son and pupil of Nicolas, a luthier and organ builder located on Faubourg Saint-Vincent, and he established himself independently at 5 rue de Pre de Paradis around 1870.
Claude Augustin Miremont was a violin and bow maker. He was the son and pupil of Sebastien Miremont.
Max Möckel was the son and pupil of Oswald Möckel . He worked in Warsaw starting in 1897, then in Moscow, and around 1899-1902 in St.

Oswald Möckel studied under Carl Grimm and Christian Adam, later establishing his own shop in Berlin in 1869. He was known for his classical models, which featured distinctive personal developments and fine red oil varnish.
Otto Möckel, son and pupil of Oswald Möckel, also trained with Carl Grimm. He worked in London for Richard Bruckner from 1890 to 1892 before rejoining his father in Berlin.

Giuseppe Modaudo worked for the Carabba Brothers in Catania starting in 1894. He emigrated to the USA in 1905 and established himself in New York, later moving to Chicago. In 1949, he returned to Rome. He produced over 500 instruments of good trade quality, including Cremonese and Brescian models, some featuring an 'antique' finish and others inlaid and decorated on the back.
William Moennig apprenticed to his uncle Adolf in Budapest, Hungary. He moved to the USA in 1896 to study with his brother-in-law Julius Guetter in Philadelphia.

William Hermann Moennig, son and pupil of William Heinrich Moennig, studied violin making with Paul Dorfel and bow making with Paul Heberlein in Markneukirchen, and later attended the Mittenwald School of Violin Making. In 1936, he became the first American to obtain his Master's certificate.
Last of the Moennig line. Headed the family business in Philadelphia from 2004 until it closed in 2010.

Alberto Fernando Moglie completed his first instrument in 1908 after studying under Antonio Sgarbi from 1920 to 1907. He worked for mandolin maker Luigi Enbergher, and later for Rudolpho Fredi and Giuseppe Rossi .
Charles Moinel was the son and pupil of François Moinel . He also trained with Emile Germain.

Charles Amedée Moinel was a bow maker and the son of François Xavier. He trained in Mirecourt with L. Bazin from 1908 to 1911. Afterward, he returned to Paris to work alongside his brother Émile. He became a casualty of the First World War. His work, which is rare, was in the style of Bazin. He branded his bows as MOINEL-CHERPITEL PARIS or MOINEL-CHERPITEL-PARIS.
Daniel Moinel was the son and pupil of Charles Moinel . He apprenticed with his uncle L.
Louis Moitessier was the son of Prosper Antoine Moitessier . He was a prolific maker known for large models.

Prosper Antoine Moitessier was primarily an organ builder. The Musical Instruments Museum in Brussels housed one violin labeled: P. A. Moitessier No. 13, / à Montpellier, 1833. A repair label indicated it was racammodè par Moitessier, à Carcassonne.

Angelo Molia was a pupil of Bernardo Calcani, likely serving as an assistant towards the end of Calcani's life, although there is no documentary evidence of this connection. His work, characterized by a somewhat rustic finish, focused on small models derived from Stradivari.
Giuseppe Molinari was presumed to be the son of Antonio Molinari. He was known solely for plucked instruments, including a mandolin located in the Musee de la Musique, Paris, which was labelled.
Guillaume Max Möller was the son, pupil, and assistant of Paul Max Moller. He trained at Mittenwald and was employed by A.

Paul Max Möller was born in Adorf and studied in Markneukirchen. He worked in the Nurnberger shop from the age of 14, moved to St Petersburg to work for Max Möckel, then joined Karel van der Meer in Amsterdam, where in 1913 he opened his own workshop. He was a prolific worker, who made both violins and bows, and employed many assistants in his shop.
Pierre Mondy created a violin with fine wood and yellow varnish, which was cited by Vannes. The manuscript label indicated the year 1828.

John King Monk produced decent work derived from Stradivari. His small output included some instruments afflicted by 'innovative' bass bar arrangements and other concepts. They featured fine materials and an oil varnish that ranged from pale yellow to golden-red tint.
August Hermann Mönnig was a fine bow maker. He was the son and successor of Johann Monnig.

Fritz Mönnig worked for A. Hollinger in Kiel, G. Winterling in Hamburg, and Hammig in Berlin as a pupil of M. Schmidt. He established himself independently in Markneukirchen in 1912. In 1913, he became a Master of the luthiers guild. He made fine copies of classical models and was a highly regarded repairer and restorer.

Domenico Montagnana arrived in Venice in around 1701 at the age of 15, shortly after Matteo Gofriller had established himself as the leading light in the emerging Venetian school. However, it seems that Montagnana apprenticed not with Goffriller but with Matteo Sellas, who later also employed Carlo ...
Atillio Montanari studied under R. Ronchini. He emigrated to Toronto, Canada in 1930. His work featured a soft golden-brown varnish.

Luigi Montanari worked for L. Bisiach and was a pupil of R. Antoniazzi. His professional work focused on Stradivari models, reflecting the style of Antoniazzi. Only cellos were recorded. The varnish was a good clear golden-orange.
Erminio Montefiori settled in Genoa in 1856. He trained as a sculptor and became a self-taught violin and bow maker starting in 1883, located at 4/4 vico S.
Armando Monterumici was a musician and a pupil of R. Fiorini.

Luigi Montevecchi trained as a cabinet-maker at the Bologna Academy of Fine Arts. He developed a personal model based on Cremonese forms. His workmanship was fine and accurate, and he created a notable varnish of his own manufacture. He was a highly regarded maker and restorer of the 20th century Emilia Romagna school. Montevecchi operated at Contrada Aldini N. 1 in Cesena.

Antonio Carlo Monzino trained at Markneukirchen, Germany, and later in Mirecourt, France. From 1929, he taught in Italy with the help of employees from the company, A. Sironi, A. Perego, P. Parravicini, and L. Galimberti. He imported Sartory bows in collaboration with his uncle C. Garlandini, who became part of the business in 1926, leading to the name `Monzino e Garlandini'.
Milan family firm of bowed and plucked instruments.
British maker based on the Isle of Man, active around the turn of the 20th century.

Arnaldo Morano was a significant maker, restorer, and connoisseur from Italy in the 20th century. Initially self-taught, he exhibited at Cremona in 1937 and received a gold medal at Cremona in 1949. In the latter part of his life, he renewed his focus on crafting new violins, utilizing Cremonese models and applying fine rich orange-red varnish.

Paolo Morara was an artist and musician. He trained as a violin maker under G. Fiorini. His output was small but well crafted and finished, and he made several cellos. He exhibited at Cremona in 1937 and received a bronze medal there in 1949.

Gio Battista Morassi was born in 1934 outside Udine and after showing early promise entered the International School of Violin Making in Cremona. He attended the school from 1951 to 1955 under the tutelage of Giuseppe Ornati and Ferdinando Garimberti , ... and after his military service returned to take up a career educating some of the best twentieth century makers from Italy and abroad.

Cited in most dictionaries, with speculation that he was the pupil of the lute maker Pietro Darden Two labels are quoted from violins or fragments in the Brussels Musical Instruments Museum (ex Snoeck) with Mantua as origin, and from P. De Wit giving Venice, but both are dubious.

Carlo Moretti was credited with 200 violins, violas, and cellos. He produced fine work featuring golden-red varnish. Carolus Moretti Anconitanus / Fecit Anconæ Anno Domini 19.. Carolus Moretti Anconitanus / Fecit Roma Anno Domini 19..

Egidio Moretti trained as a cabinet maker. He was an active luthier from around 1924 and worked in Lavagna, Genoa from 1945, but returned to Udine after 1950. He created classical Cremonese models that were well crafted and featured soft lustrous yellow or deep red varnish.

Felice Mori Costa's distinctive workmanship featured an elegantly slender design, characterized by long soundholes and low-set nicks cut in an unusual manner, reminiscent of Guadagnini. While his choice of materials could be plain, he often applied a fine varnish that varied from dark golden-yellow to plum-colored.

Alfred Moritz was a commercial manufacturer in Dresden. He sold through agents and was especially popular in London under the title 'Excelsior. ' His work was decent and featured a pale varnish. Sometimes, various names were inscribed on the back of the pegbox. He produced a copy of Antonius Straduarius / Fecit Dresdae. Anno 1889 / Expecially made for..

André Auguste Morizot specialized in cambering bow sticks for the joint output of the family workshop Morizot Frères, where he was the son of Louis Joseph Morizot. He also crafted top grade bows featuring silver or gold mounts.

The five sons of prolific and successful Mirecourt bowmaker Louis Morizot père all trained with their father and worked in partnership. They each specialized in different types of bows or aspects of the bow making process, enabling them to produce an impressive number of bows with assembly-line efficiency.

Louis Gabriel Morizot was a bow maker and the son of Louis Joseph Morizot. He was known as 'Le Pompier' and primarily focused on crafting double bass bows and frogs for the family business, Morizot Frères.

Louis Joseph Morizot, or Morizot père, learned bow making from Eugène Cuniot-Hury and Charles Nicolas Bazin , for whom he worked from about 1901 to about 1914. Morizot then began working for Eugène ...
Marcel Louis Morizot was the son of Louis Joseph Morizot and was known as 'le petit vieux'. He prepared rough bow sticks in the family workshop, Morizot Frères.
René Morizot was the sixth son of Louis Morizot and the last of the dynasty, known as 'Le Gamin'. He was a violin maker and a pupil of Audinot, who was a popular and influential teacher.

Nicolas Morlot was a pupil of Nicolas Morlot aine. He created a large Stradivari model that featured flat arches and was finished with pale brown or yellow varnish. His work was not sophisticated. The name remained in use in Mirecourt until around 1900. It was branded internally: 'A la ville de Cremonae Nicolas Morlot. '.

Georgy Alexeyevich Morozov studied under D. P. Tomashev in Moscow from 1911 to 1918. He worked for the Moscow Conservatoire and the Bolshoi Ballet. He received awards in Russian competitions in 1926 and 1933. Three violins, a viola, and a cello were included in the Russian State Collection in Moscow.

Born in Biloxi, MS on October 26, 1953 and studied classical piano at Cornish College of the Arts. After his career as a violin maker in Florida, especially praised for his violas, he died on January 19, 2018, in Tucson, AZ. Also went by his mother’s last name of Robèrt.
Archibald Morrison established himself as a musician and violin maker in Glasgow from 1854. He worked for John Mann starting in 1860 and became independent in 1865.

Robert Morrow’s bow shop is located in Port Townsend, Washington, where he completed an apprenticeship with Charles Epsey. Examples of his work have won five Gold Medals from the VSA and an Hors Concours status.
John William Mortimer created a small model with his own varnish, which was quite successful. His double basses held particular value. Cardiff. 19..
Leon Mortin succeeded Vuillaume-Darte in 1891. The model was Stradivarian and featured chestnut red varnish.
Apprenticed to G. Deroux from 1855.

Léon Mougenot first learned violin making under Émile Laurent , and later apprenticed with his father's cousin Georges in Brussels from 1887 to 1894. Mougenot returned to France, working in Lyon and Paris and later at the London firm of W.E. ...
Frederick G. Moverley made violins based on the Guarneri pattern and violas following the Terns model. He used good varnish and materials.
David Mowbray was active in Melrose from around 1878 and moved to Leith in 1890. He produced good work based on Cremonese models.
Hidalgo Moya trained in Mirecourt, France. He established himself in Aylestone around 1890 and continued until he was stricken with ill health in 1920.

Luigi Mozzani established a workshop for guitar making in Cento in 1910 and began making violins there as well from 1917. He followed a Guadagnini model for violins, violas, and cellos, using dark red varnish.
Antonio Mucchi was thought to be a pupil of Soliani. He created several good cellos in the style of Rocca and Pressenda, using yellow oil varnish.
Albert Carl Muller was a violin and bow maker who studied under Louis Prince starting in 1938 and later trained with Abraham Koodlach. He worked for Sherman & Clay and Emil Herrmann .
Carl Albert Müller was the grandson and pupil of Christian Friedrich Müller (II). He worked for M. Ficker and M. Schmidt before establishing himself independently in Markneukirchen.

Hans Muller was the son of Otto Müller. He studied under Nürnberger in Markneukirchen and later worked for Kotovice in Poland, Stowasser in Graz, Voss in Charlottenburg, and G. Ullmann in Berlin. He established himself independently in his birthplace, Leipzig, around 1934. Manuscript label.
Heinrich (Harry) Müller studied under O. Windisch from 1920 to 1923 and later with K.

Josef Müller was a violin and bow maker, the son and pupil of Andreas Müller, a wind instrument maker and dealer. He distinguished himself as a flautist and as a maker and repairer of brass and woodwind instruments, taking over his father's shop in 1873.
Ernest Mumby was a pupil of Whitmarsh. He used good workmanship and materials for his personal model.
Romedio Muncher worked for Cavalli's 'Monteverdi' workshop. He later established himself independently.

Alexander Murdoch established himself in Aberdeen by 1865, at 9 Minster Lane until around 1900. He later formed a partnership with Alexander Dingwall, creating Murdoch and Dingwall at the Market Gallery in Aberdeen.
Daniel C. Murray completed about 60 violins. The varnish featured various colors and was branded internally.
David Murray was a violinist and violin maker who had previously worked as a miner. He was credited with over 100 instruments.
James Murray was a railway engine driver and an amateur violin maker. He was credited with approximately 30 violins featuring various models and mediocre yellow oil varnish.

Umberto Muschietti was a double bass player and maker, credited with 160 violins, 18 violas, eight cellos, and two double basses. One of his basses, a replica of his own five-string Santagiuliana instrument, is now housed in the Museo Stradivariano in Cremona. He received medals at Fiume, Rome, Padua, and Florence. His work was signed internally as 'Umberto Muschietti. '.
Johann Muschke was a pupil of Jan Krampera. He established himself in Frankfurt in 1867. Instruments made by him were well crafted and appealing.

Cestmir Musil was the son of Jan Musil and created highly finished copies of classical Italian instruments. The work featured fine materials with spirit varnish and lightly rubbed edges. His brand was 'C. Musil / Praha'. A printed label indicated: Zhotovil / Cestmir Musil, housaf / V Praze, rok 1939. There was also a manuscript label: Cestmir Musil / Praha 1962.

Giuseppe Mussolesi was a pupil and assistant of C. Colombo. He established himself independently in 1941. His work featured Stradivari and Amati models with pale golden colored varnish and included manuscript labels.
Jean-Baptiste Léonidas Nadegini worked with F. Spidlen in Moscow.

Giuseppe Nadotti possibly gained experience with G. B. Guadagnini and was certainly involved with his affairs, showing a notable stylistic connection. The instruments were well modeled with flat arches and Stradivarian proportions, and the soundholes were set close to the edge. Materials were generally quite plain, but the varnish was of good golden-yellow or red-brown quality.
Jean Théodore Namy worked for Salomon's widow starting in 1772. He established himself independently in place du Louvre in 1788.
Frank Donald Napier apprenticed at W. E. Hill & Sons, where he created the 'Hill' and 'H. & S. ' model student bows. After 1918, he left Hills and worked as a civil service engineer.
William Napier joined the firm of W.E. Hill & Sons in 1889 after apprenticing as a pianomaker with the Broadwood firm.
Contemporary Italian maker based in Pistoia. Studied with Mauro Scartabelli and Ovidio Giarelli.
John H. Nebeker was a bow maker and violin repairer.

Fred Nebel was the son and pupil of Andrew Nebel and also studied with his uncle Martin, as well as in Mittenwald, Germany. He worked in Mittenwald for Johann Bader and collaborated with Michael Dötsch and Theodor Schrage in Berlin.

Trained in Germany, he moved to America in 1922 and worked at the Wurlitzer firm from 1922-1924. He then moved to Philadelphia to establish his own shop. He later returned to Mittenwald after 1947 and employed over 40 violins makers in his workshop.

Martin Nebel graduated from the Mittenwald School in 1910. He worked for Johann Stiibiger in Vienna and for Eugen Gärtner in Stuttgart. In 1927, he emigrated to the USA to work for Wurlitzer in New York. From 1928, he established a business in Philadelphia, Pennsylvania, with his brother Johannes. He was an expert restorer and maker.
Joseph Neff trained as a piano maker in Germany. He emigrated to the USA in 1840, initially settling in New York before establishing himself as a piano maker in Philadelphia, Pennsylvania.
Gilles Nehr was born in the south west region of France. He started studying bow-making at the age of 17 with his cousin, Jean-Pascal Nehr, in Marseille, and graduated from Mirecourt at age 20.

Brother of Jean-Pascal Nehr and apprenticed with him from 1995.Graduated from Mirecourt in 1998 and opened his workshop in Paris in 2002. Moved near Aix-en-Provence in 2024.Received various prizes in international competitions including the VSA 2008 & 2012, Concours Vatelot 2011, Mirecourt 2023, and a Gold medal for a cello bow in Mittenwald 2010.

Stefan W. Nemes was a descendant of Samuel Nemessanyi. He inherited his tools and patterns. He studied under Robert Laumann. He established himself independently in Budapest in 1930. Following the Soviet invasion in 1957, he emigrated to London, where he worked as head repairer and restorer for Cyril Woodcock.

Leading Hungarian violin maker Samuel Nemessanyi apprenticed with Thomas Zach and Johann Baptist Schweitzer in Pest before moving in 1858 to Prague, where he worked for Anton Sitt, gaining first-hand experience with fine Italian violins. Two years later ... he returned to Hungary and worked in various towns before settling in Pest, where he lived through multiple marriages, many children, financial tumult, and emotional instability, all the while enjoying success as the city's best maker and restorer until his premature death in 1881.
Alfred Neudörfer was the son of František Neudöfer. He produced finely finished work, including Guarneri copies made with excellent materials and transparent spirit varnish.

Rudolf Neudörfer grew up in the Czech Republic and went to the Violin Making School in Luby, learning the craft of violin making from his teachers Bohumil Pechar and Hans Schmirler. He then worked at the Cremona cooperation for string instruments there until he escaped socialist Czechia by posing as a ski coach at the Olympic Games in 1964.
Johann Christoph Neumarker created one violin with red-brown varnish, as cited by Vannes & Henley.
Joseph Neuner created a cello with amber-brown varnish, which was dated 1806 and cited by Vannes.
Son and pupil of Johann Neuner (III). Studied the cello.
Mathias Neuner was the son of Johann Neuner and used the spelling 'Neiner' on early labels. A prolific maker, he exported his work throughout Europe and employed many craftsmen.
Mathias Neuner continued the firm of Neuner & Hornsteiner. He was the grandson of Mathias Neuner (IV).
Brand used for a bowmaker named Neudorfer who made bows for the Finkel workshop.
Brand used for bowmaker Rudolf Neudorfer who made bows for the Finkel workshop.

Charles Neveu established his business in the rue Manton-Devenest from 1893 to 1905. He subsequently abandoned lutherie to become a commercial trader in instruments. His offerings included Stradivari, Guarneri, and Amati models with good oil varnish.

Utili Nicola was initially a maker of plucked instruments. He later developed an eccentric violin design featuring inverted corners. He received first prize at Turin 1928 for a quartet. His work was finely finished with a flat arch, utilizing very good oil varnish and excellent materials.
Antoine Nicolas was noted by the Principal of the Luthiers' Vannes as having 'work unknown, ' but Henley provided an unverifiable description of low-quality work. A label was supplied by De Wit.
Known as 'le Sourd' (`the deaf one'). Son of Antoine Nicolas .
François Fourrier (François II) Nicolas was the son and pupil of François Nicolas (I). He was employed by E.

Joseph Nicolas was the son and pupil of Didier Nicolas and succeeded his father in 1816, continuing his work with little noticeable differentiation. After his death, the rights to the triangular label 'A la Ville de Cremonne, ' used by Didier, were purchased by J. Derazy, who continued production. A fine cello by him is located in the museum of Hamburg. It was branded: `J. Nicolas Fils'.
Karl Niedt was a pupil of E. F.
Simpert Niggel was the son-in-law of Johann Ott and the most important maker of this family. He demonstrated fine craftsmanship on a model derived from Stainer.
Vahakn Y. Nigogosian was an active violin maker from 1925.
Gottfried Nilsson was the son, pupil, assistant, and successor of Nils Nilsson. He continued the tradition of fine instruments and restoration established by his father.

William Nisbet was a self-taught craftsman who made approximately 120 violins. His early work concentrated on Maggini models, later shifting to an Amati-derived pattern. He used broad purfling and applied spirit varnish in various shades. In 1885, he received a bronze medal at the Edinburgh Exhibition. His name was inscribed internally.

Louis Noebe was a violinist and violin maker. He created Stradivari models with decent workmanship and varnish in various suitable colors. He developed his own 'induction bass bar, ' which was related to his patented 'System Noebe' construction theory; this term was found branded internally.

Floriano Nofri was a violin and bow maker, active from approximately 1948 to 1982. He received some assistance from G. Pasqualini. He completed around 300 violins based on Stradivari models, using spirit varnish in orange or brown hues. From 1975, he focused on bow making, finishing over 3, 000 bows for various types of bowed instruments.
Contemporary Italian maker trained in Cremona. Worked for Giorgio Scolari before setting up independently in Cremona. Has won various medals for his work.
Giuseppe Nonini was a self-taught maker. He received diplomas in Trieste in 1931 and in Friuli in 1948. His work featured Stradivari models with golden-yellow varnish.

John Norborn was found in the works of other makers, particularly Jacob Ford. He was assumed to be a general music dealer. His creations typically consisted of ordinary English Stainer copies with brown varnish. He operated in London in 1764.
Heinz Nord was a pupil of M. Schuster in Markneukirchen and K. Sandherr in Bielefeld, whose business he subsequently took over. He created classical and personal models and received several awards.

The late-17th-century viol maker Barak Norman was one of the most important figures in the transition to violin-family instruments in the English tradition. His earliest known viol dates from 1689, and by 1692 he had set up a workshop in St.

John Norris was a pupil of Thomas Smith and a business partner of Robert Barnes, who was also a pupil of Smith, from 1765 to 1794 in Great Windmill Street. After their business partnership dissolved, he relocated to 35 Coventry Street, where Richard and William Davis continued his shop.
Václav Nosek was reported by Henley to be the best maker in the region during that period. He used fine materials and his own oil varnish.
John Note was brought to the USA with his family in 1890. He was employed by Gabriel Francois in Pittsburg and was a highly regarded repairer and restorer.

Natale Novelli was a pupil and nephew of G. Pedrazzini. He worked in Codogno following Pedrazzini's death in 1957. His work included Cremonese models as well as Pedrazzini's personal model. He exhibited at Cremona in 1949. His craftsmanship and finish were of high quality, featuring gold or red varnish. He was a highly regarded restorer.

Pietro Valentino (Marco) Novello apprenticed to Giorgio Serafin in the workshop known as 'Alla Cremona' around 1773 and remained there after Giorgio's death in 1779, when A. Bellosio took over the business.

Adolf Nürnberger was a bow maker and the nephew and pupil of Johann Christoph Nürnberger. He worked in Dresden alongside J. C. Süss and C. F. Knopf, and he contributed to the development of bows for Weichold. He established himself in Markneukirchen in 1875.

Throughout the late 19th and 20th centuries, the Nürnberger family workshop produced a great number of high-quality bows, many on the firm's signature Tourte model. The traditional family brand, which went into use around 1920, is "*Albert Nürnberger*" and was used by various makers of the family, including Franz ...

Carl Albert Nürnberger built on his father Franz Albert Nürnberger Jr. 's success to bring his family's long tradition of bow making to its peak in the 1920s. He apprenticed in the family workshop Markneukirchen, where he gained the skills required to produce the firm's signature Tourte -model ... bows.
Son of Karl Albert and currently the last of the Nürnberger dynasty of makers. Uses the family brand "*Albert Nürnberger*".

Franz Albert Nürnberger was the son and pupil of Christian Gottlob Nürnberger. He became a founding member of the Markneukirchen bow makers guild in 1888. His distinctive work featured a later Tourte model with a relatively rounded frog, and his craftsmanship influenced the shops of R. Weichold in Dresden and W. H. Hammig in Leipzig. He was also the father of Franz Albert Junior.

Fifth in a line of successful German bow makers, Franz Albert Nurnberger Jr gradually developed his family's model into one of the most convincing interpretations of François Xavier Tourte in modern bow making. He began branding his bows around 1890 and inherited the workshop in 1894.
Johann Christoph Nürnberger worked for five years with J. B. Vuillaume in Paris. He is not part of the Markneukirchen family.
Karl Albert Nürnberger was a bow maker and the son and assistant of Karl Albert Nürnberger (I). He was also the father of Christian Albert.
Fl. c. 1920. Commercial violins
Emigrated to USA in 1912. Originally from Schönlind (not one of the Markneukirchen family of bow makers).
Terrence O'Laughlin worked as a shoemaker in Connecticut. He was active as a violin maker in Boston starting in 1895 and was credited with several hundred violins.

Although Carlo Giuseppe Oddone apprenticed for several years at the Benedetto Gioffredo Rinaldi workshop, his more formative training took place in England, where he traveled to work for Frederick William Chanot in 1889. In light of Chanot's background ... in the French tradition, Oddone's experience in his workshop gave him an entirely different perspective on methods of construction and repair than he would have absorbed in Turin.

Son of a farmer who dabbed in woodworking, Giuseppe Odoardi first became acquainted with violin making after being called upon to repair an instrument in his rural hometown outside Ascoli Piceno. While his clients were mostly local at first, his skills quickly made him known to professional musicians passing through the region, who turned to him for repair work and also commissioned instruments from ... him.

The Cremona workshop 'Officina Claudio Monteverdi' was established by the maker Aristide Cavalli and his son Lelio in 1880. It employed many pupils and produced trade instruments of a lower quality than Cavalli's own personal ones.
Violins were made for him by Albert Lind, using the Stradivari model and featuring good varnish and materials.
Christian Olivier established a partnership in Paris with Paul Bisch to sell instruments. He was not a maker himself.

J. Olry was a pupil of Georges Chanot. He established himself independently at 49 rue des Trois Cailloux, Amiens, in 1832. The model was based on Stradivari, but the workmanship was inconsistent. He used fine red-brown varnish and good materials.

Lars Jorgen Rudolph Olsen apprenticed to Hjorth & Sons in Copenhagen from 1904 to 1909. He worked for Otto Möckel in Dresden from 1909 to 1913 and briefly in Berlin and Amsterdam for Edward Vandermeer and Max Möller, where he studied bow making.

Anton Olson was a violin and bow maker. He emigrated to the USA in 1903 and established a violin business in Worcester, Massachusetts in 1926. His work included classical models, as well as the Klotz violin pattern and the Brescian viola pattern. He used brown oil varnish and completed 70 instruments along with several bows.

James Omond was a clock maker and amateur luthier. He was active from 1872 in Stromness, Orkney, before later relocating to Kirbuster. He produced over 300 violins, violas, and cellos based on Stradivari and Guarneri models. His work was fairly crude.

Walter Opel apprenticed to Max Heischkel in Erlbach. He worked for Lüdemann in Cologne and for Storck in Strasburg. In 1925, he settled in Szczecin (Stettin), Poland, taking over the business of Adolf Pfretzschner. He later established himself in Weimar. His instruments featured a Stradivari model with fine golden-yellow varnish. Geigenbauer Walter Opel operated in Stettin in 19..
Imre Orban studied under G. Várady at the Hungarian State Violin Making school around 1922. He demonstrated good professional workmanship and applied fine varnish.

Archimede Orlandini exhibited personal models in Cremona in 1937. He received a certificate of honour in Cremona in 1949, a silver medal in Cremona in 1971, and a silver medal for varnish in Bagnocavallo in 1980.
Giuseppe Ornati was the son of Carlo, an amateur luthier. He studied under C.
Enrico Orselli was a pupil and assistant of Soffritti. He established himself independently in Pesaro from 1924.

Mariano Ortega was the son and pupil of Silverio Ortega , learning his craft alongside his father in the 1800s. His work featured a fine and tasteful style based on Amatisé or Stradivari-derived models, characterized by well-proportioned designs and the use of good materials.

Silverio Ortega was a pupil of Dom Vicenzo Ascensio and succeeded him as pithier to the Spanish court. He was unfortunately best known for his involvement in the cutting down of the 'Spanish Bass', the Stradivari cello made for the court in 1696.

William Frederick Oskay studied under Harry Stoutenburgh from 1957 to 1959. He established himself in Kingston, New York, in 1959 and moved to Port Ewen in 1961. He was professionally engaged in repair work and completed eight violins, two violas, and two cellos, all made using Stradivari and Guarneri models. He utilized commercial varnish and local materials.

George A. Ostertag apprenticed to Franz Kerschensteiner. He worked in England, Scotland, and Hamburg before relocating to the USA in 1898. He was employed by the Pepin Perfect Tone Violin Co. in Chicago. Later, he established himself independently on Van Buren Street from 1915 to 1922. He returned to Germany around 1923 and was chiefly occupied with repairs, with no new work recorded.

Anton Ostler studied at the Mittenwald School of Violin Making starting in 1908. He worked in Munich, Nancy, and Saarbrucken. From 1918, he established himself independently in Mittenwald. His work featured fine materials and orange oil varnish.
Joseph Ott was born in Langendorf in Mähren in 185..

Carl August Otto was the son and pupil of Jacob August. He established himself in Ludwigslust, Mecklenburg, in 1832. He served as a luthier to the Mecklenburg-Schwerin court. His work included some high-quality pieces, but also a significant amount of commercial quality. The varnish he used was dark, thin, and opaque.
Carl Wilhelm Friedrich Otto was the son and pupil of Jacob August Otto. He also trained with his eldest brother, George August.
Georg August Gottfried Otto was the eldest son, pupil, and successor of Jacob August Otto. He produced fine work using good varnish and materials.
Louis Otto was the son and pupil of Johann Karl August Otto. From 1865 to 1866, he worked with his cousin Ludwig in Cologne.
Ludwig Otto was the son and pupil of Georg August Gottfried Otto . He worked in Erfurt starting in 1850, in Cologne from 1855, and in St.

Rudi Otto was the son and pupil of Paul Max Otto and apprenticed to R. Fenzel. Later, he worked for Kessler in Berlin, Trautmann in Braunschweig, and Fischer in Bremen. He established himself in Erfurt from 1946, producing good professional work based on classical Italian models and fine restoration.

Wilhelm Otto was the son, pupil, and successor of Louis Otto. After serving an apprenticeship with Simoutre in Paris, he returned to Düsseldorf in 1900 and took over his father's business in 1920. He produced fine work on Stradivari and Guarneri models using good oil varnish. He was highly regarded as a restorer.
Son of Emile Auguste Ouchard, he began making at 13. At the end of 1941 he moved to Paris with his father.

Émile François Ouchard was apprenticed to Eugène Cuniot-Hury and took over the Mirecourt firm after Cuniot's death in 1910. Ouchard's son Émile Auguste began to assist him as early as 1913, and in 1923 Ouchard 'père' set up his own firm.

Bowmaker Émile Auguste Ouchard first studied with his father Émile François at the Mirecourt shop of Cuniot-Hury and began to assist him around 1913. Father and son continued to work together after Émile Francois opened his own shop in 1923, and ... Émile Auguste began to produce increasingly well-crafted work in collaboration with his father.
Jean Ouvrard was a pupil of Claude Pierray . He served as master of the violin makers guild from 1742 to 1743.

Ivy Rimmer Owen was the daughter and assistant of John William Owen. She trained as a violinist and studied wood carving with a Mr. Hamilton from Leeds before joining her father's workshop. Her work was fine and accurate on Stradivari models.

John William Owen was a trained engineer and a self-taught violin maker active from 1884. He was credited with approximately 200 violins, violas, cellos, and double basses, along with many bows, all made at his home, "Amati House. " His good oil varnish, of his own formula, ranged from yellow to deep red. A printed label featured his name within the outline of a soundhole.
Contemporary British bow maker based in Oxford. Winner of various awards.

Michel Pacherele was the son of Jacob Pacherele. Although one label indicated Paris, as cited by Vidal, he was recorded as having lived continuously in Mirecourt during his active years. His instruments featured a style reminiscent of Guersan, characterized by high arches and a clear yellow-orange varnish. His name was branded on the back button.

Pierre Pacherele was probably trained in his hometown of Mirecourt, though information about his early life is scant. In the 1820s he travelled to Italy, and spent some time in Turin, where he probably met Giovanni Francesco Pressenda .

Carl Padewet was the brother of Johann Padewet (I). He established himself in Munich in 1856. He received medals at the Munich Exhibition. His instruments featured Stradivari models, with a well-developed structure and good finish. They utilized fine materials and varnish. Reparirt / Carl Padewet / München was noted in 1862. Carl Padewet / München was located at Rindermarkt No. 20 in 1869.
Johann Padewet was the son and pupil of Johann Padewet (I) and also completed an apprenticeship with A. Riechers in Hanover.

Johann Karl Padewet was the son, pupil, and successor of Johann Padewet (II). He also studied under F. Weller and L. Doelling in Markneukirchen. Later, he worked with G. Fiorini in Munich and G. Winterling in Hamburg. He succeeded to the family business in 1901. His work was similar to that of his father.
Giuseppe Secondo Paganini worked briefly in London. He established himself in Florence in 1907.

Luigi Paganini was active in Faenza and Forli from around 1856 to 1903, before moving to Florence. He produced copies of classical Cremonese work and had a limited output. He received awards at competitions in Nice, Marseilles, Toulon, Paris, Munich, and Ghent. He also worked as a repairer and restorer.
Aldo Pagliacci was a self-taught maker. He created copies of Landolfi using red-brown or orange varnish.
Thomas D. Paine created a large personal model featuring a truncated volute and a pointed chin to the pegbox.

The son of Louis Simon Pajeot , Étienne Pajeot learned the trade early in the family workshop. Pajeot's brand first appears around 1815, and his early bows are very much in his father's style, with rather pointed heads and low, elongated frogs.

Louis Simon Pajeot was a bow maker who was active from around 1780. He also had some violins of trade quality attributed to him. He apprenticed in Mirecourt and produced various grades of work, typically with octagonal sticks. His work was rarely branded, with the inscription `PAJEOT AMT.
Giovanni Battista Pallaver studied under A. Bisiach and R. Capsoni in Milan starting in 1952. He produced fine work on classical models and used red-amber varnish.

Pietro Pallotta was active in Turin and Perugia in the late 18th to early 19th centuries. He created personal models characterized by full arching, medium height, and strong edge flute, with long soundholes that were widely set and occasionally slanted.

Edward Pamphilon was one of numerous members of his family who supposedly made violins in and around Essex in the 1600s. Historical evidence indicates that Pamphilon worked roughly from 1660 to 1700, likely transporting his instruments to London for sale in various shops.

Giovanni Battista Panizzi was a violinist and a self-taught maker of violins and bows. He utilized Stradivari and Guarneri models, employing fine materials and craftsmanship. His work featured a good golden-red varnish. He created in San Remo, 19..

George Panormo was the third son of Sicilian-born violin maker Vincenzo Panormo and worked with his younger brother Louis in Bloomsbury, London, starting in 1848. He also briefly worked for the Banks brothers in Liverpool, as indicated by a cello dated 1736 that bore his label.
Son of George Panormo and nephew of Louis Panormo. Took over Louis's instrument making business around 1855 and ran it until his death. Instruments are labeled 'G. L. Panormo'.

Son of Vincenzo Panormo , Joseph Panormo is believed to have been born in Naples and worked for his father alongside his brothers, George and Louis , in various locations. By 1801 ... he was working independently in his own shop in London, where he made guitars, violins and cellos of good craftsmanship and strong personality.
Bow maker / Violin maker (c. 1784 – 1862)

Vincenzo Panormo was assisted by his sons Joseph, George and Louis. Joseph's hand is apparent in several instruments labeled Vincenzo, some of which are signed 'J.P.' or 'Joseph Panormo' on the top block.

Born Vincenzo Trusiano in Palermo, Sicily, Panormo changed his surname after moving with his family to Naples in around 1759. From Naples he appears to have traveled to Paris via Marseille, arriving in Paris around 1770.

Silvio Vezio Paoletti was a pupil of V. de Zorzi in Florence. He established himself independently in 1925, taking over the shop of P. Ballarini. His work featured a personal model with a fine golden-yellow varnish. Fece in Firenze. 19..

Luigi Paolini was a prolific maker, active in Rimini and Todi. He worked with Rocca, Amati, and Stainer models. A medal was awarded to him at Padua. He estimated having made 30 violins, three violas, four cellos, and two double basses, all featuring golden-yellow spirit varnish. He also created plucked instruments.
Jean Baptiste Paquotte was the nephew of Sébastien Paquotte. He apprenticed with Simon as a bow maker and later studied violin making with Sébastian in Paris from 1841 to 1849.
Placide Paquotte was the son, pupil, and successor of Jean-Baptiste Paquotte. He was a fine craftsman, excelling as both a maker and a restorer. He died in a bicycle accident.

Rudolfo Paralupi was active as a guitar maker in Rome from 1922 and made his first violin in 1927. He received awards in Rome in 1929 and in Parma in 1933. He was appointed as luthier to the conservatoires in Florence and Rome. Further medals were awarded to him in Cremona in 1949. He was credited with 200 violins and 1, 000 guitars.
Gaetano Pareschi created classical Cremonese and personal models. He used golden- or red-brown varnish. He was credited with 200 violins, 20 violas, 20 cellos, and three double basses.
Rémy Parizot was active in Nantes from 1774. His workmanship was ordinary, but the arching and varnish were good.

One of the most accomplished and mysterious British violin makers, Daniel Parker seems to have been a contemporary of John Hare and may have studied with Barak Norman . He probably did much of his work for those two makers, in addition to other London shops, but there is no evidence that he served a formal apprenticeship or ever ... set up on his own.

John Parkinson was a coal miner and amateur luthier. He worked in collaboration with his brother-in-law, Fisher. The instrument featured light brown varnish and was unpurfled. It was branded internally. A printed label was included.

Romolo Parmeggiani was a violinist and a maker of violins and bows, known for his Stradivari model. He received awards in Fiume and Cremona in 1937, as well as in Rome in 1952 and 1954, and also in New York. His bows were branded with his name.
Léon Paroche was the name given in commercial Mirecourt instruments sold in London by Beare & Son. Modèle / Billancourt / B&SL Anno..
Piero Parravicini worked as a pupil of R. Antoniazzi.
Christian Franz Partl worked with coarse materials and dull brown varnish. Some instruments were unpurfled and lacked linings or blocks. There were reports of some cellos having flat backs.

Ignaz Christian Partl was the son of Michael Andreas Partl. He created a neatly made Stainer model featuring a high arch with small edges and corners. The scroll was neat and precise, characterized by a broad pegbox and a deep open throat. His instruments were noted for their very dark, thin, and opaque varnish
Frank Passa was a violin and bow maker and a pupil of S. F.

Domenico Pasta was the son of Bartolomeo Pasta and worked alongside his brother Gaetano in the workshop called 'Alla Pallada'. The models were inspired by Amati and Maggini, showcasing convincing workmanship. He was rarely identified.

Gaetano Pasta was the son of Bartolomeo Pasta and was active in Brescia from around 1694. His work featured exuberant classical forms along the lines of Amati and Rogeri, characterized by impetuous craftsmanship.
Brussels-based bow maker. Period bow copies.
Benjamin Patocka was a violin and bow maker. He was a pupil of Josef Metelka in Paseky in 1879.
Jan Bohuslav (Johannes Godefridus) Pauli was the son of Antonín Pauli. His work was unremarkable and featured a transparent yellow varnish.

Joseph Pauli was a pupil of J. G. Thir in Vienna. He established himself in Linz from 1805. His instruments featured a high-arched Stainer model with a somewhat extended soundhole. The scroll was Stradivarian, adorned with a nicely concentric volute. The varnish was dark, thin, and quite opaque.
Carl A. Paulsen was the son and pupil of P.
Peter Christian Paulsen emigrated to the USA in 1882. He was a pupil of P.

Albin Ludwig Paulus was a pupil of Dolling. He established himself independently around 1890 and was assisted by his son Kurt. His work featured Cremonese models and included very good commercial pieces with oil varnish.
Johann Georg Paulus was a luthier to the Pmssian Court. His work was fine but lacked distinction in any way. He produced good bows, with the frog inlaid with a mother of pearl lyre.

Konrad Friedrich Paulus was a pupil of his brother Albin Ludwig Paulus. He worked for J. Pater in Karlsruhe, J. Hermann in Frankfurt, and L. Otto in Düsseldorf. He established himself independently in Markneukirchen from 1903. He was a maker of plucked and bowed instruments.
Otto Paul Paulus studied under his brother Albin Paulus. He worked for Tieteman in Wolfenbütttel and established himself independently in Markneukirchen starting in 1904.

Antonio Pazarini was a very rare Genoese maker of the 18th century who worked in association with Bernardo Calcani. Surviving instruments suggest that they produced instruments together, often labeled 'Antonius Pazarinius et Calcanius / Genuae 1740.' Pazarini's models were large, featured a medium high arching, and had an orange varnish

William Pearce was the brother or father of George. He was an employee of Forster. His work was fair in the Forster style and featured red-brown varnish. A manuscript label indicated: Made by W. Pearce / at Forster's. 1838.

Son of François Peccatte and nephew of Dominique , Charles Peccatte was born into a family of great bow making pedigree. Following his father's death in 1851, he began his apprenticeship in 1862 to his stepfather Auguste ...
Dominique Peccatte was born in Mirecourt and at first was apprenticed to a hairdresser. On the recommendation of Nicolas Vuillaume he moved to Paris in 1826 to join J.B.

Younger brother of Dominique Peccatte , François Peccatte began his apprenticeship near Mirecourt but left to join his brother in Paris sometime around 1840. Dominique was a formative influence on his brother's work, which features square, strong heads and is of good quality.

Vasco Pecchini was a violin and bow maker. He studied under E. Arassi in Milan. His work featured Stradivari and Guarneri models, utilizing fine materials and golden-red varnish. He exhibited in Cremona in 1937.

Costanzo Pedicino was an artist and a self-taught luthier. He created both Stradivari and personal models. His varnish, which was both oil and spirit-based, had a clear orange-red color. He received a gold medal at Baveno in 1988.

The son of a carpenter, Pedrazzini was born in the village of Pizzeghettone near Cremona. In 1903 he moved to Milan where he appears to have taught himself violin making, with some advice from Romeo Antoniazzi .
Antonio Pedrinelli was a prolific maker known for models and copies of many classical Italian makers. His work was produced in various grades, featuring different levels of finish and materials.
Antonio Pelizon was presumably the son of Francesco Pelizon. His work was impetuous and not dissimilar to Landoll Arching, being of medium height but rather box-like.
Giuseppe Francesco Pelizon worked with the bow maker V. Schilling. He was chiefly occupied with repairs, although several instruments were held in the Gorizia museum. They were signed internally.
Giuseppe Pellacani was a violinist and a pupil in lutherie of N. Bianchi in Nice and E.
Giuseppe Peluzzi was active for some time in Turin. He produced very fine and clever work on Cappa and Rocca models.
Penzel was part of a family of luthiers and bow makers who worked in Vogtland, Germany, and Bohemia, Czech Republic.
Gustav Penzel was a bow maker of some distinction. He used good pernambuco wood and branded both octagonal and round sticks with the mark: PENZEL.

P. Robert Penzel was a pupil of O. Schiinemann from 1892 to 1898. He worked in England and France before returning to Markneukirchen in 1904. He created very fine copies of the 1716 Stradivari 'Messie' violin, as well as Guarneri models.
Ignaz Penzl was presumably connected to the Penzel family. He engaged in commercial work on an Amati/Stainer-derived model.
François Pérain used hard orange-red varnish and fine materials. It was branded on the back: 'françois perain'.
Sergio Peresson was active from 1943. He moved to Caracas, Venezuela in 1947 and was employed as a repairer for the Venezuelan Symphony Orchestra.

Eugène Perrard apprenticed to L. Mougenot. He worked for Caressa & Français and L. Bernardel in Paris, as well as for Dykes in London. He established himself in Sannois (Seine-et-Oise) in 1928. His workmanship was very fine, featuring a personal model and excellent transparent golden-yellow or brilliant red varnish. He was a highly esteemed restorer and repairer, branded `E. P. '.
Étienne Perrin created typical Mirecourt work featuring a flat arch and thin spirit varnish. It was branded: `E. Perrin & Fils a Mirecourt'.

François Perrin created typical commercial work of the time and place. It featured low arching with a decent golden-orange or brown varnish. Vannes cited a cello made with plain materials and spirit varnish. It was branded: 'Francois Perrin'.

James Perry was the younger brother of Thomas Perry (II). He produced coarse work, some of which was unpurfled. The materials used were poor, and the varnish was generally a dark brown, with occasional lighter shades. He was credited with creating 300 violins, violas, and cellos.
Stephen Perry created models based on Guarneri and Brescian designs. Approximately 12 were made using local materials. The varnish had a crackled texture. A manuscript label was included.

Thomas Perry was thought to have been a descendant of Claude Pierray of Paris. He established himself in Christ Church Yard from 1741 and was recorded as 'Mr Pierrie, Fiddle Maker' at the time of his death. His work is presently unknown. His son, Thomas Perry II, took over his shop in Dublin around 1766

Thomas Perry was an Irishman by birth, but is thought to have been related to the French violin maker Claude Pierray . He followed in the footsteps of his father, also a violin maker, and took over his shop in Dublin around 1766.

A general examination of his style suggests the influence of Nicolas Lupot , but it is not known for certain where Jean Pierre Marie Persoit learned bow making or who initially employed him. Around 1825 he began to mark some of his bows with a characteristic "P.R.S.," which explains why his name was misspelled as "Persois" for ... so long.

Jago Peternella, originally from Legnano near Verona, emigrated to the USA in 1934. He is known for his fine craftsmanship and materials, reflecting the style of Giulio Degani, with whom he is thought to have trained in Venice.
William L. Peters created classical Italian models featuring distinctive edge work. The varnish was orange-brown. He possibly used pre-cut necks. He was credited with more than 100 violins.
Zenon Wladimir Petesh made six violins around 1935. He was a pupil of Didtchenko in Warsaw, Poland from 1937 to 1940.
Emilio Petraglia was a pupil in Buenos Aires of Giovanni Capalbo . He was appointed luthier to the Argentine government in 1937.

Jakob Petz was the nephew of Franz Petz and created fine work on typical Tyrolean models. The long form featured arching of medium height. A small volute adorned the rather broad pegbox. Short sound-holes had rounded wings. He used plain materials and applied a decent brown or yellow-brown varnish.

Marianus Petz was the successor to J. F. Leidolf in Vienna in 1760. He established himself independently from 1775. The instrument featured a high-arched Stainer model with a long pegbox topped by a small, neatly carved, and concentric volute. It had a very dark, thin, and opaque varnish.
Franz Paul Petzold worked in Gablenz in 1885 and established himself in Chemnitz from 1888. He produced good professional work with spirit varnish. He was a pupil of G. Roth.
Hermann Petzold was the son and pupil of Ernst Hermann Petzold. He worked in Nuremberg, Magdeburg, and Gera in 1931, and in Berlin from 1934.

Ernesto Pevere was a pupil of E. Soffriti. He was active in Ferrara from around 1920 and was credited with 400 violins, 10 violas, and 20 cellos. His work featured classical Cremonese models and exhibited fine workmanship with golden-brown varnish.
Alfred Peyrot was a professional cellist and a self-taught amateur maker and repairer. He may have been influenced by J. Pico, who was active in Algiers around the same time.
William V. Pezzoni worked in the violin department at the C. G. Conn factory in Elkhart.

Friedrich August Pfab apprenticed in Klingenthal. He worked in Hamburg starting in 1844 and received several medals. His instruments followed the Stradivari model and exhibited professional workmanship and finish. J. Hempel succeeded him in business in 1902.
Berthold Walter Pfretzschner was a bow maker. He was the son, pupil, and assistant of Hermann Pfretzschner.
Carl Friedrich Pfretzschner was admitted to the guild of violin makers in 1765. He was a distinguished maker of the Saxon school and a prolific craftsman.

Carl Friedrich Pfretzschner worked for Bausch in Leipzig in 1863. He established himself in Markneukirchen from 1871 to 1886. His models were derived from Amati and Stradivari. He produced good professional work using fine materials, often featuring one-piece backs. His varnish was golden-yellow.

Carl Gottlob Pfretzschner was the son and pupil of Christoph Gottfried Pfretzschner. He apprenticed in Dresden and worked in Hamburg before returning to Markneukirchen around 1830. He was one of the best makers in the Pfretzschner family.

Without question one of the most important figures in the history of German bow making, Hermann Richard Pfretzschner initially studied with his father Carl Richard before working for one year in the shop of J.B. Vuillaume in Paris, in 1874.

Johann Gottfried Pfretzschner was the son of Johann Elias Pfretzschner (I). He was admitted to the Markneukirchen guild of luthiers in 1751. His labels described his work as copies of Stainer, although the workmanship was indistinct. Other labels indicated Cremona as the origin.

Johann Gottlob Pfretzschner created instruments with good workmanship on an elongated Stainer model. The high arch featured a characteristically Saxon head with an exaggerated tail behind the eye of the volute. He used a golden-brown varnish. Towards the end of his life, he was credited with some more developed instruments that resembled those of Stradivari.
Richard Hermann Pfretzschner was a bow maker. He was the son and pupil of Hermann Richard Pfretzschner. He succeeded to his business in 1921.

Edward Phillipson served as an Evangelical Minister at Aspatria from 1890 to 1925. He was a self-taught maker of violins and bows, utilizing Stradivari models. His amateur work featured a golden yellow oil varnish. Bows were crafted in the Tubbs pattern. E. Phillipson / No. 19. 1920.
Worked c. 1894. Label is branded to the back, with the date handwritten.
Gasparo Piattellini was the father or brother of Luigi Piatellini (I). He created fine work based on the Gabrielli/Amati model, featuring a medium arch and excellent red-brown varnish.

Luigi Piattellini was a pupil of G. B. Gabrielli. A fine three-stringed double bass attributed to him resided in the Cherubini Museum, Florence. It featured a Gabrielli style, utilizing plain materials and a light orange-brown varnish.

Armando Piccagliani produced 600 instruments in total, including violins, violas, cellos, and double basses. He exhibited a viola and cello at Cremona in 1937 and received awards at Genoa in 1922 and at Rome. His work featured Stradivari and Guarneri models and demonstrated fine craftsmanship.

Handel (Andrew) Pickard established a music shop at 127 Kirk Gate, Leeds, using the name 'Handel' for business purposes. He was likely a pupil or assistant of Dearlove. Known for violins, cellos, and basses, his work featured good workmanship and materials, with a flat arch and a pleasing orange spirit.

Active since 1880, he was awarded first prize at the Augsburg Exhibition in 1886. He was succeeded in business by O. Ebner in 1906. His fine work included Stradivari and Guarneri models, and he was known for good golden-brown varnish. He also produced cellos and various plucked instruments. He authored Die Schwäbischen Geigenbauer vom Jahre 1600 bis auf unsere Zeit (Leipzig, 1895).

Marie Louis Piernot was a bow maker. He studied under C. Bazin and worked for Vigneron and Bernardel before establishing himself independently in Paris in 1923. His workshop was located in Parmain, Eine-et-Oise. He was highly regarded.

Claude Pierray established himself in the rue des Fossés, Saint-Germain-des-Prés from 1725, further identified as `proche la Comédie Français'. He was the most influential maker of the 'vie= Paris' school, working with a form very similar to that of Amati.
Jean Etienne Pierre created a highly arched original model that featured brown varnish. It was rarely encountered. The back button was branded with `I. E. P. '.

Antonín Pilar apprenticed to Benjamin Patocka in Jicin. He worked for Cermak in Kutna Hora (Kuttenberg) in 1899 and for Stowasser in Graz in 1900, then spent eight years in Berlin working with Möckel.

Apprenticed to his uncle Antonín Pilar (I), in Berlin. Worked for O. Spidlen in Prague, 1919-1921, and J. Vedral in The Hague 1922-1924. Established independently in Kradni Králové from 1924. Fine accurate work on classical Italian models. Excellent materials. Karel Pilar houslar / v Kradni Králové / Roku 1927 (set within a soundhole shaped cartouche).
Paul (Pál) Pilát studied under T. Zach in Vienna from 1872 to 1877.
Paul Ernst (Pál Ernö) Pilat was the son and pupil of Paul Pilat (I). He emigrated to the USA in 1921, initially working for Frederick Markert in New York before establishing his own workshop in 1932.

François Pillement was known as 'Tillement père', as some authorities believed that 'Jean François Pillement' was his son, although this appears not to be accurate; 'Jean' and 'Jean François' referred to the same person. He produced many fine violins, cellos, and double basses, which were branded and labelled variously as 'Mirecourt', 'Paris', and 'Cremona', yet all were made in Mirecourt.
Alphonse Pillyser created Stradivari and Guarneri copies. His work featured fine craftsmanship with rich red varnish. He was a highly regarded restorer and repairer.
Joseph Pineau was a pupil of A. Tolbecque starting in 1896.

Born in Buenos Aires Piñeiro began working on string instruments at the age of fourteen under Franco Ponzo. He later worked for Henry Viret in Argentina and Pierre Gaggini in Nice.In 1977, he emigrated to America and came to work for Jacques Français.
J. Pinel produced work characterized by narrow edges and a transparent varnish in shades of orange-brown or red. It was branded: `Pinel à Chaumont 1873'.
Harold A. Pinney was a pupil of George Granger. He made approximately 500 instruments based on Stradivari and Stainer models. His own varnish was orange, and he also used commercial oil varnishes.

A contemporary and friend of Nicolas Lupot , François Pique was born in Roissy Ile de France, near Paris. It is unclear where he learned violin making, but by 1787 he had moved to Paris and set up his own workshop.

Ivo Pires was active in the USA from 1967. He was employed as a repairer by the Carl Fischer Co. in Boston. He subsequently worked for Boston String Instruments, where he made modern and historical bowed and plucked instruments. After the death of its proprietor Melvin Peabody, he took over the company.

Enrico Piretti was a maker of various types of bowed and plucked instruments. His workmanship was noted for its quality. The violins he created were rather plain and featured orange-red spirit varnish. He made instruments in Bologna in the year 1949. In the same year, he won the Primo Premio at the Concorso Internazionale di Cremona.
Claude Pirot established his workshop in the rue des Grands Augustins. He created fine Stradivari models, some featuring slightly raised arches; the best examples were comparable to those of Lupot.
Giovanni Pistucci was a pupil of V. Postiglione and was active from 1885.
Carlo Pizzamiglio trained as a cabinetmaker. He was a self-taught violin maker starting in 1932.
Mario Pizzolini was a pupil of G. Peluzzi. He was a prolific maker of instruments and bows, known for classical models featuring transparent golden-yellow varnish.
Johann Franz Placht was the son and pupil of Franz Placht (I). He created similar work featuring flat arches, one-piece backs, and deep orange varnish.
Walter Plain apprenticed as a cabinet maker in Edinburgh. He possibly trained under his neighbor in Brunswick Street, Robert Ballantyne , whose work resembled his own.
Alberto Platner was presumed to have been of Tyrolean origin. He was first recorded with the Dominicus family in Rome, who were makers of plucked instruments.

Initially thought to have been a student of fellow Roman David Tecchler Domenico Michele Platner apprenticed with his father in Rome in the early 18th century before taking over the family workshop. Little is known about his working life, but he is known to have trained his nephew Giulio ...
Emil Pliverics worked as a pupil of A. Setzer in Budapest.
Charles Plumerel worked for Daniel in Marseilles. The relationship to Charles Plumerel (II) below remained unclear.

Jean Plumerel worked in Mirecourt, producing ordinary instruments with a plain yellow varnish. Violins and cellos are extant. Although made in Mirecourt, they were generally branded: `Plumerel á Paris'.
Jan Podesva was a houslar in Brno in the year 19.. He worked with Stradivari and Guarneri models.
Timofey Filippovich Podgorny was a self-taught maker and repairer. He established himself professionally in Krasnodar (formerly Yekaterinodar) from 1900.
Frantisek Podlaha created models based on Stradivari and Guarneri. He worked in Prague in the year 19..

Probably the most renowned of Giuseppe Fiorini's students, Ansaldo Poggi owed his introduction to his teacher to the friendship between Fiorini and Poggi's father, an amateur violin maker. After learning the basics at home, Poggi moved to Zurich to train with Fiorini and then returned to Bologna several years later. ... />The impact of Fiorini's style is especially evident in Poggi's first independent work.
Dominique Poirson was a bow maker who trained in Paris with Nicolas Maire starting in 1865. He worked for J.

Elophe Poirson completed around 300 instruments (violins, violas, and cellos) between 1876 and 1918. His work was of good quality and featured his own varnish. He received a bronze medal in Paris in 1889. Some instruments were branded beneath the back button with the name 'Poirson'.
Louis Poirson modeled his work after Stradivarius. The work and varnish were of decent quality.

admin2_name: Provincia di Monza e della Brianza

info: Enrico Politi was the son, pupil, and assistant of Eugenio Politi in Rome. After his father's death in 1909, he worked with his brother Fernando before relocating to Turin.

Eugenio Politi was a pupil of E. Ceruti. He later established himself in Rome and became an important and influential maker, assisted by his sons Enrico and Fernando. His work was strongly influenced by Ceruti and featured fine workmanship and materials, along with a clear orange-red varnish.

Raul Politi was the son and pupil of Enrico Politi and also trained as a violinist. He created over 150 instruments based on the Stradivari model. His varnish was a clear red-orange, similar to that of his father. Additionally, he worked as a restorer and repairer.

Edwin Pollard was active in Blackpool from 1862. He generally used the Stradivari model, although he made 12 violins on Stainer forms between 1910 and 1912. His workmanship was good, and he utilized his own golden-brown amber varnish. He also made cellos. He created this in 1924.

One of the finest twentieth century Italian violin makers, Augusto Pollastri first worked as a messenger boy for Raffaele Fiorini and became a full-fledged apprentice around 1894. He quickly became one of the most skilled makers in the Fiorini workshop, and opened his own shop in the late 1890s.

Bolognese violin maker Gaetano Pollastri followed the models developed by his brother Augusto , with whom he worked from a young age. After Augusto died in 1927, Gaetano opened his workshop and began to sign his own instruments, inheriting a significant portion of his late brother's customers.
Anton Poller studied under J. Ringer in Luby and later worked in Markneukirchen, Mittenwald, Berlin, and other locations.
Michael Poller worked in the Klotz manner. The workmanship was generally over-sized with a medium arch. The materials were of variable quality. A fine golden-brown varnish was used.

It was thought to be of German origin. A fine viol resided in the Basle Museum. The violin, cited by Vannes, had a large form and was well proportioned with thin pale yellow varnish. Authentic works were rare. The name was greatly abused by commercial dealers and manufacturers.

Charles Jean Joseph Poncin was a violin and bow maker. He worked for the piano maker Erard in Paris before moving to Brussels in 1879. Under the direction of J. Marchand, he worked for Mougenot. He established himself independently in 1908 and completed approximately 40 violins and cellos, using Stradivari and Guarneri models.
English bowmaker of the Hill school who studied briefly with William Retford in the 1960-70s.

Until rather recently, the existing work of brilliant Marche violin maker Andrea Postacchini was thought to be by two different makers. After his father's death during Postacchini's infancy, he was sent to a religious order where he picked up violin making from a monk who made instruments in his spare time.
Vincenzo Postiglione was presumed to be the son and pupil of Vincenzo Postiglione (I). He apprenticed to Vincenzo Jorio from 1847 to 1852 and established himself independently in Naples in 1855.

Johannes Pot was a carpenter and self-taught maker. He returned to Amersfoort, Netherlands in 1931 after working several years in Indonesia. In 1949, he received a diploma at The Hague. He was credited with 80 violins, 23 violas, and seven cellos. His personal model was derived from Stradivari. He used his own spirit varnish.

Joseph Pouillé created a large Stradivari model with golden-orange or yellow varnish. It was repaired in Lille by Pouillé, luthier, on rue Basse in 1879, after being worked on by the same luthier on rue Basse in 1878.
