Hans Poulsen apprenticed in Germany, gaining experience with the Schuster and Breinl firms before establishing himself in Copenhagen around 1885. He was a prolific maker of instruments known for their consistent quality, using good materials and soft oil varnish. His workshop employed both of his sons, and he received various medals and honours for his fine work. Hans Poulsen / Kjobenhavn 1895.

R. Poulton was described by Henley as a little known English maker. Wenberg identified him as American, stating he worked in Hull, Sioux County, Iowa. The label's address of Chapel Lane is located in Hull, Yorkshire, but it does not appear in the records of Hull, Iowa.
Herman Pourtois was a doctor of medicine and an amateur violin maker starting in 1934. He studied under A.
Émile Pouzol is active in Avignon and in Rome before he emigrates to the USA around 1930.

Clark Powers trained in England, Germany, and Austria before settling in Boston, Massachusetts, in 1888. His personal model featured oil and spirit varnishes. The work was good, although some pieces appeared to be imported trade productions or constructed from prefabricated parts.

Eugenio Praga studied with Nicolò Bianchi from 1869 and soon began making instruments for himself in parallel to working with Bianchi. When Bianchi moved to Nice in 1878, Praga took over his workshop.
Gustav Adolf Prager was a bow maker who was later assisted by his son Hans.
Oskar Gustav Prager worked for A. Brückner and C. F. Pfretzschner. He established himself independently in 1907, creating fine copies of various classical models.

Ronald William Praill was an amateur maker. He created a large personal model, as well as patterns based on Stradivari and Pressenda. The materials used were fine, featuring a good golden-brown varnish, and the workmanship was good. No.. London. 19..

Hermann Wilhelm Prell was a bow maker. He was a pupil of A. Nurnberger and, according to Henley, of H. Hoyer. In 1897, he worked for Sartory in Paris. From 1898, he established himself in Markneukirchen. He was succeeded by his son. His handiwork was fine, featuring both round and octagonal sticks. He branded his work as HERM. W. PRELL.

Julius Hermann Prell studied under his brother-in-law C. A. Lippert. He worked for A. Zeitler, A. Voigt in Loewenthal, and C. & O. Moeckel in Berlin. He established himself independently in Markneukirchen from 1918. His work was of fine quality, and he also made guitars.

Ronald Prentice was a professional double bass player. He trained in violin making at Mittenwald under K. Roy. He was active in north London and later in Somerset, specializing in double basses and baroque instruments.
Active instrument maker from 1809 whilst working as a farmer. Possibly a pupil of Benjamin Willard 1805-1810.

Considered the father and perhaps finest maker of the modern Turin school, Pressenda was generally assumed to have been a student of Storioni. A more thorough comparison of their instruments, however, does not support that theory, and it is more likely that he was trained in Turin in the 1820s by French makers before opening his own shop.
John Preston cited in the Hill Archive. His work is presently unknown. He was from York and active in 1791. He created the Preston Pavement in York in 1789. John Preston was born in 1783.

John Priestnall was active in Rochdale from 1840. He was credited with 300 violins, 30 violas, six cellos, and eight double basses. The workmanship was good, but the varnish was poor. He branded his name on the back button.
Bretislav Prihonsky apprenticed to Cermak at Kutná Hora. He established himself at Vrsovice in 1925. His work was characterized by clean professionalism based on classical models.

Established in 1723 at 'the sign of the Musical Instrument Maker in the Side, Newcastle-upon-Tyne', early labels spelled his name as `Pryor', but all records and labels after around 1720 referred to him as 'Prior'. He was recorded as the Assay Master in the city and was also a wood turner and maker of false teeth.

Enrico Prochet-Didero was an amateur maker and a pupil of E. Guerra. He received several awards. A small number of very fine violins and cellos modeled after Stradivari are known. His work exhibited good Turin style.

Ladislav Prokop was the son, pupil, and successor of Franz Dominik Prokop. He established himself in Chrudim from 1876 to 1925. His fine professional work utilized excellent materials and included classical and personal models. He received several medals at European Exhibitions.

Ladislav F. Prokop specialized in the professional production of high-arched personal models using quality materials. He was known for his work in the manufacture of musical instruments in Hlinsko and was recognized as a Czech master violin maker in Chrudim. His production of musical instruments in Chrudim occurred in the year 19..
Dante Pucci was a guitarist and luthier. He crafted bowed and plucked instruments of notable finesse.
E. F. Pullar created personal models with good proportions and decent arching. He used a good oil varnish in various shades of red, brown, and yellow, along with his own amber resin preparation.

Fritz Pulpaneck trained in Berlin and was active in Leipzig and Dusseldorf before emigrating to the USA in 1904. He worked in New York prior to establishing himself in Los Angeles in 1911. He created fine copies of Stradivari and Guarneri, using oil and spirit varnishes that ranged from orange to deep red-brown, sometimes shaded. A celebrated set of instruments was made for the Brahms Quartet.

Francesco Puppati was a lawyer, violinist, and amateur maker. He claimed to have re-discovered the secret of Stradivari like many others, although his work was ordinary and loosely based on Stradivari patterns. He exhibited at Cremona in 1894.

François Marie Pupunat was a cabinet maker and a self-taught maker of violins and bows, active in Lausanne from 1836. His work was of good competence, resulting in the completion of 300 violins, 25 cellos, and 20 small violas. Bows were branded: PUPUNAT.
T. E.
Joseph Puskas studied under Paulus Pilat from 1938 to 1941. He established himself in New York before relocating to Los Angeles in 1947.

George Pyne was a violinist and self-taught maker. He worked for Edward Withers for 20 years and was credited with approximately 500 violins and violas based on classical Cremonese models, some of which utilized imported parts.

Following a tumultuous childhood in China where he learned to play the violin, Zheng Quan worked at the Beijing Music Research Institute under the tutelage of the violin maker Dai Hongxiang, before completing the violin-making course in Cremona in 1983. In 1990 one of his violins won the gold medal at the Tchaikovsky violin competition in Moscow, and in 1991, another one of his violins ... won first prize for sound at the Stradivarius competition in Cremona.

Charles Quenoil trained with V. Audinot and worked for C. Brugere in Paris starting in 1894. He established himself independently at 8 rue du Faubourg Saint-Denis and lived in Bois de Pileu, Igny, Seine-et-Oise from 1935. He specialized in copies of classical Italian instruments and restoration work, focusing on cello and double basses.
René Quenoil was the son and pupil of Victor Quenoil . He also studied under A.

Victor Quenoil was the brother of Charles Quenoil and a pupil of Deblaye. He joined his brother Charles in Paris from 1907 to 1914. After World War I, he collaborated with H. Tournier on the rue de Rome, eventually relocating to Massy, Seine-et-Oise in 1926. He produced fine copies of individual classical Italian instruments and also engaged in restoration work.

Johann (János) Racsinczky was a pupil of B. Varju. He qualified as a luthier in 1905 and worked for Bohák, primarily making cembaloms. He established himself independently in 1926 and taught L. Csiszar. His work included Stradivari and Guarneri models, showcasing very neat professional craftsmanship. Racsinczky was based in Budapest in 1937. Op: =79.
Loránd Rácz worked in The Hague for J. Vedral and L.

Johann Radeck created fine workmanship on the Stainer model. The arch was of medium height with narrow edges. It featured a dark, thin, untransparent varnish. A characteristic 'comma' shaped eye was present on the scroll.

Otello Radighieri was a self-taught maker, guided by G. Guicciardi. He followed the Stradivari model and exhibited at Baveno and Bagnacavallo. He was also a guitar maker. Liutaio / Radighieri Otello / Facet in Modena - Italy 2007.
Alessandro Radrizzani studied under Rossi and Degani in Venice. He worked for Bisiach. He established himself in Vevey with his brother Angelo Radrizzani and his nephew Carlo.
Angelo Radrizzani was the brother of Alessandro Radrizzani. He was a pupil of Bisiach and was assisted by his son Carlo.
Carlo Radrizzani was the son and pupil of Angelo Radrizzani and the uncle of Alessandro. He focused mostly on restoration and classical models.

John Rae, a Scottish violin maker and the son and grandson of bagpipe makers, worked in London from approximately 1875 to 1920. He derived personal models and completed over 130 instruments, including violas and cellos, which featured internal inscriptions.

George R. Raeburn was the brother of Alexander Raeburn (I). He was active in Leven and, for the last few years, in West Calder. His work included Stradivari and Guarneri models. He seemed to have utilized imported pre-made scrolls and commercial varnish from Harris and Whitelaw.
John Raeburn was the eldest brother of Alexander and George Raeburn. He worked as a miner, was a violinist, and made violins part-time.
Claude Victor Rambaux apprenticed to Moitessier in Mirecourt from 1820 to 1824. He worked with Thibout in Caen starting in 1824 and with Francois Gand in Paris from 1827.

Manuel Ramirez was a celebrated guitar maker. He constructed violins and guitars and served as a luthier at the Real Conservatorio in Arlaban No. 10, Madrid. Henley reported that some violins had unremarkable achievement.
William Ramsay created a small production Stradivari model that featured golden oil varnish in 1899. He was based in Biggar.

Haim Rapoport studied in St Petersburg under A. A. Shvalm Wishnegorsky and also learned with Andre Chardon in Paris. He established himself in Tel Aviv, Israel, in 1925, creating violins and bows of high quality based on Stradivari and other classical Italian models. He was also a repairer and restorer.
Born in Russia, Haim Rapoport studied violin making with Shvalm Wishnegorsky and Andre Chardon before settling in Tel Aviv.
Anders Rasmussen was a pupil of F. W.
Ludovico Rastelli modeled according to Henley. No other source exists. The work remains presently unknown.

August Rau was one of the finest bow makers of the German school. He worked for W. Knopf and A. R. Weichold in Dresden. He established himself in Markneukirchen from 1890 to 1939. His bows featured elegant heads with the best pernambuco sticks.
Sebastian Rauch produced a Stainer model. The varnish was thin and golden-yellow or pale brown.
Thomas Rauch was the son of Sebastian Rauch I and is believed to have been a pupil of Thomas Edlinger. He moved from Prague to Wroclaw around 1737.

After graduating from the Cremona International School of Violin Making in 1985, in the class of Maestro Giorgio Scolari, Frank Ravatin was first head of restoration and making in Jacques Camurat's workshop in Paris. In 1991, he opened his own shop in Le Mans and devoted himself more and more to making new instruments.

Carlo Ravizza was a pupil of Antoniazzi. He produced violins, violas, and cellos in the fine Antoniazzi style, featuring a beautiful golden-red varnish. His work closely resembled that of his teacher. He also crafted guitars.

German-born Jacob Rayman was one of the first violin makers in Britain, and his violin from 1641 is probably the oldest surviving English violin. He was trained in the Bavarian violin making center of Füssen before moving to London around 1625, and his workmanship reflects that training rather than the methods of the English viol-making tradition.
Robert John Raymond credited with 37 violins, 39 violas, and five cellos.
Rudolph Raymond described violins of gong pattern with small soundholes, deeply sunk edges, and a scroll. The work featured good materials and yellow varnish. It was not known at present.
Nicola Reale was a self-taught violin maker and repairer, active in Washington from 1909.
Dudley B. Reed was a part-time violin maker active from 1922.
Joseph Reed created models based on Stradivari and Guarneri. The oil varnish ranged from light to dark orange-brown.
Bologna maker. Studied with Otello Bignami.

Dante Paolo Regazzoni was a pupil of G. and L. Bisiach in Milan. He won medals in Rome 1956, Poznan 1957, and Ascoli Piceno 1959. His work featured classical models and had a good orange varnish. It was branded internally on the upper block: `Regazzoni Dante / Cortenova'. A manuscript label indicated: Dante Regazzoni / fece in Cortenova Valsassina l'A 1975 / anno Santo Regazzoni Dante.

August Anton Reichel was the son of Friedrich August Reichel (II) and a maker of violins and bows. He apprenticed with his uncle W. Voigt and worked for his father before collaborating with Weichold in Dresden. He established himself independently in Markneukirchen starting in 1875.
Bassist and bow maker at Guarneri House in Hudsonville, MI.

Knute Reindahl was born November 16, 1857 in Telemark, Norway, where fiddle making had long been a tradition in his mother's family. Beginning with Knute's great-grandfather, Olav Gullbekk, ca.1760 - 1836, several generations had produced makers of both Norwegian Hardanger fiddles and classic Italian instruments.
Anton Reinhard manufactured commercially good quality instruments in various grades. Models included those of Amati and Stradivari.
Johann Reiter was a violin and bow maker, the son, pupil, and successor of Johann Baptist Reiter . He established his workshop in Oberer Markt, Mittenwald.

Michael (Mihály) Reményi apprenticed to E. Bartek in 1881 and later with G. Tanczer. He qualified as a luthier in 1885 and was employed by P. Pilat from 1887 and A. Ferenczy from 1889. He established himself independently in 1890. He was a noted connoisseur, producing violins based on Guarneri and Stradivari models with oil and spirit varnishes. His work was very accurate and professional.

Jean Mathurin Remy was the son, pupil, and successor of Mathurin-François. He established himself at 30 rue de Grenelle-Saint-Honoré in 1817. His work featured small patterns, but they were generally of better workmanship and finish than those of his father. He used fine materials and red-brown oil varnish.

Jules Remy was the son and pupil of Jean Mathurin Remy. He established himself in Passage Brady in 1854 and at 60 Faubourg Saint-Denis from 1872. His work primarily involved plucked instruments and pochettes. Some violins in the Mirecourt style were branded: 'In Urbe Cremoniae Remy. '.
Established at rue des Bouchers, Paris from 1773. Moved c. 1774 to rue Saint-Honoré, at the sign 'Aux Amateurs'.
Walter Gerhard Renz was a bow maker. He apprenticed with his uncle M. E. Fenzel and established himself independently in Erlbach from 1922. He branded his work as GERH. WALT RENZ.
Luigi Repetti completed 60 violins based on his personal model of small proportions. He was later assisted by his son Giuseppe. Most of the instruments bore individual names, such as 'Rondine'.

Charles Résuche established a workshop in Lyons in 1890, in the passage de l'Arque, in partnership with Justin Diter. He later moved to rue de l'Hotel de Ville. In 1897, he dissolved the partnership to relocate to Bordeaux and retired in 1913. He produced fine copies of classical Cremonese work with high-quality varnish of appropriate color.
Probably the greatest bow craftsman of his time, William Charles Retford was initially hired by the firm of W.E. Hill & Sons as an instrument repairer.

William Richard Retford was a bow maker, the son of William Charles Retford. He studied under his father at W. E. Hill & Sons in London from 1919. Known in the Hill workshop as 'young Bill', his bows for Hill are marked with two dots.

Raoul Rettberg was a violin and bow maker. He moved to New York in 1921, where he worked with Martin Lange to develop their 'Gala' instruments. He created his own model using local materials and commercial pre-cut necks. Additionally, he made copies of Stradivari and Guarneri.

Alexander Ricard was active in Springfield from 1917. He created Cremonese and personal models, using his own oil varnish with yellow, brown, and red coloration. The workmanship was very fine. He completed 400 instruments, many with the help of his son Raoul.

Frederick George Richards was an architectural wood carver and amateur violin maker active from 1935 in Eastbourne, Tunbridge Wells, and Torrington. He completed 30 violins and 10 violas based on Stradivari models. Bows were also made by him.
Arthur Richardson took up violin making around 1915, having worked initially as a woodcarver and pattern-maker. He was largely self-taught.

Frank Richardson completed 220 instruments, utilizing a varnish that was shaded red-brown and made from local materials. He worked in Canton, Maine, and in Caribu, and included a manuscript label in his instruments.
André Richaume worked with his uncle Victor Fetique from 1927. He established himself independently in 1932 on the boulevard de Clichy.
Antoine Marius Richelme worked as a pupil of Yong in Marseille in 1850. He subsequently collaborated with Luppi-Coviaux, manufacturers of tailpieces and chinrests, among other items.

Malcolm F. Richmond was a pupil of Smillie and created fine classical models inspired by Cremonese designs, exhibiting a style very much like Mirecourt, possibly utilizing imported parts. His works featured a dark red spirit varnish and cellos were crafted on a large Stradivari pattern. He was based in Camelon, Falkirk, and his work dated to 1923.
August Riechers was a violin and bow maker. He was the son of Reichers from Hanover and was recognized as one of the finest German makers of the 19th century.

Joseph Riedel was described by Henley as typical Bohemian work with yellow-brown varnish. Its current status is unknown. The name was not included in Liitgendorff, and Vannes expressed doubts about its authenticity. There may be confusion with J. A. Reidel. A label was also provided by Henley.
Bow maker. Son and pupil of Karl Riedl. Studied at the Luby School. After 1945 worked for G. Schuster and for Siefert in Bubenreuth where he established his own business in 1968.

Dominicus Rief was the son and pupil of Matthaus Rief, below. He was generally recognized as the best maker of the family. His craftsmanship was very fine and accurate. He created a distinctive yet well-proportioned personal model featuring long 'c' bouts and outward-pointing corners. The arch was moderately high. He often used quite plain materials and dark thin intransparent varnish.
Georg Rieger created an Amatisé model with dark brown varnish. The work was decent, while double basses received more appreciation.
Joseph Rieger was the son and pupil of Philipp Jacob Rieger. The style was slightly Italianised Tyrolean, though not highly finished. It featured a deep orange-red varnish.
Archibald Ritchie was the best maker of the Dundee school. He was a part-time self-taught maker who was professionally employed at Dundee harbour.
William J. Ritchie created heavily built violins that featured thick varnish.
Hermann Ritter designed the five-stringed Viola Alta, which was made by K. A. HOrlein and P. Keller in WOrzburg.
London firm, originally named Hawkes & Son, founded in 1865 by William Henry Hawkes. In 1930 it merged with Boosey & Company to become Boosey & Hawkes.
Giacomo Rivolta worked at the sign of 'Santo Re Davide' in Contrada Santa Margherita. He derived models from Gagliano and Stradivari.

A. J. Roberts was a maker of reeds for woodwind instruments and an amateur violin maker. He was credited with approximately 20 violins and exhibited neat workmanship. In 1910, he founded a violin makers guild in Hampstead.
Studied in Cremona with Pietro Sgarabotto, Gio Batta Morassi, Francesco Bissolotti and Pier Luigi Galetti. Returned to New York City in 1975. Moved to Philadelphia in 1987.
John Robertson worked in Dalmeny before later moving to Broxburn. He was awarded medals at exhibitions in Edinburgh and Glasgow.
Alfred George Robinson accurately made a Stradivari model. It featured fine varnish and received a Diploma of Honour at The Hague in 1949.
Robert Robinson was a violin and bow maker and a pupil of his father, R. C.

William Robinson made a personal model derived from Stradivari and Guarneri patterns. He was an amateur maker, not associated with William Joseph of Plumstead. The materials used were of good quality, and the orange varnish varied in depth. The work was completed in April 1929 at 2 Silverdale Terrace, Carmoyle Road, Liverpool.
Enrico Rocca was born in Turin and moved with his family to Genoa as a young child. He studied with his father Giuseppe , who died in 1865 when Enrico was just 17.

Born in Barbaresco, Giuseppe Antonio (or Joseph) Rocca moved to Turin in 1834 to work in the shop of Giovanni Francesco Pressenda . After three years, he opened his own shop, and although his work ranks among the best of 18th century Italian violin making, his life was marked by financial struggles, illness and grief. ... />Rocca initially followed the work of his teacher, albeit with stylistic aberrations that suggest a budding interest in Cremonese models.

According to Sesto Rocchi's own account, his interest in violin making stems from an early childhood crash course in repair: after dropping his violin, he attempted to fix it without arousing the suspicion of his parents. Some years later Rocchi entered the School of Violin Making in Parma, where he studied with Gaetano Sgarabotto . ...

P. Roccus owned a cello that Vannes noted was in the possession of M. Bisiach. It featured a large Amati form, exhibited good workmanship, and had a clear yellow varnish. A manuscript label accompanied it.

David Bailey Rockwell was a violin and bow maker. He was active in Boston from 1878 to 1895, in Providence in 1901, in Detroit in 1908, in Philadelphia in 1915, in Hartford, Connecticut in 1920, and finally in New York in 1923.
Harry Victor Rockwell was the son and pupil of Joseph H. Rockwell .

Giovanni Battista Rogeri settled in Brescia around 1675 after working as an apprentice of Nicolò Amati in Cremona from 1661. After the plague devastated northern Italy in the mid-17th century and Amati died in 1684, Rogeri was one of the few active violin makers in the region.

The son of Giovanni Battista Rogeri , Pietro Giacomo Rogeri began to assist his father around 1690, though his earliest known instruments date from 1705. Rogeri usually spelled his name in a Latinized form 'Ruggerius' leading to speculation about a connection between the Brescian Rogeri and Cremonese Rugeri families, but their ... work shows few similarities.
French bow maker trained in Mirecourt by Bernard Ouchard. Worked in Paris then the Island of Bréhat.

Pieter Rombouts almost certainly began to assist his stepfather Hendrik Jacobs early in life, and the changes in Jacobs' output from about 1685 on supports that assumption. Though his craftsmanship is somewhat heavier than that of Jacobs and the heads are less delicate, Rombouts' skill in the channeling of edgework is unmistakable. ...

Adolf Romer was a musician and luthier who became the luthier for the Freiburg court in 1899. He received several exhibition medals and created copies of Stradivari and Guarneri. His work featured good materials and oil varnish. Additionally, he made small-sized instruments and developed a new form of peg.
Raffaele Ronchini was a violin and bow maker. He used a Stradivari-derived model and applied a coarse finish with yellow-brown varnish.
Pupil of Joseph Scott . Employed by him as a farm labourer and instrument maker at his farm Haltcliff, Eskett, near Caldbeck 1795-180o.

Alfred James Rope was a self-taught violin maker from 1890, while still serving as a soldier in Aldershot Barracks. He resided in Woolwich in 1927. He created over 200 instruments based on Stradivari or Guarneri models, which included violas and cellos. His work was characterized as coarse, and he used oil varnish in various shades.

Walter S. Ropes was a pupil and assistant of J. B. Squier. He moved to Boston around 1922. He received awards at Buffalo in 1901 and at Jamestown in 1907. His instruments featured a Guarneri model with shaded varnish. A total of 73 instruments were recorded.
Giovanni Rosadoni was active in Pavia, Como, and Milan. His models featured Stradivari designs with golden-yellow varnish.
The work was of decent professional quality and featured a brown varnish. It included a manuscript label that imitated print.
Christian Heinrich Wilhelm Röscher was a cabinet maker and amateur maker. He used good materials and workmanship in his Stradivari model. The varnish was a golden brown.

John Rose, son and pupil of the viol maker John Rose I, continued to work in his father's Bridewell Palace workshop after the elder John's death until 1607, when he was given notice for unpaid rent. Dating of all instruments attributed to 'John Rose' is tentative, and it is likely that the younger Rose was responsible for most surviving work.
Samuel 'Luthier' Rosenthal was a violin and bow maker. He was the son and pupil of Joseph Rosenthal starting in 1901.
Domenico Rossi made copies of individual classical Italian violins in Piacenza in the year 1903.

Emilio Rossi was the son and pupil of Domenico Rossi and focused on guitars and cellos. A cellist himself, he created a personal model featuring golden-red varnish. His work was exhibited in Cremona in 1937. Rossi was awarded a gold medal and was a technician for repairs on string instruments in Piacenza, located at Via Castello 66.
Giovanni Rossi established himself independently in 1819. He moved to Pavia in 1847. He specialized in double basses and created violins based on classical models, using good varnish and materials.
Giuseppe Rossi lived in Citadella, Padua, and studied at the Venice Academy of Fine Arts. He subsequently moved to Rome, where he developed an interest in violin making.
Stelio Rossi studied under I. Sderci in Florence. He utilized models from Stradivari, Gagliano, and his own designs. His varnish was golden-brown. He also crafted plucked instruments.

Franz Georg Rost apprenticed to August Voigt in Markneukirchen from 1883 to 1886. He worked for Paulus in Leipzig, Hammig in Dresden, Louis Otto in Düsseldorf, Laumann in Budapest, and Zimmermann in Leipzig.

A descendant of Nicolò Amati's wife, Giovanni Rota became part of Lorenzo Storioni's workshop and household in 1787. He worked for Storioni for the next five years, and almost certainly got to know Nicola Bergonzi , since Bergonzi also lived there from ... 1790 to 1795.

One of the most prolific of the Markneukirchen violin workshops, the firm of Ernst Heinrich Roth produced an enormous number of consistently high-quality instruments in the 20th century. Roth, son of a violin maker, started his firm in 1902.

François Roudhloff was a pupil of Breton. He established himself in Paris in partnership with Mauchand. He created well-made violins and cellos based on Amati models, featuring good yellow varnish. He was better known for a collection of decorated and inlaid guitars. His brand was 'F. Roudhlof Mauchand. '.

Maurice Rougier initially resided in rue Chevalier Paul. He moved to rue Grinelle, Toulon, in 1942. In 1949, he was awarded a diploma at The Hague. His work included a personal model and various shades of varnish.

Lodewich Willem Joannes Roumen was active in Gröningen around 1802, in Kampen around 1804, and in Utrecht in 1810. He established himself in Amsterdam in 1825 at 159 Kalverstraat and retired to Kampen in 1843, leaving the business to his son Joannes.

Luigi Rovatti established himself in Buenos Aires in 1885. He was a prolific maker, having created 1, 000 instruments, including violins, violas, cellos, and guitars. His work generally reflected good quality based on the Rocca model. After 1900, he branded his instruments as 'Luigi Rovatti Fabricanti'.

Azzo Rovescalli worked as a pupil of R. Antoniazzi and was employed in the Monzino shop located in Milan. His instruments, which followed the Stradivari model, include 300 violins, along with several violas and cellos. He labeled his work as 'Azzo Rovescalli Cremonese / fece l'anno 19XX A.R.' Azzo Rovescalli Cremonese made this in the year 1930.

Manilo Rovescalli, son and pupil of Azzo Rovescalli, was born in 19.. and made violins with a small pattern and dark red varnish, as well as violas and guitars. He worked in various cities, including Cremona and Milan. His instruments are labeled 'Manlio Rovescalli / Figlio /fete / nel 19..'.
Tullio Rovescalli was the son and pupil of Azzo Rovescalli. He specialized in making violins in a small pattern with dark red varnish, as well as violas and guitars.

Frederick Rowe was active from 1909. He established himself professionally in Minneapolis in 1920. He associated with Joseph Lundh starting in 1927 and later retired to Fort Lauderdale. His work included Stradivari and Guarneri models, showcasing fine workmanship and materials. He completed 168 violins, six violas, and two cellos.

John Rowe was a violinist and teacher, as well as an amateur violin maker. He completed 10-12 violins based on the Stradivari model, showcasing good workmanship. His violins featured an orange-red varnish with a crackled texture.

Arthur John Rowley was a violinist and luthier. He created over 100 violins based on classical Italian and personal models. The violins featured oil varnish in various shades and were signed internally. A printed label read: Hand Made Violin / By A. J. Rowley / 31, Collingwood Road, Coventry / A. D. 19..

Andrew Roy established himself as a clock and violin maker in Allhallowgate. The model resembled that of Testore. It featured a front made of English pine with a pale yellow spirit varnish. The workmanship was good. A manuscript label was present.
Self-taught British maker who worked in Cambridge. Made guitars and period instruments as well as violins, violas and cellos. Some experimental instruments exist, including an 8-string guitar.

Rigart Rubus created characteristic Russian school violins that featured rounded edges without overhang. The soundholes resembled those of Stradivari. The scroll was well cut but had rounded edges and was unpurfled. The varnish was dark golden or red-brown. This work was produced in St. Petersburg in 1850.
Joseph Ruddiman was the most prolific maker of the Aberdeen School. He was probably a pupil of R. Duncan and had a prolific output of all types of stringed instruments.
Johann Michael Rudert established himself in Korneuburg. He moved to Vienna in 1813. His work was of no great distinction. It featured a good dark colored oil varnish.

Carlo Rugeri was the youngest son of Francesco Rugeri and was trained by his father in the family workshop. Upon Francesco's death in 1698, Carlo inherited his father's tools, likely because he was the only son who remained in the workshop. Very few works are known to carry Carlo's individual label, and his own output is almost non-existent

Francesco Rugeri has traditionally been considered a student of Nicolò Amati , although there is no documentary evidence that he was present in the Amati workshop. The two families certainly had close a social relationship, with Amati acting as godfather to one of Rugeri's sons.

Giacinto Rugeri was the son, pupil, and assistant of Francesco Rugeri, and he predeceased his father. Very few authenticated works are known to exist today, with almost no instruments surviving that bear his own label, 'Giacinto filio di Francesco / Ruggieri detto it per 16..'.

Giovanni Battista Rugeri was the son, pupil, and assistant of Francesco Rugeri. Very few authenticated instruments bore his label, but those that did were sophisticated and beautifully crafted in the Rugeri tradition, with some featuring a deeper red varnish than typically associated with Francesco.
Vincenzo Rugeri was born in 1663 just outside the walls of Cremona. He worked in his father Francesco's shop alongside his brothers Giovanni Battista, Giacinto and Carlo.

George Ruoff created one alto viola known for its distinctive model and fine craftsmanship. It featured a medium high arch and very large open soundholes with short wings, which were set slanting across the front.

Wilhelm Ruprecht established at 102 Landstrasse, moving to 767 Weiden in 1855. He used Brescian and Guarneri models. The Brescian models were sometimes adorned with a star shape formed by superimposed triangles of purfling on the back. Several were said to have been sold as authentic Magginis.

Karl Rutz worked as a pupil of F. Sprenger in St. Gallen. He was employed by Pliverics in Berlin, M. Moller in Amsterdam, Vedral in The Hague, and R. Laumann in Budapest, before returning to Pliverics and establishing his own business in Basle and later in Lucerne. His work featured fine craftsmanship based on the Stradivari model and utilized a red-brown oil varnish.

Josef Vojtech Ruzicka studied in Prague. He worked in Opava. His workmanship was neat. He used Stradivari and Guarneri models, as well as a personal pattern featuring recurved upper bouts and a scroll with a truncated final turn. The arching was slightly high, and the edges were thick. He applied a golden-brown varnish.

Josef Antonin Čermak was a pupil of Benjamin Patocky. He worked in Kutná Hora from 1898 to 1910. His professional work was very fine and followed the styles of Amati, Stainer, Stradivari, and Guarneri.
Benigno Saccani varnished and labelled mass-produced Czech and German instruments. He exhibited at the Brussels Exhibition in 1910. A little later, he abandoned lutherie.

Simone Fernando Sacconi became an assistant in the workshop of Giuseppe Rossi while he was still a schoolboy, and had attracted his own clients by the age of 16. His tremendous skills as a copyist, particularly of Stradivari and Guarneri , ... were evident from a very young age.

Paul Sadka apprenticed with bow maker Noel Burke in Ireland, and continued his training in Cremona, Italy, later working in the south of France, Paris and Budapest. In 2009, Sadka returned to England to set up his workshop in his hometown of Cornwall, and afterwards settled in Normandy in France.

Pierre Saint Paul worked in the rue de la Comedie Francaise until 1741. He later operated in the rue Saint Andre des Arts at the sign 'A la lyre d'Apollon'. He produced small model violins with pale yellow varnish of poor quality. Viols and cellos were also recorded. The Musical Instruments Museum in Brussels has a pardessus de viole labelled:.

George Saint-George was a violinist and viola d'amore player. He was an early pioneer in researching historical musical instruments. He made at least two viols named 'Dolphin' and 'Ram's Head' due to the carvings of the heads, along with two intricately decorated viola d'amores.
Award winning contemporary bow maker, restorer and expert based in New York. Grandson of William Salchow.
Pioneering American who studied bow making in Mirecourt with Georges Barjonnet.
Jean Baptiste Deshayes Salomon established himself in Paris on the rue de Grenelle Saint Honoré at the sign 'Sainte-Cécile'. He married Barbe Deshaies, the widow of J.
Dominique Salzard created a large Stradivari model using good materials and applied a yellow-brown varnish. The instrument was branded with 'D. Salzae' or 'D. Salzard. '.
François Salzard was assumed to be the brother of Dominique Salzard. He created similar work based on a large Mirecourt/Stradivari model. His instruments were labelled and branded.
Leone Sanavia primarily made plucked instruments. One violin, dated 1946, was recorded.

Joseph Sandherr was the son of Adolf Sandherr and a pupil of Fiorini in Munich. He worked independently in Griinestrasse, Dresden from around 1918. In 1945, he moved back to Laupheim. He was an expert and restorer.
Alois Sandner made fittings and parts of violins for wholesale manufacture and sale.

Anton Sandner studied under K. Fischer in Luby. He worked for I. Metall, crafting lutes and guitars, and later for Neudorfer and R. Schuster. He established himself in Usti nad Labem around 1922, returning to Luby in 1929. He authored a pamphlet on the sound-post.
Carl Sandner was probably the son of Johann Sandner (I). He established himself in Fruhmessgasse, Luby, as a guitar maker.

Max Sandner was a pupil of U. Wourlitzer and later worked for Berger in Erlbach and Glasel in Markneukirchen. He established himself independently in Erlbach starting in 1922. His work was of good professional quality.
Wenzel Sandner worked for J. W. Schunda in Budapest. He established himself independently from 1875 to 1900.

Neapolitan maker who moved to Rome later in his career. Believed to be largely self-taught, but early instruments show stylistic similarities with the work of Giovanni Tedesco, suggesting that he may have been the pupil of Tedesco.
Giovanni Battista Sanoni created a high-arched model featuring a well-carved scroll with a good texture orange varnish. It was rarely encountered.

Gaetano Santagiuliana was probably a pupil of Giorgio Serafin. He was a maker of guitars and violins, especially well-regarded for his double basses. He established himself in Vicenza in 1781. His work was fine, featuring full arching and a dark brown or golden lemon-yellow varnish that was quite hard.
Giacinto Santagiuliana was the son and pupil of Gaetano Santagiuliana . He generally used Stradivari models with a flat arch that were not highly refined.

Giuseppe Saretta was a sculptor and a pupil of Carlo Oddone. He established himself in Montebelluna and briefly worked in Padua and Treviso. He competed in the creation of approximately 56 violins and five cellos.

Probably the finest bow maker of the 20th century, Eugène Sartory studied with his father in Mirecourt and went on to work for Charles Peccatte and Alfred Lamy in Paris. In 1889 Sartory set up his own workshop at the age of 18, becoming completely self-sufficient ... as a maker by 1910.

Mitsuaki Sasano is a Japanese bowmaker who trained with Rudolf Neudorfer in Germany and Stéphane Thomachot in France. Having initially set up a workshop in Paris in 2001, he then decided to settle on the French Riviera in Nice.
David E. Saunders studied under Goerge Orth in Seattle in 1948.
Stephen Saunders was a cello and double bass player and an amateur luthier. He gained access to the Stradivari owned by Dr.
Self-taught maker, after apprenticeship as a joiner. Followed Heron-Allen's book Violin Making as it was and is.
Edmond Saunier worked for D. F.

Giuseppe Scarampella was the son of Paolo Scarampella and a pupil of Nicola Bianchi in Paris. In 1866, he worked for Luigi Castellini in Florence, later succeeding him as the curator of the Cherubini Museum.

Younger brother of Giuseppe Scarampella , Stefano Scarampella was born in Brescia in 1843 and later worked in Mantua. A carpenter by trade, he only took up violin making seriously around 1890, and his fully mature body of work dates from about 1902 to 1915.
Arthur Scarbrow worked first in the case department of the firm of W.E. Hill & Sons before switching to bow making.

Max Schaffner was a violin and bow maker. He was a pupil of C. Dreier. He established himself independently in Markneukirchen in 1896 and moved to Hamburg in 1906. He created fine violins based on classical models and bows following French patterns, which were branded: SCHAFFNER.

Oswald Schaller was the son and pupil of Friedrich Wilhelm Schaller. He worked for Louis Otto in Düsseldorf. From 1881, he established himself independently in Frankfurt. He used classical Cremonese models and developed his own oil varnish, which produced a good transparent red-yellow.
Karl Schau was a civil engineer and an amateur maker. He created the work in Schwechat in 19.
Robert Schenk created a very original personal model. The varnish was of poor quality and brown. A manuscript label was present.
Ernst Schetelig was a pupil of A. Paulus. He established himself independently in 1891. His workmanship was fine.
Johann Scheverle was born in Augsburg, Germany. His name was rendered in various ways: Schaferle, Schefferle, Schwertle, and Schewerle.

Carlo Schiavi was a pupil of L. Digiuni and F. Kresnik. He was the first teacher appointed at the Cremona International School of Violin Making. He exhibited in Cremona in 1937 and in Berlin in 1938. He died during World War II. His work included Stradivari, Guarneri, and personal models, utilizing fine materials. He applied a golden and slightly pink-red spirit varnish.
Oswald Anton Schilbach was a pupil of E. W.

Emil Schiller worked in London at Castle Street and Soho Square until 1910. He settled in Bad Elster, Germany in 1930. His workmanship was fine, featuring a good orange-red varnish. Cellos were well reported.
Johann Georg Schlosser was the son and pupil of Johann Christian Schlosser. His instruments were in a similar style. They were branded: "I*G*S*. ".
Arnold Otto Schmidt was the son and pupil of Anton Otto. He worked for E. W. Geipel in Mannheim and later established himself independently there.
Contemporary bow maker in Dresden, Germany. Father of Daniel Schmidt.

Carl Franz Schmidt was the son of Franz Schmidt and received diplomas at the Vienna Exhibition of 1873. He created copies of classical Cremonese and Brescian models. He worked as an instrument maker in Wien at Stadt Klostergasse No. 4 in the year 1865.
Ernst Albin Schmidt was a pupil of A. A. Reichel. He later worked in Spiegelstraat, Amsterdam. His work remains unknown.

Ernst Reinhold Schmidt apprenticed to J. Kretzschman from 1871 to 1874. He worked for Bausch and Hammig in Leipzig and Riechers in Berlin. In 1880, he established himself independently in Markneukirchen. He developed the workshop into a large and productive business with his brother, employing a substantial staff. Gold medals were awarded to him at Vienna in 1892.
Johann Martin Schmidt was likely the son of Carl Schmidt. He was born in Pressburg in 1805.

Kurt Schmidt was a violin and bow maker, the son and pupil of Christian Paul Schmidt. He worked for R. Penzel, P. Rammig, and R. Paulus before establishing himself independently in Dresden. His work was fine, although it varied in several grades of finish and materials.

Max Willibald Schmidt trained in Markneukirchen, Germany. He emigrated to the USA in 1913 and worked in San Francisco and Seattle. He retired to Boise but continued to make new instruments until his death. He created 50 instruments, including some fine cellos, and used Guarneri-derived model violins with spirit varnish.
Paul Gerhard Schmidt studied under G. Jiirgens in Hamburg and A.
Lucien Schmitt studied the violin at the Geneva conservatoire. He trained for two years as a luthier in Mirecourt with Mougenot and studied bow making with Bazin.

Arthur L. Scholes worked from c. 1890 to 1914 in Rushden, Northamptonshire, before moving to Bedford. He settled in Archer Street, London in 1926, but relocated to Bedford around 1940. He completed 99 violins based on the 1714 'Dolphin' Stradivari, Guarneri, and his personal models. His instruments featured good workmanship and materials, along with oil varnish. No. 11 was made in 1914.

Herbert Emil Schönfelder worked as a brother and pupil of Emil Karl Schönfelder. He was employed by H. Dölling and Oberpottkamp in Hanover. He established himself independently in Hanover starting in 1927. He produced good copies of classical Italian works.

Johann Adam Schönfelder was the son of Johann Schönfelder and became a master of the violin makers guild in 1729. He was regarded as the finest maker in his family. His instruments featured a Stainer model with golden-brown varnish and utilized high-quality materials, often incorporating one-piece backs with narrow flame.
Most dictionaries noted some training in Italy, but this could not be determined and seemed unlikely, based on reports of a Latinised version of his label.
Johann Paul Schorn is a musician and luthier active in Innsbruck from 1654 to 1684. He is appointed to the service of the Archbishop of Stuttgart in 1684.

Canadian maker. Studied violin making with his uncle Fernand Schryer in Quebec, then took up an apprenticeship at Geo. Heinl & Co. in Toronto. Won gold medal for a cello at the 2003 Triennale competition in Cremona.

Paul Schubert was a bow maker and the son and pupil of August Schubert. His work was of fine quality. He branded his sticks with the mark: PAUL SCHUBERT, and on the frog, he used the initials: P. S. M.

Johann Heinrich Schult was a pupil of O. Schünemann and established his workshop in Königstrasse, Lübeck in 1896. In 1907, he was appointed luthier to the Mecklenburg court. His workshop was destroyed by bombing in 1942, which forced his retirement. He received medals at Turin and Schwerin. His workmanship on classical models was very fine, utilizing excellent materials and varnishes.
Henry F. Schultz was a pupil of Clark Powers from 1886 to 1891.

Otto Schünemann became a luthier under the influence of Dr. Wirsing of Prague and was mainly self-taught in Hamburg. In 1888, he founded a violin making school in Schwerin with the patronage of the Duke of Mecklenburg, returning to Hamburg in 1898. He created a finely made compact model featuring a flat arch, large scroll, fine materials, and red-brown varnish.

Arno Schünzel trained in Mittenwald. He subsequently worked for G. Grasseck in Munich and A. Wilfer in Leipzig. In 1935, he became a master of the Leipzig guild. From 1926, he established himself independently in his hometown, Wiesbaden. His work was of good professional quality.
Schuster was a family of luthiers based in Markneukirchen, Germany, and Luby (Schönbach), Czech Republic. They operated in both locations.

Adolf Curt Schuster was a bow maker who worked in Markneukirchen around 1920. He studied under Pfretzschner and served as an assistant to A. Rau, producing fine bows featuring silver and gold mounts. Schuster utilized models from Tubbs, Voirin, and Tourte in his craftsmanship.

Carl Gottlob Schuster created a large Stradivari model with medium arching and golden-brown varnish. One example is located in the Bach Museum in Eisenach. He founded the company that, under the leadership of Raymond and Ulrich Schuster, became Schuster Gebrüder. Henley provided dates a century earlier and included a label from 1741.

Edouard Schuster established his business in Brussels in 1910, initially located in the Galerie de Commerce (Passage Hirsch). In 1925, he moved to boulevard Emile-Jacquemain, and later to place de La Reine. In 1944, he relocated to Hirsingue, Alsace, on rue Saint-Nicolas. He produced Guarneri and personal models featuring red oil varnish. After 1925, he branded his work as 'Ed Schuster. '.

Kurt Schuster studied under Glässel and Fiorini in Munich. He established himself independently in Leipzig on Lampestrasse from 1908. His work was characterized by fine professional craftsmanship based on classical Italian models. He used a good, well-colored transparent varnish.

Max Schuster worked for H. Gütter in Breslau, C. Niedt in Würzburg, T. Kurt in Berlin, and E. Gaertner in Stuttgart, having been a pupil of A. Voigt. He established himself independently in Markneukirchen in 1915. His work was fine professional craftsmanship based on classical models.

Rudolph Schuster was a pupil of J. Pötzl and worked in Lemberg, Hamburg for Winterling, Stuttgart for E. Gärtner, Berlin for E. Pliverics, and Vienna for Dr. Thomastik. He established himself independently in Graz starting in 1930.
Anton Schwartz created a Stainer model with a high arch and yellow-brown varnish. It was quite an undistinguished work.

Bernhard Schwartz established himself as a musician and luthier in Strasbourg around 1764. He became a member of the city guild in 1780 and was recorded in archives as a 'luthier' in 1796 at 69 Finkwillerstrasse.
Georges Frédéric Schwartz was a bow maker and the son and pupil of Bernard Schwartz. He specialized in bow making from around 1810, creating pieces in the style of Tourte.

Theophil Wilhelm Schwartz was the younger son, pupil, and successor of Bernhard Schwartz , and he worked alongside his brother Georg Frédéric Schwartz, contributing to the instrument production labeled as "Fréres Schwartz." Throughout his career, Theophil primarily produced violins, violas, and cellos, characterized by a ... very clean style in the French manner, based on the Stradivari model. His instruments featured full low arching and a golden red-brown varnish.
Giovanni Schwarz apprenticed to his uncle E. Degani until 1888, when he established himself in Venice.
Rodolfo Schwarz was the son and pupil of Giovanni Schwarz . Active from 1920, he established himself independently in 1928.
Johann Baptist Schweitzer was a pupil of F. Geissenhof in Vienna.

Giuseppe Scialé recorded in 1842 at 91 Via del Corso as a maker of violins, guitars, wind instruments, and harps. The only authenticated works that exist are guitars, although Henley cited copies of violins by Gaetano Guadagnini. His fine work featured greenish-yellow varnish, but it was not presently verified. He made items in Roma in the year 1832.
Farmer and part-time maker. Active in Haltcliff Farm, Eskett (formerly Heskett New Market), parish of Caldbeck.
Renato Scrollavezza graduated from the Cremona School of Violin Making. He established himself in Parma and became one of the senior figures in 20th century Italian lutherie.
Igino Sderci trained as a wood carver and was initially self-taught as a violin maker before becoming a pupil of L. Bisiach in Sienna during World War I.
Luciano Sderci was the son and pupil of Igino Sderci and created work similar to that of his father. He exhibited in Cremona in 1949 and received medals in Rome in 1954.
George Seaver credited with 92 instruments. The model was Guarneri and featured red-brown varnish.
Lájos Sebok was a pupil of Braun in Szeged and worked for Remenyi in Budapest as well as Babos in Szeged. He established himself in Orosháza in 1913 and produced good professional work.

maker_name: Seidel, Adolf K. F. W. B.
info: Adolf K. F. W. B. Seidel was the son and pupil of Carl Friedrich Seidel. He produced good professional work. He was branded: `K. F. W. B. Ad. Seidel'.
Christian Wilhelm Seidel was the son of Johann Gottlob Seidel. He became a member of the violin makers guild in 1838.
Otto Seifert worked as a pupil of A. Bruckner.

F. A. Sennett worked for J. H. Stamps in Fort Worth, Texas. He was a prolific maker, credited with over 450 instruments across three personal models designated 'A', 'B', and 'C'. His varnish was golden or orange-brown. A manuscript label was used.

Carl Sennewald was an electrical engineer and a self-taught violin maker. He subsequently attended the Markneukirchen Violin-Making School and was active in Munich in 1905, Rudolstadt in 1907, Berlin in 1911, and Cologne from 1925.

Fabrizio Senta was known primarily for a viola da braccio located in the Claudius Museum, Copenhagen, which had a manuscript label dated 1716. He is recognized as one of the earliest makers to have made violins in Turin, although both Vannes and Henley claimed he worked there without providing supporting evidence.
Giorgio Serafin was the nephew and pupil of Santo Serafin. He worked for Montagnana and took over the business in 1751, marrying his daughter Antonia.

Santo Serafin is thought to have moved to Venice from his hometown of Udine around 1720, although his earliest labels date from 1725. It seems he set up his own workshop in 1733, so it is likely that he worked for other Venetian makers in the interim and developed his individual approach.

Paul Serdet apprenticed to Gaillard and J. Derazay. He worked for H. C. Silvestre in Lyons from 1877 to 1894. In 1894, he established himself independently in Paris at 28 rue de Faubourg Poissonière. He was awarded gold medals in 1889 and 1900. His work featured very fine craftsmanship on Stradivari and Guarneri models, with good transparent varnish in suitable colors.

Joseph (Giuseppe) Settin was an active luthier from 1904. He studied sculpture at the Academy of Fine Arts in Milan. In 1920, he moved to New York. His personal model was classically derived and featured red-brown oil and spirit varnish.

Originally self-taught, Gaetano Sgarabotto moved to Milan in 1901 to work with Riccardo Antoniazzi and later Leandro Bisiach . He returned to Vincenza in 1905 and moved to Parma in 1926, where he eventually ended up for good in 1957 after periods in Vincenza ... and Brescia.

Pietro Sgarabotto was the son and pupil of Gaetano Sgarabotto, working as a violinist and maker in Vicenza in 1920 and in Parma in 1930. He taught at the Parma Violin Making School from 1928 to 1932 and alongside his father from 1929 to 1936, later teaching at the Cremona School from 1959 to 1973.

Antonio Sgarbi was the son and pupil of Giuseppe Giuseppe Sgarbi . The family moved to Rome in 1878, where Antonio worked in his father's workshop until 1905, when he became luthier to the Bellini Conservatoire in Palermo, a position he held until 1911.
Giuseppe Sgarbi was known as `Jarino'. He was active in Finale Emilia from 1841 to 1890, later moving to Modena and finally to Rome.
John Henry Shal was a pupil of Frank Markusic in 1963. He completed 92 violins, seven violas, and one cello, using Stradivari and Guarneri models along with various commercial varnishes.
Elbridge G. Shaw worked in a workshop on Pleasant Street, Worcester. He was a prolific maker of many instruments of an eccentric experimental type, often utilizing unusual species of local wood.
Ian Shepherd was employed in the bow department of the firm of W.E. Hill & Sons until 1976. His bows for the firm bear the number 17.

Most sources reported small violins featuring good golden brown varnish; their current status is unknown. One viola, illustrated in Hamma (Italian Violin makers), displayed exaggeratedly bulging archings, Germanic soundholes, and a grotesque scroll, indicating it was likely a later 18th century work.
Leading American bow maker and restorer based in Port Townsend, Washington. Worked for Hans Weisshaar then established own shop in 1983.

Ettore Siega was a pupil of E. Degani and G. Schwarz. He received several gold medals. His style, derived from Degani, was sometimes a bit mannered. The soundhole featured sharply emphasised fluting all around its outer edge. The arching appeared rather boxy, and the purfling was wide. The scroll was somewhat weak. He used a deep glossy red-brown varnish that was well finished.
Iginio Siega was the son and pupil of Ettore Siega. He produced work similar to that of his father. In 1919, he received a bronze medal in Rome. Below the back button, he was branded with 'I. S. '.
Born near Nancy, violin maker Hippolyte Silvestre moved to Paris around the late 1820s to work for J.B. Vuillaume .

Hippolyte Chrétien Silvestre was the nephew, pupil, and successor of Pierre and Hippolyte Silvestre in Lyons, and the son of Pierre and Hippolyte's sister. Initially trained in Mirecourt, he joined his uncles and took over the business in 1865.
Pierre Silvestre apprenticed locally in Mirecourt before moving to Paris to work for Nicolas Lupot and Gand père . In 1829 he left the Gand workshop to start his own business in Lyon.
Pierre Silvestre apprenticed locally in Mirecourt before moving to Paris to work for Nicolas Lupot and Gand père . In 1829 he left the Gand workshop to start his own business ... in Lyon.
Elek P. Simon was an amateur maker. He used a Guarneri-derived model and applied oil or spirit varnishes. Stained wood was present, and commercial pre-cut necks were in evidence.
Franz Simon made a citizen of Salzburg in 1791 and was appointed luthier to the court. His work featured a Klotz model with narrow, outwardly projecting corners.

Nicolas Simon was a fine bow maker from Mirecourt, associated with the Pecatte school, known for strong heads, sticks of rounded triangular section, and frogs with an open throat. He established his business under his own name around 1840.
Nicolas Simon was a bow maker, the son of Jean Simon and the nephew of Nicolas Simon "FR". He worked in the Mirecourt workshop of his uncle.

Pierre Simon apprenticed and worked in his hometown of Mirecourt until 1838, when he moved to Paris to join the workshop of Dominique Peccatte . The Peccatte influence is quite strong in Simon's work, but in 1840 he decamped for the Vuillaume workshop, ... where he stayed until 1844 and produced many fine bows, some of the "picture" variety.

Amadeo Simonazzi was a pupil of S. Scarampella. He was assisted by his son Riccardo, born in 1929. He produced 130 violins and several cellos based on Guadagnini and Guarneri models. His varnish was a fine transparent golden-red and later golden-yellow. Simonazzi Amedeo worked in S. Vittoria (Emilia) in the year 1946. Simonazzi Amedeo made instruments in S. Vittoria in the year 19...

Charles Simonin apprenticed and assisted J. B. Vuillaume in Paris. He established himself independently in Geneva in 1841 and moved to Toulouse in 1849. He received an honourable mention at the Paris Exposition in 1855. His work included Guarneri models, as well as some double-purfled models in the style of Vuillaume. He branded his instruments with 'S. C. '.
Francois Simonin was born and trained in Mirecourt. He emigrated to the USA and was employed as the head of Eastman's violin repair department.
Nicolas Simoutre apprenticed to Lupot in Paris. He established himself in Mirecourt in 1817.
Nicolas-Eugéne Simoutre worked as the son and pupil of Nicholas Simoutre. He was employed by Darche in Paris in 1852 and by Roth and Schwartz in Strasbourg from 1856 to 1860.
Worked in association with John Simpson , below. Presumed to be his brother.
An important business in the London musical trade in the ifith century. Presumed to be a merchant rather than a maker himself.
Robert Simpson was an amateur maker who used commercial varnish produced by Angell of Bristol.

Thomas Simpson was a piano repairer in Walsall until 1898. Thereafter, he became a self-taught maker of violins and bows in Peterborough. He moved to Birmingham in 1900 and worked as a repairer. He retired to Brixham in 1925. His work was inconsistent and unrefined, yet careful. Violins were sometimes branded with initials, a date, and a number, while bows were branded with: SIMPSON BIRMINGHAM.

William Sinclair was an amateur maker known for a Guarneri model, as well as a unique design featuring sympathetic strings and another with double courses resembling a mandolin. His work was inconsistent and unrefined, utilizing spirit varnish. Approximately 40 instruments were completed.
Frank Sindelar was a pupil of John Hornsteiner in Chicago. He established himself independently there in 1917.

Axel Albert Sington studied under E. Lindholm and E. Hjorth in Copenhagen. He established himself independently in Stockholm from 1919. His work featured a fine red-brown varnish. He completed 50 violins and eight cellos. He received gold medals at Göteborg and Stockholm. He died in a motor-cycle accident.

Jules Sirgent worked as a pupil of E. Bourlier. He later worked for Thibouville-Lamy and subsequently for E. Germaine and L. Bernardel in Paris. He established himself independently at 105 Avenue Gambetta, Paris. In 1900, he was awarded a diploma of honour. His instruments featured classical Italian models with golden-yellow or yellow-brown oil varnish.

Ambrogio Sironi was a pupil of R. Antoniazzi, who held him in high regard. He produced excellent work based on classical Cremonese models. In 1932, he received a prize in Rome. His work became rare and sought-after due to his early death at the age of 33, with cellos and violas being particularly difficult to find.

John Walter Skinner apprenticed as a cabinet maker. He worked as a silversmith and became an amateur violin maker in retirement starting in 1908. He established his professional practice in 1923 on Psalter Lane, Sheffield, and moved to Dawlish in 1930. His work included Guarneri, Stradivari, and personal models.
Alexander Smillie was active from 1887. He was primarily self-taught but appeared to have received assistance from George Duncan and owned two of his violins.
Andrew Young Smillie was the son, pupil, and assistant of Alexander Smillie . He was credited with over 200 violins, as well as some violas and cellos.

Arthur Edward Smith was a self-taught maker of significant achievement and influence, trained as an engineer and an amateur violinist. Inspired by Hill's Stradivari, his Life and Work motivated him to pursue violin making.
Bert Smith apprenticed as a motor engineer in Bowness. He began making violins as a self-taught amateur in 1938.
Dennis G. Smith was a pupil of William Luff. He created eight violins, one Tertis model viola, and two cellos, utilizing his own Michelman formula oil varnish.

Henry Smith was an important viol maker. Several extant examples, uncertainly attributed to him, were found in museums and elsewhere, but he was generally regarded as a leading figure. His work was very highly skilled.
John Smith made a Guarneri-derived model. The work was unrefined and featured oil varnish in various shades. Teddington, London, W.

London maker who was employed by Peter Wamsley in the last years of his life and then took over the shop, the Harp and Hautboy in Piccadilly, having acquired the business from Wamsley’s widow in 1751. Smith also inherited Wamsley’s Royal Warrant and became violin maker to both George III and the Prince of York, remaining so until his death.

Thomas Smith initially established his work in Lakewood before moving to Indianapolis around 1950. He made approximately 73 violins, 12 violas, 3 cellos, and several viols, using Stradivari models and spirit varnish. Five viols by him were kept at the University of Illinois.

William Smith was a maker based in Stockport who produced undistinguished work in the London trade style. One of his instruments featured decorative purfling designs on a back made of bird's-eye maple and included a manuscript label.
Active second half of 19th century in Cambridge, MA.
William Smith established his workshop at Sycamore Street, Sheffield. One known cello is a Walmsley model featuring fine golden-brown soft textured varnish.
The relationship to Thomas Smith (I) and the surviving work remained unknown. Henley cited the label.
Early 20th-century maker based in Wetherby, Yorkshire. Label: 'William Edward Smith maker, Wetherby, 1906, no.20'
William J. Smith completed over 100 instruments. The varnish used was golden-brown and red-brown oil.
Egidius Snoeck comes from a family of organ builders in Tournai. He is a pupil and successor of P.
Louis Socquet was born in Mirecourt. He established himself in Paris at the sign 'Au Genie de l'Harmonie', Place du Vieux Louvre.

Ettore Soffritti was born in Ferrara and is one of Italy's most revered makers of the early 20th century. On his earliest labels he indicated that he was a student of his father Luigi but given that the quality of Ettore's instruments is so much more advanced than that of his father it is widely accepted that he was influenced by other makers including Giuseppe Fiorini and Augusto Pollastri. ... />Soffritti's instruments show an extremely high standard of workmanship and precision, coupled with exquisite materials and refinement.

Luigi Soffritti was a carpenter and a self-taught violin maker known for his work featuring flat arches, planned materials, and broad edges, complemented by a good golden-brown varnish. He was the father of Ettore Soffritti, who succeeded and substantially excelled him in the craft.

Remo Solferino was a pupil of E. Soffritti. He was active in Mantua until 1940. In 1953, he moved to Verona, where he almost immediately ceased work. He completed about 50 instruments, which were imitations of classical models with a good golden-yellow varnish. His manuscript labels read: Remo Solferino / fece in Mantua / l'anno 19..
Angelo Soliani, active from the 1770s, was one of the notable 18th-century makers from Modena. His craftsmanship demonstrated a direct knowledge of Cremonese instruments and was reminiscent of G.
Joseph Maurice Somny was the son and pupil of J. Somny-Ouchard.
D. Soriot made instruments in the Laberte Factory from 1890 to 1930 in Mirecourt.

Spirito Sorsana was probably a pupil of Giofreddo Cappa, as his workmanship and style were closely related. His violins featured low, flat arching across the breast, distinctive purfling with long splits in the white center, and soundholes that were set low and widely spaced, lightly fluted in the lower wings.

Berthold Specht was active from 1907. He established his workshop in Jena in 1921. Over 50 violins, 10 violas, and 10 cellos were completed. His models were derived from Stradivari and Guarneri. The varnish used was golden-brown.
Worked for Metelka and Vitacaek in Prague. Established independently in Kiev, Ukraine from 1894, where he was briefly, assisted by his nephew J.

Son of Premysl Spidlen. Studied with his father and then in Mittenwald. Has many awards for his work, including both first and second prizes at the 2003 Triennale competition in Cremona. Now works alongside Premysl in Prague.

Otakar Frantisek Spidlen was the son, pupil, and successor of Frantisek Spidlen . He devoted most of his time to managing the shop and performing fine restoration and repair work as a very refined and successful maker.

Premysl Otakar Spidlen, son and pupil of Otakar Spidlen, was a highly influential maker of the 20th century, known for his immaculate workmanship and dedication to replicating the work of Stradivari and Guarneri. He was significantly influenced by contemporary theories of plate tuning and acoustical manipulation, contributing greatly through his own research and experience.
János Spiegel apprenticed to P. Pilát.
Jóssef Spitz studied under L. Rochert. He worked for Reményi in Budapest and Lenhardt in Pécs. From 1925, he established himself independently in Budapest. His work focused on classical models.
Eugen Sprenger was the youngest son and pupil of Anton Sprenger (II). He worked with his brother Adolf in Munich starting in 1900, after gaining experience in Switzerland and England.

Josef Ferdinand Sprenger was the son and pupil of Carl Boniface Sprenger. He also worked for Vauchel before assuming his role in his father's shop in 1875. His work included good craftsmanship on classical models, and he also plucked instruments.
Jerome Bonaparte Squier was a farmer and shoemaker. He was an active violin maker in Lewiston and established his work in Boston in 1881.

Victor Carroll Squier was the adopted son and pupil of Jerome Squier. After learning the craft from his father, he worked for William Peters in Worcester, Massachusetts, as well as Treffle Gervais and George Drapier in Boston.
Ermanno Squilla worked in Rome for G. Corsini and S. De Lellis. He made violins, violas, cellos, bows, and guitars. In 1980, he was awarded second prize at Bagnacavallo.
Daniel Achatius Stadlmann produced very fine and accurate work on the Stainer model. The arching had a medium height that was not unnecessarily exaggerated.

Johann Josef Stadlmann was the son, pupil, and successor of Daniel Stadlmann, appointed as a luthier to the Viennese court. His work, while similar to that of his father, was generally regarded as inferior.

Michael Ignaz Stadlmann was the son and pupil of Johann Joseph Stadlmann. He succeeded to the position of luthier for the Viennese court and served as a double bassist in the orchestra at the Royal Chapel from 1799 to 1813.
The personal model featured full arching and long corners that projected outward. It utilized good materials and had a golden-orange oil varnish. It was branded below the back button.

John Stagg was employed by the firm of W.E. Hill & Sons until 1983, when he left to pursue independent work. His bows for the Hill firm are marked with the number 20. Also made bows for the Machold firm in Bremen branded "Machold Bremen - J. W. S."

The premier maker of the Austro-German school, Jacob Stainer may have been a student of Nicolò Amati in Cremona, though historical records are not complete enough to substantiate that assumption. In any case, his work, the earliest of which dates from the 1630s, bears strong similarities to Amati's.
Joseph Henry Stamps established a shop in Fort Worth around 1938. He created a Guarneri model and also produced copies of individual master instruments. His work featured a hard brown varnish.
Carlton F. Stanley was a pupil and assistant of his uncle Freelan Oscar from 1924.

F. E. Stanley was the grandson of Liberty Stanley and the twin brother of Freelan Oscar Stanley. He worked alongside his brother in Newton. The instruments were of the Stradivari model, featuring variable varnish; some were possibly sprayed, while others appeared as dull brown, and some had a decent red hue.

Freelan Oscar Stanley developed photography processes for Kodak and built his own historically significant steam-powered car, the 'Stanley Steamer, ' in 1906. He was a highly proficient amateur violin maker with a distinctive personal model derived from Stradivari.
Robert A. Stanley was a prolific maker. He was credited with 300 violins, violas, cellos, and double basses.
Albert Starck was the son, pupil, and successor of August Stark. He utilized a Stradivari model.
Anton Stauber was a pupil of G. Tiefenbrunner. He established himself independently in Cologne in 1874. His work was fine and accurate.

James Steele was a self-taught maker. He created the 1716 `Messie' Stradivari and personal models. He used fine materials along with various spirit and oil varnishes. Generally, his work was well finished, with significant attention paid to plate tuning and related acoustic development.
Giuseppe Stefanini studied under L. Vistoli and P.
Andreas Stelzer was cited by Vannes and Henley as a pupil of Hieronymus Amati (II). His work is currently unknown.

Alfred Stelzner studied mathematics and physics at Heidelberg University. He designed a radical new form of violin based on elliptical shapes, which were constructed for him by Wiedemann in Wiesbaden, along with his equally original cellone and violotta.
Herbert Stengel was a pupil of Petzold. He was employed by E. H. Roth and P. Petzold in La Chaux-de-Fonds.
Willibald Conrad Stenger was active in Topeka, Kansas, from 1903 and in Chicago, Illinois, from 1909 to 1942. His models were derived from Guarneri.
Charles Stéphan produced commercially made violins, violas, and cellos of good quality that featured deep red varnish.
Dale Walker Stevens was a self-taught violin and bow maker. He worked for the Salt Lake City Music Company, where he finished and varnished prefabricated instruments.
C. G. Stewart was a cellist and cello maker. He used a Stradivari model and featured purfling that came to a point beneath the back button. The varnish was orange-brown.

E. Stieber was a pupil of M. Schuster in Markneukirchen from 1921 to 1924. He worked for F. Reiner and F. Staerke in Hamburg. He established himself independently in Mannheim in 1927, Leipzig in 1931, and Tübingen in 1937. In 1940, he returned to Markneukirchen to work under P. Seckendorf and thereafter finally settled in Tübingen. His work was of good professional quality.
David Stirrat lived in Glasgow from 1794 to 1811. He established himself as a violin maker in Edinburgh starting in 1811.

Jacob Stöhr created fine work on the Stainer model. The arching was medium, and he used good materials. The table was usually made in one piece. It featured a clear pale golden-brown varnish. He was a Lauten- und Geigenmacher in St. Pölten in 1829.
Carlo Storioni was a fictitious name utilized in mass-produced factory instruments.

Though opinions of his work vary, Lorenzo Storioni merits high praise for helping to keep the great Cremonese tradition of violin making afloat in the late 18th century. An admiration for the work of Guarneri 'del Gesù' is evident, but in general Storioni followed 'del Gesù's spirit of individuality more than he copied his ... or any other maker's instruments.
Bernhard Stoss was the son of Joseph Anton Stoss. He moved to Vienna in 1806 and became a citizen in 1813.
Ignaz Stoss was the son and pupil of Eustachius Stoss. His work was rarely encountered, although small cellos were cited. He used a good transparent red varnish.
Martin (Johann Martin) Stoss. Stephan Stoss.

George Theodore Stott worked as an amateur for several years before establishing a professional business in Copperas Hill, Liverpool, around 1910. After this building was destroyed by bombing during the Second World War, he relocated to 69 Renshaw Street.

Harry L. Stoutenburgh was a self-taught violin maker who was active from 1935. He completed approximately 100 instruments. His models were derived from Stradivari and utilized local materials along with various types of red-brown oil varnish.

Antonio Stradivari is almost universally regarded as the greatest violin maker in history, and the value of his instruments reflects that exalted status. He was born in circa 1644, and his 71 year career is a vivid account of early mastery, fluid and ingenious innovation, and indelible craftsmanship and artistry.

Son of renowned Cremonese master Antonio Stradivari , Francesco Stradivari probably assisted his father in the family workshop starting in the 1690s alongside his brother Omobono , who specialized in the business side of the operation. He survived his father ... by only six years, during which time he was not particularly productive, but several printed labels from 1742 indicate that he did continue to make violins.

Omobono Stradivari was one of Cremonese titan Antonio Stradivari's sons from his first marriage. From around 1700 onward he seems to have made instruments from time to time, alongside his brother Francesco , but was largely involved in the business aspects ... of the enterprise rather than shop work and traveled frequently.
Josef Straub was the son of Simon Straub (III), as noted below. This work was typical of the family. He was a Geigenmacher in Röthenbach in 1806.
Jean Striebig was a Maître-Luthier based at 8, Rue Estivant, Mirecourt (Vosges). He produced quality work on both classical and personal models. The year was noted as 19..
Casper Strnad was a pupil of T. Hulinzky.
Michael Strobl worked for J. J. Held. He established himself independently in Linkstrasse, Berlin, from 1894. His violins and bows were of good quality.
Established in 1921. Name usually inscribed in ink with date and city on both the top and back.
Johann Stübiger trained with his uncle Adam Stiibinger. He worked for Lutz starting in 1883 and for Zach from 1885, both in Vienna.
H. C.

Johann Christian Süss was one of the finest bow makers of the German school and was sometimes called the 'German Tourte. ' A pupil of Knopf in Dresden, he created models based on Tourte and Voirin. The sticks were stained dark red. Bows were sold under the brand: WEICHOLD.
Masakichi Suzuki became interested in violins at the age of 14. His first instrument was based on Brescian lines and featured a slab cut front.

Born in Russia and studied as a violinist before moving to Israel in 1972. He studied violin making in Tel Aviv under Amnon Weinstein before enrolling in the violin making school in Cremona, Italy. Once graduated, he opened his own workshop in Israel before relocating to New York to work with Rene Morel at the Jacques Francais Violin Shop. In 1986 he opened his own New York workshop.
Seorim Swaine established himself as an amateur maker and repairer. He was a pupil of James McCauley and was active from 1910 in Rochester.
Verne Swan created a personal model featuring broad edges and pointed corners. It had a shaded orange-brown varnish and included a manuscript label.
Georg Szag was active in the USA starting in 1887 and produced good professional work based on a Stradivari-derived model.
Joseph Szendrey was a pupil of Toth in Budapest. He was a casualty of World War II. His work featured fine craftsmanship with selected materials and varnish, based on Cremonese models.
Earnest (Ernö) Szép was a self-taught maker until he apprenticed with L. Tonté around 1936.

After his apprenticeship with Alajos Engleder and Samuel Nemessányi , Hungarian violin maker Béla Szepessy went to work for Thomas Zach in Vienna from 1874 to 1879. After a probable stint in Munich, he relocated to London, where he remained for 40 ... years.

Jacques Tainturie illustrated one bow by Millant/Raffin. No details were known about the maker. It featured fine work with a screw adjuster, ivory facings on the handle and frog, and an ebony frog and button. It was branded: TAINTURIE.

Georg (Gyorgy) Tanczer was a pupil of Nemessanyi. He established himself in Budapest in 1882 and in Debreczen in 1899, and he was later employed by the Stowasser Company in Budapest. His works included copies of Stradivari and Guarneri, featuring golden-orange amber varnish.

The work exhibited a style reminiscent of Grancino, though it was of second rate quality. The outline was poorly governed, featuring small corners and soundholes that leaned outward. The scroll resembled Grancino's style but lacked a polished finish. It had a fine pale golden-yellow varnish.
Giovanni Georgio Taningard was a pupil and son-in-law of A. Platner.
Giuseppe Tarasconi was a double bass player and luthier. He studied under G.

Mirco Tarasconi was the son of Giuseppe Tarasconi and worked for Barnard in Liege, Laurent in Brussels, and Ornati in Milan. He was a student of Giuseppe Tarasconi, and his models were derived from Stradivari, featuring original designs.
Aubrey James Tarr exhibited a violin and viola at The Hague in 1949.

Thomas Tarr was a double bass player and a maker and dealer in musical instruments. His violins were of good quality, featuring full arched and well-articulated modeling. The varnish was a good golden-brown. Several double basses were known to exist.

William Tarr was a double bass player and maker, with his first instruments dated 1829. Over 200 basses were produced, later with the assistance of his sons Thomas, Joseph, and Shelley, which gained a very high reputation.
Bartolomeo Tassini created a Stainer model. The work was rather unrefined.
Pietro (Peter) Tatar was the son and pupil of Pietro Tatar. He established himself in New York from approximately 1960 to 1981. One violin and one viola were recorded.

Eduard Tauscher was a pupil of W. Meisel. He established himself independently in Erlbach in 1889. From 1908, he became a large volume producer of trade instruments under the name of Eduard Tauscher and Co. Various grades were supplied. Eduard Tauscher & Co. / Geigenbaustatt / Erlbach - Markneukirchen / Model Stradivari.

In 1956 David Taylor began his apprenticeship in the bow workshop of the firm of W.E. Hill & Sons, where he became the firm's chief restorer of bows. He left to start his own workshop in 1966. Taylor's Hill bows are marked with the numeral 13.
