Malcolm Taylor joined the firm of W.E. Hill & Sons in 1949 and returned in 1956 after leaving for military service. He left the firm to pursue independent work as a bowmaker in 1973. Taylor's Hill bows bear the number 8.

Nathan Sleeper Taylor actively worked in Lewiston, ME from 1865 to 1918, completing approximately 140 instruments that showcased fine workmanship. He utilized Stradivari and Guarneri models, employing his own oil varnish and local materials. His instruments are labeled ‘Made by Nathan S. Taylor / 19.. Lewiston, Me.’

Robert Taylor was the son of Stephen Oliver. He used a model derived from Stradivari and Guarneri. Fine materials were employed, and he created his own oil varnish in suitable colors. His work included violins, violas, cellos, and bows.

Although David Tecchler was born in Lechbruck near Füssen in circa 1666 and arrived in Rome only in his late 20s, he is nevertheless considered the most prominent maker of the Roman school. Little is known about his working life outside of historical documents confirming his presence in Rome.

Giovanni Tedesco created a fine Stradivari model cello in the style of Gagliano, characterized by plain materials and a nicely concentric scroll with a thin chamfer. The varnish was a pale golden yellow-brown. His work bears a resemblance to that of Vincenzo Sannino, who may have been his pupil, although Tedesco's instruments tend to utilize plainer materials than the more flamboyant Sannino.
George Temple derived models from Stradivari and Guarneri. He also created large violas based on those of A. Richardson.

Eugen Tenucci was of Tuscan origin. He studied under Ziist and served as the head of the violin department at Hug & Co. in Zurich starting in 1899. His instruments featured Stradivari and Guarneri models, utilizing oil and spirit varnishes of suitable colors. He was well regarded as a connoisseur and expert.
Bowmaker trained in Mirecourt, based in Quimper.
Pietro Termanini is the brother of Giuseppe Termanini. He is known for similar work, which may be of slightly better quality overall. He also creates lutes and keyboard instruments.

Gerlando Terrana was a violinist who was active as a luthier in Milan from 1924. He completed about 100 violins, utilizing personal and Gagliano models. The varnish used was bright golden-yellow or red-brown.

Carlo Antonio Testore was the son, pupil, and successor of Carlo Giuseppe Testore , at the sign `dell'Aquila' in Contrada Larga. He was a more prolific maker than his father, though generally a less conscientious craftsman.
Carlo Giuseppe Testore was active in Milan from around 1683. He was a pupil of G.

Paolo Antonio Testore was the son, pupil, and successor of Carlo Giuseppe Testore . He was the least skilled maker in the family, often producing coarse instruments characterized by high arches and shortcuts, such as scratched and inked purfling, an unfluted pegbox, and poor-quality wood.
Moses A. Tewkesbury was a pupil of Abraham Prescott. He made American 'church basses' and double basses, which were generally unpurfled.

Gabriel Adolphe Thibout was the son and pupil of Jacques-Pierre Thibout . He created work similar to that of his father but developed his own model inspired by the Guarneri del Gesii 'Rode' violin from 1734.
Jacques-Pierre Thibout worked for Koliker in Paris starting in 1796. He established himself independently at 24 rue Montmartre in 1807.

Pierre-Louis Thibout worked at the same address in Grande Rue, probably as the brother of Antoine Thibout. His work was unrefined and based on an Amati-derived model, featuring a golden-yellow varnish.
Bow maker / Violin maker (fl. 1857 – 1968)

Produced work of the highest quality in the Viennese style. The high Stainer arch was somewhat reduced after 1775. He created an elegant long model. In Vienna, he used a dark semi-transparent chocolate varnish. From 1750, he changed his recipe to a different formulation.

Probably Johann Georg Thir’s nephew. His sisters married Sebastian Dallinger and Franz Geissenhof and for a few years the three had their workshops in the same house. Ran a large and successful workshop, producing a wide range of instruments from cheap models to nicely crafted examples.

Joseph Thiriot founded an important business that was later taken over by Laberte et Magni. His work was characteristic of Mirecourt, featuring a large flat model with yellow varnish. It was branded internally as: Tiriot / A Paris or Thiriot / A La Ville/ De Cremone. The manuscript label read: Tiriot / A Paris.

Adolf Thomä was the son and pupil of Hermann Thoma. He worked at Hohendorf near Brambach from 1890 to 1938. His work was notable for classic French and German models. He received assistance from his sons Albert and Arthur.
Alexander Thoma was active from 1887. He was the son and pupil of Jacob Thoma .

Arthur Adolf Thomä was a bow maker. He was the son and pupil of Adolf Thoma. He established himself independently in Hohendorf in 1931. In 1938, he became a master of the corporation. His work included good craftsmanship on classical models. He branded his bows as ARTHUR THOMA.
Jacob Thoma established himself as a clock maker in Vienna. He became a self-taught violin maker after his daughter's success as a violinist.
Mathias Thoma is a trade name for bows manufactured by Roderich Paesold of Bubenreuth, Bavaria, Germany.
Leading contemporary bow maker, based in Paris. Has trained many of the younger generation of makers and his bows are prized for their Sartory-like qualities.

Watkin Thomas established himself in Matthew Street, Swansea. He produced about 70 violins, several violas, and two cellos. The work was inconsistent, with some pieces unpurfled and exhibiting very bulbous arching. The varnish quality was poor.
Thomassin studied under Clement. He established himself independently in Paris around 1825 and relocated to Le Mans around 1850. A fine Stradivari copy with yellow spirit varnish is cited by Vannes.

Claude Auguste Thomassin, like his first cousin Louis, broke with the family's saddle-making tradition to become a bow maker. He apprenticed with Charles Nicolas Bazin , whose influence was paramount in Mirecourt during the late 19th century, and began working for the firm of Gand ... & Bernardel in Paris around 1885.

Born in 1893, Justin Thomassin was the son of Victor Thomassin and made bows in the English tradition working for George Withers and Dykes & Sons among others. His bows were often branded with the shop for whom they were made but occasionally bear the brand "T. Lejeune" in the style of his father Victor who branded some bows "T Victor."
Louis Joseph Thomassin was a bow maker. He was a pupil of C.
Victor Thomassin, son of Nicolas Thomassin, was likely apprenticed to C. N.

By the time Charles and Samuel Thompson took charge of their family's workshop in 1763, it had become one of many London violin shops who sold instruments by various outside makers bearing the firm's labels. The business began with their father Peter around 1746 and by 1761 was known as Thompson & Sons.

Brother of Peter Thompson. Succeeded to the business of Daniel Wright: formerly the workshop of Barak Norman at the sign of the 'Bass Violin' (or the 'Bass Viol)' near the Chapter House on the north side of St.
Nathaniel Thorley was a musician and a part-time maker. His work represented typical provincial English craftsmanship of that period.

Thomas Thorley was the son and pupil of Nathaniel Thornley. Three instruments were cited in the Hill Archive: a cello featuring a flat arch, broad purfling with negligible edge flute, a large head, and 'tawny varnish, probably oil'; a small viola from 1879 with 'exceptionally long' soundholes and dark brown varnish; and a violin dated 1865. T. Thorley / Failsworth 1868 / Maker.
Henry Thorowgood was an entrepreneurial dealer in trade work made by outworkers. The shop was established at the sign of the 'Violin & Guitar' in the North Piazza, Royal Exchange.
Charles Thouvenel was active in Luneville from 1788, but his work was branded from Paris. He produced good commercial work that featured red-brown varnish. His brand was: `Thouvenel a Paris'.

Nicolas Henry Thouvenel worked in the usual Mirecourt style on a large Stradivari model. He produced violins and cellos that featured a fine finish, often with a yellow to orange varnish and good one-piece backs. He also made vielles and plucked instruments.

John Thow created enlarged models of Stradivari and Guarneri. His work featured competent craftsmanship with pale golden-brown varnish. It was branded below the back button: `Thow Dundee'. The date was marked on the scroll.
Johann Georg Thumhardt was presumed to be the son of Johann Thumhardt . He was appointed as the luthier to the court of Amberg.

Johann Stephan Thumhardt was a pupil of Buchstetter. He established himself independently in Straubing from 1769. He was considered the best maker of this family. His elegant model featured long open 'c' bouts, medium arching, and a golden-brown varnish with good depth and transparency. He also made double basses, viols, and plucked instruments.
Xavier Thumhardt was active in Ingolstadt from 1839. He moved to Munich in 1847. Few violins are known, as he was chiefly occupied with guitars and zithers.

Jakob Tiedemann was a self-taught maker known for Stradivari, Guarneri, and personal models. He created several copies of the Guarneri del Gesù 'Cannone' violin from 1743, which was owned by Paganini.

Joachim Tielke was the son of Gottfried Tielke and a celebrated and prolific maker of lutes, viols of all kinds, violins, and cellos. His workshop in Hamburg produced over 150 instruments, many of which are preserved in museums across Europe.
Hans Tietgen was self-taught, receiving some guidance from C. G.
C. W. Tiller was a cabinet maker active in Bournemouth from 1927. He also made violins as an amateur and produced good work using fine golden-yellow oil varnish.

Thomas Tilley was a presumed pupil of Joseph Hill. He established himself in St. James' around 1744. He was a prolific maker, particularly known for cellos. Amatise model violins were also recognized. His workmanship was fair, though not of the consistent quality of Hill. He used a thin golden-yellow spirit varnish.

Léon Tinceau established his work in the Rue du Bannier, Orleans. He produced fine work based on Cremonese models, featuring exceptional varnish that was sometimes shaded to imitate age. His pieces were branded internally with 'Tinceau, Orleans' and included an inscription with the number, date, and signature.

Arrigo Tivoli-Fiorini was the nephew and pupil of G. Fiorini. He was a cellist and luthier active in San Remo starting in 1925. His work featured Cremonese models. He received diplomas in Rome, Padua, and Cremona.

Richard Tobin was born in Ireland, where he is thought to have apprenticed with Thomas Perry and worked independently before moving to London with the encouragement of Vincenzo Panormo sometime before 1810. In the 1810s he seems to have worked ... for Henry Lockey Hill before setting up his own shop in 1823.

Ernst Edmond Todt was a pupil of E. L. Giitter and later worked for A. Wunderlich, Ledere, and Greinberg. He established himself independently in Markneukirchen. His work was of good professional quality.
Heinrich Hermann Todt worked for C. Ficker in Budapest. He established himself in Markneukirchen in 1900. His trade work catered to dealers and wholesalers.

Domenico Tomassini was a musician and luthier. He created Cremonese and personal models, using golden-yellow or red varnish. A prize was awarded for a double bass in Rome in 1954. Tomassini Domenico /fu Mariano / Da Viterbo Anno 1949. Op 26.
Giasone Tomasucci was a pupil of Leopardi in Spoleto. He established himself in Rome in 1926. His work included Stradivari, Amati, and personal models. He generously applied golden-orange varnish.
Carlo Annibale Tononi was the son and pupil of Giovanni Tononi . He worked in Bologna, largely in his father's style, until 1717 when he relocated to Venice.
This maker appears in reference books but there appears to be no evidence that he existed.
Giovanni Tononi, born in Bologna, was the son of Gaspare, a case-maker, and was active from 1688. He produced fine and distinguished instruments, though their quality varied.

Timothy Toomey apprenticed as a chemist. He became a professional violinist in 1891 and produced 12 violins and one viola in Bolsover, Derbyshire, up to 1897. From 1920, he established himself as a violin dealer and repairer in Fulham, London. His personal model was derived from Cremonese designs, and he was known for his accurate work using orange-red varnish.

Angelo Toppani recorded as a 'violinaro' near Chiesa Novara in Rome from 1742 to 1748 and from 1760 'near the Palazzo del Drago'. His work was fair in the Tecchler style and featured pale golden-brown varnish.
Johannes (János) Tóth was a very influential Hungarian maker. He was the son of a cabinet maker and a pupil of J.

Theresia Toth studied the violin at Budapest Conservatoire and was the daughter and pupil of Janos Toth. She began making violins under her father's guidance in 1916 and received awards in Venice and Budapest.
Joseph Alexis Tournier was a Paris-based maker known for crafting violins, violas, cellos, and guitars. His work included a guitar located in the Musee de la Musique in Paris, branded as 'J.
Son and pupil of Nicolas Leonard Tourte, below. His contribution to the output of the Tourte workshop is not easily assessed.

Also known as Tourte 'le Jeune' to distinguish him from his older brother Nicolas Léonard , François Xavier Tourte is probably the most important figure in the history of modern bow making. His violin, viola, and cello bows are exquisite and command the highest prices for their extraordinary craftsmanship, elegance and appreciation ... by players.
Sons of Nicolas Pierre Tourte, the brothers Nicolas Léonard and François Xavier worked together and later separately in Paris.

While his younger brother François Xavier has historically been credited with the creation of the modern bow, research shows that Nicolas Léonard Tourte 'l'Ainé' began making bows before his brother, during a time of great innovation in French bow making. Desire for a wider tonal palette and accompanying modifications in ... playing technique in the mid-18th century led to sweeping new demands in bow construction.
Bow maker / Violin maker (c. 1700 – 1764)
Rodolfo Tramonti received medals in Bolzano, Rome, and Padua. Over 165 violins were completed using the Stradivari model with red oil varnish. They were branded: `Rodolpho Tramonti fete a Forli'.
Raffaele Trapani's craftsmanship featured very finely finished details on a distinctive personal model with a large form. The full arch, of medium height, was inspired by Maggini.
Hermann Trapp studied under Pfretzschner. He worked at several shops before finally establishing himself in Wildstein in 1893. Medals were awarded to him at Paris, Vienna, Linz, and Prague.

Hans Trautner was a hairdresser and an amateur maker in Ansbach starting in 1898. He established himself professionally in Bandelstrasse in 1907. His work was inconsistent, and he created a personal model.

Giuseppe Traverso trained as a carpenter. He produced 18 violins, three cellos, three guitars, and a mandolin. His personal model featured red spirit varnish and had ugly heads. A manuscript label was present.
Jean Trocard worked typically in Mirecourt, featuring pale yellow varnish and painted purfling. The back button was branded with a fleur-de-lys.
Carlo Troiani made musical instruments in Rome in the year 1885.

Silvio Tua studied under L. Bisiach in Milan from 1921. From 1922 to 1929, he worked for Radrizzani in Vevey. He later established himself independently in Nice, assisted by his son Sergio Tua. He completed over 150 violins using Guarneri and personal models, utilizing fine materials and orange varnish.

The Tubbs bow making dynasty stretched over five generations, from the 1800s to the early 20th century, and was based in London. Thomas Tubbs, the first of the family whose bows can be identified, was active during the early 19th century and his son, William, continued the craft, probably receiving training from Edward Dodd as well as his father.

Alfred Tubbs was the son and pupil of James Tubbs and was active throughout his life in his father's shop, with most of his work bearing James' brand. He briefly ran the business between his father's retirement around 1888 and his own death in 1909, which led to James' return to work.

Charles Edward Tubbs is the son and pupil of William Tubbs. He works with his brother Edward in Castle Street starting in 1878, moving to 22 St Martin's Street around 1882. He subsequently leaves to open a general music shop in St. Austell.

Edward Tubbs, the third son and pupil of William Tubbs , began assisting his father William around 1850 and trained as a bow maker. He established his business at 38 Castle Street, London, in 1872, but emigrated to New York in 1878, where he was joined ... by his brother John in 1888.

James Tubbs was one of the finest and most prolific bow makers in the history of British violin making. His work rivals that of the finest French makers, and he is said to have made thousands of bows in his lifetime.

First in a line of important English bow makers that gradually succeeded the Dodd family, Thomas Tubbs probably trained with either James II or Edward Dodd I. He set up his own workshop around 1830, where he worked until 1863, supplying bows to various ... shops, including the Dodd's, John Betts , and Thomas Kennedy , which bear those makers' brands.

William Tubbs was the son and pupil of British bowmaker Thomas Tubbs and trained with James and Edward Dodd (II), becoming the successor to Edward Dodd III in 1851 after his death. He worked at several addresses in Lambeth and Soho, including Crescent Street Euston in 1841, 19 and later 41 Princes Street Soho in 1850, 45 Rupert Street in 1857, and finally at 1 Rupert Court from 1863.

Antonio Tunioli was a self-taught maker. He completed over 150 instruments, which included many ancient and plucked examples. His violins were based on the models of Sofritti and Gotti. He received medals at Florence in 1955, Bagnacavallo in 1967, and Cremona in 1973.

British bow maker. Started career as an engineering patternmaker (in wood) and taught woodwork, metalwork and engineering drawing. Turned to bow making after meeting Arthur Bultitude in the late 1970s. Makes baroque and modern bows.

William Turner worked in Aldgate, London, around 1650. Several fine viols existed in various collections. Most of them were characterized by a heart-shaped rose in the table and single broad purflings. These features were also noted on a viol by C. Wise, suggesting a possible connection between the two makers. No authenticated violins were known.
Charles L. Tweedale was a parish priest who claimed to have received the recipe for Stradivari's varnish from the spirit world.
Herbert William Tyson worked on classical Italian models. He was quite prolific and used Millington's oil varnish, which had a golden-brown color.
Crescenzio Ugar was the son of Carlo Ugar from Urbino and was likely of German descent. He was first recorded in the Roman archives as a violin maker in SS.
Raised in Virginia, Christopher Ulbricht is the owner and luthier of Indianapolis Violins. He studied under Charles Woolf, Peter Prier, and Michael Vann.

Nicolò Ulcigrai was active in Trieste from 1912 and moved to Venice in 1931. He ceased work during the Second World War, resuming around 1945. He completed 20 violins, five cellos, and several plucked instruments.
Georg Ullmann trained in Markneukirchen with E. R.

Antonio Ungarini created eccentric work characterized by a rather inelegant outline featuring short corners and a very full but low arching. The corners were short and broad, following the Brescian manner.
Raynaldo Ungarini was the son of Antonio Ungarini. He produced similar work, although the varnish was of lesser quality.

Very little is known about the life of Thomas Urquhart, though he was probably Scottish, and historical evidence suggests that he worked outside London and brought his instruments into the city for sale in various shops. Jacob Rayman is the only known maker in London who preceded him, as Urquhart's first verifiable instrument ... dates from the 1660s, and his work may even go as far back as the 1640s.

Eraldo Usuelli was a pupil of Paolo Erba. He established himself at Mariano Comense in 1913. His work was fine and accurate, utilizing excellent materials and a good golden-brown oil varnish. Some examples featured decorative inlay.

Utili was a guitarist who taught himself to make instruments. He initially specialised in guitars and mandolins before turning to violins. His instruments feature a low arch, a good-quality oil varnish and excellent materials. He experimented with models and invented a cornerless violin. Awarded first prize for a quartet in Turin, 1928.
Raffaele Vaccari was a pupil of C. G.
Geza Vadon studied under G. Varady at his Grosswardein Violin Making School in Budapest from 1923 to 1927.

Born in Asti in the mid 18th century, Giovanni Maria Valenzano was perhaps the most itinerant violin maker in Italian history. According to his labels, over the course of his career Valenzano worked in at least seven cities in three different countries, and he allegedly made keyboard instruments of all kinds in addition to guitars and violins.
Petrus Valenzano was presumed to be a brother of Giovanni Maria Valenzano, although no evidence is known. He mostly made plucked instruments, but a few violins are ascribed to him.
Bow maker / Violin maker (c. 1913 – 1930)
Bow maker / Violin maker (fl. 1892 – 1920)

Alphonse van Hoof trained in Markneukirchen. He worked in 'S Hertogenbosch, Holland in 1920, Borgerhout near Antwerp in 1923, and later in Tilburg and Antwerp. He was credited with approximately 150 instruments, which were closely observed copies of various Italian masters. Aures te Fidibus Juret Oblec / tare Canoris / Alphonsius Van Hoof / Liutaro fecit Antverpiae 19..
Dr van Leeuwen was a doctor of medicine at The Hague. He was an experimental maker and received a silver medal for tone at Brussels in 1910.

Pier (Pietro) Lorenzo Vangelisti exhibited some influence from Stradivari in the scroll and soundholes, while belonging to the School of Gabrielli. The arching was of medium height, yet it appeared full and box-like with a deep and narrow edge flute.
Contemporary maker, who started career in 1975. Trained in New York with William Salchow. Now based in British Columbia, Canada, and at Victoria Conservatory of Music.
Gyula Várady trained as an artist painter. He was introduced to violin making in the workshop of G.
Ferrucio Varagnolo was a talented maker from Padua who apprenticed with Leandro Bisiach in Milan. Unfortunately, he died early in his mid-30s after being committed to an asylum.
Nicholas Vasich studied under Bela Truppel in Novi Sad, Serbia before attending school in Markneukirchen, Germany. He later worked for Jan Dvorak and D.
Marcel Vatelot was born to parents from Mirecourt and was the great grandson of Blaise, a violin maker from Lorraine. He apprenticed with E.
Joseph Vautrin apprenticed as a violin maker to Chipot-Vuillaume in Mirecourt. He was appointed head of the workshop of Emile Germain in Paris.

Alfons Frantisek Vavra, son and pupil of Jana Baptisty Vávra, began working independently in Banská Bystrica, Slovakia, in 1920 under his father's name. He was joined by his brother Karel until 1925, after which he returned to Prague and opened a shop at 30 Jindrisska Street, where he remained for the rest of his life.
Son and pupil of Alfons Frantisek Vavra Sr. Together with his sister he worked for his father until his death. He emigrated to the USA in 1968.
Josef Vávra was the nephew of Jan Baptista Vávra and a pupil from 1927 to 1931.
Karel Vávra worked with his cousin Alfons Vávra in Banská Bystrica until 1931. He established himself independently in Korunni Street, Prague, in 1932.

Lorenzo Ventapane, nephew of Vincenzo Ventapane , was a significant maker in the Neapolitan tradition, presumed to be a pupil of Giovanni or Nicolo (II) Gagliano. He established his workshop in Calata Borgo di Loreto and later at 35 Strada Donnaregina, Naples, from 1800.
Pasquale Ventapane was possibly the son or brother of Lorenzo Ventapane . He claimed to have been a pupil of Antonio Gagliano according to his labels.
Vincenzo Ventapane was the presumed uncle of Lorenzo Ventapane . He was a pupil of Antonio and Raffaele Gagliano.
Verbeek was the stepson of Henryk Jacobs, from his first marriage. He worked in Jacobs' shop alongside Pieter Rombouts, another stepson from Jacobs' second marriage.
F. Verhasselt worked at the sign 'A Ste Cécile' from 1820 to 1850. His work was from Mirecourt, possibly imported and labeled by him.
Andrea Verini was the son and pupil of Serafino Verini. He created Guarneri models that featured red varnish.

Carlo Vettori was born in 1940 in Firenzuola, a mountain town between Florence and Bologna. The son of master violin maker Dario I Vettori, he began his training at a young age in the family workshop and later refined his skills with master luthier Giuseppe Ornati.
Dario Vettori was a violinist and a member of the Benelli Quartet. He was a pupil of P.
The eldest son of Paolo Vettori and grandson of Dario Vettori.

François Vial was a pupil of T. Hüfner. He established himself professionally in Monaco in 1902. In 1910, he received a gold medal in Brussels for a quartet. His work featured a Stradivari-derived model and demonstrated good workmanship. It was signed internally: 'Fait par François Vial / Luthier a Monaco / année 19.. F. Vial'.

Giuseppe Viccari was an amateur maker of six violins based on the Stradivari model. He may also have been the same person as Rustom Vacaji, who was listed in Henley as a resident of Hampstead, London, in 1911; he was the inventor of a tubular 'tone improving' device.

J. E. Vickers was a violin and bow maker. He completed around 40 violins, 14 violas, six bows, and ten guitars, using Stradivari-derived violin models with oil varnish. Each instrument was signed and dated internally, with a label printed in blue.
Alfred Vidoudez was a pupil of G. Bazin in Mirecourt.
Pierre Vidoudez was the son and successor of Alfred Vidoudez . He graduated from business school in Geneva before attending the Mittenwald School of Violin Making.
Bernard Viedenhofer was a pupil of A. Hüber. His work on Guarneri and Maggini models was inconsistent. He used a good golden-brown varnish, which sometimes featured a crackled texture.

Holm Viertel was a pupil of R. Paulus. He worked for Riechers in Berlin before establishing himself independently in Markneukirchen in 1888. In 1896, he moved to Aachen, returning to Markneukirchen in 1915. His work on the Stradivari model was notable, utilizing Riecher's spirit and oil varnish. He was assisted by his sons, Karl Georg and Walter.
Karl Georg Viertel was the son, pupil, and assistant of Holm Viertel. He established himself independently in Markneukirchen and qualified in the luthiers corporation in 1947.

Giuseppe Vignali was a cooper by profession and an amateur luthier of some distinction. He received honors at exhibitions in Rome, Milan, and Palermo. His instruments featured a Stradivari-derived model with fine varnish.

Born in Mirecourt, bowmaker André Vigneron began to assist his father Joseph Arthur Vigneron at his shop in Paris at the end of the 19th century. His model at first hewed closely to that of his father, with somewhat thinner heads, and the two worked well together until the father's death in 1905.

Step-son of Mirecourt bow maker Claude Nicolas Husson , Joseph Arthur Vigneron apprenticed under his step-father until his death in 1872, when he began to work for Jean-Joseph Martin . In 1880 Vigneron left for Paris to work for the firm of Gand ... & Bernardel until 1888.
Luigi Villa was a self-taught violin maker. He was active from 1923 and later studied with Pallavicini in Boviso Masciago. He exhibited at the 2nd Triennale in Cremona.

Italian violin maker who studied with Giorgio Scolari and Stefano Conia at the Cremona violin making school. Now works in Cremona and has received medals for his instruments from various competitions, including the Cremona Triennale of 1991.

Alexis Villaume worked for C. Aubert in Troyes until 1791, when he joined his colleague from the workshop, Charles Giron, to establish the company Villaume et Giron. He produced violins, guitars, and strings, demonstrating good consistent workmanship on classical models.

Gustave Villaume studied under Mougenot and Jacquet Gand in Mirecourt. He worked for Caressa and Français in Paris, as well as Jacquot in Nancy. He received first prizes in Mirecourt in 1911, 1912, and 1913. His instruments followed the Guarneri model and featured fine oil varnish in suitable shades.
Vinaccia was a family of luthiers in Naples, Italy. They were chiefly known for making mandolins, but they also produced some very good violins in the Neapolitan style of the Gagliano family.

Antonio Vinaccia was the son, pupil, and assistant of Gennaro Vinaccia . He established himself independently in via Constantii from 1756, but evidence from known labels indicated that he returned to his father's workshop in rua Catalana around 1770.
Gaetano Vinaccia was the son of Antonio Vinaccia . He was active in the Rua Catalana, Naples, starting in 1779.

Gennaro Vinaccia was a senior member of this family of mandolin and violin makers. He established his business in Naples on rua Catalana at the sign 'Cremona. ' He was the father of Antonio, Giovanni, and Vincenzo. His fine violins and cellos were crafted in the Gagliano style, featuring dark golden-yellow or brown varnish of good texture.
Pasquale Vinaccia was regarded as the final of all mandolin makers. He was succeeded by his sons Gennaro (II) and Achille.

Vincenzo Vinaccia was the son, pupil, and assistant of Gennaro Vinaccia. He worked at the family shop located at rua Catalana, which later became known as 20 Calata de l'Ospedaletto. He created decorative mandolins and several fine violins in the Gagliano style, with some showing a Guarneri influence.
Alfred Vincent was the first violin at His Majesty's Theatre and a violin maker. He was active in London starting in 1902 and was later assisted by his brother.

Giovanni Virzi, the son and pupil of Rosario Virzi, emigrated to the USA in 1912 and worked in New York with his brother Joseph Virzi from 1913. He established his own business in 1934, creating instruments based on Stradivari, Guarneri, and Amati models, characterized by his distinctive golden-orange oil varnish.
Younger brother of Giovanni Virzi. Emigrated to New York around 1913.

Louis Vissenaire established himself in Lyons around 1823. He was a prolific maker known for a large model featuring broad open soundholes and a bright red and red-brown varnish. He also created various types of plucked instruments.

Louis Nicolas Vissenaire was the younger son of Louis Vissenaire (I) and served as a violinist in the orchestra at the Grand Theatre in Lyons. He succeeded to his father's business alongside his elder brother, Louis Vissenaire II , and his work was characterized by fine professional accuracy on a large model.

Luigi Vistoli established himself independently from 1910 after being a pupil of Nicola Utili. He spent several years in Venice and was a prolific maker. His work featured an accurate Stradivari model and fine varnish of his own creation in suitable colors, utilizing first quality materials.
Worked as a musician and made a large number of cello bows in the 1930s. Mostly self-taught.

Dominicus C. Vlummens was a pupil of his father, Isidor Vlummens, an amateur repairer. He worked for various shops in London and established himself independently in 1926. His work featured Stradivari and Guarneri models, showcasing good professional workmanship and shades of golden-brown varnish. He was chiefly occupied with restoration and repair work. Dominicus Vlummens + / London 1927 D. V.

Johann Georg Vogler was a violinist and cellist in the Würzburg court orchestra. He was the father of composer Abbé Georg Joseph Vogler. He trained as a luthier in Füssen. His instruments featured large forms and exhibited very finely finished and accurate workmanship. They included a high-arched Stainer model with a thin dark opaque red-brown varnish.

Arnold Voigt was a violin and bow maker, a pupil of H. T. Heberlein Jr. and Schüneman. He relocated to London in 1885 before establishing his own shop in Gartenstrasse, Markneukirchen, where he worked independently from 1890 to 1927. Voigt was a prolific maker of classical Cremonese models and utilized his own oil and spirit varnishes of golden-yellow color. His bows were branded: A. V.
The company was listed at 51 Monmouth Street, Cambridge Circus, and later in Sussex. It primarily focused on imported trade work.

Paul Voigt was the son of Paul Arno Voigt. He apprenticed in Markneukirchen and returned to his father's workshop in Manchester. Subsequently, he opened his own business on Shaftesbury Avenue in London, assisted by his brother Ernst and later by Ernst's son, Peter Ernest.

Simon Voigt was the presumed son of Johann Georg Voigt. He entered the corporation of luthiers in 1732. His work featured a large broad pattern and utilized dark red-brown or golden-brown varnish. Good materials were used in his creations.

Walter Voigt was the son of Johann Hermann Voigt. He worked for M. Bruckner and M. Otto in Markneukirchen. In 1933, he succeeded his father in Stuttgart. He became a master in the luthiers' corporation in 1939.

Werner August Theodor Voigt studied under H. Reichel. He worked with H. Werro in Berne and F. Hamma in Stuttgart. He later gained experience with Leistner in Nuremburg and Kochendorfer in Stuttgart. He returned to Markneukirchen to serve as the director of the M. G. Voigt company.
Wilhelm Johannes Voigt was the son of William Albin. He studied under R. B. Krauss. His work was of good professional quality.

After his initial training and early work in Mirecourt, François Nicolas Voirin moved to Paris in 1855 to join his cousin Jean Baptiste Vuillaume . His work in the Vuillaume workshop gave him access to distinguished bow makers of the Peccatte school, ... but Voirin was inspired largely by the work of Pierre Simon and of Vuillaume himself.
Brother of Francois Nicolas Voirin. Bow maker.
Charles Pierre Voiry trained in Mirecourt. He established himself in Toulouse in 1925. His workmanship was notable on Cremonese models. A dark red oil varnish was used.

Perhaps the most gifted copyists in the history of violin making, the Voller brothers William, Charles, and Alfred are the objects of equal admiration for their tremendous talents and contempt for their nefarious business practices. They began in the London shop of George Hart and Son, where they were tasked with creating copies of important instruments that passed through the firm, which were always ... clearly labeled as such.

Stephan von Baehr began his instrument-making studies in 1988 in Markneukirchen, then went on to learn with Reinhard Bönsch in southern Germany. Further working experiences with Andreas Kägi and Christoph Götting inspired him and impacted his making.
Arthur Voss was a pupil of August Diehl . He worked in various locations across Germany before spending eight years with O.

Claude-François Vuillaume was the son of Charles-François Vuillaume (III) and the father of Jean-Baptiste. He was associated with coarse commercial work from the Mirecourt school. The arch resembled that of High Stainer. He was branded: 'Claude Vuillaume / Mirecourt /1820'.

In addition to being a renowned and prolific maker of fine violins, Jean Baptiste Vuillaume was a highly successful dealer, connoisseur and inventor. His workshop in Paris employed and trained some of the finest 19th-century violin and bow makers, including Maucotel , Silvestre , ...

Nicolas Vuillaume was the third son of Claude-François Vuillaume (IV). He worked for his brother Jean-Baptiste from 1832 to 1842, then returned to Mirecourt to produce student-grade instruments known as 'Stentor. ' These instruments were primarily made by A.

Nicolas François Vuillaume was the fourth son and pupil of Claude François Vuillaume (IV) and the younger brother of Jean-Baptiste Vuillaume . He worked in Paris alongside his brother Jean-Baptiste before establishing himself in Brussels around 1828, initially at 11 rue Mamix and later at 30 rue de l'Evêque.
Sebastien Vuillaume was the son and pupil of Claude-François Vuillaume (V). He worked for his uncle, Jean-Baptiste.

Anton Wachter was a prolific maker. His models were generally derived from Stradivari, although they exhibited strong Tyrolean influences. He sold through dealers and wholesalers. The soundholes were inspired by Stainer. He used good materials and applied a deep golden-brown varnish.
Lorenz Wachter crafted viols, violins, and cellos in Germany. The work was neatly done and featured an opaque yellow-brown varnish, following a Tyrolean model.
Benedikt Wagner worked in Dürrwangen around 1750, according to some sources. His work on the Stainer model was very precise and commendable.

Joseph Wagner was appointed luthier to the court in 1774. His work from Tyrol featured a medium high arch and neat, precise craftsmanship. The soundholes were wide-set Stainer style, and the varnish had a slightly opaque yellow-brown hue. He used pear wood for the scrolls, which were nicely concentric but had a prominent eye.
Sebastian Wagner was the brother of Joseph Wagner . His fine work featured a slightly elongated Stainer model, characterized by rather flat curves into the corners from the upper and lower bouts.
Xavier Wagner was the son, pupil, and successor of Benedict Wagner. He created a flat-arched model using plain materials and a thick red-brown varnish.

Eugen Wahl was a wood sculptor and violinist who spent some time studying in Switzerland. He became an amateur violin maker in 1908 and established himself professionally in Kreuzstrasse, Karlsruhe, from 1909. A master's certificate was obtained in 1913. His work included Stradivari, Guarneri, and personal models, using spirit varnish in appropriate shades and fine wood.
Born in Illinois. Studied with Carl Becker.
William Walker was a hairdresser and self-taught violin maker. He produced over 200 instruments based on Stradivari and Guarneri models by reading Heron-Allen Violin Making.

William Walton was a retired stationmaster and a self-taught violin and bow maker who began in 1887. He established himself professionally in 1922. His personal model, derived from Stradivari and Guarneri, exhibited careful workmanship. He used his own amber oil varnish, which featured red-brown shades over a yellow ground. The bows were not branded or marked in any distinctive manner.

The tradition of great English cellos starts with Peter Wamsley, who worked in London in the early 1700s. His workshop had various names over the course of his career, all of them with the word "harp," which was typical of the period.
Michael Wanner produced fine work that was superior to much of what came from the town during this period.

George Ward produced fine work in the style of Perry, with whom he may have been associated. Perry held the Angelsey Street address listed on Ward's label from 1771. The varnish and materials were of very good quality. One example was in the Museum of Dublin.

Albert E. Warrick, a pupil and assistant of G. A. Chanot in Manchester, established himself independently in Leeds from 1889. He was known for his classical Cremonese models, showcasing fine and professional craftsmanship, and he utilized his own varnish in various suitable shades. Warrick was awarded a gold medal at the Leeds International Exhibition in 1895.
Joseph Wassermann produced good and prolific work on classical models, especially Amati. Varnish featured shades of orange-brown and yellow-brown. He also recorded violas and viols.
Frank Watson created over 200 instruments that featured orange shades of varnish.
The last pupil of William Retford , William Watson worked for the firm of W.E. Hill & Sons from 1945 to 1962.
Robert J. Webb worked in London. He produced good, accurate work on the Stradivari model and used his own golden or red-brown varnish. He created Tertis model violas.

Orrin Weeman worked for A. W. White from 1869 to 1872. He established himself independently in 1872, with assistance from his son starting in 1900, and partnered with two musicians as Weeman, Beal & Holmberg from 1910 until his retirement in 1919. The company continued operations until 1938. He was a prolific maker of violins, cellos, violas, viols, and viola d'amores.
Roelof Weertman trained as a naval architect. He became an amateur maker around 1915, receiving guidance from Hanken in Rotterdam.
Richard Weichold was a violin and bow maker. He was the son of August Weichold and a pupil of Pfretzschner in Markneukirchen.

Richard Weidemann worked for Sutterlin and later established himself independently. He created models based on Stradivari and Guarneri. His personal model was from 1916. He used fine varnish in various shades.
Ewald Weidhaas was a bow maker. He produced good gold-mounted dark brown octagonal sticks. His work was branded: E. WEIDHAAS.

Paul Weidhaas worked for Liebig in Wroclaw and Winterling in Hamburg. He established himself in Markneukirchen with S. Finkel, creating fine copies of master French models. Bows were supplied to J. Stiiber of The Hague. He branded his work: LA HAYE.
Johann Blasius Weigert made violins, viols, and plucked instruments. His violins were without distinction and featured a dull brown varnish.
Eugenio Weiss was a professor of the violin at Trieste from 1880. He was a self-taught maker who created personal models and used shaded dark varnish.
All types of plucked and bowed instruments were known in various civic collections. Violins held little interest due to their meager varnish.
Johannes (Hans) Michael Weisshaar studied at the Mittenwald School. He subsequently pursued acoustics at Basle University.

Frederick Weller was a coffin maker and a self-taught luthier. He completed approximately 200 instruments by 1952, utilizing models derived from Stradivari, Guarneri, and Rocca, and applied his own varnish in a wide range of shades.
Frederick Wilhelm Weller was the son and pupil of Carl Wilhelm Weller. He established himself independently in 1881 and later relocated to Leipzig, where he worked on the Arndstrasse.
Gregor Ferdinand Wenger was active in Augsburg from 1701, when he married the widow of J. Fichtl.

Francis Werentle was presumed to be a member of the Werendle/Wörnle family of Mittenwald, representing an important link between the two similar schools of work in London and Germany. His work featured a fine Stainer model, allied in style to Thomas Smith or John Johnson.
Henry Werro was the eldest son of Jean Werro . He studied at Markneukirchen under August Wunderlich from 1910 to 1914.
Jean Werro was a pupil of Leon Fischesser in Geneva. He established himself in Islington, north London, in 1890.

Walter William Whitbread was an amateur violinist. He created personal models as well as those derived from Stradivari. His work was inconsistent. The varnish he used was brown, consisting of both oil and spirit.
Asa Warren White was the son and pupil of John White . He worked for Henry Prentiss and formed a partnership with his brother Ira in Boston from 1849 to 1863.
Ira Johnson White was the son of John White and was active from 1825. He moved to Boston in 1829.
John White was the earliest violin maker established in Massachusetts in 1802. He moved to Barre in 1806 and made approximately a dozen violins.
Nehemiah White created American 'church basses' and double basses. He utilized local materials and incorporated inked purfling.
Edwin Whitmarsh was the son and assistant of Emanuel Whitmarsh. His work was generally considered inferior, but it was rarely found with his own label.

Emanuel Whitmarsh was a stonemason and self-taught violin maker who worked in Berwick Street, London from 1856 to 1885, where he repaired instruments for various dealers and produced a significant number of violins of good trade quality, utilizing various models and both oil and spirit varnish. In 1885, he moved to Kentish Town Road, followed by a relocation to Hackney in 1887, and finally established ... himself in Dalston in 1908, assisted by his son Edwin.

Maurice Whittaker was a musical instrument dealer. Instruments associated with his name are rare and typically exhibit the anonymous appearance characteristic of London trade work from that period, suggesting he may have also been a maker of original work. In 1782, John Betts took over his premises or the rooms sharing the same address.

Milton O. Wickes worked with Andrew Hyde in Northampton until 1904. He established himself independently in Holyoke and returned to Northampton in 1911. His models were derived from Amati and Stradivari. He used various shades of oil varnish.

Anton Widhalm was the third son and pupil of Leopold Widhalm. He worked with G. D. Buchstetter in Regensburg, taking over the business in 1771. His early work was closely modeled on Stainer, but later instruments showed evident Italian influence. He demonstrated fine craftsmanship with dark transparent red-brown varnish applied over a dark ground. His brand was circled by foliage and read: 'AM'.

Gallus Ignatius Widhalm was the second son, pupil, and assistant of Leopold Widhalm. Most experts generally praised him, but no instruments with his personal label are known, as he worked for either his father or elder brother, both of whom used the Leopold Widhalm label. Vannes mentioned cellos resembling those of Guadagnini.
Leopold Widhalm was generally recognized as the best German maker following Stainer. His model was very distinctive and graceful, featuring a medium high arch and deeply fluted edges.

Martin Leopold (Leopold II) Widhalm was the son, pupil, and successor of Leopold Widhalm and continued with his father's label. His work was generally less refined than that of his father, featuring higher arching and slightly uneven purfling.
Ernest E. Wild was a violinist, violin maker, and repairer who was active in Billings before relocating to San Francisco around 1956.

Franz Anton Wild created unattractive Stainer copies. They were well made but featured a high arch, deeply worked edge flute, and thin dark opaque varnish. Henley noted that the ground consisted of a glue base, which was notorious for its vulnerability to water and lack of adhesion.
Albin Wilfer descended from a line of amateur luthiers. He studied under E.
William Wilkanowski was a Polish maker active from 1895 who emigrated to the USA in 1920. He worked for Oliver Ditson in Boston and later for Gretsch in New York starting in 1938.

admin2_name: Lower Connecticut River Valley Planning Region

info: Violinist-turned-maker, who carried out restorations for and sold instruments through J.&A. Beare Ltd in London for four decades.

Samuel Blakely Wilkinson worked at 'Ye Olde Fiddle Shoppe. ' He had inconsistent work on models derived from Stradivari. The materials were generally good, but the varnish varied in both colour and success.
William Wilkinson was the son-in-law, apprentice, and successor to Thomas Perry. He entered into partnership with Perry in 1790 and took over the business following Perry's death in 1818.
Hendrick Willems was presumed to be the son of Jooris Willems. Known or recorded are three large violas, a cello, a pochette, and a bass viol.

Johann Michael Willer granted citizenship in Prague in 1780 and became head of the luthiers guild in 1825. He successfully evolved from the Stainer-Tyrolean model of his early work to an elegant Amatise Stradivari model, while still retaining a slightly exaggerated edge flute to the arch.

Arthur Williams was a piano-tuner and repairer. He was taught violin making by Frank Howard. Active in Barnes, London, from 1924, he demonstrated very precise workmanship on models derived from Stradivari and Guarneri. His work featured distinctive arching with deeply worked edges. The pegbox sides were hollowed in the manner of Degani, and he used an orange-brown varnish.

R. J. Williams was a self-taught amateur maker active from 1895 in Llandudno, who later established himself professionally. He produced quality work on classical models featuring a clear light golden-brown varnish. In 1918, he received a silver medal at the Royal National Eisteddfod.
Josiah John Willmore studied under Frank Howard. He utilized Stradivari and Guarneri models. The varnish was a red-brown oil type.
Derek Wilson was trained as a bow maker and repairer in the bow department of the firm of W.E. Hill & Sons in the latter part of the 20th century. He set up his own business in 1991.

One of the last of the Hill bow makers, Garner Wilson joined the W.E. Hill & Sons firm aged 16, receiving his training from William Watson and Arthur Bultitude , with frequent ... advice from William Retford .
Bow maker / Violin maker (1864 – after 1929)
James L. Wilson studied under J.
The model was a flat-arched Stradivari. It featured decent work with yellow-orange varnish and was in the style of Hardie, possibly indicating a pupil. The name was branded on the back button.

Otto Windisch apprenticed to Himmer. He worked for Glaesel and Herwig. From 1886, he established himself independently in Schoneck. His workshop grew into one of the major stringed instrument manufacturing businesses in the region. He retired in 1903, passing the management of the firm to his son Paul Windt.

Franz Winkler was a bow maker employed by Julius Guetter from around 1900 to 1910. His personal model was known as the 'Winkler', along with Tourte and Voirin models. He branded his work as JULIUS GUETTER PHILADELPHIA PA.

Georg Winterling worked in Frankfurt, Dresden, and Vienna as a pupil of B. Klier. He was employed by Pfab in Hamburg and was awarded a diploma in 1889. From 1890, he established himself independently in Hamburg. He retired in 1920, leaving the business to his assistants Anton Schreiber and Anton Lugert. His professional work was of good quality.
Established in Vine Court, Bishopsgate from 1650, moving nearby to Half Moon Alley 1656. Returned to Vine Court 1661, working closely with G.
Edward Withers was a cellist and the son of a confectioner. He purchased the shop of R. & W.
Edward Newman (Edward II) Withers. Son and pupil of Edward (I), active from 1856.

George Thomas Henry Withers was the younger son of Edward Withers (I). In 1878, he separated from his brother and opened his own shop in St Martin's Lane, moving to 22 Leicester Square in 1891. His sons, Guarnerius and Walter George, succeeded him. He offered Stradivari and Guarneri models in various grades. Bows were branded as G. W. & S. or GEORGE WITHERS & SONS.

Joseph Withers was active from 1856 and worked for J. Brown in Spitalfields starting in 1869, later establishing himself independently in Caledonian Road, north London, in 1879. That same year, he received the first prize at the Westminster Exhibition. He completed 150 violins, 12 violas, and some cellos, with violins modeled on the Stradivari 1716 'Messie. '.

Anton Wittmann studied under E. Heidegger in Linz starting in 1892 and later with Stiibinger in Vienna from 1897. He established himself independently in Baleinestrasse in 1910. His work featured fine professional quality with richly colored oil varnish. His instruments are labeled 'Anton Wittmann Geigenmacher. / Wien in Jahre 19..'

Sebastian Wojtyszyn was the son and pupil of Gregory Wojtyszyn. He established himself in Philadelphia in 1971 and in Denver, Colorado, in 1972. He made approximately 20 violins modeled after Guarneri and four violas, using red-brown spirit varnish.
Leopold & Ferdinand Wolff established a factory in 1864. Many instruments were exported to England and labeled as if they were made there.

Daniel L. Wood recorded over loo instruments. The personal model featured Guarneri-like soundholes and utilized local materials. It had a pale brittle varnish. Some instruments were branded below the button with 'D. L. Wood. '.

C. F. Woodward uses the Stradivari 1715 `Alard' violin as a model. He completes about nine violins, nine violas based on the Tertis model, and one cello. Local maple and sycamore serve for the backs, and he applies his own oil varnish.

James Worden was a musician, cabinet maker, organ builder, and violin maker. He completed about 50 instruments, which featured models derived from Stradivari and Guarneri. The work was neatly executed and utilized commercial oil varnish in various shades. Labels included dedications to different saints and the motto "Princeps Pacis. ".

Johann Paul Wörle worked in Nüremberg, Nagysombat, and Trnava before settling in Bratislava. He became a citizen in 1811. His professional work included a flat-arched model and a high-arched model with yellow varnish.
Christoph Wörnle traded work for wholesalers. Some instruments were signed internally: `Christoph Wornle in / Mittenwald ao 1821'.
Matthias Wörnle was the son and pupil of Christoph Wornle. He engaged in trade work for wholesalers.
Anthony T. Wrona suffered a spinal injury during the Second World War. He established himself professionally as a maker and repairer in 1945. He was instructed by S. Sacconi until 1965.

Ignaz Wrozina was born and trained in Budapest. From 1885, he worked as a piano tuner for Methven and Simpson in Edinburgh. Later, he established a partnership with his son, R. A. Wrozina, in Perth. His Stradivari model violins received a medal at the Edinburgh Exhibition.
Emile Wuichet created amateur work. The piece featured classical Italian models and was finished with orange-red varnish. It was made in Rennes in 1866.
George Wulme-Hudson trained as a jeweller and violinist. He was a self-taught maker with some guidance from Thomas Jacques Holder and was active from 1897.
August Wunderlich worked for Raautmann in Magdeburg after being a pupil of L. Glaesel. He then joined F. Deubner. From 1887, he established himself independently in Markneukirchen.

Friedrich Wilhelm Wunderlich was a bow maker and a pupil of Nurnberger. He established himself in Markneukirchen and moved to Leipzig in 1898. In 1949, he relocated to Landskrona, Sweden, with his son Joachim, later residing in Malmö. He ultimately returned to Markneukirchen. His work included very fine craftsmanship on Tourte and other classical French models.

Gustav Wunderlich was a pupil of Karl Kohler in Schönbach. He was a violin and bow maker who worked in Berlin and established himself in Leipzig from 1897. He used Cremonese models and claimed to create the smallest instruments in the world, which were sold as souvenirs and curiosities. In 1926, he patented his own bow design.
Bow maker / Violin maker (1873 – after 1918)
Anton Wurmer created a Klotz school with a very high arch in Garmisch in the year 17.

Sydney Braithwaite Yeoman was a bow maker for W. E. Hill & Sons at Hanwell. He trained in the Hill workshop under W. C. Retford starting in 1891. He suffered a grievous injury during World War I, which affected his subsequent work, although finishing and correction were overseen by Retford.
The work is fairly coarse and is based on a Stradivari-derived model, finished with yellow-red spirit varnish. It is branded: 'Young ABDN'.

Marshall Youngman was a self-taught amateur maker from 1880. He established himself professionally in Halifax around 1890. He completed 36 violins, one viola dated 1917, and seven cellos. His work included Stradivari and personal models. The sound-holes featured a Guadagnini style with pear-shaped eyes. He used a fine transparent oil varnish in various shades.
Carl Zach was the son and successor of Thomas Zach . In 1897, he founded the Carl Zach Company, which had offices in Budapest, Mannheim, and Darmstadt.

Trained with J. B. Dvorak and A. Sitt in Prague. Worked in Budapest with J. B. Schweitzer, succeeding to his business in 1857. He sold the shop to Schunda 1862, moving to Subotica, Croatia, 1863, Pecs, Hungary 1864 and Bucharest, Romania 1865. There he was appointed luthier to Prince Sturdza supplying the court with.

It was not known by whom he was taught. Henley described violins and violas featuring high arches, some of which had carved children's heads and golden yellow varnish. Vannes cited only viola d'amores and viola da braccias, with one of the former located in the Berlin Music Museum and dated 1730. He worked in Breslau in the year 1732. Maximilian Zacher fecit, Wratisl. 1730.

Giuseppe Zamberti established himself independently in via Piccoli, Vicenza, after 1918. He was a pupil of Sgarabotto. Medals were awarded to him at Florence in 1929, Padua from 1930 to 1932, and Cremona in 1937. He completed a total of 67 violins, six violas, 12 cellos, and four double basses based on a Stradivari-derived model. His varnish was golden-red.
Francesco Zanardi was a pupil of I. Sderci in Florence, Italy. He primarily focused on repair and restoration, using a Stradivari-derived model. A prize was awarded to him in Bagnacavallo in 1980.

Aldo Zani was self-taught and used personal and classical models. He applied a clear yellow varnish, which sometimes appeared as a darker golden-brown. He received medals in Rome in 1956 and 1957, Florence in 1957, Ascoli Piceno in 1959, Pegi in 1960, and Bagnacavallo in 1971 and 1978. A viola and two cellos were exhibited by him at Cremona in 1937.

Ferruccio Zanier was a dentist and part-time luthier. He was active in Genoa and Trieste, creating classical models as well as some experimental types featuring flat unarched plates. He received a gold medal at Turin in 1923 and exhibited at Cremona in 1937. He authored Il Violino - I principe tecnici-costruttivi in 1934.
Active 1980s
Giacomo Zanoli was the son of Giovanni Battista Zanoli . Evidence indicated his presence in Venice in 1742, and one cello contained an internal inscription reading 'Padua 1737'.
Giovanni Battista Zanoli rarely produced work that was slightly Germanic in style and not of the same quality as that of his son, Giacomo. He was from Verona and was active in the 17th century.

Antonio Zanotti was a notable 18th-century maker based in Mantua, contemporary with Camilli. He may have received instruction from Hieronymus (II) Amati or Pietro Guarneri, who died in Mantua in 1720.

Alessandro Zanti was possibly a descendant of Antonio Zanotti . Born in Reggio and later moving to Mantua with his family, he was a professional and well-known violinist who made instruments as an amateur, of which very few survive.

Francesco Zapelli studied under Batelli in Bologna and Muschietti in Udine. He worked for F. Kresnik in Rijeka (Fiume) in 1938. In 1949, he established himself independently in Trieste. His models included personal and Guarneri designs, featuring yellow-red oil varnish. Fece Zapelli Francesco / Via Cavour No. 6. / Fiume ll. 194..
Fratelli Zari created a Stradivari model featuring golden-yellow varnish, as described by Henley. It is currently unknown and unverified.

Antonio Zatta was known as Tonino. He studied under O. Martini and G. Ornati. His work included various models inspired by Scarampella, Balestrieri, and his own designs. Instruments typically featured a large form, flat arch, and thick red-orange varnish. He was a prolific maker, having created over 600 instruments.

William H. Zeswitz was a pupil and assistant of his brother-in-law, William Moennig, for 15 years. He established himself independently in Reading, Pennsylvania, in 1927. His work generally featured Guarneri models, though he also created some Stradivari copies. He completed 25 instruments using red-brown varnish and fine materials.
Karl Otto Zimmer was the brother and pupil of Karl Zimmer. From 1885, he worked for Jüling and Weichold in Dresden.
Julius Heinrich Zimmermann established a music publishing company in St. Petersburg in 1876, eventually opening branches in Moscow in 1882, Leipzig in 1886, and Riga in 1905.
László Zsiga was the son of violin dealer and repairer Lajos Zsiga. He studied with D.
Annibale Zuffa was an amateur maker from c. 1980. In 1991, he was associated with Zuffa / 48027 Sloarolo (Ra).
J. Emil Zust was a pupil of W.

Anton Zwerger was active in Salzburg from around 1800 and illustrated a fine piccolo violin dated 1810 in Passau, Germany. His work was typical of the Mittenwald style, following the Klotz model, characterized by a medium high arch and thin red-brown varnish.

Samuel Zygmuntowicz, born 1956 in Philadelphia, was already a prize-winning sculptor before beginning his instrument-making studies at age 13. He studied violin making and restoration with Carl Becker and Rene Morel, and at the Violin Making School of America in Salt Lake City.
